Question: Let H : \[\frac{{ - {x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1\] be the hyperbola, whose eccentricity is \[\sqrt 3 \] and the length of the latus rectum is \[4\sqrt 3 .\] Suppose the point \[\left( {\alpha ,\;6} \right),\;\alpha  > 0\] lies on H. If \[\beta \] is the product of the focal distances of the point \[\left( {\alpha ,\;6} \right),\]then \[{\alpha ^2} + \beta \] is equal to :
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  H:\frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} - \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} = 1,\;e = \sqrt 3 \\
  e = \sqrt {1 + \frac{{{a^2}}}{{{b^2}}}} = \sqrt 3 \Rightarrow \frac{{{a^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 2 \\
  {a^2} = 2{b^2} \\
  length\;of\;L.R. = \frac{{2{a^2}}}{b} = 4\sqrt 3 \\
  a = \sqrt 6 \\
  P\left( {\alpha ,\;6} \right)lie\;on\;\frac{{{y^2}}}{3} - \frac{{{x^2}}}{6} = 1 \\
  12 - \frac{{{\alpha ^2}}}{6} = 1 \Rightarrow \;{\alpha ^2} = 66 \\
  Foci = \left( {0,\; \pm be} \right) = \left( {0,\;3} \right)\;\& \;\left( {0,\; - 3} \right) \\
  Let\;{d_1}\;\& \;{d_2}\;be\;focal\;{\text{distances}}\;{\text{of}}\;{\text{P}}\left( {\alpha ,\;6} \right) \\
  {d_1} = \sqrt {{\alpha ^2} + {{\left( {6 + be} \right)}^2}} ,\;{d_2} = \sqrt {{\alpha ^2} + {{\left( {6 - be} \right)}^2}} \\
  {d_1} = \sqrt {66 + 81} ,\;{d_2} = \sqrt {66 + 9} \\
  \beta = {d_1}{d_2} = \sqrt {147 \times 75} = 105 \\
  {\alpha ^2} + \beta = 66 + 105 = 171 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A small block starts slipping down from a point B on an inclined plane AB, which is making an angle \[\theta \] with the horizontal section BC is smooth and the remaining section CA is rough with a coefficient of friction \[\mu .\] It is found that the block comes to rest as it reaches the bottom (point A) of the inclined plane. If BC = 2AC, the coefficient of friction is given y \[\mu \]= k \[\tan \,\theta .\] The value of k is
Solution:
From work energy theorem
\[{W_g} + \;{W_f} = \;\Delta \,kE\]
\[mg\;3x\sin \theta - \mu mg\,\cos \theta \;x = 0 - 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;mg3x\,\sin \theta = \;\mu mg\,\cos \theta x\]
\[3\;\tan \theta = \;\mu \]
k = 3

Question: A mixture of one mole each of H<sub>2</sub>, He and O<sub>2</sub> each are enclosed in a cylinder of volume V at temperature T. If the partial pressure of H<sub>2</sub> is 2 atm, the total pressure of the gases in the cylinder is :
Solution:
According to Dalton's law of partial pressure pi = \[x_i\] \[ \times \] PT

pi = partial pressure of the ith component

\[x_i\] = mole fraction of the ith component

pT = total pressure of mixture

⇒ 2 atm = \[\left( {\frac{{{{\text{n}}_{{{\text{H}}_2}}}}}{{{{\text{n}}_{{{\text{H}}_2}}} + {{\text{n}}_{{{\text{H}}_{\text{e}}}}} + {{\text{n}}_{{{\text{O}}_2}}}}}} \right)\] \[ \times \] pT

⇒ pT = 2 atm \[ \times \] \[\frac{3}{1}\] = 6 atm

Hence, correct option is (a)

Question: When a body slides down from rest along a smooth inclined plane making an angle of $${30^o}$$ with the horizontal, it takes time T. When the same body slides down from the rest along a rough inclined plane making the same angle and through the same distance, it takes time $$\alpha T$$, where $$\alpha $$ is a constant greater than 1. The co-efficient of friction between the body and the rough plane is $$\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}\,\left( {\frac{{{\alpha ^2} - 1}}{{{\alpha ^2}}}} \right)$$ where x = ………
Solution:
On smooth incline
$$a = g\,\sin {30^o}$$
by $$S = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}$$
$$S = \frac{1}{2}\frac{g}{2}{T^2} = \frac{g}{4}{T^2}$$ ……(i)

On rough incline
$$a = g\,\sin {30^o} - \mu g\,\cos \,{30^o}$$
by $$S = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}$$
$$S = \frac{1}{4}g\left( {1 - \sqrt 3 \mu } \right){\left( {\alpha T} \right)^2}$$ …..(ii)
By (i) and (ii)
$$\eqalign{
  & \frac{1}{4}g{T^2} = \frac{1}{4}g\left( {1 - \sqrt 3 \mu } \right){\alpha ^2}{T^2} \cr
  & \Rightarrow 1 - \sqrt 3 \mu = \frac{1}{{{\alpha ^2}}} \Rightarrow \mu = \left( {\frac{{{\alpha ^2} - 1}}{{{\alpha ^2}}}} \right).\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} \cr
  & \Rightarrow x = 3.00 \cr} $$

Question: In a vernier callipers, each cm on the main scale is divided into 20 equal parts. If tenth vernier scale division coincides with nineth main scale division. Then the value of vernier contant will be ______ $ \times {10^{ - 2}}mm$.

Solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T} \sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{2} \mathrm{~g} \\

& \tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{2 \mathrm{~T}_{0}} \\
& \mathrm{~T}_{0}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{mg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: The pitch and the number of divisions, on the circular scale for a given screw gauge are 0.5 mm and 100 respectively. When the screw gauge is fully tightened without any object, the zero of its circular scale lies 3 division below the mean line. The readings of the main scale and the circular scale, for a thin sheet, are 5.5 mm and 48 respectively, the thickness of the sheet is:
Solution:
Least count of screw gauge,
\[\begin{gathered}
  LC = \frac{{{\text{Pitch}}}}{{{\text{No}}{\text{.}}\,{\text{of division}}}} \\
  \,\,\,\,  = 0.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,mm \\
  \,\,\,\, + ve\,{\text{error }} = 3 \times 0.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}mm \\
  \,\,\,{\text{Reading }} = MSR + CSR - \left( { + ve\,\,{\text{error}}} \right) \\
  \,\,\,\, = 5.5\,mm + \left( {48 \times 0.5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right) - 0.015 \\
  \,\,\,\, = 5.5\, + 0.24 - 0.015 = 5.725\,mm \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A message signal of frequency 100 MHz and peak voltage 100 V is used to execute amplitude modulation on a carrier wave of frequency 300 GHz and peak voltage 400 V.The modulation index and the difference between the two sideband frequencies respectively are:
Solution:
${f_m} = 100MHz = {10^8}Hz,{\left( {{V_m}} \right)_0} = 100V,{\left( {{V_c}} \right)_0} = 400V$
Modulation index $ = \frac{{{{\left( {{V_m}} \right)}_0}}}{{{{\left( {{V_c}} \right)}_0}}} = \frac{{100}}{{400}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$
Upper band frequency $\left( {UBF} \right) = {f_c} + {f_m}$
Lower band frequency $\left( {LBF} \right) = {f_c} - {f_m}$
$\therefore \,UBF - LBF = 2{f_m} = 2 \times {10^8}Hz$

Question:
A body of mass 1 kg rests on a horizontal floor with which it has a coefficient of static friction \[\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\] . It is desired to make the body move by applying the minimum possible force F N. The value of F will be __________. (Round off to the Nearest Integer)
[Take g = \[10m/{s^2}\]]
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  F\cos \theta = \mu N \\
  F{\text{sin}}\theta {\text{ + N = mg}} \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{F = }}\frac{{\mu mg}}{{\cos \theta + \mu \sin \theta }} \\
  {F_{\min }} = \frac{{\mu mg}}{{\sqrt {1 + {\mu ^2}} }} = \frac{{\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} \times 10}}{{\frac{2}{{\sqrt 3 }}}} = 5 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Consider the bcc unit cells of the solids 1 and 2 with the position of atoms are shown below. The radius of atom B is twice that of atom A. The unit cell edge length is 50% more in solid 2 than in 1. What is the approximate packing efficiency in solid 2?
Solution:
Volume occupied by atoms in solid 2
$ = \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^2} + \frac{4}{3}\pi {\left( {2r} \right)^3} = 12\pi {r^3}$
Relationship between body diagonal and radius of atom (r),
$\begin{gathered}
  6r = \sqrt 3 a \\
   \Rightarrow a = \frac{{6r}}{{\sqrt 3 }} \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{Packing efficiency}} = \frac{{{\text{vol}}{\text{. of particles}}}}{{{\text{vol}}{\text{. of unit cells}}}} \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{Packing efficiency}} = \frac{{12\pi {r^3}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{{6r}}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^3}}} \times 100 = 90\% \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: If K1 and K2 are the thermal conductivities L1 and L2 are the lengths and A1 and A2 are the cross sectional areas of steel and copper rods respectively such that \[\frac{{{K_2}}}{{{K_1}}} = 9,\;\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}} = 2,\;\frac{{{L_1}}}{{{L_2}}} = 2\]. Then, for the arrangement as shown in the figure. The value of temperature T of the steel – copper junction in the steady state will be :
Solution:
\[\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}} = \frac{{{K_1}{A_1}}}{{{I_1}}}\left( {{T_1} - T} \right) = \frac{{{K_2}{A_2}}}{{{I_2}}}\left( {T - {T_2}} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\frac{{450 - T}}{{T - 0}} = \frac{{{K_2}{A_2}{I_1}}}{{{K_1}{A_1}{I_2}}} = 9 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 2\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;450 - T = 9T \Rightarrow T = 45^\circ C\]

Question: During an adiabatic process, the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of its absolute temperature. The ratio of $\frac{{{C_p}}}{{{C_v}}}$ for the gas is:
Solution:
$P \propto {T^3} \Rightarrow P{T^{ - 3}} = $constant
$P{V^\gamma } = $const
$P{\left( {\frac{{nRT}}{P}} \right)^\gamma } = {\text{const}}$
${P^{1 - \gamma }}{T^\gamma } = $const
$P{T^{\frac{\gamma }{{1 - \gamma }}}} = $ const
$\frac{\gamma }{{1 - \gamma }} = - 3$
$\gamma = - 3 + 3\gamma $
$3 = 2\gamma $
$\gamma = \frac{3}{2}$

Question: A bead $P$ sliding on a frictionless semi-circular string $(A C B)$ and it is at point $S$ at $\mathrm{t}=0$ and at this instant the horizontal component of its velocity is $v$. Another bead $Q$ of the same mass as $P$ is ejected from point $A$ at $\mathrm{t}=0$ along the horizontal string $A B$, with the speed $v$, friction between the beads and the respective strings may be neglected in both cases. Let $t_{p}$ and $t_{Q}$ be the respective times taken by beads $P$ and $Q$ to reach the point $B$, then the relation between $t_{p}$ and $t_{Q}$ is
Solution:
Horizontal displacement of Q is more then P .
$$
X_{Q}>X_{P}
$$
Horizontal component of velocity is same
So $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{v}}$
$$
\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{Q}}}{\mathrm{v}}
$$
$\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{Q}}>\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{p}}$

Question: A hyperbola has transverse axis along the major axis of the conic, $\frac{{{x^2}}}{3} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{4} = 4$ and has vertices at the foci of this conic. If the eccentricity of the hyperbola is $\frac{3}{2}\,,$ then which of the following points does NOT lie on it ?
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

$\frac{{{x^2}}}{3} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{4} = 4 \Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2}}}{{12}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{16}} = 1$
This is an ellipse whose major axis is along Y-axis and foci $\left( {0, \pm \sqrt {16 - 12} } \right)$ i.e. $\left( {0, \pm 2} \right)$
Hence, for hyperbola transverse axis is along Y-axis and vertices $\left( {0, \pm 2} \right)$
So, equation of hyperbola is
$\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{4} = - 1$
Also, eccentricity of the hyperbola is $\frac{3}{2}$
${a^2} = 4\left( {\frac{9}{4} - 1} \right) = 5$
Hence, equation of hyperbola becomes
$\frac{{{x^2}}}{5} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{4} = - 1$
Clearly point $\left( {5,2\sqrt 3 } \right)$ does not lies on it.

Question: Two forces having magnitude A and $\frac{A}{2}$ are perpendicular to each other. The magnitude of their resultant is:
Solution:

$\left| {\vec F} \right| = \sqrt {F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2{F_1}{F_2}\cos 90^\circ } $
$ = \sqrt {{A^2} + \frac{{{A^2}}}{4}} = \frac{{A\sqrt 5 }}{2}$

Question: If the line y = mx + c is a common tangent to the hyperbola \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{100}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{64}} = 1\]and the circle x2 + y2 = 36, then which one of the following is true?
Solution:
For the hyperbola
\[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{100}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{64}} = 1\]
\[\therefore \] The equation of tangent is
\[y = mx \pm \sqrt {{a^2}{m^2} - {b^2}} \]
\[y = mx \pm \sqrt {100{m^2} - 64} \]
Equation of tangent to the circle \[{x^2} + {y^2} = 36\] is
\[y = mx \pm a\sqrt {1 + {m^2}} \]
\[y = mx \pm 6\sqrt {1 + {m^2}} \]
Given,
\[mx \pm \sqrt {100{m^2} - 64} = mx \pm 6\sqrt {1 + {m^2}} \]
\[100{m^2} - 6a = 36{m^2} + 36\]
\[64{m^2} = 100\]
\[m = \pm \frac{{10}}{8}\]
Here,
\[c = \pm \sqrt {100{m^2} - 64} \]
\[{c^2} = 100 \times \frac{{100}}{{64}} - 64\]
\[{c^2} = \frac{{\left( {164} \right).\left( {36} \right)}}{{64}}\]
\[4{c^2} = 369\]

Question: The stream of a river is flowing with a speed of 2 km/h. A swimmer can swim at a speed of 4 km/h. What should be the direction of the swimmer with respect to the flow of the river to cross the river straight?
Solution:
To cross the river straight
$\begin{gathered}
  {v_s}\sin \theta = {v_r}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\sin \theta = \frac{{{v_r}}}{{{v_s}}} = \frac{2}{4} = \frac{1}{2} \\
   \Rightarrow \,\theta = 30^\circ \\
\end{gathered} $
Direction of swimmer with respect to flow
$ = 90^\circ + 30^\circ = 120^\circ $

ae = \[\sqrt {10} \] and $\frac{{\text{a}}}{{\text{e}}} = \frac{9}{{10}}$

Question: Rods $x$ and $y$ of equal dimensions but of different materials are joined as shown in figure. Temperatures of end points $A$ and $F$ are maintained at $100{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $40{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. Given the thermal conductivity of rod x is three times of that of rod y , the temperature at junction points $B$ and $E$ are (close to) :
Solution:
Let $\left[\mathrm{R}=\frac{\ell}{3 \mathrm{KA}}\right]$

$$
\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{A}}=100 \bullet \stackrel{11 \mathrm{R} / 2}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{F}}=40
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\left[\mathrm{H}=\frac{100-40}{\frac{11 \mathrm{R}}{2}}\right] \ldots \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
H=\frac{100-T_{B}}{R} \ldots \ldots \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
H=\frac{T_{E}-40}{3 R} \ldots . \tag{3}
\end{equation*}
$$
using (1) and (2)
$120=1100-11 \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{A}}$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{B}}=89^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
using (1) and (3)
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{E}}=73^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Question: A person standing on an open ground hears the sound of a jet aeroplane, coming from north at an angle 60<sup>o</sup> with ground level. But he finds the aeroplane right vertically above his position. If ‘v’ is the speed of sound, speed of the plane is nv, where n is:
Solution:
Distance, \[QP = {v_p} \times t\] (Distance = speed × time)
Distance, \[QR = v.\,t\]
\[\cos {60^ \circ } = \frac{{QP}}{{QR}}\]
\[\frac{1}{2} = \frac{{{v_p} \times t}}{{v.\,t}} \Rightarrow {v_p} = \frac{v}{2}\]

So, $n=\frac{1}{2}$

Question: The vertical component of the earth's magnetic field is 6 × 10<sup>-5 </sup> T at any place where the angle of dip is 37º. The earth's resultant magnetic field at that place will be (Given $\tan 37^\circ  = \frac{3}{4})$
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \left( {\delta = 37^\circ } \right) \\
  {B_v} = B\sin \delta \\
  6 \times {10^{ - 5}} = B\frac{3}{5} \\
  B = 10 \times {10^{ - 5}}T \\
   = {10^{ - 4}}T \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: The vernier constant of Vernier callipers is 0.1 mm and it has zero error of -0.05 cm. While measuring diameter of a sphere, the main scale reading is 1.7 cm and coinciding vernier division is 5. The correct diameter will be _____
Solution:
V.C = 0.1 mm = 0.01 cm
Zero error = -0.05 cm
$\therefore $correction = 0.05 cm
M.S.R. = 1.7 cm
V.S.R =$5 \times V.C = 5 \times 0.01 = 0.05cm$
Diameter = MSR + VSR + Correction
$1.7 + 0.05 + 0.05 = 1.8cm$
$ = 180 \times {10^{ - 2}}cm$

Question:

Solution:
$${\omega _m} = \frac{{2\pi }}{{100 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 2\pi  \times {10^4}{\sec ^{ - 1}}$$

$${\omega _c} = \frac{{2\pi }}{{8 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 0.25\pi  \times {10^6}{\sec ^{ - 1}}$$

$${v_{\max }} = 10 = {v_c} + {v_m}$$

$${v_{\min }} = 8 = {v_c} - {v_m}$$

$$\therefore {v_c} = 9V\,\& {v_m} = 1{\mkern 1mu} V$$

Equation of AM wave -

$$ = \left[ {9 + \sin \left( {2\pi  \times {{10}^4}} \right)t} \right]\sin \left( {2.5\pi  \times {{10}^5}} \right)t$$

Question:
An element M crystallises in a body centred cubic unit cell with a cell edge of 300 pm. The density of the element is 6.0 g cm–3. The number of atoms present in 180 g of the element is ______ × 1023.

M is body centred cubic, ∴ Z = 2

Edge length = 300 pm
Density = 6g/cm3
\[\therefore 6g/c{m^3} = \frac{{Z \times A}}{{{{\left( {300 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}} \right)}^3}}} = \frac{{2 \times A}}{{27 \times {{10}^{ - 24}}}}\]
A = 81 ✕ 10-24 g
∴ Atomic mass = 48.6g
∴ Mole in 180g = \[\frac{{180}}{{48.6}} = 3.7\,moles\]
Atoms of M = 3.7 ✕ 6 ✕ 1023
= 22.22 ✕ 1023 atoms

Question: A carrier signal C(t) = 25\[\sin \left( {2.512 \times {{10}^{10}}t} \right)\] is amplitude modulated by a message signal m(t) = \[5\sin \left( {1.57 \times {{10}^8}t} \right)\]and transmitted through an antenna. What will be the bandwidth of the modulated signal ?

Let \[E:\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1,\,\,a > b\,\,and\,H = \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{A^2}}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{B^2}}} = 1\]
Let the distance between the foci of E and the foci of H be \[2\sqrt 3 .\] If a – A = 2, and the ratio of the eccentricities of E and H is \[\frac{1}{3},\] then the sum of the lengths of their latus rectums is equal to :
Solution:
\[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1\,\]foci are (ae, 0) and (− ae, 0)
\[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{A^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{B^2}}} = 1\,\]foci are (Ae′ , 0) and (Ae′ , 0)
\[ \Rightarrow 2ae = 2\sqrt 3 \Rightarrow ae = \sqrt 3 \,\]
\[and\,\,2Ae' = 2\sqrt 3 \Rightarrow Ae' = \sqrt 3 \]
\[ \Rightarrow ae = Ae' \Rightarrow \frac{e}{{e'}} = \frac{A}{a}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3} = \frac{A}{a} \Rightarrow a = 3A\]
\[Now\,\,a - A = 2 \Rightarrow a - \frac{a}{3} - 2 \Rightarrow a = 3\,and\,A = 1\]
\[Ae = \sqrt 3 \Rightarrow e = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\,and\,e' = \sqrt 3 \]
\[{b^2} = {a^2}(1 - {e^2})\]
\[{b^2} = 6\]
\[and\,\,{B^2} = {A^2}\left( {{{\left( {e'} \right)}^2} - 1} \right) = (2) \Rightarrow {B^2} = 2\]
\[sum\,\,of\,\,LR\,\, = \frac{{2{b^2}}}{a} + \frac{{2{B^2}}}{A} = 8\]

Question: An LPG cylinder contains gas at a pressure of 300 kPa at 27°C. The cylinder can withstand the pressure of 1.2 × 10<sup>6</sup> Pa. The room in which the cylinder is kept catches fire. The minimum temperature at which the bursting of cylinder will take place is __________ °C. (Nearest integer)
Solution:
Since the volume and amount of gas inside the cylinder will remain constant.
\[\frac{P}{T} = {\text{constant}}\]
\[\frac{{{P_1}}}{{{T_1}}} = \frac{{{P_2}}}{{{T_2}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{300 \times {{10}^3}}}{{300}} = \frac{{1.2 \times {{10}^6}}}{T}\]
\[T = \frac{{1.2 \times {{10}^6}}}{{{{10}^3}}} = 1200{\text{ K = 92}}{{\text{7}}^ \circ }{\text{C}}\]

Question:
At a given temperature T, gases Ne, Ar, Xe and Kr are found to deviate from ideal gas behavior. Their equation of state is given as $P = \frac{{RT}}{{V - b}}$at T.
Here, b is the van der Waals constant. Which gas will exhibit steepest increase in the plot of Z (compression factor) vs P?
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  P = \frac{{RT}}{{{v_m} - b}} \\
   \Rightarrow P{V_m} - Pb = RT \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{{P{V_m}}}{{RT}} = 1 + \frac{{Pb}}{{RT}} \Rightarrow Z = 1 + \frac{{Pb}}{{RT}} \\
\end{gathered} $
[Where Z (compressibility factor) \[ = \frac{{PVm}}{{RT}}\]
Slope of Z vs P curve (Straight line) $ = \frac{b}{{RT}}$
$\therefore $Higher the value of b, moresteep will be the curve. Constant ‘b’ value depends on size of atom or molecule.

Question: The angle between vector \[\vec Q\] and the resultant of \[\left( {2\vec Q + 2\vec P} \right)\;and\;\left( {2\vec Q - 2\vec P} \right)\] is :
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \vec R = \left( {2\vec Q + 2\vec P} \right) + \left( {2\vec Q - 2\vec P} \right) \\
  \vec R = 4\vec Q \\
  Angle\;between\;\vec Q\;and\;\vec R\;is\;zero \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Ship A is sailing towards north-east with velocity $\vec v = 30\hat i + 50\hat j$ km/hr, where $\hat i$points east and $\hat j$ north. Ship B is at a distance of 80 km east and 150 km north of Ship A and is sailing towards west at 10 km/hr. A will be at minimum distance from B in:
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  {{\vec v}_A} = 30\hat i + 50\hat j{\text{ km/hr}} \\
  {{\vec v}_B} = \left( { - 10\hat i} \right){\text{ km/hr}} \\
  {{\vec r}_{BA}} = \left( {80\hat i + 150\hat j} \right){\text{ km}} \\
  {{\vec v}_{BA}} = {{\vec v}_B} - {{\vec v}_A} = - 10\hat i - 30\hat i - 50\hat i = -40\hat i - 50\hat j \\
  {t_{{\text{minimum}}}} = \frac{{\left| {\left( {{{\vec r}_{BA}}} \right)\left( {{{\vec v}_{BA}}} \right)} \right|}}{{{{\left( {{{\vec v}_{BA}}} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{\left| {\left( {80\hat i + 150\hat j} \right)\left( { - 40\hat i - 50\hat j} \right)} \right|}}{{{{\left( {10\sqrt {41} } \right)}^2}}} \\
  \therefore t = \frac{{10700}}{{10\sqrt {41} \times 10\sqrt {41} }} = \frac{{107}}{{41}} = 2.6{\text{ hr}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: The aperture of the objective is 24.4 cm. The resolving power of this telescope, if a light of wavelength $2440\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^ \circ  $is used to see the object will be:

An inclined plane is bent in such a way that the vertical cross-section is given by \[y = \frac{{{x^2}}}{4}\] where y is in vertical and x in horizontal direction (both x and y are in metres). If the upper surface of this curved plane is rough with coefficient of friction μ = 0.5, the maximum height in cm at which a stationary block will not slip downward is _____ cm.
Solution:
$N = mg\cos \theta $
${f_\ell } = mg\sin \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 1 \right)$
${f_\ell } = \mu N = \mu mg\cos \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)$
Using (1) and (2),
$\tan \theta  = \mu $
$\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \mu $
$\frac{x}{2} = \frac{1}{2},\,\,x = 1$
So, $h = \frac{{{x^2}}}{4} = 0.25\;m$ = 25cm

Question: In an experiment with vernier calipers of least count 0.1 mm, when two jaws are joined together the zero of vernier scale lies right to the zero of the main scale and 6th division of vernier scale coincides with the main scale division. While measuring the diameter of a spherical bob, the zero of vernier scale lies in between 3.2 cm and 3.3 marks, and 4th division of vernier scale coincides with the main scale division. The diameter of bob is measured as
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

LC = 0.1mm
Zero Error = 6×LC = 0.6 mm
Reading = MSR + VSR × LC – Zero Error
= 32mm + 4(0.1mm) – 0.6 mm
= 31.8 mm
= 3. 18 cm

Question: A bandwidth of 6 MHz is available for A.M. transmission. If the maximum audio signal frequency used for modulating the carrier wave is not to exceed 6 kHz. The number of stations that can be broadcasted within this band simultaneously without interfering with each other will be______.

A wedge Y with mass of 10 kg and all frictionless surfaces and the inclined surface making $37^{\circ}$ with horizontal. A block X with mass 2 kg is placed at the highest point of the wedge as shown in figure is at rest. At $\mathrm{t}=0$ wedge (Y) is pulled toward right with constant force $(f)$ of 24 N. Taking the block X at rest at $\mathrm{t}=0$, the time taken by it to slide down 8.8 m on the slope, while Y is on the move, is $\_\_\_\_$ s. (take $\tan \left(37^{\circ}\right)=3 / 4$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ )
Solution:
$20 \sin 37^{\circ}-2 \mathrm{a}_{\omega} \cos 37^{\circ}=2 \mathrm{a}_{\text {rel }}$
$20 \cos 37^{\circ}-\mathrm{N}+2 \mathrm{a}_{\omega} \sin 37^{\circ}=0$
$24-\mathrm{N} \sin 37^{\circ}=10 \times \mathrm{a}_{\omega}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 24-\left(20 \cos 37^{\circ}+2 \mathrm{a}_{\omega} \sin 37^{\circ}\right) \times \sin 37^{\circ}=10 \mathrm{a}_{\omega} \\
& 24-\left(16+2 \mathrm{a}_{\omega} \times \frac{3}{5}\right) \frac{3}{5}=10 \mathrm{a}_{\omega} \\
& 24-\frac{48}{5}-\frac{18}{25} \mathrm{a}_{\omega}=10 \mathrm{a}_{\omega} \\
& \frac{72}{5}=\left(10-\frac{18}{25}\right) \mathrm{a}_{\omega} \\
& \frac{72}{5}=\mathrm{a}_{\omega}=\frac{180}{116}=\frac{90}{58} \\
& \mathrm{a}_{\omega}=\frac{45}{29} \\
& 20 \sin 37^{\circ}-2 \times \frac{45}{29} \times \frac{4}{5} \times \cos 37^{\circ}=2 \mathrm{a}_{\text {rel }} \\
& 12-\frac{72}{29}=2 \mathrm{a}_{\text {rel }} \\
& \frac{348-72}{29}=2 \mathrm{a}_{\text {rel }} \\
& \frac{276}{29 \times 2}=\mathrm{a}_{\text {rel }} ; \frac{138}{29}=\mathrm{a}_{\text {rel }} \\
& \mathrm{t}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8.8 \times 29}{138}} \\
& \mathrm{t}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8.8 \times 29}{10 \times 138}} \\
& \mathrm{t}=\sqrt{3.69} \\
& \mathrm{t} \approx 2 \mathrm{sec}
\end{aligned}
$$
After rounding off

If a boat capable of moving at a maximum speed of 27 km h<sup>–1</sup> in still water, crosses the river in half a minute, while moving with maximum speed at an angle of 150° to direction of river flow, then the width of the river is :

\[\therefore \,\,{V_ \bot } = river\,flow\, = 27 \times \cos {60^o} = \frac{{27}}{2}km/hr.\]
Time taken = 30 sec.
\[\therefore \,\,S = Vt = \frac{{27}}{2} \times \frac{5}{{18}} \times 30m = 112.5m\]

Question: If we need a magnification of 375 from a compound microscope of tube length 150 mm and an objective of focal length 5 mm, the focal length of the eye-piece should be close to:
Solution:
Magnification of compound microscope for least distance of distinct vision setting(strained

eye)

\[M = \frac{L}{{{f_0}}}\left( {1 + \frac{D}{{{f_e}}}} \right)\]
Where L is the tube length
 \[{f_0}\]is the focal length of objective
D is the least distance of distinct vision =25 cm
\[i.e.\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,375 = \frac{{150 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}\left( {1 + \frac{{25 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{{f_e}}}} \right)\]
\[i.e.\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,12.5 = 1 + \frac{{25 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{{f_e}}}\]
\[i.e.\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{25 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{{f_e}}} = 11.5\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,{f_e} \approx \,21.7 \times {10^{ - 3}}m = 22mm\]

Question: Two short magnetic dipoles \[{m_1}\] and \[{m_2}\] each having magnetic moment of \[1\,\,A{m^2}\] are placed at point O and P respectively. The distance between OP is 1 meter. The torque experienced by the magnetic dipole \[{m_2}\] due to the presence of \[{m_1}\] is \[..... \times {10^{ - 7}}\,\,Nm\].
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \vec \tau = {{\vec M}_2} \times {{\vec B}_1} \\
  \tau = {M_2}{B_1}\sin {90^o} \\
   = 1 \times \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{4\pi }}\,\frac{{{M_1}}}{{{{\left( 1 \right)}^3}}}1 \\
   = {10^{ - 7}}\,\,N.m \\
\end{gathered} \]
Ans. 1.00

Question: If the curves, $\frac{{{x^2}}}{a} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{b} = 1$ and $\frac{{{x^2}}}{c} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{d} = 1$ intersect each other at an angle of 90°, then which of the following relations is TRUE?
Solution:
Let common point of curves be (p, q).
For $\frac{{{x^2}}}{a} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{b} = 1$
Differentiating, we get
$\frac{{2x}}{a} + \frac{{2y}}{b}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{bx}}{{ay}}$
So, slope for first curve at (p, q)
$\left( { = {m_1}} \right) = - \frac{{bp}}{{aq}}$
Similarly slope for second curve at (p, q)
$\left( { = {m_2}} \right) = - \frac{{dp}}{{cq}}$
Now, as both curves are orthogonal
$ \Rightarrow {m_1}{m_2} = - 1$
$\frac{{bd}}{{ac}}\left( {\frac{{{p^2}}}{{{q^2}}}} \right) = - 1\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)$
Now, (p, q) lies on both the curves
So, $\frac{{{p^2}}}{a} + \frac{{{q^2}}}{b} = 1$ and \[\frac{{{p^2}}}{c} + \frac{{{q^2}}}{d} = 1\]
Subtracting these
${p^2}\left( {\frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{c}} \right) + {q^2}\left( {\frac{1}{b} - \frac{1}{d}} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{p^2}}}{{{q^2}}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{1}{d} - \frac{1}{b}} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{c}} \right)}} = \frac{{\left( {b - d} \right)ac}}{{\left( {c - a} \right)bd}}$
Putting this value in (1), we get
$\frac{{bd}}{{ac}} \cdot \frac{{\left( {b - d} \right)ac}}{{\left( {c - a} \right)bd}} = - 1$
$ \Rightarrow b - d = a - c$
$ \Rightarrow a - b = c - d$

An inclined plane making an angle of 300 with the horizontal is placed in a uniform horizontal electric field \[200\frac{N}{C}\] as shown in the figure. A body of mass 1 kg and charge 5mC is allowed to slide down from rest at a height of 1m. If the coefficient of friction is 0.2, find the time taken by the body to reach the bottom.
\[\left[ {g = 9.8\,m/{s^2};\,\sin 30^\circ = \frac{1}{2};\cos 30^\circ = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right]\]
Solution:
FBD

So \[a = \frac{{9.8\sin 30 - 1\cos 30 - \mu N}}{1}\]
\[a = 2.233m/{s^2}\]
By \[S = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}\]
$$\frac{1}{{\operatorname{Sin} 30^\circ }} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {2.233} \right){t^2}$$

\[t \approx 1.3\sec \]

Question:
Two radioactive materials A and B have decay constants ${10\lambda }$ and 

<!-StartFragment->${\lambda}$<!-EndFragment->   respectively. If initially they have the same number of nuclei, then the ratio of the number of nuclei of A to that of B will be 1/e after a time:
Solution:
\[\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  {{\text{ }}As,{\text{ }}N = {N_0}{e^{ - \lambda t}}} \\
  {{\text{ }}so,{\text{ }}\frac{{{N_A}}}{{{N_B}}} = {e^{\left( {{\lambda _B} - {\lambda _A}} \right)t}} = \frac{1}{e} \Rightarrow \left( {{\lambda _B} - {\lambda _A}} \right)t = - 1} \\
  { \Rightarrow \left( {{\lambda _A} - {\lambda _B}} \right) \cdot t = 1} \\
  { \Rightarrow t = \frac{1}{{\left( {{\lambda _A} - {\lambda _B}} \right)}} = \frac{1}{{10\lambda - \lambda }} = \frac{1}{{9\lambda }}}
\end{array}\]

Question: Which polymer has chiral, monomer(s)?

Consider the hyperbola \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1\]having one of its focus at P(–3, 0). If the latus rectum through its other focus subtends a right angle at P and \[{a^2}{b^2} = \alpha \sqrt 2  - \beta ,\alpha ,\beta  \in \mathbb{N}\]
Solution:
\[{f_1} \equiv ( - ae,0) \equiv P( - 3,0)\]
\[ \Rightarrow ae = 3\]

\[\tan {45^o} = \frac{{{b^2}/a}}{{2ae}}\]
\[2ae = \frac{{{b^2}}}{a}\]
b2 = 6a
Also a2e2 = a2 + b2
9 = a2 + 6a
a2 + 6a – 9 = 0
\[a = - 3 \pm 3\sqrt 2 = - \left( {1 \pm \sqrt 2 } \right)\]
\[\therefore {a^2}{b^2} = {a^2}.6a = 6{a^3}\]
\[ = 6\left( {135\sqrt 2 - 189} \right)\]
\[\alpha = 810\]and\[\beta = 1134\]
\[\therefore \alpha + \beta = 1944\]

Question: Let the domain of the function $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\log _{3} \log _{5} \log _{7} \left(9 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{2}-13\right)$ be the interval $(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})$. Let the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ have eccentricity $\frac{n}{3}$ and the length of the latus rectum $\frac{8 m}{3}$. Then $b^{2}-a^{2}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\log _{5}\left(\log _{7}\left(9 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{2}-13\right)\right)>0$
$ \Rightarrow 9x - {x^2} - 13 > 7$
${x^2} - 9x + 20 < 0 \Rightarrow 4 < x < 5$
$m = 4,n = 5$
$ \Rightarrow e = \sqrt {1 + \frac{{{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}} = \frac{5}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{{{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}} = \frac{{25}}{9} - 1 = \frac{{16}}{9}$
$\frac{b}{a} = \frac{4}{3}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{2{b^2}}}{a} = \frac{{8m}}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{{2{b^2}}}{a} = \frac{{32}}{3}$
$ \Rightarrow 2{b^2} = \frac{{32}}{3} \times \frac{{3b}}{4} \Rightarrow b = 4,a = 3$
${b^2} - {a^2} = 16 - 9 = 7$

Question: A heat source at T = 10<sup>3</sup> K is connected to another heat reservoir at T = 10<sup>2</sup> K by a copper slab which is 1 m long. The thermal conductivity of copper is 0.1W K<sup>-1 </sup>m<sup>-1</sup>. The energy flux (in W m<sup>–2</sup>) through it in the steady state is:
Solution:
\[\left( {\frac{{dQ}}{{dt}}} \right) = \frac{{kA\Delta T}}{l}\]
Energy flux, \[\frac{1}{A}\left( {\frac{{dQ}}{{dt}}} \right) = \frac{{k\Delta T}}{l}\]
\[ = \frac{{\left( {0.1} \right)\left( {900} \right)}}{1} = 90\,W/{m^2}\]

Question:
The number of statement's, which are correct with respect to the compression of carbon dioxide from point (a) in the Andrews isotherm from the following is _________.

A. Carbon dioxide remains as a gas upto point (b)
B. Liquid carbon dioxide appears at point (c)
C. Liquid and gaseous carbon dioxide coexist between points (b) and (c)
D. As the volume decreases from (b) to (c), the amount of liquid decreases
Solution:
At
(a) → CO2 exist as gas
(b) → liquefaction of CO2 starts
(c) → liquefaction ends
(d) → CO2 exist as liquid.
Between (b) & (c) → liquid and gaseous CO2 co-exist.
As volume changes from (b) to (c) gas decreases and liquid increases.
(A), (C) → correct

Question: If the foci of a hyperbola are same as that of the ellipse $\frac{{{x^2}}}{9} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{25}} = 1$ and the eccentricity of the hyperbola is $\frac{{15}}{8}$ times the eccentricity of the ellipse, then the smaller focal distance of the point $\left( {\sqrt 2 ,\frac{{14}}{3}\sqrt {\frac{2}{5}} } \right)$ on the hyperbola, is equal to
Solution:
$\frac{{{x^2}}}{9} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{25}} = 1$
$a = 3,b = 5$
$e = \sqrt {1 - \frac{9}{{25}}} = \frac{4}{5}$
$\therefore \,\,$foci $ = \left( {0, \pm \,be} \right) = \left( {0, \pm \,4} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,{e_H} = \frac{4}{5} \times \frac{{15}}{8} = \frac{3}{2}$
Let equation hyperbola
$\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{A^2}}} - \frac{{y2}}{{{B^2}}} = - 1$
$\therefore \,\,B \cdot {e_H} = 4$
$\therefore \,\,B = \frac{8}{3}$
$\therefore \,\,{A^2} = {B^2}\left( {{e_H}^2 - 1} \right) = \frac{{64}}{9}\left( {\frac{9}{4} - 1} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,{A^2} = \frac{{80}}{9}$
$\therefore \,\,\frac{{{x^2}}}{{\frac{{80}}{9}}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{\frac{{64}}{9}}} = - 1$
Directrix: $y = \pm \frac{B}{{{e_H}}} = \pm \frac{{16}}{9}$
$PS = e \cdot PM = \frac{3}{2}\left| {\frac{{14}}{3} \cdot \sqrt {\frac{2}{5}} - \frac{{16}}{9}} \right|$
$ = 7\sqrt {\frac{2}{5}} - \frac{8}{3}$

Question: A paramagnetic substance in the form of a cube with edge 1 cm has a magnetic dipole moment of 20 × 10<sup>–6</sup> J/T when a magnetic intensity of 60 × 10<sup>3</sup> A/m is applied. Its magnetic susceptibility is:
Solution:
Magnetic susceptibility,
\[\chi = \frac{I}{H}\]
Where,
 $\begin{gathered}
  I = \frac{{{\text{Magnetic moment}}}}{{{\text{Volume}}}} = \frac{{20 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 20{\text{ N/}}{{\text{m}}^{\text{2}}} \\
  {\text{Now, }}\chi = \frac{{20}}{{60 \times {{10}^3}}} = \frac{1}{3} \times {10^{ - 3}} = 3.3 \times {10^{ - 4}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A telescope has an objective lens of focal length 150 cm and an eyepiece of focal length 5 cm. If a 50 m tall tower at a distance of 1 km is observed through this telescope in a normal setting, the angle formed by the image of the tower is u, then u is close to:
Solution:
\[MP = \frac{{{f_0}}}{{{f_e}}} = \frac{{150}}{5} = 30\]
\[\tan \,{\theta _0} = \frac{{50}}{{1000}} = \frac{1}{{20}}{\mkern 1mu} rad\]
\[\tan \theta  = MP \times {\theta _0}\]
\[\tan \theta  = 30 \times \frac{1}{{20}}\]
\[\tan \theta  = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5{\mkern 1mu} \,rad\]
\[ = {\tan ^{ - 1}}1.5 \approx {60^o}\]

Question: The locus of the point of intersection of the lines $\left( {\sqrt 3 } \right)kx + ky - 4\sqrt 3  = 0$ and $\sqrt 3 x - y - 4\left( {\sqrt 3 } \right)k = 0$ is a conic, whose eccentricity is _____.
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

$\sqrt 3 kx + ky = 4\sqrt 3 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {\text{i}} \right)$
$\sqrt 3 kx - ky = 4\sqrt 3 {k^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {{\text{ii}}} \right)$
On adding (i) and (ii)
$2\sqrt 3 kx = 4\sqrt 3 \left( {{k^2} + 1} \right)$
$x = 2\left( {k + \frac{1}{k}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {{\text{iii}}} \right)$
On subtracting (i) and (ii)
$2ky = 4\sqrt 3 \left( {1 - {k^2}} \right)$
$y = 2\sqrt 3 \left( {\frac{1}{k} - k} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( {{\text{iv}}} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\left( {\frac{x}{2}} \right)^2} - {\left( {\frac{y}{{2\sqrt 3 }}} \right)^2} = {\left( {k + \frac{1}{k}} \right)^2} - {\left( {k - \frac{1}{k}} \right)^2} = 4$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2}}}{{16}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{48}} = 1$
$\therefore \,$ Eccentricity of Hyperbola
$ \Rightarrow {e^2} = 1 + \frac{{48}}{{16}} = 4$
$\therefore \,\,e = 2$

Question: A monkey of mass 50kg climbs on a rope which can withstand the tension (T) of 350N. If monkey initially climbs down with an acceleration of 4m/s<sup>2</sup> and the climbs up with an acceleration of 5m/s<sup>2</sup>. Choose the correct option (g = 10m/s<sup>2</sup>)
Options: Rope will break while climbing upward
Rope will break while going downward
Solution:
F.B.D of monkey while moving downward

Using Newton’s second law
mg – T = ma1
∴ 500 – T = 50×4 ⇒ T = 300 N
F.B.D of monkey while moving up

Using Newton’s second law of motion
T – mg = ma2
⇒ T – 500 = 50×5
⇒ T = 750 N
Breaking strength of string = 350 N
∴ String will break while monkey is moving upward

Question:
A magnet of total magnetic moment \[{10^{ - 2}}\,\widehat i\,A - {m^2}\] is placed in a time varying magnetic field, \[B\,\widehat i\,(\cos \omega t)\] where B = 1 Tesla and \[\omega = 0.125\,rad/s.\] The work done for reversing the direction of the magnetic moment at t = 1 second, is close to:

(Take $$\cos (.125) = .99$$)
Solution:
Let required work done be W.
We know that,
$W = - \left| {\vec M} \right|.\left| {\vec B} \right|\left( {\cos {\theta _2} - \cos {\theta _1}} \right)$
Now, ${\theta _2} = 180^\circ $
${\theta _1} = 0$
$\left| {\vec M} \right| = {10^{ - 2}}$
$W = - \left| {\vec M} \right|\left| {\vec B} \right|\left( {\cos {\theta _2} - \cos {\theta _1}} \right)$
$W = - {10^{ - 2}} \times 1 \times \cos \left( {0.125} \right) \times \cos \left( {180^\circ - \cos 0^\circ } \right)$
$W = - {10^2} \times \cos \left( {0.125} \right) \times \left( { - 1 - 1} \right)$
$W = 2 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times 0.99$
$W = 0.0198\,J$

Question: Let a conic \[C\] pass through the point \[\left( {4,-2} \right)\] and \[P\left( {x,y} \right),x \geqslant 3,\] be any point on \[C.\] Let the slope of the line touching the conic \[C\] only at a single point \[P\] be half the sloe of the line joining the points \[P\] and \[\left( {3,-5} \right).\] If the focal distance of the point \[\left( {7,1} \right)\] on \[C\] is \[d,\] then \[12d\] equals ________.
Solution:
\[P\left( {x,y} \right)\] and \[x \geqslant 3\]
Slope of line at \[P\left( {x,y} \right)\] will be \[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{y + 5}}{{x-3}}} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2\frac{{dy}}{{\left( {y + 5} \right)}} = \frac{1}{{\left( {x-3} \right)}}dx\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2\ell n\left( {y + 5} \right) = \ell n\left( {x-3} \right) + C\]
Passes through \[\left( {4,-2} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2\ell n\left( 3 \right) = \ell n\left( 1 \right) + C\]
\[ \Rightarrow C = 2\ell n\left( 3 \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2\ell n\left( {y + 5} \right) = \ell n\left( {x-3} \right) + 2\ell n\left( 3 \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2\left( {\ell n\left( {\frac{{y + 5}}{3}} \right)} \right) = \ell n\left( {x-3} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{y + 5}}{3}} \right)^2} = \left( {x-3} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow {\left( {y + 5} \right)^2} - 9\left( {x-3} \right)\]
           \[ \downarrow \]
Parabola
\[4a = 9\]
\[a = \frac{9}{4}\]

\[d = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{7}{4}} \right)}^2} + {6^2}} \]
\[d = \frac{{\sqrt {625} }}{4}\]
\[d = \frac{{25}}{4}\]
\[12d = 75\]

Question: The equation of a tangent to the hyperbola $4{x^2} - 5{y^2} = 20$ parallel to the line $x - y = 2$ is:
Solution:
Given, the equation of line,
$x - y = 2 \Rightarrow y = x - 2$
$\therefore $ Its slope = m = 1
Equation of hyperbola is:
$\frac{{{x^2}}}{5} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{4} = 1$
$ \Rightarrow {a^2} = 5,\,\,{b^2} = 4$
The equation of tangent to the hyperbola is,
$\begin{gathered}
  y = mx \pm \sqrt {{a^2}{m^2} - {b^2}} \\
  \,\,\,\, = x \pm \sqrt {5 - 4} \\
   \Rightarrow y = x \pm 1 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A block kept on a rough inclined plane, as shown in the figure, remains at rest upto a maximum force 2 N down the inclined plane. The maximum external force up the inclined plane that does not move the block is 10 N. The coefficient of static friction between the block and the plane is : [Take g = 10 m/s2]
Solution:
$$mg\sin \theta  + 2 = \mu mg\cos \theta \,\,\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)$$

$$10 - mg\sin \theta  = \mu mg\cos \theta \,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 2 \right)$$

On adding (1) + (2) 

$$12 = 2\mu mg\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2};$$

$$\mu mg = \frac{{12}}{{\sqrt 3 }}$$ .....................(3)

On (1) - (2);

$$8 = 2mg \times \frac{1}{2}$$

$$mg = 8$$                (using this in equation (3))

$$\mu  = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}$$

Question: Let H be the hyperbola, whose foci are $\left( {1 \pm \sqrt 2 ,0} \right)$ and eccentricity is $\sqrt 2 $. Then the length of its latus rectum is ______________.

$2ae = \left| {\left( {1 + \sqrt 2 } \right) - \left( {1 + \sqrt 2 } \right)} \right| = 2\sqrt 2 $
$ae = \sqrt 2 $
$a = 1$
$ \Rightarrow b = 1$
$\because \,\,e = \sqrt 2 \Rightarrow $ Hyperbola is rectangular
$ \Rightarrow $L.R. $ = \frac{{2{b^2}}}{a} = 2$

Question:

Statement (C) is correct because, the magnetic field outside the toroid is zero and they form closed loops inside the toroid itself. Statement (E) is correct because we know that super conductors are materials inside which the net magnetic field is always zero and they are
${\mu _r} = 1 + \chi $
For superconductor
${\mu _r} = 0$
$0 = 1 + \chi $
$\chi  =  - 1$

Question: If the eccentricity of the standard hyperbola passing through the point (4, 6) is 2, then the equation of the tangent to the hyperbola at (4, 6) is:
Solution:
Let equation of hyperbola be,
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1....(i) \\
  \because e = \sqrt {1 + \frac{{{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}} \Rightarrow {b^2} = {a^2}\left( {{e^2} - 1} \right) \\
  e = 2 \Rightarrow {b^2} = 3{a^2}....(ii) \\
\end{gathered} \]
Equation (i) passes through (4, 6),
$\frac{{16}}{{{a^2}}} - \frac{{36}}{{{b^2}}} = 1....(iii)$
On solving (i) and (ii), we get
${a^2} = 4,\,\,\,{b^2} = 12$
Now equation of hyperbola is $\frac{{{x^2}}}{4} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{12}} = 1$
Now equation of tangent to the hyperbola at (4, 6) is
$\frac{{4x}}{4} - \frac{{6y}}{{12}} = 1 \Rightarrow x - \frac{y}{2} = 1 \Rightarrow 2x - y = 2$

Question:
Given below are two statements.
Statement I: O2, Cu2+ and Fe3+ are weakly attracted by magnetic field and are magnetized in the same direction as magnetic field.
Statement II: NaCl and H2O are weakly magnetized in opposite direction to magnetic field.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer form the options given below:
Options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.
Solution:
${O_2},C{u^{2 + }}$ and $F{e^{3 + }}$ have 2, 1 and 5 unpaired electrons respectively, so these are the paramagnetic species. Hence, they are attracted by magnetic field. NaCl and H2O are the diamagnetic species so they are repelled by the magnetic field.
Hence, both statement I and statement II are correct.

Question: The eccentricity of the hyperbola whose length of the latus rectum is equal to 8 and the length of its conjugate axis is equal to half of the distance between its foci, is -
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

Let eqn. of hyperbola is $\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1$
Length of its conjugate axis = half of the distance between its foci
$ \Rightarrow 2b = \frac{1}{2}\left( {2ae} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow b = \frac{{ae}}{2}$
Now ${b^2} = {a^2}\left( {{e^2} - 1} \right)$
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \frac{{{a^2}{e^2}}}{4} = {a^2}\left( {{e^2} - 1} \right) \\
   \Rightarrow {e^2} - \frac{{{e^2}}}{4} = 1 \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{{3{e^2}}}{4} = 1 \\
   \Rightarrow e = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 3 }} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Two vectors \[\overrightarrow P \,\,and\,\overrightarrow Q \] have equal magnitudes. If the magnitude of \[\overrightarrow P \,\, + \,\overrightarrow Q \] is n times the magnitude of \[\overrightarrow P \,\, - \,\overrightarrow Q ,\] then angle between \[\overrightarrow P \,\,and\,\overrightarrow Q \] is:
Solution:
\[|\vec P + \vec Q{|^2} = {n^2}|\vec P - \vec Q{|^2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow {P^2} + {Q^2} + 2PQ\cos \theta = {n^2}\left( {{P^2} + {Q^2} - 2PQ\cos \theta } \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow 1 + 1 + 2\cos \theta = {n^2}(1 + 1 - 2\cos \theta )\]
\[ \Rightarrow 1 + \cos \theta = {n^2}(1 - \cos \theta )\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{1 + \cos \theta }}{{1 - \cos \theta }} = \frac{{{n^2}}}{1}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{\cos \theta }} = \frac{{{n^2} + 1}}{{{n^2} - 1}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{{n^2} - 1}}{{{n^2} + 1}}} \right)\]

Question: A screw gauge with a pitch of 0.5 mm and a circular scale with 50 divisions is used to measure the thickness of a thin sheet of Aluminium. Before starting the measurement, it is found that when the two jaws of the screw gauge are brought in contact, the 45<sup>th</sup> division coincides with the main scale line and that the zero of the main scale is barely visible. What is the thickness of the sheet if the main scale reading is 0.5 mm and the 25<sup>th</sup> division coincides with the main scale line?
Solution:
When jaws are closed, the zero error will be
= main scale reading + (circular scale reading) (Least count)
= –0.5 mm+ (45)(0.01)
Zero error = –0.05 mm
When the sheet is placed between the jaws; measured thickness
= 0.5 mm + (25)(0.01) = 0.75 mm
⇒ Actual thickness
= 0.75 mm –(–0.05)
= 0.80 mm

Question: Three particles P, Q and R are moving along the vectors \[\overrightarrow A  = \hat i + \hat j,\,\,\overrightarrow B  = \hat j + \hat k\] and \[\overrightarrow C  =  - \hat i + \hat j\] respectively. They strike on a point simultaneously and start to move in different directions. Now particle P is moving normal to the plane which contains vector \[\overrightarrow A \,\,and\,\,\overrightarrow B .\] Similarly particle Q is moving normal to the plane which contains vector \[\overrightarrow A \,\,and\,\,\overrightarrow C .\] The angle between the direction of motion of P and Q is \[{\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt x }}} \right).\] Then the value of x is _______.
Solution:
\[{\hat n_1} = \frac{{\vec A \times \vec B}}{{|\vec A \times \vec B|}} = \frac{{\hat i - \hat j + \hat k}}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]
\[{\hat n_2} = \frac{{\vec A \times \vec C}}{{|\vec A \times \vec C|}} = \hat k\]
\[\cos \theta = {\hat n_1} \cdot {\hat n_2} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]

Question: If the tangents drawn to the hyperbola $4{y^2} = {x^2} + 1$ intersect the co-ordinate axes at the distinct points A and B, then the locus of the mid-point of AB is:
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  4{y^2} = {x^2} + 1 \\
   \Rightarrow - {x^2} + 4{y^2} = 1 \\
\end{gathered} $
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\, - \frac{{{x^{\mathbf{2}}}}}{{{1^{\mathbf{2}}}}}{\mathbf{ + }}\frac{{{y^{\mathbf{2}}}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)}^{\mathbf{2}}}}}{\mathbf{ = }}1\]
\[a{\mathbf{ = }}1,b{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{1}{2}\]
Let, tangent to the curve is at point (x1, y1)
\[\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,4{\mathbf{ \times }}2y.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{\mathbf{ = }}2x\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{2{x_{\mathbf{1}}}}}{{8{y_{\mathbf{1}}}}}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{{x_{\mathbf{1}}}}}{{4{y_{\mathbf{1}}}}}\]
\[\therefore \] Eqn of tangent y = mx + c
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,y{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{{x_{\mathbf{1}}}}}{{4{y_{\mathbf{1}}}}}.x{\mathbf{ + }}c\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,{y_{\mathbf{1}}}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{{x_{\mathbf{1}}}{x_{\mathbf{1}}}}}{{4{y_{\mathbf{1}}}}}{\mathbf{ + }}c\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,c{\mathbf{ = }}{y_{\mathbf{1}}}{\mathbf{ - }}\frac{{x_{\mathbf{1}}^{\mathbf{2}}}}{{4{y_{\mathbf{1}}}}}\]
\[{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{4y_{\mathbf{1}}^{\mathbf{2}}{\mathbf{ - }}x_{\mathbf{1}}^{\mathbf{2}}}}{{4{y_{\mathbf{1}}}}}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{1}{{4{y_{\mathbf{1}}}}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,y{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{{x_{\mathbf{1}}}}}{{4{y_{\mathbf{1}}}}}x{\mathbf{ + }}\frac{1}{{4{y_{\mathbf{1}}}}}\]
$ \Rightarrow 4{y_1}y = {x_1}x + 1..........\left( I \right)$
Intersects x axis at \[\left( {\frac{{{\mathbf{ - }}1}}{{{x_{\mathbf{1}}}}},0} \right)\]
and y-axis at \[{\mathbf{ = }}\left( {0,\frac{1}{{4{y_{\mathbf{1}}}}}} \right)\]
\[h{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{{\mathbf{ - }}1}}{{2{x_{\mathbf{1}}}}}\]
\[{x_{\mathbf{1}}}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{{\mathbf{ - }}1}}{{2h}}\]
\[{y_{\mathbf{1}}}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{1}{{8k}}\]
Midpoint: \[\left( {\frac{{{\mathbf{ - }}1}}{{2{x_{\mathbf{1}}}}},\frac{1}{{8{y_{\mathbf{1}}}}}} \right):\left( {h,k} \right)\]
\[4y_{\mathbf{1}}^{\mathbf{2}} \ = x_{\mathbf{1}}^{\mathbf{2}}{\mathbf{ + }}1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,4{\left( {\frac{1}{{8k}}} \right)^{\mathbf{2}}}{\mathbf{ = }}{\left( {\frac{{{\mathbf{ - }}1}}{{2h}}} \right)^{\mathbf{2}}}{\mathbf{ + }}1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\frac{1}{{16{k^{\mathbf{2}}}}}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{1}{{4{h^{\mathbf{2}}}}}{\mathbf{ + }}1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,1{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{16{k^{\mathbf{2}}}}}{{4{h^{\mathbf{2}}}}}{\mathbf{ + }}16{k^{\mathbf{2}}}\]
$\begin{gathered}
  {h^2} = 4{k^2} + 16{h^2}{k^2} \\
  {x^2} - 4{y^2} - 16{x^2}{y^2} = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $
This is the required equation.

Question:
Let
$S = \left\{ {\left( {x,y} \right) \in {{\mathbf{R}}^2}:\frac{{{y^2}}}{{1 + r}} - \frac{{{x^2}}}{{1 - r}} = 1} \right\},$
where $r \ne \pm 1.$ Then S represents:
Options: a hyperbola whose eccentricity is $\frac{2}{{\sqrt {1 - r} }},$ when $0 < r < 1$
an ellipse whose eccentricity is $\sqrt {\frac{2}{{r + 1}}} $, when $ r > 1$
a hyperbola whose eccentricity is $\frac{2}{{\sqrt {r + 1} }},$ when $0 < r < 1$
an ellipse whose eccentricity is $\frac{1}{{\sqrt {r + 1} }},$ when $r > 1$
Solution:
Since, $r \ne \pm 1,$ then there are two cases,
when r > 1
$\frac{{{x^2}}}{{r - 1}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{r + 1}} = 1\,\,\,\left( {{\text{Ellipse}}} \right)$
Then,
\[\begin{gathered}
  \left( {r - 1} \right) = \left( {r + 1} \right)\left( {1 - {e^2}} \right) \Rightarrow 1 - {e^2} = \frac{{\left( {r - 1} \right)}}{{\left( {r + 1} \right)}} \\
   \Rightarrow {e^2} = 1 - \frac{{\left( {r - 1} \right)}}{{\left( {r + 1} \right)}} = \frac{2}{{\left( {r + 1} \right)}} \\
   \Rightarrow e = \sqrt {\frac{2}{{\left( {r + 1} \right)}}} \\
\end{gathered} \]
When $0 < r < 1,$ then
$\frac{{{x^2}}}{{1 - r}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{1 + r}} = - 1\,\,\,\,\left( {{\text{Hyperbola}}} \right)$
Then,
$\begin{gathered}
  \left( {1 - r} \right) = \left( {1 + r} \right)\left( {{e^2} - 1} \right) \\
   \Rightarrow {e^2} = 1 + \frac{{\left( {1 - r} \right)}}{{\left( {r + 1} \right)}} = \frac{{2}}{{\left( {r + 1} \right)}} \\
   \Rightarrow e = \sqrt {\frac{{2}}{{r + 1}}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question:

M = 305.4 x 10–4 = 0.0305 kg/mol

Question: Element 'B ' forms ccp structure and 'A' occupies half of the octahedral voids, while oxygen atoms occupy all the tetrahedral voids. The structure of bimetallic oxide is :
Solution:
No. of lattice points = No. of Oh voids
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times {\text{No}}{\text{. of Td voids in ccp structure}}$
No. of atoms of B = 4
No. of atoms of A = $\frac{1}{2} \times {\text{No}}{\text{. of Oh voids}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 4 = 2$
No. of atoms of O = No. of all Td voids
$\begin{gathered}
   = 2 \times {\text{No}}{\text{. of lattice points}} \\
   = 2 \times 4 = 8 \\
  {\text{Hence, A : B : O = 1 : 2 : 4}} \\
\end{gathered} $
Therefore, the formula of the compound is AB2O4

Question: An open vessel at 27°C is heated until two fifth of the air (assumed as an ideal gas) in it has escaped from the vessel. Assuming that the volume of the vessel remains constant, the temperature at which the vessel has been heated is :
Solution:
At 27°C or 300 K number of moles of an ideal gas = n2. At T2 K number of moles of the ideal gas = n2
Number of moles escaped $ = \frac{{2{n_1}}}{5}$
${n_2} = {n_1} - \frac{{2{n_1}}}{5} = \frac{{3{n_1}}}{5}$
PV = nRT (Ideal gas equation) At constant volume and pressure.
\[\begin{gathered}
  n \propto \frac{1}{T};\,\,{n_1}{T_1} = {n_2}{T_2} \\
  {T_2} = \frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_2}}}{T_1};{T_2} = \frac{{{n_1}}}{{\frac{{3{n_1}}}{5}}} \times {T_1} \\
   = \frac{5}{3} \times 300 = 500\,{\text{K}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Let $P$ be a point on the ellipse $\frac{{{x^2}}}{9} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{4} = 1$. Let the line passing through $P$ and parallel to $y$-axis meet the circle ${x^2} + {y^2} = 9$ at point $Q$ such that $P$ and $Q$ are on the same side of the $x$-axis. Then, the eccentricity of the locus of the point $R$ on $PQ$ such that $PR:RQ = 4:3$ as $P$ moves on the ellipse is:
Solution:
$h = 3\cos \theta ;$
$k = \frac{{18}}{7}\sin \theta $
$\therefore $ locus $ = \frac{{{x^2}}}{9} + \frac{{49{y^2}}}{{324}} = 1$
$e = \sqrt {1 - \frac{{324}}{{49 \times 9}}} = \frac{{\sqrt {117} }}{2} = \frac{{\sqrt {13} }}{7}$

Question: The sum of two forces $\vec P$and $\vec Q$is $\vec R$, such that $\left| {\vec R} \right| = \left| {\vec P} \right|$. The angle $\alpha$ (in degree) that the resultant of $2\vec P$and $\vec Q$ will make with $\vec Q$ is _____
Solution:
$\left| {\vec P + \vec Q} \right| = \left| {\vec P} \right|$
${P^2} + {Q^2} + 2PQ\cos \theta = {P^2}$  [$\theta$ is the angle between ${\vec P}$ and ${\vec Q}$]
$ \Rightarrow Q + 2P\,\cos \theta = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = - \frac{Q}{{2P}}$
$\tan \alpha = \frac{{2P\sin \theta }}{{2P\cos \theta + Q}} = \infty $
$\because \,\left[ {2P\cos \theta + Q} \right] = 0$
$\alpha = 90^\circ $

Question: In the given figure the blocks $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C weigh $4 \mathrm{~kg}, 6 \mathrm{~kg}$ and 8 kg respectively. Solution:
For 8 kg to move with constant velocity $\mathrm{F}_{\text {net }}=0$.

$\therefore \mathrm{F}=90+\mathrm{T}+50$ (for 8kg block)

Question: A balloon and its content having mass m is moving up with an acceleration 'a'. The mass that must be released from the content so that the balloon starts moving up with an acceleration '3a' will be : (Take 'g' as acceleration due to gravity)
Solution:
F – mg = ma
F = ma + mg
F – (m – x)g = (m – x) 3a
Put F
Ma + mg – mg + xg = 3ma – 3xa
\[x = \frac{{2ma}}{{g + 3a}}\]

Question: A 100 V carrier wave is made to vary between 160 V and 40 V by a modulating signal. What is the modulation index?
Solution:
Maximum amplitude = Em + Ec = 160
Em + 100 = 160
Em = 160 – 100 = 60
Modulation index,
\[\begin{gathered}
  \mu = \frac{{{E_m}}}{{{E_c}}} = \frac{{60}}{{100}} \\
  \mu = 0.6 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A monatomic ideal gas, initially at temperature $${T_1}$$ is enclosed in a cylinder fitted with a frictionless piston. The gas is allowed to expand adiabatically to a temperature $${T_2}$$ by releasing the piston suddenly. If $${l_1}\,and\,\,{l_2}$$ are the lengths of the gas column, before and after the expansion respectively, then the value of $$\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}}$$ will be:
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

For Adiabatic process
$T{V^{\gamma  - 1}} = C$
${T_1}V_1^{\gamma  - 1} = {T_2}V_2^{\gamma  - 1}$
$\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{V_2}}}{{{V_1}}}} \right)^{\gamma  - 1}} = {\left( {\frac{{A{l_2}}}{{A{l_1}}}} \right)^{\gamma  - 1}}$ where $A$ is base area of container
$ = {\left( {\frac{{{l_2}}}{{{l_1}}}} \right)^{\frac{5}{3} - 1}}$
$\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{l_2}}}{{{l_1}}}} \right)^{\frac{2}{3}}}$

Question: Let the foci of the ellipse \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{16}} + \;\frac{{{y^2}}}{7} = \;1\] and the hyperbola \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{144}} - \;\frac{{{y^2}}}{\alpha } = \;\frac{1}{{25}}\] coincide. Then the length of the latus rectum of the hyperbola is :-
Solution:
Ellipse : \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{16}} + \;\frac{{{y^2}}}{7} = \;1\]
Eccentricity \[ = \;\sqrt {1 - \frac{7}{{16}}} \; = \;\frac{3}{4}\]
Foci \[ \equiv \;( \pm \;a\;e,\;0)\; \equiv \;( \pm \;3,\;0)\]
Hyperbola : \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{\left( {\frac{{144}}{{25}}} \right)}} - \;\frac{{{y^2}}}{{\left( {\frac{\alpha }{{25}}} \right)}} = \;1\]
Eccentricity = \[\sqrt {1 + \;\frac{\alpha }{{144}}} \; = \;\frac{1}{{12}}\;\sqrt {144 + \;\alpha } \]
Foci \[ \equiv \;( \pm \;ae,\;0)\; \equiv \;\left( { \pm \;\frac{{12}}{5}\;.\;\frac{1}{{12}}\;\sqrt {144 + \;\alpha } ,\;0} \right)\]
If foci coincide then \[3 = \;\frac{1}{5}\;\sqrt {144 + \;\alpha } \;\; \Rightarrow \;\;\alpha = \;81\]
Hence, hyperbola is \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{{12}}{5}} \right)}^2}}} - \;\frac{{{y^2}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{9}{5}} \right)}^2}}}\; = \;1\]
Length of latus rectum \[ = \;2\; \cdot \frac{{\frac{{81}}{{25}}}}{{\frac{{12}}{5}}} = \;\frac{{27}}{{10}}\]

Question: 10 divisions on the main scale of a Vernier calliper coincide with 11 divisions on the Vernier scale. If each division on the main scale is of 5 units, the least count of the instrument is:

$ = 1MSD - \frac{{10}}{{11}}MSD$
$ = \frac{{1MSD}}{{11}} = \frac{5}{{11}}{\text{units}}$

Question: A paramagnetic material has 10<sup>28</sup> atoms/m<sup>3</sup>. Its magnetic susceptibility at temperature 350 K is 2.8 × 10<sup>–</sup><sup>4</sup>. Its susceptibility at 300 K is:
Solution:
According to Curie’s law for paramagnetic substances
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{{\chi _1}}}{{{\chi _2}}} = \frac{{{T_{{C_2}}}}}{{{T_{{C_1}}}}} \\
  \frac{{2.8 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{{\chi _2}}} = \frac{{300}}{{350}} \\
  {\chi _2} = \frac{{2.8 \times 350 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{300}} \\
   = 3.266 \times {10^{ - 4}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question:
A particle of mass m is moving in a straight line with momentum p. Starting at time t = 0, a force F = kt acts in the same direction on the moving particle during time interval T so that its momentum changes from p to 3p. Here k is a constant. The value of T is:

(all quantities are measured in S.I unit)
Solution:
From Newton's second law
$\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{dp}}{{dt}} = F = kt \\
  {\text{Integrating both sides we get,}} \\
  \int_p^{3p} {dp = \int_0^T {kt\,dt \Rightarrow \left[ p \right]_p^{3p}} } = k\left[ {\frac{{{t^2}}}{2}} \right]_0^T \\
   \Rightarrow 2p = \frac{{k{T^2}}}{2} \Rightarrow T = 2\sqrt {\frac{p}{k}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Let the product of the focal distances of the point \[{\text{P}}\left( {4,2\sqrt 3 } \right)\]on the hyperbola \[{\text{H}}:\frac{{{{\text{x}}^2}}}{{{{\text{a}}^2}}} - \frac{{{{\text{y}}^2}}}{{{{\text{b}}^2}}} = 1\]be 32. Let the length of the conjugate axis of H be p and the length of its latus rectum be q. Then p2 + q2 is equal to ……
Solution:
\[\frac{{{{\text{x}}^2}}}{{{{\text{a}}^2}}} - \frac{{{{\text{y}}^2}}}{{{{\text{b}}^2}}} = 1\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,.......\left( 1 \right)\]
\[{\text{P}}\left( {4,2\sqrt 3 } \right)\]
PS1.PS2 = 32
|PS1 – PS2| = 2a
\[{\text{P}}\left( {4,2\sqrt 3 } \right)\]lies on H
\[\therefore \frac{{16}}{{{{\text{a}}^2}}} - \frac{{12}}{{{{\text{b}}^2}}} = 1\]
16b2 – 12a2 = a2b2 …………....(2)
|PS1 – PS2|2 = 4a2
\[{\text{PS}}_1^2 + {\text{PS}}_2^2 - 2{\text{P}}{{\text{S}}_1}.{\text{P}}{{\text{S}}_2} = 4{{\text{a}}^2}\]
(ae – 4)2 + 12 + (ae + 4)2 + 12 – 64 = 4a2
2a2e2 – 8 = 4a2
a2 + b2 – 4 = 2a2
b2 – a2 = 4
(2) & (3) ⇒ 16(a2 + 4) – 12a2 = a2(a2 + 4)
⇒ 16a2 + 64 – 12a2 = a4 + 4a2
⇒ a4 = 64
⇒ a2 = 8
∴ b2 = 12
\[{{\text{p}}^2} + {{\text{q}}^2} = 4{{\text{b}}^2} + \frac{{4{{\text{b}}^2}}}{{{{\text{a}}^2}}}\]
= 120

Question: An evacuated glass vessel weighs 40.0 g when empty, 135.0 g when filled with a liquid of density 0.95 g mL<sup>–1</sup> and 40.5 g when filled with an ideal gas at 0.82 atm at 250 K. The molar mass of the gas in g mol<sup>–1</sup> is: (Given: R = 0.082 L atm K<sup>–1</sup> mol<sup>–1</sup>)
Solution:
Weight of gas = 40.5 – 40 = 0.5g
Mass of liquid = 135 – 40 = 95g
Volume of glass vessel \[ = \frac{{95.0g}}{{0.95g/ml}} = 100\,\,ml\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \therefore PV = \left( {\frac{W}{M}} \right)RT \\
  \left( {{\text{0}}{\text{.82 atm}}} \right)\left( {100 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right) = \left( {\frac{{0.5}}{M}} \right)\left( {0.0821} \right)\left( {250} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]
M = 125 g/mol

Question:
Hydrogen atom from excited state comes to the ground by emitting a photon of wavelength $\lambda .$ The value of principal quantum number ‘n’ of the excited state will be:
(R: Rydberg constant)
Solution:
$\frac{{ - Rch}}{{{{\left( n \right)}^2}}} + \frac{{Rch}}{1} = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }$
$\frac{{ - R}}{{{n^2}}} + R = \frac{1}{\lambda }$
$R - \frac{1}{\lambda } = \frac{R}{{{n^2}}}$
$\frac{{\lambda R - 1}}{\lambda } = \frac{R}{{{n^2}}}$
${n^2} = \frac{{\lambda R}}{{\lambda R - 1}} \Rightarrow n = \sqrt {\frac{{\lambda R}}{{\lambda R - 1}}} $

Question: A block of mass 10 kg is kept on a rough inclined plane as shown in the figure. A force of 3 N is applied on the block. The coefficient of static friction between the plane and the block is 0.6. What should be the minimum value of force P, such that the block does not move downward? (take g = 10 ms-2)
Solution:
\[mg\sin 45^\circ = \frac{{100}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 50\sqrt 2 \]
\[\left[ {\because m = 10kg,\,\,g = 9.8m{s^{ - 2}}} \right]\]
\[\mu mg\cos \theta = 0.6 \times mg \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 0.6 \times 50\sqrt 2 \]
\[3 + mg\sin \theta = P + \mu mg\cos \theta \]
\[3 + 50\sqrt 2 - P + 30\sqrt 2 \]
\[\therefore P = 31.28 = 32N\]

Question: A rocket is fired vertically from the earth with an acceleration of 2g, where g is the gravitational acceleration. On an inclined plane inside the rocket, making an angle θ with the horizontal, a point object of mass m is kept. Question: The top of a water tank is open to air and its water level is maintained. It is giving out 0.74 m<sup>3</sup> water per minute through a circular opening of 2 cm radius in its wall. The depth of the centre of the opening from the level of water in the tank is close to:
Solution:
Here, volumetric flow rate
\[\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  { = \frac{{0.74}}{{60}} = \pi {r^2}v = \left( {\pi \times 4 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}} \right) \times \sqrt {2gh} } \\
  { \Rightarrow \sqrt {2gh} = \frac{{74 \times 100}}{{240\pi }} \Rightarrow \sqrt {2gh} = \frac{{740}}{{24\pi }}} \\
  { \Rightarrow 2gh = \frac{{740 \times 740}}{{24 \times 24 \times 10}}\left( {\because {\pi ^2} = 10} \right)} \\
  { \Rightarrow h = \frac{{74 \times 74}}{{2 \times 24 \times 24}} \approx 4.8m}
\end{array}\]
i.e., The depth of the centre of the opening from the level of water in the tank is close to 4.8 m

Question: In an experiment to verify Newton's law of cooling, a graph is plotted between, the temperature difference (ΔT) of the water and surroundings and time as shown in figure. The initial temperature of water is taken as 80°C. The value of t2 as mentioned in the graph will be___________.
Solution:
\[\Delta T = {T_{water}} - {T_{sourounding}} = T - {T_s}\]
\[At\,\,t = 0\]
\[\Delta T = 60\,\,and\,\,T = 80^\circ C\]
\[80 - {T_s} = 60\]
Therefore, \[{T_s} = 20^\circ C\]
Newton law of cooling
\[ - \frac{{\left( {{T_f} - {T_i}} \right)}}{{\Delta t}} = k\left( {\frac{{{T_i} + {T_{fi}}}}{2} - {T_s}} \right)\]
Between 0 to 6 minute
\[\frac{{20}}{{6\min }} - k\left[ {\left( {\frac{{60 + 40}}{2} + 20} \right) - 20} \right]\]
Between 6 to t2 minute
\[\frac{{20}}{{{t_2} - 6}} = k\left[ {\left( {\frac{{40 + 20}}{2} + 20} \right) - 20} \right]\]
After dividing by t2 = 16 min.

Question: For some \[\theta \; \in \;\left( {0,\;\frac{\pi }{2}} \right),\] if the eccentricity of the hyperbola, \[{x^2} - {y^2}\,{\sec ^2}\theta  = \;10\] is \[\sqrt 5 \] times the eccentricity of the ellipse, \[{x^2}{\sec ^2}\theta  + {y^2} = 5,\] then the length of the latus rectum of the ellipse, is :
Solution:
\[{\text{Equation}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{hyperbola}}:\,\,{x^2} - {y^2}\;{\sec ^2}\theta = \;10\]
\[{\text{Equation}}\,\,{\text{ellipse}}:\;{x^2}{\sec ^2}\theta \; + \;{y^2} = \;5\]
Given,
\[\sqrt {1 + \;\frac{{10\;{{\cos }^2}\,\theta }}{{10}}} = \;\sqrt 5 \;\;\sqrt {1 - \frac{{5{{\cos }^2}\theta }}{5}} \]
\[1 + {\cos ^2}\theta = \;5 - 5\;{\cos ^2}\theta \]
\[6\;{\cos ^2}\theta = \;4\]
\[\cos \theta = \; \pm \;\sqrt {\frac{2}{3}} \]
Length of latus rectum \[ = \frac{{2{a^2}}}{b}\]

$ = \frac{{2\left( {5\,{{\cos }^2}\theta } \right)}}{{\sqrt 5 }} = 2\sqrt 5 \left( {\frac{2}{3}} \right) = \frac{{4\sqrt 5 }}{3}$

Question: In a uniform magnetic field, the magnetic needle has a magnetic moment $$9.85 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,A/{m^2}$$ and moment of inertia $$5 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,kg\,{m^2}.$$ If it performs 10 complete oscillations in 5 seconds then the magnitude of the magnetic field is _____ mT. [Take $${\pi ^2}\,as\,9.85]$$

$$\eqalign{
  & {\text{T}} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{I}{{{\text{MB}}}}} \cr

  & {\text{B}} = 8 \times {10^{ - 3}}\;{\text{T}} \cr} $$

Question: For the determination of refractive index of glass slab, a travelling microscope is used whose main scale contains 300 equal divisions equals to 15 cm. The vernier scale attached to the microscope has 25 divisions equals to 24 divisions of main scale. The least count (LC) of the travelling microscope is (in cm) :

In a screw gauge, there are 100 divisions on the circular scale and the main scale moves by $0.5{\text{mm}}$ on a complete rotation of the circular scale. The zero of circular scale lies 6 divisions below the line of graduation when two studs are brought in contact with each other. When a wire is placed between the studs, 4 linear scale divisions are clearly visible while ${46^{{\text{th}}}}$ division the circular scale coincide with the reference line. The diameter of the wire is ____ $ \times {10^{ - 2}}{\text{mm}}$.
Solution:
Least count $ = \frac{{{\text{Pitch}}}}{{{\text{No}}{\text{.ofcirculardivisions}}}}$$ = \frac{{0.5{\text{mm}}}}{{100}}$
Least count $ = 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{mm}}$
Positive Error $ = $ MSR $ + {\text{CSR}}\left( {{\text{LC}}} \right)$
$ = 0{\text{mm}} + 6\left( {5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}{\text{mm}}} \right)$
Reading of Diameter $ = $ MSR $ + $ CSR $($ LC) $ - $ Positive zero error $ = 4 \times 0.5{\text{mm}} + \left( {46\left( {5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)} \right) - 6\left( {5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right){\text{mm}}$
$ = 2{\text{mm}} + 40 \times 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{mm}} = 2.2{\text{mm}}$

Question:
KBr is doped with 10–5 mole percent of SrBr2. The number of cationic vacancies in 1 g of KBr crystal is ................x 1014. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
[Atomic Mass: K: 39.1 u, Br : 79.9 u
\[{N_A} = \;6.023\; \times \;{10^{23}}]\]
Solution:
For every \[S{r^{ + 2}}\] ion, 1 cationic vacancy is created. Hence, no. of \[S{r^{ + 2}}\] ion = Number of cationic vacancies
Since mole percentage of \[SrB{r_2}\] dropped is \[{10^{ - 5}}\] to that of total moles of KBr.
Hence,
No. of cationic vacancy\[ = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{119}} \times {N_A}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   = \frac{1}{{119}} \times {10^{ - 7}} \times 6.022 \times {10^{23}} \\
   = 5 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times {10^{ - 7}} \times {10^{23}} = 5 \times {10^{14}} \\
\end{gathered} \]
Answer is 5

  mg\;\cos \;45 = N \\
  {f_L} = {\mu _s}N \\
  {\mu _s} = \tan 45 = 1 \\
  or \\
  \tan \theta = {\mu _s}\left( {\theta \;is\;angle\;of\;repose} \right) \\
  \tan \;45 = {\mu _s} = 1 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Two masses ${M_1}$ and ${M_2}$ are tied together at the two ends of a light inextensible string that passes over a frictionless pulley. When the mass ${M_2}$ is twice that of ${M_1}$. the acceleration of the system is${a_1}$. When the mass ${M_2}$ is thrice that of ${M_1}$. The acceleration of The system is ${{\text{a}}_2}$. The ratio $\frac{{{{\text{a}}_1}}}{{{{\text{a}}_2}}}$ will be:

A spring whose unstretched length is $$l$$ has a force constant $$k$$. The spring is cut into two pieces of unstretched lengths \[{l_1}\] and \[{l_2}\] where, \[{l_1} = n{l_2}\]and n is an integer. The ratio \[\frac{{{k_1}}}{{{k_2}}}\]of the corresponding force constants, $${k_1}$$ and $${k_2}$$ will be:
Solution:
\[{l_1} + {l_2} = l\]and \[{l_1} = n{l_2}\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,{l_1} = \frac{{nl}}{{n + 1}}\]and \[{l_2} = \frac{l}{{n + 1}}\]
As \[k \propto \frac{1}{l}\],
\[\therefore \,\,\,\frac{{{k_1}}}{{{k_2}}} = \frac{{\frac{l}{{\left( {n + 1} \right)}}}}{{\frac{{\left( {nl} \right)}}{{\left( {n + 1} \right)}}}} = \frac{1}{n}\]

Question:
Read the following statements :
A. When small temperature difference between a liquid and its surrounding is doubled the rate of loss of heat of the liquid becomes twice.
B. Two bodies P and Q having equal surface areas are maintained at temperature 10°C and 20°C. The thermal radiation emitted in a given time by P and Q are in the ratio 1:1.15
C. A carnot Engine working between 100 K and 400 K has an efficiency of 75%
D. When small temperature difference between a liquid and its surrounding is quadrupled, the rate of loss of heat of the liquid becomes twice.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Solution:
Heat transfer
A. by Newton’s low of colling \[\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}} = \propto \Delta T\]
B. \[H = \frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}} = \sigma eA{T^4} \Rightarrow \frac{{{H_P}}}{{{H_Q}}}{\left( {\frac{{{T_P}}}{{{T_Q}}}} \right)^4} = {\left( {\frac{{283}}{{293}}} \right)^4}\]
\[{H_P}:{H_Q} = 1{\left( {1.03} \right)^4} = 1:{\left( {1.03} \right)^4} = 1:1.15\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;B\;is\;correct\]
C. \[\eta = 1 - \frac{{100}}{{400}} = \frac{3}{4} = 75\% \]
D. is wrong as \[\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}} \propto \Delta T\]

Question: Least count of a vernier caliper is \[\frac{1}{{20N}}cm.\] The value of one division on the main scale is 1 mm. Then the number of divisions of main scale that coincide with N divisions of vernier scale is :
Solution:
Least count of vernier calipers = \[\frac{1}{{20N}}cm\]
\[\because \]Least count = 1 MSD – 1 VSD
let x no. of divisions of main scale coincides with N division of vernier scale, then
\[1\;VSD = \frac{{x \times 1mm}}{N}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \;\frac{1}{{20N}}cm = 1mm - \frac{{x \times 1mm}}{N} \\
  \frac{1}{{2N}}mm = 1mm - \frac{x}{N}mm \\
  x = \left( {1 - \frac{1}{{2N}}} \right)N \\
  x = \frac{{2N - 1}}{2} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: An object viewed from a near point distance of 25 cm, using a microscopic lens with magnification ‘6’, gives an unresolved image. A resolved image is observed at infinite distance with a total magnification double the earlier using an eyepiece along with the given lens and a tube of length 0.6 m, if the focal length of the eyepiece is equal to _____ cm.
Solution:
For simple microscope
\[{\text{m}} = 1 + \frac{{\text{D}}}{{{{\text{f}}_0}}}\]
\[6 = 1 + \frac{{\text{D}}}{{{{\text{f}}_0}}}\]
\[5 = \frac{{25}}{{{{\text{f}}_0}}}\]
\[{{\text{f}}_0} = 5\;{\text{cm}}\]
For compound microscope
\[{\text{m}} = \frac{{{\text{l}}.{\text{D}}}}{{{{\text{f}}_0} \cdot {{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}}}}\]
\[12 = \frac{{60 \times 25}}{{5{{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}}}}{{\text{f}}_{\text{e}}} = 25\;{\text{cm}}\]

Question: A certain gas obeys $P\left( {{V_m} - b} \right) = RT$. The value of ${\left( {\frac{{\partial Z}}{{\partial P}}} \right)_T}$ is $\frac{{xb}}{{RT}}$. The value of x is _____. (Z : compressibility factor)
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  P\left( {{V_m} - b} \right) = RT \\
  P{V_m} - Pb = RT \\
  \left( {\frac{{P{V_m}}}{{RT}}} \right) - \frac{{Pb}}{{RT}} = 1 \\
  Z - \frac{{Pb}}{{RT}} = 1 \\
  So\,\,Z = 1 + \frac{{Pb}}{{RT}} \\
  {\left( {\frac{{\partial Z}}{{\partial P}}} \right)_T} = \frac{b}{{RT}} \\
  So\,\,x = 1 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: What will be the projection of vector \[\overrightarrow A  = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k\] on vector \[\overrightarrow B  = \hat i + \hat j\,\,?\]
Solution:
Projection \[ = \frac{{\vec A \cdot \vec B}}{{|\vec B|}}(\hat B)\]
\[ = \frac{{(\hat i + \hat j + \hat k) \cdot (\hat i + \hat j)}}{{\sqrt 2 }}\frac{{(\hat i + \hat j)}}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]
\[ = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 2 }}\frac{{(\hat i + \hat j)}}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]
\[ = (\hat i + \hat j)\]

Question: Which of the following polymers is synthesized using a free radical polymerization technique ?

Let the ellipse $\mathrm{E}: \frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{144}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{169}=1$ and the hyperbola $H: \frac{x^{2}}{16}-\frac{y^{2}}{\lambda^{2}}=-1$ have the same foci. If $e$ and $L$ respectively denote the eccentricity and the length of the latus rectum of H , then the value of $24(\mathrm{e}+\mathrm{L})$ is :
Solution:
Equation of hyperbola : $\frac{y^{2}}{\lambda^{2}}-\frac{x^{2}}{16}=1$
Equation of ellipse : $\frac{x^{2}}{144}+\frac{y^{2}}{169}=1$
$e^{\prime}=\sqrt{1-\frac{144}{169}}=\frac{5}{13}$
focus $\Rightarrow(0,5)$
$\Rightarrow \lambda \sqrt{1+\frac{16}{\lambda^{2}}}=5$
$\Rightarrow \lambda^{2}+16=25$
$\lambda=3$
Eccentricity of hyperbola $=\sqrt{1+\frac{16}{\lambda^{2}}}=\frac{5}{3}$
Length of latus rectum of hyperbola $=\frac{2(16)}{3}=\frac{32}{3}$
$24(\mathrm{e}+\ell)=24\left[\frac{5}{3}+\frac{32}{3}\right]=8 \times 37=296$

Question:
As shown in the figure, a block of mass $\sqrt 3 $kg is kept on a horizontal rough surface of coefficient of friction $\frac{1}{{3\sqrt 3 }}.$ The critical force to be applied on the vertical surface as shown at an angle 60° with horizontal such that it does not move, will be 3x. The value of x will be _____.
[g =10 m/s2 ; sin 60° = $\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2};$ cos 60° = $\frac{1}{2}$]
Solution:
$F\cos 60^\circ = \mu N\,\,or\,\,\frac{F}{2} = \frac{1}{{3\sqrt 3 }}N$ …(1)
& $N = \sin 60^\circ + \sqrt 3 g$ …(2)
From equation (1) & (2)
$\begin{gathered}
  \frac{F}{2} = \frac{1}{{3\sqrt 3 }}\left( {\frac{{F\sqrt 3 }}{2} + \sqrt 3 g} \right) \\
   \Rightarrow F = g = 10\,{\text{Newton}}\, = 3x \\
\end{gathered} $
So, $x = \frac{{10}}{3} = 3.33$

Question: A small ball of mass m is thrown upward with velocity u from the ground. The ball experiences a resistive force mkv<sup>2</sup>, where v is its speed. The maximum height attained by the ball is:
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \overrightarrow a = \frac{{\overrightarrow F }}{m} = - \left[ {k{v^2} + g} \right] \\
   \Rightarrow v.\frac{{dv}}{{dh}} = - \left[ {k{v^2} + g} \right] \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \int\limits_u^0 {\frac{{v.dv}}{{k{v^2} + g}}} = - \int\limits_0^H {dh} \\
  \frac{1}{{2k}}\ln \left[ {k{v^2} + g} \right]_u^0 = - H \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{2k}}\ln \left[ {\frac{{k{u^2} + g}}{g}} \right] = H \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: If the line $\alpha x+2 y=1$, where $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$, does not meet the hyperbola $x^{2}-9 y^{2}=9$, then a possible value of $\alpha$ is :
Solution:
$\mathrm{y}=\frac{1-\alpha \mathrm{x}}{2}$
Put this in equation of hyperbola
$\therefore \mathrm{x}^{2}-9\left(\frac{1-\alpha \mathrm{x}}{2}\right)^{2}=9$
$\left(4-9 \alpha^{2}\right) x^{2}+18 \alpha x-45=0$
$\because $ line does not intersect hyperbola
$\therefore \mathrm{D}<0$
$\Rightarrow \alpha^{2}-\frac{5}{9}>0$
$\Rightarrow \alpha \in\left(-\infty,-\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}\right) \cup\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}, \infty\right)$
Here $\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3} \simeq 0.74$

Question: Two vectors $$\vec X$$and $$\vec Y$$have equal magnitude. The magnitude of $$\left( {\vec X - \vec Y} \right)$$ is n times the magnitude of $$\left( {\vec X + \vec Y} \right)$$. The angle between $$\vec X$$and $$\vec Y$$is :
Solution:
$$\eqalign{
  & |\vec X + \vec Y{|^2} = {X^2} + {Y^2} + 2XY\cos \theta \cr
  & |\vec X - \vec Y{|^2} = {X^2} + {Y^2} - 2XY\cos \theta \cr
  & {n^2} = \frac{{2 - 2\cos \theta }}{{2 + 2\cos \theta }} \cr
  & {n^2} = \frac{{1 - \cos \theta }}{{1 + \cos \theta }} \cr
  & {n^2} + {n^2}\cos \theta = 1 - \cos \theta \cr
  & \cos \theta = - \left( {\frac{{{n^2} - 1}}{{{n^2} + 1}}} \right) \cr} $$

Question:
Consider the equilibrium
\[CO({\text{g}}) + 3{{\text{H}}_2}({\text{g}}) \rightleftharpoons C{H_4}({\text{g}}) + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}({\text{g}})\]
If the pressure applied over the system increases by two fold at constant temperature then
(A) Concentration of reactants and products increases.
(B) Equilibrium will shift in forward direction.
(C) Equilibrium concentration of products increases.
(D) Equilibrium constant remains unchanged as concentration of reactants and products remain same.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
Solution:
Increasing the pressure on a gaseous equilibrium system at constant temperature will cause the equilibrium to shift in the direction that reduces the number of gas molecules. In this reaction, there are 4 moles of gas on the reactant side (1 CO + 3 H2) and 2 moles of gas on the product side (1 CH4 + 1 H2O).
Le Chatelier's principle states that if a change of condition is applied to a system in equilibrium, the system will shift in a direction that relieves the stress. The stress here is the increased pressure. The system will shift to the side with fewer gas molecules to reduce the pressure.
Therefore, the equilibrium will shift to the right (forward direction), increasing the concentration of products (CH4 and H2O) and decreasing the concentration of reactants (CO and H2). However, the total number of moles decreases, leading to an increase in the overall concentration of both reactants and products.
(A) and (B) only

Question: A paramagnetic sample shows a net magnetisation of 6 A/m when it is placed in an external magnetic field of 0.4 T at a temperature of 4 K. When the sample is placed in an external magnetic field of 0.3 T at a temperature of 24 K, then the magnetisation will be:
Solution:
\[M = \frac{{C{B_{ext}}}}{T}\]
\[6 = \frac{{C \times 0.4}}{4}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,C = 60\]
\[\therefore \] Case – II :- \[M = \frac{{60 \times 0.3}}{{24}} = \frac{{60 \times 3}}{{240}} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75\,A/m\]

Question: The locus of the midpoints of the chord of the circle \[{x^2} + {y^2} = 25\] which is tangent to the hyperbola $\frac{{{x^2}}}{9} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{16}} = 1$ is
Solution:
Tangent of hyperbola
\[y = mx \pm \sqrt {9{m^2} - 16} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( i \right)\]
Which is a chord of circle with mid-point \[\left( {h,\,\,k} \right)\]
So equation of chord \[T = {S_1}\]
\[hx + ky = {h^2} + {k^2}\]
\[y = - \frac{{hx}}{k} + \frac{{{h^2} + {k^2}}}{k}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {ii} \right)\]
By \[\left( i \right)\] and \[\left( {ii} \right)\]
\[m = - \frac{h}{k}\] and \[\sqrt {9{m^2} - 16} = \frac{{{h^2} + {k^2}}}{k}\]
\[9\frac{{{h^2}}}{{{k^2}}} - 16 = \frac{{{{\left( {{h^2} + {k^2}} \right)}^2}}}{{{k^2}}}\]
$\therefore $ Locus is \[9{x^2} - 16{y^2} = {\left( {{x^2} + {y^2}} \right)^2}\]

Question:
A block of mass m slides along a floor while a force of magnitude F is applied to it at an angle $\theta $ as shown in figure. The coefficient of kinetic friction is ${\mu _K}$. Then, the block’s acceleration ‘a’ is given by:
(g is acceleration due to gravity)
Solution:
\[N = mg - F\sin \theta \]
\[F\cos \theta - {\mu _k}N = ma\]
\[F\cos \theta - {\mu _k}\left( {mg - F\sin \theta } \right) = ma\]
\[a = \frac{F}{m}\cos \theta - {\mu _K}\left( {g - \frac{F}{m}\sin \theta } \right)\]

Question:

Choose the most appropriate answer for the statements from the options given below:

Solution:
In an ionic compound showing Frenkel defect, the smaller ion (usually cation) is dislocated from its normal site to an interstitial site. It creates a vacancy defect at its original site and an interstitial defect at its new location.
F-centers occur due to metal excess defect. So, statement II is false.

Question:

Along incline

Question:

$\mathrm{V}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{SB}}$
$\mathrm{V}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{S}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{B}}$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}+\mathrm{V}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{S}}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{V}+80$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{SA}}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}=18 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{S}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{A}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{SA}}$
$\mathrm{V}+80-100=18 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$
$\mathrm{V}-20=18$
$\mathrm{V}=38 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$

Question:
In amplitude modulation, sinusoidal carrier frequency used is denoted by ${\omega _c}$
and the signal frequency is denoted by ${\omega _m}.$ The bandwidth $\left( {\Delta {\omega _m}} \right)$ of the signal is such
that $\Delta {\omega _m} < < {\omega _c}.$ Which of the following frequencies is not contained in the modulated wave ?
Solution:
Let $c\left( t \right) = {A_C}\sin {\omega _c}t$ represent carrier wave and $m\left( t \right) = {A_m}\sin {\omega _m}t$ represent the message or the
modulating signal where ${\omega _m} = 2\pi {f_m}$ is the angular frequency of the message signal. The modulated signal cm (t) can be written as
${c_m}\left( t \right) = \left( {{A_C} + {A_m}\sin {\omega _m}t} \right)\sin {\omega _c}t$
$ = {A_C}\left( {1 + \frac{{{A_m}}}{{{A_c}}}\sin {\omega _m}t} \right)\sin {\omega _c}t$ … (i)
Note that the modulated signal now contains the message signal. From Eq. (i), we can write,
${c_m}\left( t \right) = {A_c}\sin {\omega _c}t + \mu {A_c}\sin {\omega _m}t\sin {\omega _c}t$ …(ii)
Here $\mu = {A_m}/{A_c}$ is the modulation index; in practice, $\mu $ is kept $ \leqslant 1$ to avoid distortion.
Using the trigonometric relation sin A sin B = 1/2 (cos (A – B) – cos (A + B), we can write cm (t) of Eq. (ii) as
${c_m}\left( t \right) = {A_c}\sin {\omega _c}t + \frac{{\mu {A_c}}}{2}\cos \left( {{\omega _C} - {\omega _m}} \right)t - \frac{{\mu {A_c}}}{2}\cos \left( {{\omega _C} + {\omega _m}} \right)t$ …(iii)
In amplitude modulated wave, the frequencies contained are${\omega _c} - {\omega _m},{\omega _c},{\omega _c} + {\omega _m}.$
The frequency of ${\omega _m}$ is not contained in A.M. wave
