Question: Let the foci of hyperbola coincide with the foci of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{36}+\frac{y^{2}}{16}=1$. If the eccentricity of the hyperbola is 5 , then the length of its latus rectum is:
Solution:
Let $\mathrm{e}_{1}$ be eccentricity of ellipse
$\Rightarrow e_{1}=\sqrt{1-\frac{16}{36}}=\sqrt{1-\frac{4}{9}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}$
So $\mathrm{ae}_{1}=6 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}=2 \sqrt{5}$
Now H : $\frac{x^{2}}{p^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{q^{2}}=1$
p.e $=\mathrm{ae}_{1}$
p. $5=2 \sqrt{5}$
$\mathrm{p}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{2}=1+\frac{\mathrm{q}^{2}}{\mathrm{p}^{2}} \Rightarrow 25=1+\frac{5 \mathrm{q}^{2}}{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{q}^{2}=\frac{96}{5}$
So length of $L R \frac{2 q^{2}}{p}=\frac{96}{\sqrt{5}}$

Question: Let a line \[L:2x + y = k,k > 0\]be a tangent to the hyperbola \[{x^2} - {y^2} = 3.\] If L is also a tangent to the parabola \[{y^2} = \alpha x,\] then \[\alpha \] is equal to :
Solution:
Tangent to hyperbola of
Slope \[m = - 2\] (given)
\[y = - 2x \pm \sqrt {3\left( 3 \right)} \]
\[\left( {y = mx \pm \sqrt {{a^2}{m^2} - {b^2}} } \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow y + 2x = \pm \,3 \Rightarrow 2x + y = 3\left( {k > 0} \right)\]
For parabola \[{y^2} = ax\]
\[y = mx + \frac{\alpha }{{4m}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow y = - 2x + \frac{\alpha }{{ - 8}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{\alpha }{{ - 8}} = 3\]
\[ \Rightarrow = - 24.\]

The relationship between the magnetic susceptibility (χ ) and the magnetic permeability (μ ) is given by :
(μ 0 is the permeability of free space and μ r is relative permeability)
Solution:
We have
\[{\mu _r} = \left( {1 + \chi } \right) \Rightarrow \chi = ({\mu _r} - 1)\]
\[{\mu _r} = {\mu _0}{\mu _r} \Rightarrow {\mu _r} = \frac{\mu }{{{\mu _0}}}\]
\[\therefore \,\,\chi = \left( {\frac{\mu }{{{\mu _0}}} - 1} \right)\]

Question: At 100°C, copper (Cu) has FCC unit cell structure with cell edge length of x \[\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} \]. What is the approximate density of Cu (in g cm-3) at this temperature? [Atomic Mass of Cu = 63.55 u]
Solution:
We know that density = \[\frac{{Z\left( M \right)}}{{{N_A} \times {a^3}}}\]
Given that: Z = 4 (fcc)
M= 63.5 g
NA= 6 × 1023
a = x × 10-8cm
After putting the values, we get
\[d = \frac{{4 \times 63.5}}{{6 \times {{10}^{23}} \times {x^3} \times {{10}^{ - 24}}}}\]
\[ = \frac{{422\,g\,c{m^{ - 3}}}}{{{x^3}}}\]

Question:
The following reaction occurs in the Blast Furnace where iron ore is reduced to iron metal :
Fe2O3(s) + 3 CO(g) $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2 Fe(l)+3 CO2(g)
Using the Le Chatelier’s principle, predict which one of the following will not disturb the equilibrium ?
Solution:
Fe2O3 +3CO⇌2Fe+3CO
According to Le Chatelier's principle change in concentration by changing the amount of reactant or product affect the equilibrium. However, the addition of solid reactant won't affect the concentration.
Therefore, addition of solid Fe2O3 will not disturb the equilibrium.

Question:
The pressure of a moist gas at 27°C is 4 atm. The volume of the container is doubled at the same temperature. The new pressure of the moist gas is …. ×10–1 atm. (Nearest integer)
(Given: The vapour pressure of water at 27°C is 0.4 atm)
Solution:
Pressure of moist gas = 4 – vapour pressure = 4 – 0.4 = 3.6 atm
Volume and pressure are inversely proportional.
If the volume is doubled, the pressure becomes half.
New pressure = \[\frac{{3.6}}{2}\] = 1.8 atm
Total new pressure = 1.8 + 0.4 = 2.2 atm = 22 × 10–1 atm

Consider a block kept on an inclined plane (inclined at $45^\circ $) as shown in the figure. If the force required to just push it up the incline is 2 times the force required to just prevent it from sliding down, the coefficient of friction between the block and inclined plane (µ) is equal to:
Solution:
${F_1} = mg\sin 45^\circ + f = mg\sin 45^\circ + \mu N$
${F_1} = \frac{{mg}}{{\sqrt 2 }} + \mu mg\cos 45^\circ $
${F_1} = \frac{{mg}}{{\sqrt 2 }}\left( {1 + \mu } \right)$

${F_2} = mg\sin 45^\circ - f = mg\sin 45^\circ - \mu N = \frac{{mg}}{{\sqrt 2 }}\left( {1 - \mu } \right)$
${F_1} = 2{F_2}$
\[\frac{{mg}}{{\sqrt 2 }}\left( {1 + \mu } \right) = 2\frac{{mg}}{{\sqrt 2 }}\left( {1 - \mu } \right)\]
$1 + \mu = 2 - 2\mu $
$\mu = \frac{1}{3} = 0.33$

Question:
The locus of the point of intersection of the straight lines, \[tx - 2y - 3t = 0\]
\[x - 2ty + 3 = 0\,\,\,\left( {t \in R} \right),\] is:
Options: A hyperbola with the length of conjugate axis 3
An ellipse with the length of major axis 6
Solution:
Given lines are \[tx - 2y - 3t = 0\], \[x - 2ty + 3 = 0\]
From the 2nd equation \[t = \frac{{x + 3}}{{2y}}\]
Substituting \[t = \frac{{x + 3}}{{2y}}\] in first equation
\[t\left( {x - 3} \right) - 2y = 0\]
\[\frac{{\left( {x + 3} \right)\left( {x - 3} \right)}}{{2y}} - 2y = 0\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{{x^2} - 9 - 4{y^2}}}{{2y}} = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,{x^2} - 4{y^2} = 9 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{{x^2}}}{9} - \frac{{4{y^2}}}{9} = 1 \\
  i.e\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{{x^2}}}{9} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{\frac{9}{4}}} = 1 \\
\end{gathered} \]
This is a hyperbola with eccentricity equal to \[\sqrt {1 + \frac{1}{4}} = \frac{{\sqrt 5 }}{2}\]
also, semi conjugate axis \[ = \sqrt {\frac{9}{4}} \]\[ = \frac{3}{2}\]
\[\therefore \] The length of conjugate axis is 3

Tags.Chapter: Straight lines & pair of st. lines

$$

& =5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m} \\
& =5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: A block starts moving up an inclined plane of inclination 30º with an initial velocity of \[{\nu _0}.\] It comes back to its initial position with velocity \[\frac{{{\nu _0}}}{2}.\] The value of the coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the inclined plane is close to \[\frac{I}{{1000}}.\] The nearest integer to I is …………. .
Solution:
For upward motion 

$$ \frac{1}{2}mv_0^2 = \left( {mg\frac{S}{2} + \mu mg\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}S} \right)\,\,\,\,\,.....\left( i \right) $$

For downward motion

 $$\frac{1}{2}\frac{{mv_0^2}}{4} = \left( {mg\frac{S}{2} - \mu mg\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}.S} \right)\,\,\,\,\,....\left( {ii} \right)$$

From (i) and (ii)

$4=\frac{{\left( {1 + \mu \sqrt 3 } \right)}}{{\left( {1 - \mu \sqrt 3 } \right)}}$

  $$  \Rightarrow 4 - 4\mu \sqrt 3  = 1 + \mu \sqrt 3$$   

 $$  \Rightarrow 3 = 5\mu \sqrt 3$$   

 $$∴\mu  = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{5} = \frac{{346.41}}{{1000}}$$

 $$I = 346 $$

Question: The correct decreasing order for acid strength is :-
Solution:
The acidic strength of a compound or an acid depends on the inductive effect (- I). Higher the (- I) effect of a substituent higher will be acidic strength. Now, the decreasing order of (-I) effect of the given substituents is NO2 > CN > F > Cl.
∴ The correct decreasing order of acidic strength amongst the given carboxylic acids is:
O2NCH2COOH > NCCH2COOH > FCH2COOH > ClCH2COOH

Question: A light unstretchable string passing over a smooth light pulley connects two blocks of masses m<sub>1</sub> and m<sub>2</sub>. If the acceleration of the system is \[\frac{g}{8},\] then the ratio of the masses \[\frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}}\] is:
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  {a_{sys}} = \left( {\frac{{{m_2} - {m_1}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}} \right)g = \frac{g}{8} \\
   \Rightarrow \;\frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}} = \frac{9}{7} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A given object takes n times the time to slide down 45° rough inclined plane as it takes the time to slide down an identical perfectly smooth 45° inclined plane. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the object and the surface of inclined plane is :
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  Case - I:\;No\;friction \\
  a = g\;\sin \theta \\
  \ell = \frac{1}{2}\left( {g\sin \theta } \right)t_1^2 \\
  {t_1} = \sqrt {\frac{{2\ell }}{{g\sin \theta }}} \\
  Case - 2:\;With\;friction \\
  a = g\sin \theta - \mu g\;\cos \theta \\
  \ell = \frac{1}{2}\left( {g\;\sin \;\theta - \mu g\;\cos \theta } \right)t_2^2 \\
  \sqrt {\frac{{2\ell }}{{g\sin \theta - \mu g\cos \theta }}} = n\sqrt {\frac{{2\ell }}{{g\sin \theta }}} \\
  \mu = 1 - \frac{1}{{{n^2}}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Choose the correct statement:
Options: In amplitude modulation the amplitude of the high frequency carrier wave is made to vary in proportion to the amplitude of the audio signal.
In amplitude modulation the frequency of the high frequency carrier wave is made to vary in proportion to the amplitude of the audio signal.
In frequency modulation the amplitude of the high frequency carrier wave is made to vary in proportion to the amplitude of the audio signal.
In frequency modulation the amplitude of the high frequency carrier wave is made to vary in proportion to the frequency of the audio signal.
Solution:
In amplitude modulation amplitude of carrier wave (high frequency) is varied in proportion to the amplitude of signal.
In frequency modulation frequency of carrier wave (high frequency) is varied in proportion to amplitude of signal.

PM = dRT

Question: In  the  presence  of  a  small  amount  of phosphorous, aliphatic carboxylic acids react with chlorine or bromine to yield a compound in which α - hydrogen has been replaced by halogen.  This reaction is known as :
Solution:
In the presence of a small amount of phosphorous, aliphatic carboxylic acids react with chlorine or bromine to yield a compound in which alpha-hydrogen has been replaced by halogen.
This reaction is known as Hell–Volhard–Zelinsky reaction.
\[RC{H_2}COOH\xrightarrow[{{\text{ii}}{\text{. }}{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}}]{{{\text{i}}{\text{. P/C}}{{\text{l}}_2}}}RCH\left( {Cl} \right)COOH + HCl\]

Question: Vectors $a\hat i + b\hat j + \hat k$ and $2\hat i - 3\hat j + 4\hat k$ are perpendicular to each other when 3a + 2b = 7, the ratio of a to b is $\frac{x}{2}$. The value of x is _______.
Solution:
For two perpendicular vectors
$\begin{gathered}
  \left( {a\hat i + b\hat j + \hat k} \right).\left( {2\hat i - 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) = 0 \\
  2a - 3b + 4 = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $
On solving, 2a – 3b = –4
Also given
$3a + 2b = 7$
We get a = 1, b = 2
$\frac{a}{b} = \frac{x}{2} \Rightarrow x = \frac{{2a}}{b} = \frac{{2 \times 1}}{2} \Rightarrow x = 1$

Question: A travelling microscope has 20 divisions per cm on the main scale while its Vernier scale has total 50 divisions and 25 Vernier scale divisions are equal to 24 main scale divisions, what is the least count of the travelling microscope ?
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  1\,MSD = \frac{1}{{20}}cm \\
  1\,VSD = \frac{{24}}{{25}}MSD = \frac{{24}}{{25}} \times \frac{1}{{20}}cm \\
\end{gathered} $
$\therefore $ Least count $ = \frac{1}{{20}}\left( {1 - \frac{{24}}{{25}}} \right)cm$
$\begin{gathered}
   = \frac{1}{{20}} \times \frac{1}{{25}} = \frac{1}{{500}}cm \\
   = 0.002\,cm \\
\end{gathered} $

Question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : Mohr's salt is composed of only three types of ions-ferrous, ammonium and sulphate.
Statement II : If the molar conductance at infinite dilution of ferrous, ammonium and sulphate ions are x1, x2 and x3 S cm2 mol–1, respectively then the molar conductance for Mohr's salt solution at infinite dilution would be given by x1 + x2 + 2x3
In the light of the given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
Options: Both statements I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II are false
Both statements I and Statement II are true
Solution:
Mohr's salt : FeSO4. (NH4)2SO4.6H2O

For nitrogen (N2), the mole fraction

Fe2+ (ferrous ion)

2NH4+ (ammonium ions)

2SO42-(sulfate ions)

Using Kohlrousch law
\[\lambda _m^\infty \left( {Mohr's{\text{ salt}}} \right) = {x_1} + 2{x_2} + 2{x_3}\]

Question: A uniform cylinder of mass M and radius R is to be pulled over a step of height a (a < R) by applying a force F at its centre ‘O’ perpendicular to the plane through the axes of the cylinder on the edge of the step (see figure). The minimum value of F required is
Solution:
\[Cos\,\theta = \;\frac{{\sqrt {{R^2} - \;{{(R - a)}^2}} }}{R}\]
\[ = \;\sqrt {\frac{{{R^2}}}{{{R^2}}} - \;{{\left( {\frac{{R - a}}{R}} \right)}^2}} \]
\[ = \;\sqrt {1 - {{\left( {\frac{{R - a}}{R}} \right)}^2}} \]
To pull up, torque due to F must be greater than torque due to mg i.e., \[{\tau _F}\; \geqslant \;{\tau _{mg}}\]
\[FR\; \geqslant \;mg\,\cos \,\theta R\]
For min F, Fmin = mg cos \[\theta \]
\[{F_{\min }} = \;mg\;\sqrt {1 - {{\left( {\frac{{R - a}}{R}} \right)}^2}} \]

Question: A compass needle of oscillation magnetometer oscillates 20 times per minute at a place P of dip 30°. The number of oscillations per minute become 10 at another place Q of 60° dip. The ratio of the total magnetic field at the two places (B<sub>Q</sub> : B<sub>P</sub>) is:
Solution:
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{I}{{{B_H}M}}} $
${T_1} = 3\sec = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{I}{{\left( {{B_P}\cos 30^\circ } \right)M}}} $
${T_2} = 6\sec = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{I}{{\left( {{B_Q}\cos 60^\circ } \right)M}}} $
$\frac{3}{6} = \sqrt {\frac{1}{{\left( {{B_P}\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right)}} \times \left( {{B_Q}/2} \right)} $
$\frac{3}{6} = \sqrt {\left( {\frac{{{B_Q}}}{{\sqrt 3 {B_P}}}} \right)} $
$\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{4} = \frac{{{B_Q}}}{{{B_P}}}$
${B_Q}:{B_P} = \sqrt 3 :4$

Question: A sinusoidal wave $y\left( t \right) = 40\sin \left( {10 \times {{10}^6}\pi t} \right)$is amplitude modulated by another sinusoidal wave $x\left( t \right) = 20\sin \left( {1000\pi t} \right)$. The amplitude of minimum frequency component of modulated signal is:
Solution:
$\mu = \frac{{Am}}{{Ac}} = \frac{{20}}{{40}} = \frac{1}{2}$
Modulated amplitude $ = \frac{{\mu Ac}}{2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{40}}{2} = 10$

Question: A short bar magnet placed with its axis at $30^{\circ}$ with an external field of 800 Gauss, experiences a torque of 0.016 N.m. The work done in moving it from most stable to most unstable position is $\alpha \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\tau=\mu \mathrm{B} \sin \theta \Rightarrow 0.016=\mu \times \mathrm{B} \times \frac{1}{2}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \mu=\frac{0.032}{B} \\
& W_{e x t}=U_{f}-U_{i}=\mu B-(\mu B)=2 \mu B \\
& =2 \times \frac{0.032}{B} \times B \\
& =0.064 \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: Let ${H_n}:\frac{{{x^2}}}{{1 + n}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{3 + n}} = 1,n \in N$. Let $k$ be the smallest even value of $n$ such that the eccentricity of ${H_k}$ is a rational number. If $l$ is the length of the latus rectum of ${H_k}$, then $21l$ is equal to _____.

Let the line \[2x + 3y - k = 0,k > 0\], intersect the x-axis at the points A and B, respectively. If the equation of the circle having the line segment AB as a diameter is \[{x^2} + {y^2} - 3x - 2y = 0\] and the length of the latus rectum of the ellipse \[{x^2} + 9{y^2} = {k^2}\] is \[\frac{m}{n}\], where m and n are coprime, then \[2m + n\] is equal to
Solution:
Centre of the circle = \[\left( {\frac{3}{2},1} \right)\]
Equation of diameter = 2x + 3y – k = 0
\[2\left( {\frac{3}{2}} \right) + 3\left( 1 \right) - k = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow k = 6\]
Now, Equation of ellipse becomes
\[{x^2} + 9{y^2} = 36\]
\[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{6^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{2^2}}} = 1\]
length of LR = \[\frac{{2{b^2}}}{a} = \frac{{{{2.2}^2}}}{6} = \frac{8}{6} = \frac{4}{3} = \frac{m}{n}\]
\[\therefore \,2m + n = 2\left( 4 \right) + 3 = 11\]

Question: The vertices of a hyperbola $H$ are $\left( { \pm \,6,0} \right)$ and its eccentricity is $\frac{{\sqrt 5 }}{2}$. Let $N$ be the normal to $H$ at a point in the first quadrant and parallel to the line $\sqrt 2 x + y = 2\sqrt 2 $. If $d$ is the length of the line segment of $N$ between $H$ and the y-axis then ${d^2}$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
$H:\frac{{{x^2}}}{{36}} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{9} = 1$
equation of normal is $6x\cos \theta + 3y\cot \theta = 45$
Slope $ = - 2\sin \theta = - \sqrt 2 $
$ \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{4}$
Equation of normal is $\sqrt 2 x + y = 15$
$P:\left( {a\sec \theta ,b\tan \theta } \right)$
$ \Rightarrow P\left( {6\sqrt 2 ,3} \right)$ and $K\left( {0,15} \right)$
${d^2} = 216$

Question: A uniform chain of 6m length is placed on a table such that a part of its length is hanging over the edge of the table. The system is at rest. The co-efficient of static friction between the chain and the surface of the table is 0.5, the maximum length of the chain hanging from the a table is _______ m.
Solution:
$(6 - x),\frac{m}{6}(6 - x)$
\[x,\frac{m}{6}x\]
To prevent sliding $\left( {\frac{m}{6}x} \right)g \leqslant \left( {\frac{m}{6}(6 - x)g} \right){\mu _s}$
$x \leqslant 2\quad \Rightarrow \quad {x_{\max }} = 2$

Question: Let the latus rectum of the parabola $\frac{{{x^2}}}{9} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1$ subtend an angle of $\frac{\pi }{3}$ at the centre of the hyperbola. If ${b^2}$ is equal to $\frac{l}{m}\left( {1 + \sqrt n } \right),$ where $l$ and $m$ are co-prime numbers, then ${l^2} + {m^2} + {n^2}$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
LR subtends 60° at centre

$ \Rightarrow \tan 30^\circ = \frac{{\frac{{{b^2}}}{a}}}{{ae}} = \frac{{{b^2}}}{{{a^2}e}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}$
$ \Rightarrow e = \frac{{\sqrt 3 {b^2}}}{9}$
Also, ${e^2} = 1 + \frac{{{b^2}}}{9} \Rightarrow 1 + \frac{{{b^2}}}{9} = \frac{{3{b^4}}}{{81}}$
$ \Rightarrow {b^4} = 3{b^2} + 27$
$ \Rightarrow {b^4} - 3{b^2} - 27 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow {b^2} = \frac{3}{2}\left( {1 + \sqrt {13} } \right)$
$ \Rightarrow l = 3,m = 2,n = 13$
$ \Rightarrow {l^2} + {m^2} + {n^2} = 182$

Question: A force on an object of mass 100 g is $(10\hat i + 5\hat j){\text{N}}$. The position of that object at t = 2 is $({\mathbf{a}}\hat i + {\mathbf{b}}\hat j){\text{m}}$ after starting from rest. The value of $\frac{{\text{a}}}{{\text{b}}}$ will be
Solution:
$\vec a = \frac{f}{m} = 20\hat i + 10\hat jm/s$
$s = \frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times {2^2}\hat i + \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {2^2}\hat jm$
$\frac{a}{b} = \frac{2}{1}$

Question: Let \[{x^k} + {y^k} = {a^k},\left( {a,k > 0} \right)\,\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\,\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + {\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}} = 0\], then $k$ is
Solution:
\[{x^k} + {y^k} = {a^k}\]
Differentiate w.r.to x
\[k{x^{k - 1}} + k{y^{k - 1}}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - {\left( {\frac{x}{y}} \right)^{k - 1}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + {\left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right)^{1 - k}} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 1 - k = \frac{1}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow k = \frac{2}{3}\]

Question: Let $\left| {{{\vec A}_1}} \right| = 3,\left| {{{\vec A}_2}} \right| = 5{\text{ and }}\left| {{{\vec A}_1} + {{\vec A}_2}} \right| = 5.$ The value of $\left( {2{{\vec A}_1} + 3{{\vec A}_2}} \right).\left( {3{{\vec A}_1} - 2{{\vec A}_2}} \right)$ is :
Solution:
Using,
$\begin{gathered}
  {R^2} = A_1^2 + A_2^2 + 2{A_1}{A_2}\cos \theta \\
  {5^2} = {3^2} + {5^2} + 2 \times 3 \times 5\cos \theta \\
  {\text{or }}\cos \theta = - 0.3 \\
  \left( {2{{\vec A}_1} + 3{{\vec A}_2}} \right).\left( {3{{\vec A}_1} - 2{{\vec A}_2}} \right) = 2{A_1} \times 3{A_1} \\
   + \left( {3{A_2}} \right)\left( {3{A_1}} \right)\cos \theta - \left( {2{A_1}} \right)\left( {2{A_2}} \right)\cos \theta - 3{A_2} \times 2{A_2} \\
   = 6A_1^2 + 9{A_1}{A_2}\cos \theta - 4{A_1}{A_2}\cos \theta - 6A_2^2 \\
   = 6A_1^26A_2^2 + 5{A_1}{A_2}\cos \theta \\
   = 6 \times {3^2} - 6 \times {5^2} + 5 \times 3 \times 5\left( { - 0.3} \right) = - 118.5 \\
\end{gathered} $

$$\eqalign{
  & {f_m} = \frac{{2\pi \times {{10}^5}}}{{2\pi }} = {10^5}\;{\text{Hz}} \cr

Question: A boy reaches the airport and finds that the escalator is not working. He walks up the stationary escalator in time \[{t_1}.\]  If he remains stationary on a moving escalator then the escalator takes him up in time \[{t_2}.\]  The time taken by him to walk up on the moving escalator will be:
Solution:
Let $l$ be the length of escalator, \[{v_1}\] be the velocity of man w.r.t. escalator, \[{v_2}\] be the velocity of escalator.
\[{t_1} = \frac{l}{{{v_1}}}\]
\[{t_2} = \frac{l}{{{v_2}}}\]
\[t = \frac{l}{{{v_1} + {v_2}}} = \frac{l}{{\frac{l}{{{t_1}}} + \frac{l}{{{t_2}}}}}\]
\[t = \frac{{{t_1}{t_2}}}{{{t_1} + {t_2}}}\]

Question: The volume of gas A is twice than that of gas B. The compressibility factor of gas A thrice than that of gas B at same temperature. The pressures of the gases for equal number of moles are:
Solution:
Compressibility factor is given by,
\[Z = \frac{{PV}}{{RT}}\]
\[\therefore \frac{{{Z_A}}}{{{Z_B}}} = \frac{{{P_A}{V_A}}}{{{P_B}{V_B}}}\]
Given \[{Z_A} = 3{Z_B}\]
\[{V_A} = 2{V_B}\]
\[\frac{{3{Z_B}}}{{{Z_B}}} = \frac{{{P_A} \times 2{V_B}}}{{{P_B} \times {V_B}}}\]
\[3 = \frac{{{P_A}}}{{{P_B}}} \times 2\]
\[2{P_A} = 3{P_B}\]

Question:
Consider the following sequence of reactions to give the major product (X)

P g of the major product ( X ) formed is reacted with $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ soluion to liberate a gas which occupied $11.2 \mathrm{dm}^{3}$ at STP.
$\mathrm{P}=$ $\_\_\_\_$ . g.
(Given molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12$, $\mathrm{O}: 16, \mathrm{Cl}: 35.5)$
Solution:
Moles of $\mathrm{P}=\frac{11.2}{22.4}=0.5$ mole
$\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{C}_{7} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}}=156.5 \mathrm{gm}$
$\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{C}_{7} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}\right)}=\frac{156.5}{2}=78.25 \approx 78$ grams

Question: The molar conductance of an infinitely dilute solution of ammonium chloride was found to be 185 S cm<sup>2</sup> mol<sup>–1</sup> and the ionic conductance of hydroxyl and chloride ions are 170 and 70 S cm<sup>2</sup> mol<sup>–1</sup>, respectively. If molar conductance of 0.02 M solution of ammonium hydroxide is 85.5 S cm<sup>2</sup> mol<sup>–1</sup>, its degree of dissociation is given by x × 10<sup>–1</sup>. The value of x is _____. (Nearest integer)
Solution:
\[\lambda _m^{\text{o}}\,{\text{of}}\,N{H_4}Cl = 185\]
\[{\left( {\lambda _m^{\text{o}}} \right)_{NH_4^ + }} + {\left( {\lambda _m^o} \right)_{C{l^ - }}} = 185\]
\[{\left( {\lambda _m^{\text{o}}} \right)_{NH_4^ + }} = 185 - 70 = 115\,{\text{Sc}}{{\text{m}}^2}\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}}\]
\[{\left( {\lambda _m^{\text{o}}} \right)_{N{H_4}OH}} = {\left( {\lambda _m^{\text{o}}} \right)_{NH_4^ + }} + {\left( {\lambda _m^o} \right)_{O{H^ - }}}\]
\[ = 115 + 170\]
\[{\left( {\lambda _m^{\text{o}}} \right)_{N{H_4}OH}} = 285\,Sc{m^2}\,mo{l^{ - 1}}\]
degree of dissociation \[ = \frac{{{{\left( {{\lambda _m}} \right)}_{N{H_4}OH}}}}{{{{\left( {\lambda _m^{\text{o}}} \right)}_{N{H_4}OH}}}}\]
\[ = \frac{{85.5}}{{285}}\]
\[ = 0.3\]
\[ = 3 \times {10^{ - 1}}\]

Question: A hard substance melts at high temperature and is an insulator in both solid and in molten state. This solid is most likely to be a/an :
Solution:
If substance is insulator in solid & molten both phase, then it can't be ionic or metallic solid. If melting pt. is higher, then it can't be molecular solid.
Therefore, It should be covalent network solid.

Question:
'x' g of molecular oxygen (O2) is mixed with 200 g of neon (Ne). The total pressure of the nonreactive mixture of O2 and Ne in the cylinder is 25 bar. The partial pressure of Ne is 20 bar at the same temperature and volume. The value of 'x' is_____.
[Given: Molar mass of O2 = 32 g mol–1. Molar mass of Ne = 20 g mol–1]
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  {P_{Ne}} = \left[ {{P_{Total}}} \right]{X_{Ne}} \\
  20 = 25\left[ {{X_{Ne}}} \right] \\
  \left[ {{X_{Ne}}} \right] = \frac{4}{5} \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \left[ {\frac{{\frac{{200}}{{20}}}}{{\frac{{200}}{{20}} + \frac{x}{{32}}}}} \right] = \frac{4}{5} \\
  \frac{{10}}{{10 + \left( {\frac{x}{{32}}} \right)}} = \frac{4}{5} \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  50 = 40 + \frac{x}{8} \\
  400 = 320 + x \\
  x = 80\,\,{\text{gram}} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A particle moving in the xy plane experiences a velocity dependent force \[\vec F = k\left( {{v_y}\widehat i + {v_x}\widehat j} \right),\] where v<sub>x</sub> and v<sub>y</sub> are the x and y components of its velocity \[\vec v\]. If \[\vec a\] is the acceleration of the particle, then which of the following statements is true for the particle?
Options: Kinetic energy of particle is constant in time
quantity \[\vec v \times \vec a\]is constant in time
quantity \[\vec v.\vec a\] is constant in time
\[\vec F\]arises due to a magnetic field
Solution:
$\frac{{md{v_x}}}{{dt}} = k{v_y}\,\,...\left( 1 \right)\,{\text{and}}\,\frac{{md{v_y}}}{{dt}} = k{v_x}\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Dividing $\frac{{\left( 2 \right)}}{{\left( 1 \right)}}$ we get $\frac{{d{v_y}}}{{d{v_x}}} = \frac{{{v_x}}}{{{v_y}}}$
$\begin{gathered}
  {v_y}d{v_y} = {v_x}d{v_x} \\
  v_y^2 = v_x^2 + C \\
\end{gathered} $
$v_y^2 - v_x^2 = C = {\text{Constant}}$
Now, $\vec v \times \vec a = \left( {{v_x}\hat i + {v_y}\hat j} \right) \times \frac{k}{m}\left( {{v_y}\hat i + {v_x}\hat j} \right)$
$ = \frac{k}{m}\left[ {v_x^2\hat k - v_y^2\hat k} \right] = \frac{k}{m}\left( {v_x^2 - v_y^2} \right)\hat k = {\text{Constant}}$

Question: A telescope with objective diameter R is used to observe a distant star emitting light of wavelength 500 nm , at a resolution of $5 \times 10^{-7}$ radian. The value of $R$ is $\_\_\_\_$ cm .
Solution:
$\theta=5 \times 10^{-7}$ radian
$\lambda=500 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}$
$\theta=\frac{1.22 \lambda}{R}$
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{1.22 \lambda}{\theta}=1.22 \mathrm{~m}=122 \mathrm{~cm}$

Question: A bar magnet with a magnetic moment $5.0{\text{A}}{{\text{m}}^2}$ is placed in parallel position relative to a magnetic field of $0.4{\text{T}}$. The amount of required work done in turning the magnet form parallel to antiparallel position relative to the field direction is
Solution:
$u = - MB\cos \theta $
${\text{W}} = {{\Delta u}}$
$W = - MB\cos {180^ \circ }\left( { - mB\cos 0^\circ } \right)$
$W = 2MB = 2 \times 5 \times 0.4 = 4J$

Question: A body takes 4 min. to cool from $${61^o}C\,\,to\,\,{59^o}C\,.$$ If the temperature of the surroundings is $${30^o}C,$$ the time taken by the body to cool from $${51^o}C\,\,to\,{49^o}C$$is:
Solution:
$$\eqalign{
  & - \frac{{dT}}{{dt}} = {\text{k}}\left( {{\text{T}} - {{\text{T}}_0}} \right) \cr
  & \frac{{(60 - 30)}}{{(50 - 30)}} = \frac{t}{4} \cr
  & t = 6{\text{ min}}{\text{. }} \cr} $$

Question:
In a screw gauge, the zero of the circular scale lies 3 divisions above the horizontal pitch line when their metallic studs are brought in contact. Using this instrument thickness of a sheet is measured. If pitch scale reading is 1 mm and the circular scale reading is 51 then the correct thickness of the sheet is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
[Assume least count is 0.01 mm ]
Solution:
Zero error $\mathrm{e}=-3 \times \mathrm{LC}=-0.03 \mathrm{~mm}$
Reading taken $=1 \mathrm{~mm}+51(0.01 \mathrm{~mm})$
$=1.51 \mathrm{~mm}$
So, correct reading $=1.51-(-0.03)$
$=1.54 \mathrm{~mm}$

Question: A body cools in 7 minutes from 60°C to 40°C. The temperature of the surrounding is 10°C. The temperature of the body after the next 7 minutes will be:
Solution:
using average rate of Newton's law of cooling
$$\frac{{{T_1} - {T_2}}}{t} = K\left( {\frac{{{T_1} + {T_2}}}{2} - {T_s}} \right)$$
$${\text{Given}}\;\frac{{60 - 40}}{7} = K\left( {50 - 10} \right)\;...\;\left( i \right)$$
$$\& \;\frac{{40 - T}}{7} = K\left( {\frac{{40 + T}}{2} - 10} \right)\;...\left( {ii} \right)$$
From (i) & (ii)
T = 28°C

Question:
The distance between Na+ and Cl– ions in solid NaCl of density 43.1 g cm–3 is _____ x 10–10 m. (Nearest Integer)
(Given: NA = 6.02 x 1023 mol–1)
Solution:
For NaCl,
Z = 4 & M = 58.5 g
\[\begin{gathered}
  d = \frac{{Z \times M}}{{{N_A} \times {{\left( a \right)}^3}}} \\
  43.1 = \frac{{4 \times 58.5}}{{6 \times {{10}^{23}} \times {{\left( a \right)}^3}}} \\
  a = 2.08 \times {10^{ - 8}}cm \\
  {d_{N{a^ + } + C{l^ - }}} = \frac{a}{2} = \frac{{2.08 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}n}}{2} \\
   = 1 \times {10^{ - 10}}m \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question:
The pressure exerted by a non-reactive gaseous mixture of 6.4 g of methane and 8.8 g of carbon dioxide in a 10 L vessel at 27ºC is ………….. kPa.
(Round off to the Nearest Integer).
[Assume gases are ideal, R = 8.314 J mol–1 K–1
Atomic masses : C : 12.0 u, H : 1.0 u, O : 16.0 u]
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  V = 10L,T = 27^\circ C = 300K \\
  {\left( m \right)_{methane}} = 6.4\,g,\,{\left( m \right)_{c{o_2}}} = 8.8g \\
  PV = {n_{total}}RT \\
  P \times 10 \times {10^{ - 3}} = \left( {\frac{{6.4}}{{16}} + \frac{{8.8}}{{44}}} \right) \times 8.314 \times 300 \\
  P \times {10^{ - 2}} = \left( {0.4 + 0.2} \right) \times 8.314 \times 300 \\
  P = 149652\,Pa \\
  P = 149.652\,KPa \approx 150\,kPa \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A light string passing over a smooth light fixed pulley connects two blocks of masses ${m_1}$ and ${m_2}$. If the acceleration of the system is $\frac{g}{8}$, then the ratio of masses is:

Two forces 

<!-StartFragment->\[{\vec F_1}\;and\;{\vec F_2}\]<!-EndFragment->  are acting on a body . One force has magnitude thrice that of the other force and the resultant of the two forces is equal to the force of larger magnitude. The angle between \[{\vec F_1}\;and\;{\vec F_2}\] is \[{\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{n}} \right)\]. The value of |n| is ____.
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \left| {{{\vec F}_1}} \right| = F \\
  \left| {{{\vec F}_R}} \right| = \left| {{{\vec F}_2}} \right| = 3F \\
  F_R^2 = F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2{F_1}{F_2}\cos \theta \\
  9{F^2} = {F^2} + 9{F^2} + 6{F^2}\cos \theta \\
  \cos \theta = - \frac{1}{6} \\
  \theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{{ - 6}}} \right) \\
  n = - 6 \\
  |n| = 6 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: There are 100 divisions on the circular scale of a screw gauge of pitch 1 mm. Solution:
Least count = \[\frac{1}{{100}}\] mm 0.01mm
zero error = +0.05 mm
Reading = 4 × 1 mm + 60 × 0.01 mm – 0.05 mm = 4.55 mm

Question: A block of mass M slides down on a rough inclined plane with constant velocity. The angle made by the incline plane with horizontal is θ . The magnitude of the contact force will be :
Solution:
N = Mgcosθ
f = Mgsinθ
${\text{R}} = \sqrt {{{\text{N}}^2} + {{\text{f}}^2}} $
R = Mg

Question: Most suitable salt which can be used for efficient clotting of blood will be
Solution: Blood is a negative sol. According to Hardy-Schulz’s rule the cation with high charge has high coagulation power. Hence \[FeC{l_3}\] can be used for clotting blood.

Question: Let $S = \left\{ {x \in \left( { - \frac{\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2}} \right):{9^{1 - {{\tan }^2}x}} + {9^{{{\tan }^2}x}} = 10} \right\}$ and $\beta  = \sum\limits_{x \in s} {{{\tan }^2}\left( {\frac{x}{3}} \right)} $, then $\frac{1}{6}{\left( {\beta  - 14} \right)^2}$ is equal to
Solution:
Let ${9^{{{\tan }^2}x}} = P$
$\frac{9}{P} + P = 10$
${P^2} - 10P + 9 = 0$
$\left( {P - 9} \right)\left( {P - 1} \right) = 0$
$P = 1,9$
$9{\tan ^2}x = 1,\,\,{9^{{{\tan }^2}x}} = 9$
${\tan ^2}x = 0,\,\,{\tan ^2}x = 1$
$x = 0, \pm \frac{\pi }{4}\,\,\,\,\,\,\therefore \,\,x \in \left( { - \frac{\pi }{2},\frac{\pi}{2}} \right)$
$\beta = {\tan ^2}\left( 0 \right) + {\tan ^2}\left( { + \frac{\pi }{{12}}} \right) + {\tan ^2}\left( { - \frac{\pi }{{12}}} \right)$
$ = 0 + 2{\left( {\tan 15^\circ } \right)^2}$
$2{\left( {2 - \sqrt 3 } \right)^2}$
$2\left( {7 - 4\sqrt 3 } \right)$
Then $\frac{1}{6}{\left( {14 - 8\sqrt 3 - 14} \right)^2} = 32$

$(6-2) \times 10 \times 6=\frac{1}{2}(8) v^{2}$

Question: Let \[{S_1}:\;{x^2} + {y^2} = 9\;and\;{S_2}:\;{(x - 2)^2} + \;{y^2} = 1.\] Then the locus of centre of a variable circle S which touches \[{S_1}\] internally and S2 externally always passes through the points :
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

\[P{S_1} = 3 - r{\kern 1pt} \]

\[\begin{gathered}
  P{S_2} = 1 + {r_1} \\
  P{S_1} + P{S_2} = 4 \\
\end{gathered} \]
So locus is ellipse and \[2a = 4\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;a = 2\;{\text{and}}\;2ae = 2\]

Equation of ellipse is \[\frac{{{{\left( {x - 1} \right)}^2}}}{{{2^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{3} = 1\;\;\;\;\;e = \frac{1}{2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;{b^2} = 3\]
Option (c) lies on the ellipse

Question: Assuming ideal gas behaviour, the ratio of density of ammonia to that of hydrogen chloride at same temperature and pressure is: (Atomic wt. of Cl = 35.5 u)
Solution:
Ideal gas equation -
PV = nRT
n =m/M
So, PV = nRT/M
P = mRT/MV
P = dRT/M
It temperature and pressure is constant then
\[d \propto M\]
d1/d2 = M1/M2
(d1 and M1 are density and molecular mass ammonia)
(d2 and M2 are density and molecular mass of hydrogen chloride)
d1/d2 = 17/36.5
d1/d2 = 0.46

Question: A compound microscope consists of an objective lens of focal length 1 cm and an eye piece of focal length 5 cm with a separation of 10 cm. The distance between an object and the objection lens, at which the strain on the eye is minimum is \[\frac{n}{{40}}\,cm.\] The value of n is ………….. .

If \[{e_1}\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{e_2}\] are the eccentricities of the ellipse, $\frac{{{x^2}}}{{18}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{4} = 1$ and the hyperbola, $\frac{{{x^2}}}{9} - \frac{{{y^2}}}{4} = 1$ respectively and \[\left( {{e_1},\,\,{e_2}} \right)\] is a point on the ellipse, $15{x^2} + 3{y^2} = k$. Then k is equal to:
Solution:
${e_1} = \sqrt {1 - \frac{4}{{18}}} = \frac{{\sqrt 7 }}{3}\,\,\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\,\,{e_2} = \sqrt {1 + \frac{4}{9}} = \frac{{\sqrt {13} }}{3}$
\[\because \,\,\,\left( {{e_1},\,\,{e_2}} \right)\] lies on the ellipse $15{x^2} + 3{y^2} = k$
$15e_1^2 + 3e_2^2 = k$
$ \Rightarrow 15 \times \frac{7}{9} + 3 \times \frac{{13}}{9} = k \Rightarrow k = 16$

Question: If the lines $\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{2 - y}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{\alpha }$ and $\frac{{x - 4}}{5} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{z}{\beta }$ intersect, then the magnitude of the minimum value of $8\alpha \beta $ is ______.
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

If the lines $\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{2 - y}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{\alpha }$ and $\frac{{x - 4}}{5} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{z}{\beta }$ intersect
Point on first line $\left( {1,2,3} \right)$ and point on second line $\left( {4,1,0} \right)$
Vector joining both points is $ - 3\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k$
Now vector along first line is $2\hat i + 3\hat j + \alpha \hat k$
Also vector along second line is $5\hat i + 2\hat j + \beta \hat k$
Now these three vectors must be coplanar
$ \Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  2&3&\alpha \\
  5&2&\beta \\
  { - 3}&1&3
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 2\left( {6 - \beta } \right) - 3\left( {15 + 3\beta } \right) + \alpha \left( {11} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha - \beta = 3$
Now $\alpha = 3 + \beta $
Given expression $8\left( {3 + \beta } \right) \cdot \beta = 8\left( {{\beta ^2} + 3\beta } \right)$
$ = 8\left( {{\beta ^2} + 3\beta + \frac{9}{4} - \frac{9}{4}} \right) = 8{\left( {\beta + \frac{3}{2}} \right)^2} - 18$
So magnitude of minimum value = 18

Question: If the shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x - \lambda }}{3} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 1}}{1}\;and\;\frac{{x + 2}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y + 5}}{2} = \frac{{z - 4}}{4}\] is \[\frac{{44}}{{\sqrt {30} }},\] then the largest possible value of |λ| is equal to _______.
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  {{\bar a}_1} = \lambda \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k \\
  {{\bar a}_2} = - 2\hat i - 5\hat j + 4\hat k \\
  \vec p - = 3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k \\
  \vec q - = - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k \\
  \left( {\lambda + 2} \right)\hat i + 7\hat j - 3\hat k = {{\bar a}_1} - {{\bar a}_2} \\
  \vec p \times \vec q - = - 6\hat i - 15\hat j + 3\hat k \\
  \frac{{44}}{{\sqrt {30} }} = \frac{{\left| { - 6\lambda - 12 - 105 - 9} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( { - 6} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 15} \right)}^2} + {3^2}} }} \\
  \frac{{44}}{{\sqrt {30} }} = \frac{{\left| {6\lambda + 126} \right|}}{{3\sqrt {30} }} \\
  132 = \left| {6\lambda + 126} \right| \\
  \lambda = 1,\;\lambda = - 43 \\
  \left| \lambda \right| = 43 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Coordinates of Q(k, 2k, 4k)

Question:
Let $\mathrm{P}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ be the point on the line
$\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+1}{-3}=\mathrm{z}$ at a distance $4 \sqrt{14}$ from the point $(1,-1,0)$ and nearer to the origin. Then the shortest distance, between the lines $\frac{x-\alpha}{1}=\frac{y-\beta}{2}=\frac{z-\gamma}{3}$ and $\frac{x+5}{2}=\frac{y-10}{1}=\frac{z-3}{1}$ , is equal to
Solution:
Let $\mathrm{P}(2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda-1, \lambda)$
Then $4 \lambda^{2}+9 \lambda^{2}+\lambda^{2}=16 \cdot 14 \Rightarrow \lambda= \pm 4 \Rightarrow-4$ (nearer to origin)
$\therefore \mathrm{P}(-7,11,-4)$
$\therefore $ Shortest distance $=\frac{\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & -1 & 7 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|}{\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \mathrm{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|}$
$=\frac{28}{\sqrt{1+25+9}}=\frac{4 \sqrt{7}}{\sqrt{5}}$

Question: Let P be the plane passing through the points $\left( {5,3,0} \right),\left( {13,3, - 2} \right)$ and $\left( {1,6,2} \right)$. For $\alpha  \in \mathbb{N}$, if the distances of the points $A\left( {3,4,\alpha } \right)$ and $B\left( {2,\alpha ,a} \right)$ from the plane P are 2 and 3 respectively, then the positive value of a is
Solution:
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  8&0&{ - 2} \\
  4&{ - 3}&{ - 2}
\end{array}} \right| = \hat i\left( { - 6} \right) + 8\hat j - 24\hat k$
Normal of the plane $ = 3\hat i - 4\hat j + 12\hat k$
Plane: $3x - 4y + 12z = 3$
Distance from $A\left( {3,4,\alpha } \right)$
$\left| {\frac{{9 - 16 + 12\alpha - 3}}{{13}}} \right| = 2$
$\alpha = 3$
$\alpha = - 8$ (rejected)
Distance from $B\left( {2,3,a} \right)$
$\left| {\frac{{6 - 12 + 12a - 3}}{{13}}} \right| = 3$
$a = 4$

Question: Let $Q$ and $R$ be the foot of perpendiculars from the point $P\left( {a,a,a} \right)$ on the lines $x = y,z = 1$ and $x = - y,z = - 1$ respectively. If $\angle QPR$ is a right angle, then $12{a^2}$ is equal to ____.
Solution:
$\frac{x}{1} = \frac{y}{1} = \frac{{z - 1}}{0} = r \to Q\left( {r,r,1} \right)$
$\frac{x}{1} = \frac{y}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + 1}}{0} = k \to R\left( {k, - k, - 1} \right)$
$\overline {PQ} = \left( {a - r} \right)\hat i + \left( {a - r} \right)\hat j + \left( {a - 1} \right)\hat k$
$a = r + a - r = 0$
$2a = 2r \to a = r$
$\overrightarrow {PR} = \left( {a - k} \right)\hat i + \left( {a + k} \right)\hat j + \left( {a + 1} \right)\hat k$
$a - k - a - k = 0 \Rightarrow k = 0$
As, $PQ \bot PR$
$\left( {a - r} \right)\left( {a - k} \right) + \left( {a - r} \right)\left( {a + k} \right) + \left( {a - 1} \right)\left( {a + 1} \right) = 0$
$a = 1\,\,{\text{or}}\,\, - 1$
$12{a^2} = 12$

Question: If the equation of the plane passing through the line of intersection of the planes \[2x - 7y + 4z - 3 = 0,3x - 5y + 4z + 11 = 0\]and the point (–2, 1, 3) is \[ax + by + cz - 7 = 0\], then the value of 2a + b + c – 7 is ……………. .
Solution:
Let equation of plane be \[2x - 7y + 4z - 3 + \lambda \left( {3x - 5y + 4z + 11} \right) = 0\]
This passes through \[\left( { - 2,1,3} \right)\]
So, \[ - 4 - 7 + 12 - 3 + \lambda \left( { - 6 - 5 + 12 + 11} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{6}\]
\[\therefore \] Plane is \[15x - 47y + 28z - 7 = 0\]
\[\therefore a = 15\]
\[b = - 47\]
\[c = 28\]

Question: Let $P$ be the point of intersection of the line $\frac{{x + 3}}{3} = \frac{{y + 2}}{1} = \frac{{1 - z}}{2}$ and the plane $x + y + z = 2$. If the distance of the point $P$ from the plane $3x - 4y + 12z = 32$ is $q$, then $q$ and $2q$ are the roots of the equation
Solution:
$P = \left( {3\lambda - 3,\lambda - 2,1 - 2\lambda } \right)$
$P$ lies on the plane, $x + y + z = 2$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = 3$
$P = \left( {6,1, - 5} \right)$
$q = \left| {\frac{{18 - 4 - 60 - 32}}{{\sqrt {9 + 16 + 144} }}} \right| = \frac{{78}}{{13}} = 6$
$q = 6,\,\,2q = 12$
Equation, ${x^2} - 18x + 72 = 0$

Question: Let the equation of the plane passing through the line $x - 2y - z - 5 = 0 = x + y + 3z - 5$ and parallel to the line $x + y + 2z - 7 = 0 = 2x + 3y + z - 2$ be $ax + by + cz = 65$. Then the distance of the point $\left( {a,b,c} \right)$ from the plane $2x + 2y - z + 16 = 0$ is _____.
Solution:
Equation of plane is
$\left( {x - 2y - z - 5} \right) + b\left( {x + y + 3z - 5} \right) = 0$
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {1 + b}&{ - 2 + b}&{ - 1 + 3b} \\
  1&1&2 \\
  2&3&1
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$ \Rightarrow b = 12$
$\therefore \,\,$Plane is $13x + 10y + 35z = 65$
Distance from given point to plane = 9

Question: Let the plane $P$ pass through the intersection of the planes $2x + 3y - z = 2$ and $x + 2y + 3z = 6$, and be perpendicular to the plane $2x + y - z + 1 = 0$. If ${\text{d}}$ is the distance of ${\text{P}}$ from the point $\left( { - 7,1,1} \right)$, then ${{\text{d}}^2}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$P \equiv {P_1} + \lambda {P_2} = 0$ $\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)x + \left( {3 + 2\lambda } \right)y + \left( {3\lambda - 1} \right)z - 2 - 6\lambda = 0$ Plane $P$ is perpendicular to ${P_3}\therefore \vec n \cdot {\vec n_3} = 0$
$2\left( {\lambda + 2} \right) + \left( {2\lambda + 3} \right) - \left( {3\lambda - 1} \right) = 0$
$\lambda = - 8$
$P \equiv - 6x - 13y - 25z + 46 = 0$
$6x + 13y + 25z - 46 = 0$
Distance from $\left( { - 7,1,1} \right)$
$d = \left| {\frac{{ - 42 + 13 + 25 - 46}}{{\sqrt {36 + 169 + 625} }}} \right| = \frac{{50}}{{\sqrt {830} }}$
${d^2} = \frac{{50 \times 50}}{{830}} = \frac{{250}}{{83}}$

Question: If the point of intersection of the lines $\frac{x+1}{3}= \frac{y+a}{5}=\frac{z+b+1}{7}$ and $\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-b}{4}=\frac{z-2 a}{7}$ lies on $x y$-plane, then the value of $a+b$ is :
Solution:
Line $\left(\mathrm{L}_{1}\right)$
$\frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{a}}{5}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+\mathrm{b}+1}{7}=\mathrm{r}_{1}$
⇒ General point P on $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ is
$\left(3 \mathrm{r}_{1}-1,5 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{a}, 7 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{b}-1\right)$
Line ( $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ )
$\frac{\mathrm{x}-2}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{b}}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-2 \mathrm{a}}{7}=\mathrm{r}_{2}$
⇒ General point Q on $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ is
$\left(\mathrm{r}_{2}+2,4 \mathrm{r}_{2}+\mathrm{b}, 7 \mathrm{r}_{2}+2 \mathrm{a}\right)$
For point of intersection
$$
\begin{equation*}
3 \mathrm{r}_{1}-1=\mathrm{r}_{2}+2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_{2}=3 \mathrm{r}_{1}-3 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
5 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{a}=4 \mathrm{r}_{2}+\mathrm{b} \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
$$
\begin{equation*}
7 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{b}-1=7 \mathrm{r}_{2}+2 \mathrm{a} \tag{3}
\end{equation*}
$$
Since point lies on XY plane
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{z}-$ coordinate $=0$
From $\mathrm{L}_{1}: 7 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{b}-1=0 \Rightarrow 7 \mathrm{r}_{1}=\mathrm{b}+1$
From $\mathrm{L}_{2}: 7 \mathrm{r}_{2}+2 \mathrm{a}=0 \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{a}=-7 \mathrm{r}_{2}$
Substitute $\mathrm{r}_{2}=3 \mathrm{r}_{1}-3$
$2 \mathrm{a}=-7\left(3 \mathrm{r}_{1}-3\right)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\frac{-2 \mathrm{lr}_{1}+21}{2}$
Put in equation (2)
$5 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\mathrm{a}=4 \mathrm{r}_{2}+\mathrm{b}$
$5 \mathrm{r}_{1}-\left(\frac{-21 \mathrm{r}_{1}+21}{2}\right)=4\left(3 \mathrm{r}_{1}-3\right)+\left(7 \mathrm{r}_{1}-1\right)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_{1}=\frac{5}{7}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{b}=7 \mathrm{r}_{1}-1=4$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=3 \therefore \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=7$

Question:
Let the line of the shortest distance between the lines
${L_1}:\vec r = \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right)$ and ${L_2}:\vec r = \left( {4\hat i + 5\hat j + 6\hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {\hat i + \hat j - \hat k} \right)$ intersect ${L_1}$ and ${L_2}$ at $P$ and $Q$ respectively. If $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$ is the mid point of the line segment $PQ,$ then $2\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right)$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
$\vec b = \hat i - \hat j + \hat k$ (DR’s of ${L_1}$)
$\vec d = \hat i + \hat j - \hat k$ (DR’s of ${L_2}$)
$\vec b \times \vec d = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&{ - 1}&1 \\
  1&1&{ - 1}
\end{array}} \right|$
$ = 0\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k$ (DR's of Line perpendicular to ${L_1}$ and ${L_2}$)
DR of AB line
$ = \left( {0,2,2} \right) = \left( {3 + \mu - \lambda ,3 + \mu + \lambda ,3 - \mu - \lambda } \right)$
$ = \frac{{3 + \mu - \lambda }}{0} = \frac{{3 + \mu + \lambda }}{2} = \frac{{3 - \mu - \lambda }}{2}$
Solving above equation we get $\mu = - \frac{3}{2}$ and $\lambda = \frac{3}{2}$
Point $A = \left( {\frac{5}{2},\frac{1}{2},\frac{9}{2}} \right)$
$B = \left( {\frac{5}{2},\frac{7}{2},\frac{{15}}{2}} \right)$
Point of $AB = \left( {\frac{5}{2},2,6} \right) = \left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$
$2\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right) = 5 + 4 + 12 = 21$

Question: Let S be the set of all real values of λ  such that a plane passing through the points \[\left( { - {\lambda ^2},\,\,1,\,\,1} \right),\left( {1,\,\, - {\lambda ^2},\,\,1} \right)\] and \[\left( {1,\,\,1,\,\, - {\lambda ^2}} \right)\] also passes through the point \[\left( { - 1,\,\, - 1,\,\,1} \right)\]. Then S is equal to
Solution:
Let \[A\left( { - {\lambda ^2},\,\,1,\,\,1} \right),\,\,B\left( {1,\,\, - {\lambda ^2},\,\,1} \right),\,\,C\left( {1,\,\,1,\,\, - {\lambda ^2}} \right)\], \[D\left( { - 1,\,\, - 1,\,\,1} \right)\] lie on same plane, then
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {1 - {\lambda ^2}}&2&0 \\
  2&{1 - {\lambda ^2}}&0 \\
  2&2&{ - {\lambda ^2} - 1}
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \,\,\,\left( {{\lambda ^2} + 1} \right)\left( {\left( {1 - {\lambda ^2}} \right){\,^2} - 4} \right) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,\left( {3 - {\lambda ^2}} \right)\left( {{\lambda ^2} + 1} \right) = 0\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,{\lambda ^2} = 3 \\
  \lambda = \pm \,\sqrt 3 \\
  {\text{Hence,}}\,\,{\text{S}} = \left\{ { - \sqrt 3 ,\,\,\sqrt 3 } \right\} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: If the shortest distance between the lines \[\mathop {{r_1}}\limits^ \to   = \alpha \mathop i\limits^ \wedge   + 2\mathop j\limits^ \wedge   + 2\mathop k\limits^ \wedge   + \lambda \left( {\mathop i\limits^ \wedge   - 2\mathop j\limits^ \wedge   + 2\mathop k\limits^ \wedge  } \right),\lambda  \in R,\alpha  > 0\] and \[\mathop {{r_2}}\limits^ \to   =  - 4\mathop i\limits^ \wedge   - \mathop k\limits^ \wedge   + \mu \left( {3\mathop i\limits^ \wedge   - 2\mathop j\limits^ \wedge   - 2\mathop k\limits^ \wedge  } \right),\mu  \in R\] is 9, then \[\alpha \] is equal to ______.
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

If \[\mathop r\limits^ \to = \mathop a\limits^ \to + \lambda \mathop b\limits^ \to \] and  \[\mathop r\limits^ \to = \mathop c\limits^ \to + \mu \mathop d\limits^ \to \]
then the shortest distance between two lines is
\[L = \frac{{\left( {\mathop a\limits^ \to - \mathop c\limits^ \to } \right).\left( {\mathop b\limits^ \to \times \mathop d\limits^ \to } \right)}}{{\left| {\mathop b\limits^ \to \times \mathop d\limits^ \to } \right|}}\]
\[\therefore \mathop a\limits^ \to - \mathop c\limits^ \to = \left( {\left( {\alpha + 4} \right)\mathop i\limits^ \wedge + 2\mathop j\limits^ \wedge + 3\mathop k\limits^ \wedge } \right)\] and
\[\frac{{\mathop b\limits^ \to \times \mathop d\limits^ \to }}{{\left| {\vec b \times \vec d} \right|}} = \frac{{\left( {2\mathop i\limits^ \wedge + 2\mathop j\limits^ \wedge + \mathop k\limits^ \wedge } \right)}}{3}\]
\[\therefore \left( {\left( {\alpha + 4} \right)\mathop i\limits^ \wedge + 2\mathop j\limits^ \wedge + 3\mathop k\limits^ \wedge } \right).\frac{{\left( {2\mathop i\limits^ \wedge + 2\mathop j\limits^ \wedge + \mathop k\limits^ \wedge } \right)}}{3} = 9\]
\[ \Rightarrow \alpha = 6\]

Question:
Let the plane $ax + by + cz = d$ pass through (2, 3, –5) and is perpendicular to the planes $2x + y - 5z = 10$ and $3x + 5y - 7z = 12$.
If $a,b,c,d$ are integers $d > 0$ and gcd \[(|a|,\;|b|,\;|c|,\;d)\] = 1, then the value of \[a + \;7b + \;c + \;20d\] is equal to

The coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular from the point (1, –2, 1) on the plane containing the lines,
\[\frac{{x + 1}}{6} = \frac{{y - 1}}{7} = \frac{{z - 3}}{8}\;{\text{and}}\;\frac{{x - 1}}{3} = \frac{{y - 2}}{5} = \frac{{z - 3}}{7},\] is:
Solution:
Here,
\[\begin{gathered}
  {{\bar n}_1} = 6\hat i + 7\hat j + 8\hat k\,\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\,{{\bar n}_2} = 3\hat i + 5\hat j + 7\hat k \\
  \bar n = {{\bar n}_1} \times {{\bar n}_2} \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
   = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\widehat i}&{\widehat j}&{\widehat k} \\
  6&7&8 \\
  3&5&7
\end{array}} \right| \\
   = 9\hat i - 18\hat j + 9\hat k \\
   = 9\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]
Direction vectors \[ = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k\]
Equation of line \[1\left( {x + 1} \right) - 2\left( {y - 1} \right) + \left( {z - 3} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,x - 2y + z = 0\]
foot of the perpendicular
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y + 2}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 1}}{1} = - \frac{{\left( {1 + 4 + 1} \right)}}{6} \\
  \therefore \,\,\,x = 0,\;y = 0,\;z = 0 \\
\end{gathered} \]
The coordinates are (0, 0, 0)

Question: Let P be a plane containing the line \[\frac{{x - 1}}{3} = \;\frac{{y + 6}}{4} = \;\frac{{z + 5}}{2}\]and parallel to the line \[\frac{{x - 3}}{4} = \;\frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 3}} = \;\frac{{z + 5}}{7}.\] If the point \[\left( {1,\; - 1,\;\alpha } \right)\] lies on the plane, P, then the value of \[|5\alpha |\] is equal to _______.
Solution:
Equation of plane is
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 1}&{y + 6}&{z + 5} \\
  3&4&2 \\
  4&{ - 3}&7
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
Point \[\left( {1,\; - 1,\;\alpha } \right)\] satisfy the above determinant
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  0&5&{\alpha + 5} \\
  3&4&2 \\
  4&{ - 3}&7
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
\[0 - 5\left[ {21 - 8} \right] + \left( {\alpha + 5} \right)\left[ { - 9 - 16} \right] = 0\]
\[ = - 65 - 25\alpha - 125 = 0\]
\[ - 25\alpha = 190\]
\[5\alpha = - 38\]
\[\left| {5\alpha } \right| = 38\]

Question: If a variable plane, at a distance of 3 units form the origin, intersects the coordinate axes at A, B and C, then the locus of the centroid of \[\Delta ABC\] is.
Solution:
Let the plane equation be \[ax + by + cz + d = 0\]
The distance of the plane from the origin is 3
Therefore,
 \[\frac{d}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }} = 3\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,{d^2} = 9\left( {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 1 \right)\]
Now, the plane intersects x-axis at point \[A\left( {\frac{{ - d}}{a},\,\,0,\,\,0} \right)\], y-axis at
Point \[\left( {0,\,\,\frac{{ - d}}{a},\,\,0} \right)\] and z-axis at
Point \[C\left( {0,\,\,0,\,\,\frac{{ - d}}{c}} \right)\]
Let \[h = \frac{{ - d}}{{3a}},\,\,k = \frac{{ - d}}{{3b}},\,\,l = \frac{{ - d}}{{3c}}\]
Therefore, from eqn (1), we get
\[\frac{1}{{9{h^2}}} + \frac{1}{{9{k^2}}} + \frac{1}{{9{l^2}}} = \frac{1}{9}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{1}{{{h^2}}} + \frac{1}{{{k^2}}} + \frac{1}{{{l^2}}} = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{1}{{{x^2}}} + \frac{1}{{{y^2}}} + \frac{1}{{{z^2}}} = 1\]

Question: If the shortest between the lines $\frac{{x + \sqrt 6 }}{2} = \frac{{y - \sqrt 6 }}{3} = \frac{{z - \sqrt 6 }}{4}$ and $\frac{{x - \lambda }}{3} = \frac{{y - 2\sqrt 6 }}{4} = \frac{{z + 2\sqrt 6 }}{5}$ is 6, then the square of sum of all possible values of $\lambda $ is
Solution:
Shortest distance between the lines
$\frac{{x + \sqrt 6 }}{2} = \frac{{y - \sqrt 6 }}{3} = \frac{{z - \sqrt 6 }}{4}$
$\frac{{x - \lambda }}{3} = \frac{{y - 2\sqrt 6 }}{4} = \frac{{z + 2\sqrt 6 }}{5}$ is 6
Vector along line of shortest distance
$ = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&3&4 \\
  3&4&5
\end{array}} \right| \Rightarrow - \hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k$ (its magnitude is $\sqrt 6 $)
Now $\frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\sqrt 6 + \lambda }&{\sqrt 6 }&{ - 3\sqrt 6 } \\
  2&3&4 \\
  3&4&5
\end{array}} \right| = \pm 6$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = - 2\sqrt 6 ,10\sqrt 6 $
So, square of sum of these values is 384.

Question: If two straight lines whose direction cosines are given by relations \[l + \;m - \;n = \;0,\] \[3{l^2} + \;{m^2} + \;cnl\; = \;0\] are parallel, then the positive value of c is :
The line, that is coplanar to the line $\frac{{x + 3}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y - 1}}{1} = \frac{{z - 5}}{5}$, is
Solution:
Condition of co-planarity
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {{x_2} - {x_1}}&{{a_1}}&{{a_2}} \\
  {{y_2} - {y_1}}&{{b_1}}&{{b_2}} \\
  {{z_2} - {z_1}}&{{c_1}}&{{c_2}}
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
where ${a_1},{b_1},{c_1}$ are direction cosine of 1st line and ${a_2},{b_2},{c_2}$ are direction cosine of 2nd line.
Now, solving options
Point $\left( { - 3,1,5} \right)$ & point $\left( { - 1,2,5} \right)$
(1) \[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  { - 3}&1&5 \\
  1&2&5 \\
  { - 2}&{ - 1}&0
\end{array}} \right| = - 3\left( 5 \right) - \left( {10} \right) + 5\left( { - 1 + 4} \right)\]
$\begin{gathered}
   = - 15 - 10 + 15 \\
   = - 10 \\
\end{gathered} $
(2) Point $\left( { - 1,2,5} \right)$
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  { - 3}&1&5 \\
  { - 1}&2&5 \\
  { - 2}&{ - 1}&0
\end{array}} \right| = 3\left( 5 \right) - \left( {10} \right) + 5\left( {1 + 4} \right)$
$\begin{gathered}
   = - 25 + 25 \\
   = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $
(3) Point $\left( { - 1,2,5} \right)$
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  { - 3}&1&5 \\
  { - 1}&2&4 \\
  { - 2}&{ - 1}&0
\end{array}} \right| = - 3\left( 4 \right) - \left( 8 \right) + 5\left( {1 + 4} \right)$
$ - 12 - 8 + 25 = 5$
(4) Point $\left( { - 1,2,5} \right)$
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  { - 3}&1&5 \\
  { - 1}&2&5 \\
  4&1&0
\end{array}} \right| = - 3\left( { - 5} \right) - \left( { - 20} \right) + 5\left( { - 1 - 8} \right)$
$\begin{gathered}
   = 15 + 20 - 45 \\
   = - 10 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: If the foot of the perpendicular drawn from $\left( {1,9,7} \right)$ to the line passing through the point $\left( {3,2,1} \right)$ and parallel to the planes $x + 2y + z = 0$ and $3y - z = 3$ is $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$, then $\alpha + \beta + \gamma $ is equal to
Solution:
Direction ratio of line $ = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&2&1 \\
  0&3&{ - 1}
\end{array}} \right|$
$ = \hat i\left( { - 5} \right) - \hat j\left( { - 1} \right) + \hat k\left( 3 \right)$
$ = - 5\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k$

$M\left( { - 5\lambda + 3,\lambda + 2,3\lambda + 1} \right)$
$\overrightarrow {PM} \bot \left( { - 5\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k} \right)$
$ - 5\left( { - 5\lambda + 2} \right) + \left( {\lambda - 7} \right) + 3\left( {3\lambda - 6} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 25\lambda + \lambda + 9\lambda - 10 - 7 - 18 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = 1$
Point $M = \left( { - 2,3,4} \right) = \left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$
$\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 5$

Question: The square of the distance of the point $\mathrm{P}(5,6,7)$ from the line $\frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{y-5}{3}=\frac{z-2}{4}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}=0$
$\Rightarrow(2 \lambda-3) 2+(3 \lambda-1) 3+(4 \lambda-5) 4=0$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=1$

Point $\mathrm{Q}=(4,8,6)$
$\mathrm{PQ}^{2}=6$

Question: Let L be the line $\frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+1}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+3}{6}$ and let S be the set of all points $(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c})$ on L , whose distance from the line $\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z+9}{0}$ along the line L is 7 . Then $\sum_{(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}) \in \mathrm{S}}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})$ is equal to :
Solution:
$M$ is the point of intersection of $L_{1} \& L_{2}$
$\Rightarrow 2 \lambda-1=2 \mu-1,3 \lambda-1=3 \mu-1,6 \lambda-3=9$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=2=\mu$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}(3,5,9)$
Now let point P be $(2 \mathrm{~K}-1,3 \mathrm{~K}-1,6 \mathrm{~K}-3)$ on $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ such that $\mathrm{PM}=7$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{(2 \mathrm{~K}-4)^{2}+(3 \mathrm{~K}-6)^{2}+(6 \mathrm{~K}-12)^{2}}=7$
$\Rightarrow 49 \mathrm{~K}^{2}+196-196 \mathrm{~K}=49$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{K}^{2}+4-4 \mathrm{~K}=1$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{K}^{2}-4 \mathrm{~K}+3=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{K}=1,3$
So points $\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{Q}$ are $(1,2,3) \&(5,8,15)$
So sum of all co-ordinates of $\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{Q}=34$

Question: If the line \[\frac{{2 - x}}{3} = \frac{{3y - 2}}{{4\lambda + 1}} = 4 - z\] makes a right angle with the line \[\frac{{x + 3}}{{3\mu }} = \frac{{1 - 2y}}{6} = \frac{{5 - z}}{7}\], then \[4\lambda + 9\mu \] is equal to:
Solution:
\[\frac{{2 - x}}{3} = \frac{{3y - 2}}{{4\lambda + 1}} = 4 - z\,\,\,...\left( 1 \right)\]
\[\frac{{x - 2}}{{\left( { - 3} \right)}} = \frac{{y - \frac{2}{3}}}{{\left( {\frac{{4\lambda + 1}}{3}} \right)}} = \frac{{z - 4}}{{\left( { - 1} \right)}}\]
\[\frac{{x + 3}}{{3\mu }} = \frac{{1 - 2y}}{6} = \frac{{5 - z}}{7}\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)\]
\[\frac{{x + 3}}{{3\mu }} = \frac{{y - \frac{1}{2}}}{{\left( { - 3} \right)}} = \frac{{z - 5}}{{\left( { - 7} \right)}}\]
Right angle \[ \Rightarrow \left( { - 3} \right)\left( {3\mu } \right) + \left( {\frac{{4\lambda + 1}}{3}} \right)\left( { - 3} \right) + \left( { - 1} \right)\left( { - 7} \right) = 0\]
\[ - 9\mu - 4\lambda - 1 + 7 = 0\]
\[4\lambda + 9\mu = 6\]

Question: If the shortest distance between the lines $\frac{{x - \lambda }}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{y - 2}}{1} = \frac{{z - 1}}{1}$ and $\frac{{x - \sqrt 3 }}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 2}}{1}$ is 1, then the sum of all possible values of $\lambda $ is:
Solution:
Passing points of lines ${L_1}\,\,\& \,\,{L_2}$ are $\left( {\lambda ,2,1} \right)\,\,\& \,\,\left( {\sqrt 3 ,1,2} \right)$
S.D. $ = \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\sqrt 3 - \lambda }&{ - 1}&1 \\
  { - 2}&1&1 \\
  1&{ - 2}&1
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  { - 2}&1&1 \\
  1&{ - 2}&1
\end{array}} \right|}}$
$1 = \left| {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - \lambda }}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right|$
$\lambda = 0,\,\,\lambda = 2\sqrt 3 $

Question: Let the foot of perpendicular of the point $P\left( {3, - 2, - 9} \right)$ on the plane passing through the points $\left( { - 1, - 2, - 3} \right),\left( {9,3,4} \right),\left( {9, - 2,1} \right)$ be $Q\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$. Then the distance of $Q$ from the origin is
Solution:
$P\left( {3, - 2, - 9} \right)$

Equation of plane through A, B, C
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x + 1}&{y + 2}&{z + 3} \\
  {10}&5&7 \\
  {10}&0&4
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$2x + 3y - 5z - 7 = 0$
Foot of perpendicular of $P\left( {3, - 2, - 9} \right)$ is
$\frac{{x - 3}}{2} = \frac{{y + 2}}{3} = \frac{{z + 9}}{{ - 5}} = - \frac{{\left( {6 - 6 + 45 - 7} \right)}}{{4 + 9 + 25}}$
$\frac{{x - 3}}{2} = \frac{{y + 2}}{3} = \frac{{z + 9}}{{ - 5}} = - 1$
$Q\left( {1, - 5, - 4} \right) \equiv \left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$
$OQ = \sqrt {{\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2} + {\gamma ^2}} = \sqrt {42} $

Question: Suppose the line $\frac{{x - 2}}{\alpha } = \frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 5}} = \frac{{z + 2}}{2}$ lies on the plane $x + 3y - 2z + \beta  = 0$, then $\left( {\alpha  + \beta } \right)$ is equal to ____
Solution:
Point (2, 2, –2) lies on the given plane
So, $2 + 3 \times 2 - 2\left( { - 2} \right) + \beta = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 2 + 6 + 4 + \beta = 0 \Rightarrow \beta = - 12$
Also, $\alpha \times 1 - 5 \times 3 + 2 \times - 2 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha - 15 - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha = 19$
$\therefore \,\,\alpha + \beta = 19 - 12 = 7$

Question: Let d be the distance of the point of intersection of the lines \[\frac{{x + 6}}{3} = \frac{y}{2} = \frac{{z + 1}}{1}\] and \[\frac{{x - 7}}{4} = \frac{{y - 9}}{3} = \frac{{z - 4}}{2}\] from the point (7, 8, 9). Then \[{d^2} + 6\] is equal to :
Solution:
\[\frac{{x + 6}}{3} = \frac{y}{2} = \frac{{z + 1}}{1} = \lambda \,\,\,.....\left( 1 \right)\]
\[x = 3\lambda - 6,y = 2\lambda ,z = \lambda - 1\]
\[\frac{{x - 7}}{4} = \frac{{y - 9}}{3} = \frac{{z - 4}}{2} = \mu \,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)\]
\[x = 4\mu + 7,y = 3\mu + 9,\,z = 2\mu + 4\]
\[3\lambda - 6 = 4\mu + 7 \Rightarrow 3\lambda - 4\mu = 13\,\,\,\,...\left( 3 \right) \times 2\]
\[2\lambda = 3\mu + 9 \Rightarrow 2\lambda - 3\mu = 9\,\,\,\,...\left( 4 \right) \times 3\]
\[6\lambda - 8\mu = 26\]
\[6\lambda - 9\mu = 27\]
\[\mu = - 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3\lambda - 4\left( { - 1} \right) = 13\]
\[3\lambda = 9\]
\[\lambda = 3\]
int. point (3, 6, 2); (7, 8, 9)
\[{d^2} = 16 + 4 + 49 = 69\]
Ans. \[{d^2} + 6 = 69 + 6 = 75\]

Question: The distance of the point $\left( {7, - 3, - 4} \right)$ from the plane passing through the points $\left( {2, - 3,1} \right),\left( { - 1,1, - 2} \right)$ and $\left( {3, - 4,2} \right)$ is :
Solution:
Equation of Plane is
$ = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 2}&{y + 3}&{z - 1} \\
  { - 3}&4&{ - 3} \\
  4&{ - 5}&4
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$x - z = 0$
Distance of $P\left( {7, - 3, - 4} \right)$ from plane is
$d = \left| {\frac{{7 + 4 - 1}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right| = 5\sqrt 2 $

Question: Let $Q$ and $R$ be two points on the line \[\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \;\frac{{y + 2}}{3} = \;\frac{{z - 1}}{2}\;\]at a distance \[\sqrt {26} \] from the point $P (4, 2, 7)$. Then the square of the area of the triangle PQR is …………. .
Solution:
Let \[(2\lambda - 1,\;3\lambda - \;2,\;2\lambda \; + \;1)\] be any point on the line
\[{(2\lambda - 5)^2} + \;{(3\lambda - \;4)^2} + \;{(2\lambda - \;6)^2} = \;26\]
\[\lambda = \;1,\;3\]
\[Q\;(1,\;1,\;3)\;;\;R\;(5,\;7,\;7)\;;\;\;P\;(4,\;2,\;7)\]
Are of triangle \[PQR = \;1/2\;\left| {\mathop {PQ}\limits^ \to \; \times \;\mathop {PR}\limits^ \to } \right|\]
\[ = \;\sqrt {153} \]

Question: If the mirror image of the point (2, 4, 7) in the plane $3x - y + 4z = 2$ is $\left( {a,b,c} \right)$, then $2a + b + 2c$ is equal to :

If the shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x - \lambda }}{2} = \frac{{y - 4}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}\;and\;\frac{{x - 2}}{4} = \frac{{y - 4}}{6} = \frac{{z - 7}}{8}\] \[is\;\frac{{13}}{{\sqrt {29} }},\]then a value of \[\lambda \] is:
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \left. \begin{gathered}
  {{\bar r}_1} = \left( {\lambda \hat i + 4\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) + \alpha \left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) \\
  {{\bar r}_2} = \left( {2\hat i + 4\hat j + 7\hat k} \right) + \beta \left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \right\}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\bar b = 2\hat i + 3j + 4\hat k} \\
  \begin{gathered}
  {{\bar a}_1} + \lambda \hat i + 4\hat j + 3\hat k \\
  {{\bar a}_2} = 2\hat i + 4\hat j + 7\hat k \\
\end{gathered} \\
  {}
\end{array} \\
  Shortest\;dist. = \frac{{\left| {\bar b \times \left( {{{\bar a}_2} - {{\bar a}_1}} \right)} \right|}}{{\left| b \right|}} = \frac{{13}}{{\sqrt {29} }} \\
  \frac{{\left| {\left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) \times \left( {\left( {2 - \lambda } \right)\hat i + 4\hat k} \right)} \right|}}{{\sqrt {29} }} = \frac{{13}}{{\sqrt {29} }} \\
  \left| { - 8\hat j - 3\left( {2 - \lambda } \right)\hat k + 12\hat i + 4\left( {2 - \lambda } \right)\hat j} \right| = 13 \\
  \left| {12\hat i - 4\lambda \hat j + \left( {3\lambda - 6} \right)\hat k} \right| = 13 \\
  144 + 16{\lambda ^2} + {\left( {3\lambda - 6} \right)^2} = 169 \\
  16{\lambda ^2} + {\left( {3\lambda - 6} \right)^2} = 25 = \lambda \Rightarrow = 1 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: If the line, $\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{3} = \frac{{z - 2}}{4}$meets the plane, $x + 2y + 3z = 15$at a point P, then the distance of P from the origin is:
Solution:
Let point on line be $P\left( {2k + 1,3k - 1,4k + 2} \right)$
Since, point P lies on the plane $x + 2y + 3z = 15$
$\therefore 2k + 1 + 6k - 2 + 12k + 6 = 15$
$ \Rightarrow k = \frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore P \equiv \left( {2,\frac{1}{2},4} \right)$
Then the distance of the point P from the origin is
$OP = \sqrt {4 + \frac{1}{4} + 16} = \frac{9}{2}$

Question: The shortest distance between the lines $x + 1 = 2y = - 12z$ and $x = y + 2 = 6z - 6$ is
Solution:
$\frac{{x + 1}}{1} = \frac{y}{{\frac{1}{2}}} = \frac{z}{{\frac{{ - 1}}{{12}}}}$ and $\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y + 2}}{1} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{\frac{1}{6}}}$
$ \Rightarrow $ Shortest distance $ = \frac{{\left( {\vec b - \vec a} \right) \cdot \left( {\vec p \times \vec q} \right)}}{{\left| {\vec p \times \vec q} \right|}}$
S.D. $ = \left( { - \hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k} \right).\frac{{\left( {\vec p \times \vec q} \right)}}{{\left| {\vec p \times \vec q} \right|}}$
$\left\{ {\vec p \times \vec q \equiv \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&{\frac{1}{2}}&{\frac{{ - 1}}{{12}}} \\
  1&1&{\frac{1}{6}}
\end{array}} \right| = \frac{1}{6}\hat i - \frac{1}{4}\hat j + \frac{1}{2}\hat k\,\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,2\hat i - 3\hat j + 6\hat k} \right\}$
S.D. $ = \frac{{\left( { - \hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k} \right) \cdot \left( {2\hat i - 3\hat j + 6\hat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {6^2}} }} = \left| {\frac{{ - 14}}{7}} \right| = 2$

Question: Let the line L intersect the lines $x - 2 = - y = z - 1$, $2\left( {x + 1} \right) = 2\left( {y - 1} \right) = z + 1$ and be parallel to the line $\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{y - 1}}{1} = \frac{{z - 2}}{2}$. Then which of the following lies on L ?
Solution:
${L_1}:\frac{{x - 2}}{1} = \frac{y}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 1}}{1} = \lambda $
${L_2} = \frac{{x + 1}}{{\frac{1}{2}}} = \frac{{y - 1}}{{\frac{1}{2}}} = \frac{{z + 1}}{1} = \mu $
dr of line MN will be
$ < 3 + \lambda - \frac{\mu }{2}, - 1 - \lambda - \frac{\mu }{2},$ $2 + \lambda - \mu > $ and it will be proportional to < 3,1,2 >
$\therefore \,\,\frac{{3 + \lambda - \frac{\mu }{2}}}{3} = \frac{{ - 1 - \lambda - \frac{\mu }{2}}}{1} = \frac{{2 + \lambda - \mu }}{2}$

$ \Rightarrow \lambda = - \frac{4}{3}$ and $\mu = - \frac{2}{3}$
∴ Coordinates of M will be $ < \left( {\frac{2}{3},\,\frac{4}{3},\, - \frac{1}{3}} \right)$
And equation of required line will be.
$\frac{{x - \frac{2}{3}}}{3} = \frac{{y - \frac{4}{3}}}{1} = \frac{{z + \frac{1}{3}}}{2} = k$
So any point on this line will be
$\left( {\frac{2}{3} + 3k,\,\,\frac{4}{3} + k,\, - \frac{1}{3} + 2k} \right)$
$\because \,\frac{2}{3} + 3k = - \frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow k = - \frac{1}{3}$
∴ Point lie on the line for
$k = - \frac{1}{3}$ is $\left( { - \frac{1}{3},1, - 1} \right)$

Tags.Subject: Math
Tags.Topic: Equation of lines in 3D and vector form

Question: Let a line passing through the point $\left( { - 1,2,3} \right)$ intersect the lines ${L_1}:\frac{{x - 1}}{3} = \frac{{y - 2}}{2} = \frac{{z + 1}}{{ - 2}}$ at $M\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$ and ${L_2}:\frac{{x + 2}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 1}}{4}$ at $N\left( {a,b,c} \right).$ Then, the value of $\frac{{{{\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {a + b + c} \right)}^2}}}$ equals ____.
Solution:
$M\left( {3\lambda + 1,2\lambda + 2, - 2\lambda - 1} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\therefore \,\,\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 3\lambda + 2$
$N\left( { - 3\mu - 2, - 2\mu + 2,4\mu + 1} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\therefore \,\,\,a + b + c = - \mu + 1$

$\frac{{3\lambda + 2}}{{ - 3\mu - 1}} = \frac{{2\lambda }}{{ - 2\mu }} = \frac{{ - 2\lambda - 4}}{{4\mu - 2}}$
$3\lambda \mu + 2\mu = 3\lambda \mu + \lambda $
$2\mu = \lambda $
$2\lambda \mu - \lambda = \lambda \mu + 2\mu $
$\lambda \mu = \lambda + 2\mu $
$ \Rightarrow \lambda \mu = 2\lambda $
$ \Rightarrow \mu = 2\,\,\,\,\left( {\lambda \ne 0} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,\lambda = 4$
$\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 14$
$a + b + c = - 1$
$\frac{{{{\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {a + b + c} \right)}^2}}} = 196$

Tags.Topic: Equation of lines in 3D and vector form

Question:
The sum of all values of $\alpha$, for which the shortest distance between the lines
$\frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{\alpha}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-2}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-4}{-\alpha}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\alpha}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{2 \alpha}$ is $\sqrt{2}$, is
Solution:
$\sqrt{2}=\frac{\left|\begin{array}{ccc}-1 & 1 & 3 \\ \alpha & -1 & -\alpha \\ \alpha & 2 & 2 \alpha\end{array}\right|}{\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & j & k \\ \alpha & -1 & -\alpha \\ \alpha & 2 & 2 \alpha\end{array}\right|}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{2}= & \frac{-1(-2 \alpha+2 \alpha)-1\left(2 \alpha^{2}+\alpha^{2}\right)+3(2 \alpha+\alpha)}{\left|\hat{\mathrm{i}}(-2 \alpha+2 \alpha)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}\left(2 \alpha^{2}+\alpha^{2}\right)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(2 \alpha+\alpha)\right|} \\
& \sqrt{2}=\frac{-3 \alpha^{2}+9 \alpha}{\sqrt{9 \alpha^{4}+9 \alpha^{2}}} \\
& \sqrt{2}=\frac{-\alpha+3}{\sqrt{\alpha^{2}+1}} \\
& \Rightarrow 2 \alpha^{2}+2=\alpha^{2}+9-6 \alpha \\
& \alpha^{2}+6 \alpha-7=0 \\
& (\alpha+7)(\alpha-1)=0 \\
& \alpha=-7,1 \\
& \text { sum }=-7+1=-6
\end{aligned}
$$
option (b)

Question: The shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x-3}}{2} = \frac{{y + 15}}{{-7}} = \frac{{z-9}}{5}\] and \[\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y-1}}{1} = \frac{{z-9}}{{-3}}\] is
Solution:
\[\frac{{x-3}}{2} = \frac{{y + 15}}{{-7}} = \frac{{z-9}}{5}\] and \[\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y-1}}{1} = \frac{{z-9}}{{-3}}\]
\[S.D = \frac{{\left| {\left( {{{\bar a}_2}.{{\bar a}_1}} \right).\left( {{{\bar b}_1}.{{\bar b}_2}} \right)} \right|}}{{\left| {{{\bar b}_1} \times {{\bar b}_2}} \right|}}\]
\[{a_1} = 3,-15,9\] \[{b_1} = 2,-7,5\]
\[{a_2} = -1,1,9\] \[{b_2} = 2,1,-3\]
\[{a_2}-{a_1} = -4,16,0\]
\[{\bar b_1} \times {\bar b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&{-7}&5 \\
  2&1&{-3}
\end{array}} \right| = \hat i\left( {16} \right)-\hat j\left( {-16} \right) + \hat k\left( {16} \right)\]
\[16\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right)\]
\[\left| {{{\bar b}_1} \times {{\bar b}_2}} \right| = 16\sqrt 3 \]
\[\therefore \left( {{{\bar a}_2}.{{\bar a}_1}} \right).\left( {{{\bar b}_1}-{{\bar b}_2}} \right) = 16\left[ {-4 + 16} \right] = \left( {16} \right)\left( {12} \right)\]
\[S.D. = \frac{{\left( {16} \right)\left( {12} \right)}}{{16\sqrt 3 }} = 4\sqrt 3 \]

Question:
An angle between the lines whose direction cosines are given by the equations,
 l + 3m + 5n = 0 and 5lm – 2mn + 6nl = 0, is :
Solution:
Given \[l{\mathbf{ }} + {\mathbf{ }}3m{\mathbf{ }} + {\mathbf{ }}5n{\mathbf{ }} = {\mathbf{ }}0\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 1 \right)\]
and \[5lm{\mathbf{ }} - {\mathbf{ }}2mn{\mathbf{ }} + {\mathbf{ }}6nl{\mathbf{ }} = {\mathbf{ }}0\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)\]
Here l, m, n are directional cosines
From (1), \[l{\mathbf{ = }} - 3m - 5n\]
Substituting equation (1) in equation (2)
mn)mmn + 6nmn) = 0
15m2 + 45mn + 30n2 = 0
\[ \Rightarrow \] m2 + 3mn + 2n2 = 0
\[ \Rightarrow \] m2 + 2mn + mn + 2n2 =0
\[ \Rightarrow \] (m + n) (m + 2n) = 0
\[\therefore \] mn or mn
If mn; ln
And for mn; l = n
\[\therefore \left( {l,m,n} \right){\mathbf{ = }}\left( { - 2n, - n,n} \right)\]
or \[\left( {l,m,n} \right){\mathbf{ = }}\left( {n, - 2n,n} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\left( {l,m,n} \right){\mathbf{ = }}\left( { - 2, - 1,1} \right) = \bar A\]
or \[ \Rightarrow \,\,\left( {l,m,n} \right){\mathbf{ = }}\left( {1, - 2,1} \right) = \bar B\]
\[\cos \left( \theta \right){\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{\bar A.\bar B}}{{\left| {\bar A} \right|\left| {\bar B} \right|}},\,\,\theta \] is angle between the lines
\[ \Rightarrow \cos \left( \theta \right){\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{ - 2.1{\mathbf{ + }}\left( { - 1} \right).\left( { - 2} \right){\mathbf{ + }}1.1}}{{\sqrt 6 .\sqrt 6 }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \cos \left( \theta \right){\mathbf{ = }}\frac{1}{6}\]
\[\therefore \,\,\theta {\mathbf{ = }}{\cos ^{ - {\mathbf{1}}}}\left( {\frac{1}{6}} \right)\]

Question: Let the image of the point P (1, 2, 3) in the line \[L\;:\;\frac{{x - 6}}{3} = \;\frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \;\frac{{z - 2}}{3}\] be Q. let \[R\;(\alpha ,\;\beta ,\;\gamma )\]be a point that divides internally the line segment PQ in the ratio 1 : 3. Then the value of \[22\;(\alpha  + \;\beta \; + \;\gamma )\]is equal to
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"


Let M be the mid-point of PQ
\[\therefore \;\;M = \;(3\lambda + \;6,\;2\lambda + 1,\;3\lambda + \;2)\]
Now, \[\overrightarrow {PM} = \;(3\lambda + \;5)\;\hat i + \;(2\lambda - 1)\;\hat j + \;(3\lambda - 1)\;\hat k\]
\[\because \;\;\overrightarrow {PM} \; \bot \;(3\hat i + \;2\hat j + \;3\hat k)\]
\[\therefore \;\;3\;(3\lambda + \;5)\; + \;2\;(2\lambda - 1)\; + \;3\;(3\lambda - 1) = \;0\]
\[\lambda = \;\frac{{ - 5}}{{11}}\]
\[\therefore \;\;\;M\;\left( {\frac{{51}}{{11}},\;\frac{1}{{11}},\;\frac{7}{{11}}} \right)\]
Since R is mid-point of PM
\[22\;(\alpha + \;\beta + \;\gamma )\; = \;125\]

Question:
Let a plane P pass through the point (3,7, –7) and contain the line,
\[\frac{{x - 2}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y - 3}}{2} = \frac{{z + 2}}{1}.\] If distance of the plane P from the origin is d, then \[{d^2}\]  is equal to ______.
Solution:
Let $A \equiv \left( {3,7, - 7} \right)$ and $B \equiv \left( {2,3, - 2} \right)$
\[\overrightarrow {BA} = \left( {\mathop i\limits^ \wedge + 4\mathop j\limits^ \wedge - 5\mathop k\limits^ \wedge } \right)\]
\[\vec n = - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k\]
\[\overrightarrow {BA} \times \overrightarrow n = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\mathop i\limits^ \wedge }&{\mathop j\limits^ \wedge }&{\mathop k\limits^ \wedge } \\
  { - 3}&2&1 \\
  1&4&{ - 5}
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[a\mathop i\limits^ \wedge + b\mathop j\limits^ \wedge + c\mathop k\limits^ \wedge = - 14\mathop i\limits^ \wedge - 14\mathop j\limits^ \wedge - 14\mathop k\limits^ \wedge \]
\[a:b:c = 1:1:1\]
Plane is \[\left( {x - 3} \right) + \left( {y - 7} \right) + \left( {z + 7} \right) = 0\]
\[x + y + z - 3 = 0\]
\[d = \sqrt 3 \Rightarrow {d^2} = 3\]

Question: Two lines \[\frac{{x\, - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y + 1}}{3} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 1}}\]and \[\frac{{x\, + 5}}{7} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 6}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}\]intersect at the point R. The reflection of R in the xy-plane has coordinates:
Solution:
Let the coordinate of R with respect to line

$\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y + 1}}{3} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 1}} = \lambda \\
  \frac{{x + 5}}{7} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 6}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4} = \mu \\
  {L_1} = \left( {\lambda + 3,3\lambda - 1, - \lambda + 6} \right) \\
  {\text{and coordinate of R w}}{\text{.r}}{\text{.t}}{\text{.}} \\
  {\text{line}}\,{L_2} = \left( {7\mu - 5,\, - 6\mu + 2,\,4\mu + 3} \right) \\
  \therefore \,\,\lambda - 7\mu = - 8,3\lambda + 6\mu = 3,\lambda + 4\mu = 3 \\
  {\text{From above equation : }}\lambda = - {\text{1, }}\mu \,{\text{ = 1}} \\
  {\text{Coordinate of point of intersection}}\,R = \left( {2, - 4,7} \right) \\
  {\text{Image of R w}}{\text{.r}}{\text{.t}}{\text{. xy plane}}\, = \left( {2, - 4, - 7} \right). \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Let a line pass through two distinct points P(–2, –1, 3) and Q, and be parallel to the vector \[3\hat i + 2\hat j + 2k\]. If the distance of the point Q from the point R (1, 3, 3) is 5, then the square of the area of \[\Delta PQR\] is equal to :
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow {PQ} \,\,parallel\,\,to\;\;3\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k,\,\,R(1,3,3)\]
\[ \Rightarrow Q(3\lambda - 2,\,\,2\lambda - 1,\,\,2\lambda + 3),\,\,\lambda \in R - \left\{ 0 \right\}\]
\[\left| {\overrightarrow {QR} } \right| = 5 = \sqrt {{{\left( {3\lambda - 3} \right)}^2} + {{(2\lambda - 4)}^2} + {{\left( {2\lambda } \right)}^2}} \]
\[\therefore 17{\lambda ^2} - 34\lambda + 25 = 25 \Rightarrow \lambda = 2\left( {\because \lambda \ne 0} \right)\]
\[\therefore Q(4,\,\,3,\,\,7),P( - 2, - 1,\,\,3),R(1,\,\,3,\,\,3)\]
\[Area\,\,of\,\,\Delta PQR = [PQR] = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\overrightarrow {PQ} \times \overrightarrow {PR} } \right|\]
\[\left[ {PQR} \right] = \frac{1}{2}\left\| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  6&4&4 \\
  3&4&0
\end{array}} \right\| = \left\| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  3&2&2 \\
  3&4&0
\end{array}} \right\|\]
\[\left[ {PQR} \right] = | - 8\hat i + 6\hat j + 6\hat k| = \sqrt {136} \]
\[\therefore {[PQR]^2} = 136\]

Question:
If the distance between the plane, \[23x - 10y - 2z + 48 = 0\] and the plane containing the lines \[\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{4} = \frac{{z + 1}}{3}\] and \[\frac{{x + 3}}{2} = \frac{{y + 2}}{6} = \frac{{z - 1}}{\lambda },\left( {\lambda \in R} \right)\], is equal to \[\frac{k}{{\sqrt {633} }}\], then k is equal to
_______
Solution:
We find the point of intersection of the two lines, and the distance of given plane from the two lines is the distance of plane from the point of intersection.
\[\therefore \,\,\,\left( {2p - 1,\,\,4p + 3,\,\,3p - 1} \right) = \left( {2q - 3,\,\,6q - 2,\,\,\lambda q + 1} \right)\]
\[p = - \frac{1}{2}\] and \[q = \frac{1}{2}\]
\[\lambda = - 7\]
\[\therefore \] The point of intersection is \[\left( { - 2,\,\,1,\,\, - \frac{5}{2}} \right)\]
Now,
The distance of given plane from the point of intersection \[ = \frac{k}{{\sqrt {633} }}\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,\frac{k}{{\sqrt {633} }} = \left| {\frac{{ - 46 - 10 + 5 + 48}}{{\sqrt {633} }}} \right|\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,k = 3\]

Question: The line of shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x - 2}}{0} = \;\frac{{y - 1}}{1} = \;\frac{z}{1}\] and \[\frac{{x - 3}}{2} = \;\frac{{y - 5}}{2} = \;\frac{{z - \;1}}{1}\] makes an angle of \[{\cos ^{ - 1}}\;\left( {\sqrt {\frac{2}{{27}}} } \right)\] with the plane \[P\;:\;ax - \;y - \;z = \;0,\]\[(a\; > \;0).\] Then Number of Integral values of ‘a’ for Plane P is equal to ………… .
Solution:
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  0&1&1 \\
  2&2&1
\end{array}} \right| = \; - \hat i\; + \;2\hat j\; - \;2\hat k\]
angle between line and plane is \[{\cos ^{ - 1}}\;\sqrt {\frac{2}{{27}}} = \;\alpha \]
\[\cos \;\alpha \; = \;\sqrt {\frac{2}{{27}}} ,\;\sin \;\alpha = \;\frac{5}{{3\sqrt 3 }}\]
\[\sin \;\alpha = \;\left| {\frac{{ - a\; - \;2\; + \;2}}{{\sqrt {4 + 4 + \;1} \;\sqrt {{a^2} + \;1 + \;1} }}} \right|\; = \;\frac{5}{{3\sqrt 3 }}\]
\[\sqrt 3 \;|a|\; = \;5\sqrt {{a^2} + \;2} \]
\[3{a^2} = \;25{a^2} + \;50\]
No value of (a)

Question: If the point (2, α, β) lies on the plane which passes through the points (3, 4, 2) and (7, 0, 6) and is perpendicular to the plane 2x – 5y = 15, then 2α – 3β is equal to :
Solution:
Let the normal to the required plane is $\vec n,$ then
$\vec n = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  4&{ - 4}&4 \\
  2&{ - 5}&0
\end{array}} \right| = 20\hat i + 8\hat j - 12\hat k$
Equation of the plane
\[\begin{gathered}
  \left( {x - 3} \right) \times 20 + \left( {y - 4} \right) \times 8 + \left( {z - 2} \right) \times \left( { - 12} \right) = 0 \\
  5x - 15 + 2y - 8 - 3z + 6 = 0 \\
  5x + 2y - 3z - 17 = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(1) \\
  {\text{Since, equation of plane (1) passes through (2, }}\alpha {\text{, }}\beta {\text{), th}}en \\
  10 + 2\alpha - 3\beta - 17 = 0 \Rightarrow 2\alpha - 3\beta = 7 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: On which of the following lines lies the point of inter-section of the line $\frac{{x - 4}}{2} = \frac{{y - 5}}{2} = \frac{{z - 3}}{1}$ and the plane, $x + y + z = 2?$
Solution:
Let any point on the line $\frac{{x - 4}}{2} = \frac{{y - 5}}{2} = \frac{{z - 3}}{1}$ be $A\left( {2\lambda + 4,2\lambda + 5,\lambda + 3} \right)$ which lies on the plane $x + y + z = 2$
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow 2\lambda + 4 + 2\lambda + 5 + \lambda + 3 = 2 \\
   \Rightarrow 5\lambda = - 10 \\
   \Rightarrow \lambda = - 2 \\
\end{gathered} $
Then, the point of intersection is (0, 1, 1) which lies on the line $\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 3}}{2} = \frac{{z + 4}}{{ - 5}}$
