Question: Let P(x, y, z) be a point in the first octant, whose Session projection in the xy-plane is the point Q. Let OP = \[\gamma \]; the angle between OQ and the positive x-axis be \[\theta \]; and the angle between OP and the positive z-axis be \[\phi \], where O is the origin. Then the distance of P from the x-axis is :
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  P\left( {x,y,z} \right),\;Q\left( {x,y,O} \right);\;{x^2} + {y^2} + {z^2} = {\gamma ^2} \\
  \overline {OQ} = x\hat i + y\hat j \\
  \cos \theta = \frac{x}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} }} \\
  \cos \phi = \frac{z}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2} + {z^2}} }} \\
   \Rightarrow \;{\sin ^2}\phi = \frac{{{x^2} + {y^2}}}{{{x^2} + {y^2} + {z^2}}} \\
  {\text{distance of P from x - axis}}\;\sqrt {{y^2} + {z^2}} \\
   \Rightarrow \;\sqrt {{\gamma ^2} - {x^2}} \Rightarrow \;\gamma \sqrt {1 - \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{\gamma ^2}}}} \\
   = \gamma \sqrt {1 - {{\cos }^2}\theta {{\sin }^2}\phi } \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A plane passing through the points $\left( {0, - 1,0} \right)$and $\left( {0,0,1} \right)$and making an angle $\frac{\pi }{4}$with the plane $y - z + 5 = 0,$also passes through the point:
Solution:
Let the required plane passing through the points $\left( {0, - 1,0} \right){\text{and}}\left( {0,0,1} \right){\text{be }}\frac{x}{\lambda } + \frac{y}{{ - 1}} + \frac{z}{1} = 1$and the given plane is $y - z + 5 = 0$
$\therefore \cos \frac{\pi }{4} = \frac{{ - 1 - 1}}{{\sqrt {\left( {\frac{1}{{{\lambda ^2}}} + 1 + 1} \right)} \sqrt 2 }}$
$ \Rightarrow {\lambda ^2} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda } = \pm \sqrt 2 $
Then, the equation of plane is
$ \pm \sqrt 2 x - y + z = 1$
Then the point $\left( {\sqrt 2 ,1,4} \right)$satisfies the equation of plane
$ - \sqrt 2 x - y + z = 1$

Question: The vector equation of the plane passing through the intersection of the planes $\overrightarrow r  \cdot (\hat i + \hat j + \hat k) = 1{\text{ }}\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{\text{ }}\overrightarrow r  \cdot (\hat i - 2\hat j) =  - 2$, and the point (1, 0, 2) is:
Solution:
Plane passing through intersection of plane is
$\left\{ {\overrightarrow r .\left( {\widehat i + \widehat j + \widehat k} \right) - 1} \right\} + \lambda \left\{ {\overrightarrow r .\left( {\widehat i - 2\widehat j} \right) + 2} \right\} = 0$
This plane passes through \[\widehat i + 2\widehat k,\] so we get
\[\left( {3 - 1} \right) + \lambda \left( {1 + 2} \right) = 0\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;\lambda = - \frac{2}{3}\]
Hence, equation of plane is \[3\left\{ {\overrightarrow r .\left( {\widehat i + \widehat j + \widehat k} \right) - 1} \right\} - 2\left\{ {\overrightarrow r .\left( {\widehat i - 2\widehat j} \right) + 2} \right\} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;\overrightarrow r \cdot (\hat i + 7\hat j + 3\hat k) = 7\]

Question: If the plane \[2x + \;y - \;5z = \;0\] is rotated about its line of intersection with the plane \[3x - \;y + \;4z - 7\; = \;0\] by an angle of \[\frac{\pi }{2},\] then the plane after the rotation passes through the point :

Shortest distance between the lines
$\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 8}}{{ - 7}} = \frac{{z - 4}}{5}$ and $\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{1} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 3}}$ is
Solution:
$\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 8}}{{ - 7}} = \frac{{z - 4}}{5}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\vec a = \hat i - 8\hat j + 4\hat k$
$\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{1} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 3}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\vec b = \hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k$
$\vec p = 2\hat i - 7\hat j + 5\hat k,\,\,\vec q = 2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k$
$\vec p \times \vec q = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&{ - 7}&5 \\
  2&1&{ - 3}
\end{array}} \right|$
$ = \hat i\left( {16} \right) - \hat j\left( { - 16} \right) + \hat k\left( {16} \right)$
$ = 16\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right)$
$d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {a - b} \right) \cdot \left( {\vec p \times \vec q} \right)}}{{\left| {\vec p \times \vec q} \right|}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{\left( {-10\hat j - 2k} \right) \cdot 16\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right)}}{{16\sqrt 3 }}} \right|$
$ = \left| {\frac{{ - 12}}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right|$
$ = 4\sqrt 3 $

Question: If the shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x + 2}}{2} = \frac{{y + 3}}{3} = \frac{{z-5}}{4}\] and \[\frac{{x-3}}{1} = \frac{{y-2}}{{-3}} = \frac{{z + 4}}{2}\] is \[\frac{{38}}{{3\sqrt 5 }}\,k,\] and \[\int\limits_0^k {{{[x]}^2}dx = \alpha -\sqrt \alpha  } ,\] where \[[x]\] denotes the greatest integer function, then \[6{\alpha ^3}\] is equal to__________.
Solution:
\[\frac{{38}}{{3\sqrt 5 }}\widehat k = \frac{{\left( {5\widehat i + 5\widehat j - 9\widehat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt 5 }}.\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\widehat i}&{\widehat j}&{\widehat k} \\
  2&3&4 \\
  1&{ - 3}&2
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[\frac{{38}}{{3\sqrt 5 }}\widehat k = \frac{{19}}{{\sqrt 5 }}\]
\[k = \frac{{19}}{{\sqrt 5 }}\]
\[k = \frac{3}{2}\]
\[\int_0^{3/2} {\left[ {{x^2}} \right]} = \int_0^1 0 + \int_1^{\sqrt 2 } 1 + \int_{\sqrt 2 }^{3/2} 2 \]
\[ = \sqrt 2 - 1 + 2\left( {\frac{3}{2} - \sqrt 2 } \right)\]
\[ = 2 - \sqrt 2 \]
\[\alpha = 2\]
\[ \Rightarrow 6{\alpha ^3} = 48\]

Question: If the line \[\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{y + 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 1}}\] intersects the plane 2x + 3y – z + 13 = 0 at a point P and the plane 3x + y + 4z = 16 at a point Q, then PQ is equal to
Solution:
Let points ${\text{P}}\left( {3\lambda + 2,\,\,2\lambda - 1,\,\, - \lambda + 1} \right)$ and
$Q\left( {3\mu + 2,\,\,2\mu - 1,\,\, - \mu + 1} \right)$
⸪ P lies on 2x + 3y – z + 13 = 0
$ \Rightarrow \,\,13\lambda = - 13\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda = - 1$
Hence, P(−1, −3, 2)
Similarly, Q lies on 3x + y + 4z = 16
$\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \,\,\,9\mu + 6 + 2\mu - 1 - 4\mu + 4 = 16 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,\,7\mu = 7\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\mu = 1 \\
\end{gathered} $
Hence, Q is (5, 1 , 0)
Now, $PQ = \sqrt {36 + 16 + 4} = \sqrt {56} = 2\sqrt {14} $

Question: If the equation of the plane passing through the line of intersection of the planes $2x - y + z = 3,\,\,4x - 3y + 5z + 9 = 0$ and parallel to the line $\frac{{x + 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 2}}{5}$ is $ax + by + cz + 6 = 0$, then $a + b + c$ is equal to
Solution:
Equation of family of plane
$\left( {2x - y + z - 3} \right) + \lambda \left( {4x - 3y + 5z + 9} \right) = 0$
$x\left( {2 + 4\lambda } \right) - y\left( {1 + 3\lambda } \right) + z\left( {1 + 5\lambda } \right) - 3 + 9\lambda = 0$
Parallel to the line
$ - 2\left( {2 + 4\lambda } \right) - \left( {1 + 3\lambda } \right)4 + \left( {1 + 5\lambda } \right)5 = 0$
$\begin{gathered}
  5\lambda = 3 \\
  \lambda = \frac{3}{5} \\
\end{gathered} $
Equation of plane
$11x - 7y + 10z + 6 = 0$
$a + b + c = 14$

Question: Let $\alpha $ be the angle between the lines whose direction cosines satisfy the equations $l + m - n = 0$ and ${l^2} + {m^2} - {n^2} = 0$. Then the value of ${\sin ^4}\alpha  + {\cos ^4}\alpha $ is:
Solution:
${l^2} + {m^2} + {n^2} = 1$
$\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \;\;2{n^2} = 1\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;n = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} \\
  \therefore \;\;{l^2} + {m^2} = \frac{1}{2}\;{\text{and}}\;l + m = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} \\
\end{gathered} $
$ \Rightarrow \;\;\frac{1}{2} - 2l\;m = \frac{1}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \;\;lm = 0\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;l = 0\;{\text{or}}\;m = 0$
$\therefore \;\;l = 0,\;m = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\;{\text{or}}\;l = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }},\;m = 0$
$ < 0,\;\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }},\;\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} > \;{\text{or}}\; < \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }},\;0,\;\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} > 0$
$\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \;\;\cos \alpha = \left| {0 + 0 + \frac{1}{2}} \right| = \frac{1}{2} \\
  \therefore \;\;{\sin ^4}\alpha + {\cos ^4}\alpha = \frac{9}{{16}} + \frac{1}{{16}} = \frac{5}{8} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Let the values of λ  for which the shortest distance between the lines $\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}$ and  $\frac{{{\text{x}} - \lambda }}{3} = \frac{{{\text{y}} - 4}}{4} = \frac{{{\text{z}} - 5}}{5}$ is $\frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}$ be λ <sub>1</sub> and λ <sub>2</sub>. Then the radius of the circle passing through the points (0, 0), (λ <sub>1</sub>, λ <sub>2</sub>) and (λ <sub>2</sub>, λ <sub>1</sub>) is
Solution:
$\vec p = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k,\,\,\vec q = 3\hat i + 4\hat j + 5\hat k$
$ \Rightarrow \vec p \times \vec q = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&3&4 \\
  3&4&5
\end{array}} \right| = - \hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k$
${\text{A}} \equiv (1,2,3){\text{B}} \equiv (\lambda ,4,5)$
Shortest Distance \[ = \left| {\frac{{\overrightarrow {AB} .\left( {\vec p \times \vec q} \right)}}{{\left| {\vec p \times \vec q} \right|}}} \right|\]
$ = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }} = \left| {\frac{{\left( {\left( {\lambda - 1} \right)\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k} \right).\left( { - \hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt 6 }}} \right|$
$ \Rightarrow \left| { - \lambda + 1 + 4 - 2} \right| = 1 \Rightarrow \left| {\lambda - 3} \right| = 1$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = 3 \pm 1 = 4,2$
Radius of circle passing through points
(0, 0), (4, 2) & (2, 4)
$ = \frac{{abc}}{{4\Delta }} = \frac{{\sqrt {20} \times \sqrt {20} \times \sqrt 8 }}{{4 \times \frac{1}{2}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  1&1&1 \\
  0&4&2 \\
  0&2&4
\end{array}} \right|}} = \frac{{20 \times 2\sqrt 2 }}{{2 \times 12}}$
$ = \frac{{5\sqrt 2 }}{3}$

Question: Let \[\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \;\frac{{y + \;1}}{{ - \,2}}\; = \;\frac{{z + \;3}}{{ - \,1}}\] lie on the plane \[px - \;qy\; + \;z = \;5,\] for some \[p,\;q\; \in \;R.\] The shortest distance of the plane from the origin is :
Solution:
\[(2,\; - 1,\; - \;3)\] satisfy the given plane.
So \[2p\; + \;q\; = \;8\] .............. (i)
Also given line is perpendicular to normal plane so
\[3p\; + \;2q\; - \;1 = \;0\] ........... (ii)
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;p\; = \;15,\;q = - \;22\]
Eq. of plane \[15x - \;22y\; + \;z - \;5 = \;0\]
its distance from origin \[ = \;\frac{6}{{\sqrt {710} }} = \;\sqrt {\frac{5}{{142}}} \]

Question: The lines $\frac{{x - 2}}{2} = \frac{y}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 7}}{{16}}$ and $\frac{{x + 3}}{4} = \frac{{y + 2}}{3} = \frac{{z + 2}}{1}$ intersect at the point $P$. If the distance of $P$ from the line $\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 1}}{3} = \frac{{z - 1}}{1}$ is $l,$ then $14{l^2}$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
$\frac{{x - 2}}{2} = \frac{y}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 7}}{{16}} = \lambda $
$\frac{{x + 3}}{4} = \frac{{y + 2}}{3} = \frac{{z + 2}}{1} = k$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda + 2 = 4k - 3$
$ - \lambda = 3k - 2$
$ \Rightarrow k = 1,\lambda = - 1$
$8\lambda + 7 = k - 2$
$\therefore \,\,P = \left( {1,1, - 1} \right)$

Projection of $2\hat i - 2\hat k$ on $2\hat i + 3\hat j + \hat k$ is
$ = \frac{{4 - 2}}{{\sqrt {4 + 9 + 1} }} = \frac{2}{{\sqrt {14} }}$
$\therefore \,\,{l^2} = 8 - \frac{4}{{14}} = \frac{{108}}{{14}}$
$ \Rightarrow 14{l^2} = 108$

Question: The vertices B and C of a triangle ABC lie on the line $\frac{\mathrm{x}}{1}=\frac{1-\mathrm{y}}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-2}{3}$. The coordinates of A and B are $(1,6,3)$ and $(4,9, \alpha)$ respectively and C is at a distance of 10 units from B . The area (in sq. units) of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is:
Solution:
$\frac{4}{1}=\frac{9-1}{2}=\frac{\alpha-2}{3} \Rightarrow \alpha=14$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=0 \\
& (\lambda-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(2 \lambda-5) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(3 \lambda-1) \hat{\mathrm{k}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}} \\
& \Rightarrow \lambda-1+4 \lambda-10+9 \lambda-3=0 \\
& \Rightarrow 14 \lambda=14 \Rightarrow \lambda=1 \\
& \mathrm{D}=(1,3,5) \\
& \mathrm{AD}=\sqrt{3^{2}+2^{2}}=\sqrt{13} \\
& \operatorname{Ar}(\triangle \mathrm{ABC})=\frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt{13} \times 10=5 \sqrt{13}
\end{aligned}
$$

Option (A)

Question: Let the image of the point ${\text{P}}\left( {2, - 1,3} \right)$ in the plane $x + 2y - z = 0$ be Q. Then the distance of the plane $3x + 2y + z + 29 = 0$ from the point $Q$ is

eq. of line PM $\frac{{x - 2}}{1} = \frac{{y + 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 3}}{{ - 1}} = \lambda $
any point on line $ = \left( {\lambda + 2,2\lambda - 1, - \lambda + 3} \right)$
for point '$m$' $\left( {\lambda + 2} \right) + 2\left( {2\lambda - 1} \right) - \left( {3 - \lambda } \right) = 0$
$\lambda = \frac{1}{2}$
Point $m\left( {\frac{1}{2} + 2,2 \times \frac{1}{2} - 1,\frac{{ - 1}}{2} + 3} \right)$
$ = \left( {\frac{5}{2},0,\frac{5}{2}} \right)$
For Image ${\text{Q}}\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$
$\frac{{\alpha + 2}}{2} = \frac{5}{2},\frac{{\beta - 1}}{2} = 0$,
$\frac{{\gamma + 3}}{2} = \frac{5}{2}$ Q : $\left( {3,1,2} \right)$
$d = \left| {\frac{{3\left( 3 \right) + 2\left( 1 \right) + 2 + 29}}{{\sqrt {{3^2} + {2^2} + {1^2}} }}} \right|$
$d = \frac{{42}}{{\sqrt {14} }} = 3\sqrt {14} $

Question: Let the shortest distance between the lines $L:\frac{{x - 5}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{y - \lambda }}{0} = \frac{{z + \lambda }}{1},\lambda \geqslant 0$ and ${L_1}:x + 1 = y - 1 = 4 - z$ be $2\sqrt 6 $. If $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$ lies on $L$, then which of the following is NOT possible?
Solution:
$\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  { - 2}&0&1 \\
  1&1&{ - 1}
\end{array}} \right| = - \hat i - \hat j - 2\hat k$
$\overrightarrow {{a_2}} - \overrightarrow {{a_1}} = 6\hat i + \left( {\lambda - 1} \right)\hat j + \left( { - \lambda - 4} \right)\hat k$
$2\sqrt 6 = \left| {\frac{{ - 6 - \lambda + 1 + 2\lambda + 8}}{{\sqrt {1 + 1 + 4} }}} \right|$
$\left| {\lambda + 3} \right| = 12 \Rightarrow = 9, - 15$
$\alpha = - 2k + 5,\,\,\gamma = k - \lambda $ where $k \in R$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha + 2\gamma = 5 - 2\lambda = - 13,35$

Question: Let L be the line of intersection of planes \[\overrightarrow {r.} \left( {\mathop i\limits^ \wedge   - \mathop j\limits^ \wedge   + 2\mathop k\limits^ \wedge  } \right) = \] 2 and \[\overrightarrow {r.} \left( {2\mathop i\limits^ \wedge   + \mathop j\limits^ \wedge   - \mathop k\limits^ \wedge  } \right) = 2.\] If \[P\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)\]is the foot of perpendicular on L from the point (1, 2, 0), then the value of \[35\left( {\alpha  + \beta  + \gamma } \right)\]is equal to :
Solution:
\[{P_1}:x - y + 2z = 2\]
     \[{P_2} = 2x + y - z = 2\]


Let line of intersection of planes \[{P_1}\] and \[{P_2}\] cuts \[xy\] plane in point Q.
\[ \Rightarrow \] z – coordinate of point Q is zero
\[ \Rightarrow \left. {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - y = 2} \\ 
  {2x + y = 2}
\end{array}} \right\} \Rightarrow x = \frac{4}{3},y = \frac{{ - 2}}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow Q\left( {\frac{4}{3},\frac{{ - 2}}{3},0} \right)\]
Vector parallel to the line of intersection
\[\overrightarrow a = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\mathop i\limits^ \wedge }&{\mathop j\limits^ \wedge }&{\mathop k\limits^ \wedge } \\
  1&{ - 1}&2 \\
  2&1&{ - 1}
\end{array}} \right| = - \mathop i\limits^ \wedge + 5\mathop j\limits^ \wedge + 3\mathop k\limits^ \wedge \]
Equation of line of intersection
\[\frac{{x - \frac{4}{3}}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{y + \frac{2}{3}}}{5} = \frac{{z - 0}}{3} = \lambda \left( {{\text{say}}} \right)\]
Let coordinates of foot of perpendicular be
\[F\left( { - \lambda + \frac{4}{3},5\lambda - \frac{2}{3},3\lambda } \right)\]
\[\overline {PF} = \left( { - \lambda + \frac{1}{3}} \right)\mathop i\limits^ \wedge + \left( {5\lambda - \frac{8}{3}} \right)\mathop j\limits^ \wedge + \left( {3\lambda } \right)\mathop k\limits^ \wedge \]
\[\overline {PF} .\,\overline a = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda - \frac{1}{3} + 25\lambda - \frac{{  40}}{3} + 9\lambda = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 35\lambda = \frac{{41}}{3} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{41}}{{105}}\]
Now, \[\alpha = - \lambda + \frac{4}{3},\beta = 5\lambda - \frac{2}{3},\gamma = 3\lambda \]
\[ \Rightarrow \alpha + \beta + \gamma = 7\lambda + \frac{2}{3}\]
\[ = 7\left( {\frac{{41}}{{105}}} \right) + \frac{2}{3}\]
\[ = \frac{{51}}{{15}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 35\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right) = \frac{{51}}{{15}} \times 35 = 119\]

Question: The perpendicular distance from the origin to the plane containing the two lines, $\frac{{x + 2}}{3} = \frac{{y - 2}}{5} = \frac{{z + 5}}{7}$ and $\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 4}}{4} = \frac{{z + 4}}{7}$ , is
Solution:
$\therefore \,\,\,{\text{D}}{\text{.R}}{\text{.}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{plane}} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  3&5&7 \\
  1&4&7
\end{array}} \right| = 7\hat i - 14\hat j + 7\hat k$
Now, equation of plane is,
$\begin{gathered}
  7\left( {x - 1} \right) - 14\left( {y - 4} \right) + 7\left( {z + 4} \right) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,\,x - 1 - 2y + 8 + z + 4 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,x - 2y + z + 11 = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $
Hence, distance from (0, 0, 0) to the plane,
$ = \frac{{11}}{{\sqrt {1 + 4 + 1} }} = \frac{{11}}{{\sqrt 6 }}$

Question: Let the image of the point $\mathrm{P}(1,6, \mathrm{a})$ in the line $\mathrm{L}: \frac{\mathrm{x}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{a}+1}{\mathrm{~b}}, \mathrm{~b}>0$, be $\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{3}, 0, \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}\right)$. If $\mathrm{S}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma), \alpha>0$, is the point on L such that the distance of S from the foot of perpendicular from the point P on L is $2 \sqrt{14}$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma$ is equal to :
Solution:
Let Q be the point $\perp^{\mathrm{r}}$ from $\mathrm{P}(1,6, \mathrm{a})$ to the line
$\mathrm{L}: \frac{\mathrm{x}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{a}+1}{\mathrm{~b}}$
The image of point P in line L is $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{3}, 0, \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}\right)$
∵ Q is the mid point $\mathrm{PP}^{\prime}$ :
$\mathrm{Q} \equiv\left(\frac{1+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{3}}{2}, \frac{6+0}{2}, \frac{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}}{2}\right) \equiv\left(\frac{3+\mathrm{a}}{6}, 3, \frac{2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}}{2}\right)$
comparing $x$ and $y, \frac{3+a}{6}=1 \Rightarrow a=3$
$\therefore Q \equiv\left(1,3, \frac{6+c}{2}\right)$
Direction vector of L is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=(1,2, \mathrm{~b})$
vector PQ is $\perp^{\mathrm{r}}$ to $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}$
$\therefore \overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}=\left(1-1,3-6, \frac{6+\mathrm{c}}{2}-3\right)$
$=\left(0,-3, \frac{\mathrm{c}}{2}\right)$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=0$
$(0) .(1)+(-3)(2)+\left(\frac{\mathrm{c}}{2}\right)(\mathrm{b})=0$
$\mathrm{bc}=12$
Now comparing z with $\mathrm{Q}\left(1,3, \frac{6+\mathrm{c}}{2}\right)$
$\frac{3-1}{2}=\frac{\frac{6+c}{2}-3+1}{b}$
$2 \mathrm{~b}-\mathrm{c}=2$
solving $b c=12$ and $c=2 b-2$
$\mathrm{b}=3,-2$
$\mathrm{b}=3 \quad(\mathrm{~b}>0)$
$\mathrm{c}=4$
$\therefore \mathrm{Q}(1,3,5)$
Now, any point S on the line L can be written as $(\mathrm{k}, 2 \mathrm{k}+1,3 \mathrm{k}+2)$
$S Q=\sqrt{(k-1)^{2}+(2 k-2)^{2}+(3 k-3)^{2}}=2 \sqrt{14}$
$\mathrm{k}=3,-1$
for $\mathrm{k}=3, \mathrm{~S} \equiv(3,7,11)$
$\therefore \alpha+\beta+\gamma=21$

Question: The distance of the point having position vector $ - \hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k$ from the straight line passing through the point (2, 3, −4) and parallel to the vector, $6\hat i + 3\hat j - 4\hat k$ is:
Solution:
Equation of the line is $\frac{{x - 2}}{6} = \frac{{y - 3}}{3} = \frac{{z + 4}}{{ - 4}}$


Let the point M on the line l is $\left( {6\lambda + 2,3\lambda + 3, - 4\lambda - 4} \right)$
Direction ratio’s of PM is \[\left( {6\lambda + 3,3\lambda + 1, - 4\lambda - 10} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {6\lambda + 3} \right)\left( 6 \right) + \left( {3\lambda + 1} \right)\left( 3 \right) + \left( { - 4\lambda - 10} \right)\left( { - 4} \right) = 0\]
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = - 1 \Rightarrow M \equiv \left( { - 4,0,0} \right)$
$\therefore PM = \sqrt {9 + 4 + 36} = 7$

Question: If the lines $\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{2} = \frac{{z + 3}}{1}$ and $\frac{{x - a}}{2} = \frac{{y + 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{1}$ intersects at the point $P$, then the distance of the point $P$ from the plane $z = a$ is:
Solution:
Point on \[{L_1} \equiv \left( {\lambda + 1,2\lambda + 2,\lambda - 3} \right)\]
Point on ${L_2} \equiv \left( {2\mu + a,3\mu - 2,\mu + 3} \right)$
$\lambda - 3 = \mu + 3 \Rightarrow \lambda = \mu + 6\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)$
$2\lambda + 2 = 3\mu - 2\, \Rightarrow 2\lambda = 3\mu - 4\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)$
Solving (1) and (2)
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = 22\,\,\,\& \,\,\,\mu = 16$
$ \Rightarrow P \equiv \left( {23,46,19} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow a = - 9$
Distance of $P$ from $z = - 9$ is 28.

Question: Let the co-ordinates of one vertex of $\Delta ABC$ be $A\left( {0,2,\alpha } \right)$ and the other two vertices lie on the line $\frac{{x + \alpha }}{5} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z + 4}}{3}$. For $\alpha \in \mathbb{Z}$, if the area of $\Delta ABC$ is 21 sq. units and the line segment $BC$ has length $2\sqrt {21} $ sq. units, then ${\alpha ^2}$ is equal to _______.
Solution:
$A\left( {0,2,\alpha } \right)$

$\left| {\frac{1}{2} \cdot 2\sqrt {21} \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  i&j&k \\
  \alpha &1&{\alpha + 4} \\
  5&2&3
\end{array}} \right|\frac{1}{{\sqrt {25 + 4 + 9} }}} \right| = 21\sqrt {21} $
$\sqrt {{{\left( {2\alpha + 5} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {2\alpha + 20} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {2\alpha - 5} \right)}^2}} = \sqrt {21} \sqrt {38} $
$ \Rightarrow 12{\alpha ^2} + 80\alpha + 450 = 798$
$ \Rightarrow 12{\alpha ^2} + 80\alpha - 348 = 0$
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \alpha = 3 \\
   \Rightarrow {\alpha ^2} = 9 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Let P be the image of the point Q(7, –2, 5) in the line \[L:\frac{{x-1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{3} = \frac{z}{4}\] and R(5, p, q) be a point on L. Then the square of the area of \[\Delta \]PQR is ____
Solution:
Let \[R\left( {2\lambda + 1,3\lambda -1,4\lambda } \right)\]
\[2\lambda + 1 = 5\]
\[\lambda = 2\]
\[R\left( {5,5,8} \right)\]
Let \[T\left( {2\lambda -6} \right)\hat i + \left( {3\lambda + 1} \right)\hat j + \left( {4\lambda -5} \right)\hat k\]
\[\overrightarrow {QT} = \left( {2\lambda -6} \right)\hat i + \left( {3\lambda + 1} \right)\hat j + \left( {4\lambda -5} \right)\hat k\]
\[\vec b = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k\]
\[\overrightarrow {QT} \cdot \vec b = 0\]
\[4\lambda -12 + 9\lambda + 3 + 16\lambda -20 = 0\]
\[\lambda = 1\]
\[T\left( {3,2,4} \right)\]
\[QT = \sqrt {33} \] \[RT = \sqrt {29} \]
\[{\left( {{\text{area of }}\Delta PQR} \right)^2} = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}\sqrt {29} .2\sqrt {33} } \right)^2}\]
\[ = 957\]

Question: Let the line $L$ pass through the point $\left( {0,1,2} \right)$, intersect the line $\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}$ and be parallel to the plane $2x + y - 3z = 4$. Then the distance of the point $P\left( {1, - 9,2} \right)$ from the line $L$ is
Solution:
$\overrightarrow {AB} \cdot \vec n$
$ \Rightarrow \left[ {\left( {1 + 2\lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {1 + 3\lambda } \right)\hat j + \left( {1 + 4\lambda } \right)\hat k} \right] \cdot \left( {2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k} \right)$
$2 + 4\lambda + 1 + 3\lambda - 3 - 12\lambda = 0$
$5\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 0$
Line $\overrightarrow {AB} ,\,\,\vec r = \hat j + 2\hat k + \mu \left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right)$
General form: $Q\left( {\mu ,1 + \mu ,2 + \mu } \right)$
$\therefore \,\,\overrightarrow {PQ} \cdot \overrightarrow {AB} = 0$
$\left( {\mu - 1} \right) + \left( {10 + \mu } \right) + \mu = 0$

$\therefore \,\,$distance $ = \sqrt {16 + 49 + 9} = \sqrt {74} $

Question: Let \[{\lambda _1},{\lambda _2}\] be the value of $\lambda $ for which the points $\left( {\frac{5}{2},1,\lambda } \right)$ and $\left( { - 2,0,1} \right)$ are at equal distance from the plane $2x + 3y - 6z + 7 = 0$. If ${\lambda _1} > {\lambda _2}$, then the distance of the point $\left( {{\lambda _1} - {\lambda _2},{\lambda _2},{\lambda _1}} \right)$ from the line $\frac{{x - 5}}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z + 7}}{2}$ is _____.
Solution:

Question: A plane P contains the line \[x + 2y + 3z + 1 = 0 = x - y - z - 6\], and is perpendicular to the plane \[ - 2x + y + z + 8 = 0.\] Then which of the following points lies on P?
Solution:
Equation of plane P can be assumed as

\[P:x + 2y + 3z + 1 + \lambda \left( {x - y - z - 6} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,P:\left( {1 + \lambda } \right)x + \left( {2 - \lambda } \right)y + \left( {3 - \lambda } \right)z + 1 - 6\lambda = 0\]
\[{\bar n_1} = \left( {1 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {2 - \lambda } \right)\hat j + \left( {3 - \lambda } \right)\hat k\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,{\bar n_1}.\,{\bar n_2} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,2\left( {1 + \lambda } \right) - \left( {2 - \lambda } \right) - \left( {3 - \lambda } \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,2 + 2\lambda - 2 + \lambda - 3 + \lambda = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda = \frac{3}{4}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,P:\frac{{7x}}{4} + \frac{{5y}}{4} + \frac{{9z}}{4} - \frac{{14}}{4} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,7x + 5y + 9z = 14\]
\[\left( {0,\,\,1,\,\,1} \right)\] lies on P

Question: The distance of line \[3y - 2z - 1 = 0 = 3x - z + 4\] from the point \[\left( {2, - 1,6} \right)\] is
Solution:
\[3y - 2z - 1 = 0 = 3x - z + 4\]
\[3y - 2z - 1 = 0\] \[D.R's \Rightarrow \left( {0,3, - 2} \right)\]
\[3x - z + 4 = 0\] \[D.R's \Rightarrow \left( {3,0, - 1} \right)\]
Let DR’s of given line are \[a,b,c\]
Now \[3b - 2c = 0\& 3a - c = 0\]
\[\therefore 6a = 3b = 2c\]
\[a:b:c = 3:6:9\]
putting \[x = -1\] in given equation we get  \[\left( {-1,1,1} \right)\]
 Since  \[\left( {-1,1,1} \right)\]   lie on line, hence any point on line can be
\[3K - 1,6K + 1,9K + 1\]
Now \[3\left( {3K - 1 - 2} \right) + 6\left( {6K + 1 + 1} \right) + 9\left( {9K + 1 - 6} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow K = \frac{1}{3}\]
Point on line \[ \Rightarrow \left( {0,3,4} \right)\]
Given point \[\left( {2, - 1,6} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \] Distance \[ = \sqrt {4 + 16 + 4} = 2\sqrt 6 \].

Question: The plane containing the line $\frac{{x - 3}}{2} = \frac{{y + 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 1}}{3}$ and also containing its projection on the plane 2x + 3y – z = 5, contains which one of the following points?
Solution:
Let normal to the required plane is $\vec n$
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \vec n{\text{ is perpendicular to both vector}}\,2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k{\text{ and }}2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k \\
   \Rightarrow \vec n = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&{ - 1}&3 \\
  2&3&{ - 1}
\end{array}} \right| = - 8\hat i + 8\hat j + 8\hat k \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{Equation of the required plane is}} \\
   \Rightarrow \left( {x - 3} \right)\left( { - 8} \right) + \left( {y + 2} \right) \times 8 + \left( {z - 1} \right) \times 8 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \left( {x - 3} \right)\left( { - 1} \right) + \left( {y + 2} \right) \times 1 + \left( {z - 1} \right) \times 1 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow x - 3 - y - 2 - z + 1 = 0 \\
  x - y - z{\text{ = 4 passes through (2, 0,}} - {\text{2)}} \\
  {\text{plane contains }}(2,{\text{ }}0, - 2). \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The equation of the plane passing through the line of intersection of the planes \[\overrightarrow r .\left( {\mathop i\limits^ \wedge   + \mathop j\limits^ \wedge   + \mathop k\limits^ \wedge  } \right) = 1\] and \[\overrightarrow r .\left( {2\mathop i\limits^ \wedge   + 3\mathop j\limits^ \wedge   - \mathop k\limits^ \wedge  } \right) + 4 = 0\] and parallel to the \[x\]-axis is :
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow r .\left( {\mathop i\limits^ \wedge + \mathop j\limits^ \wedge + \mathop k\limits^ \wedge } \right) - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow x + y + z - 1 = 0\]
and \[\overrightarrow r \times \left( {2\mathop i\limits^ \wedge + 3\mathop j\limits^ \wedge - \mathop k\limits^ \wedge } \right) + 4 = 2x + 3y - z + 4 = 0\]
equation of planes through line of intersection of these plane is :
\[\left( {x + y + z - 1} \right) + \lambda \left( {2x + 3y - z + 4} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {1 + 2\lambda } \right)x + \left( {1 + 3\lambda } \right)y + \left( {1 - \lambda } \right)z - 1 + 4\lambda = 0\]
But this plane is parallel to \[x\]–axis.
\[\therefore \left( {1 + 2\lambda } \right).1 + \left( {1 + 3\lambda } \right).0 + \left( {1 - \lambda } \right).0 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda = - \frac{1}{2}\]
\[\therefore \] Required equation of plane is
\[0.x + \left( {1 - \frac{3}{2}} \right)y + \left( {1 + \frac{1}{2}} \right)z - 1 + 4\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{ - y}}{2} + \frac{3}{2}z - 3 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow y - 3z + 6 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow r .\left( {\mathop j\limits^ \wedge - 3\mathop k\limits^ \wedge } \right) + 6 = 0\]

Question:
The shortest distance between the lines
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\left(\frac{1}{3} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\frac{8}{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$
and $\vec{r}=\left(-\frac{2}{3} \hat{i}-\frac{1}{3} \hat{k}\right)+\mu(\hat{j}-\hat{k}), \lambda, \mu \in \mathbb{R}$, is :
Solution:
$\quad \vec{a}_{1}=\frac{1}{3} \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\frac{8}{3} \hat{k}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{2}=\frac{-2}{3} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{1}{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}_{1}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}_{2}=\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}_{1} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 2 & -5 & 6 \\ 0 & 1 & -1\end{array}\right|=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\vec{a}_{1}-\vec{a}_{2}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$
S.D $=\left|\frac{\left(\vec{a}_{1}-\vec{a}_{2}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right|}\right|$
$S . D=\left|\frac{(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{3}\right|$
$=\frac{9}{3}=3$

Question: The plane through the intersection of the plane \[x + y + z = 1\;{\text{and}}\;2x + 3y - z + 4 = 0\] and parallel to y – axis also pass through the point:
Solution:
Equation of required plane is
\[\left( {x + y + z - 1} \right) + \lambda \left( {2x + 3y - z + 4} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;\left( {1 + 2\lambda } \right)x + \left( {1 + 3\lambda } \right)y + \left( {1 - \lambda } \right)z + \left( { - 1 + 4\lambda } \right) = 0\]
Since given plane is parallel to y – axis
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;3\lambda + 1 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\; = - \frac{1}{3}\]
Hence, equation of plane is \[x + 4z - 7 = 0\]

Question: Let the image of the point $\left( {\frac{5}{3},\frac{5}{3},\frac{8}{3}} \right)$ in the plane $x - 2y + z - 2 = 0$ be $P$. If the distance of the point $Q\left( {6, - 2,\alpha } \right),\alpha  > 0$, from $P$ is 13, then $\alpha $ is equal to _____.
Solution:
Image of point $\left( {\frac{5}{3},\frac{5}{3},\frac{8}{3}} \right)$
$\frac{{x - \frac{5}{3}}}{1} = \frac{{y - \frac{5}{3}}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - \frac{8}{3}}}{1} = \frac{{ - 2\left( {1 \times \frac{5}{3} + \left( { - 2} \right) \times \frac{8}{3} + 1 \times \frac{8}{3} - 2} \right)}}{{{1^2} + {2^2} + {1^2}}}$
$ = \frac{1}{3}$
$\therefore \,\,\,x = 2,y = 1,z = 3$
${13^2} = {\left( {6 - 2} \right)^2} + {\left( { - 2 - 1} \right)^2} + {\left( {\alpha - 3} \right)^2}$
$ \Rightarrow {\left( {\alpha - 3} \right)^2} = 144$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = 15\,\,\,\,\,\left( {\because \,\alpha > 0} \right)$

Question: Let the image of the point $P\left( {1,2,3} \right)$ in the plane $2x - y + z = 9$ be $Q$. If the coordinates of the point $R$ are $\left( {6,10,7} \right)$, then the square of the area of the triangle $PQR$ is _____.
Solution:
Let $Q\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$ be the image of P, about the plane $2x - y + z = 9$
$\frac{{\alpha - 1}}{2} = \frac{{\beta - 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{\gamma - 3}}{1} = 2$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = 5,\beta = 0,\gamma = 5$
Then area of triangle PQR is $ = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\overrightarrow {PQ} \times \overrightarrow {PR} } \right|$
$ = \left| { - 12\hat i - 3\hat j + 21\hat k} \right|$
$\begin{gathered}
   = \sqrt {144 + 9 + 441} \\
   = \sqrt {594} \\
\end{gathered} $
Square of area $ = 594$

Question: Let a line L passing through the point $(1,1,1)$ be perpendicular to both the vectors $2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$. If $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c})$ is the foot of perpendicular from the origin on the line L , then the value of $34(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})$ is :
Solution:
Direction vector of line is
$=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right|=-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& (2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda+1,2 \lambda+1) \\
\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{2}= & \frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{-3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
So equation of line is
$\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{-3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{2}=\lambda$ let
let ( $2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda+1,2 \lambda+1$ ) be the foot of perpendicular from $(0,0,0)$ upon given line
$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OM}} \cdot(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \mathrm{j}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=0$
$\Rightarrow 2(2 \lambda+1)-3(-3 \lambda+1)+2(2 \lambda+1)=0$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=-\frac{1}{17}$
So $(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}) \equiv\left(\frac{-2}{17}+1, \frac{3}{17}+1, \frac{-2}{17}+1\right)$
$\Rightarrow 34(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})=100$

Question: Let the plane \[ax + by + cz + d = 0\] bisect the line joining the points (4, –3, 1) and (2, 3, –5) at the right angles. If \[a,\,\,b,\,\,c,\,\,d\] are integers, then the minimum value of \[({a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2} + {d^2})\] is .................. .
Solution:
Plane is \[1\left( {x - 3} \right) - 3\left( {y - 0} \right) + 3\left( {z + 2} \right) = 0\]
\[x - 3y + 3z + 3 = 0\]
\[{\left( {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2} + {d^2}} \right)_{\min }} = 28\]

Question: If ${\lambda _1} < {\lambda _2}$ are two values of $\lambda $such that the angle between the planes ${P_1}:\vec r\left( {3\hat i - 5\hat j + \hat k} \right) = 7$ and ${P_2}:\vec r.\left( {\lambda \hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k} \right) = 9$ is ${\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2\sqrt 6 }}{5}} \right),$ then the square of the length of perpendicular from the point $\left( {38{\lambda _1},10{\lambda _2},2} \right)$ to the plane ${P_1}$ is _____.
Solution:
${P_1} = \vec r.\left( {3\hat i - 5\hat j + \hat k} \right) = 7$
${P_2} = \vec r.\left( {\lambda \hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k} \right) = 9$
$\theta = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2\sqrt 6 }}{5}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \sin \theta = \frac{{2\sqrt 6 }}{5}$
$\therefore \cos \theta = \frac{1}{5}$
$\cos \theta = \frac{{\vec r.\vec r}}{{\left| {{{\vec r}_1}} \right|\left| {{{\vec r}_2}} \right|}}$
$ = \frac{{\left( {3i - 5j + k} \right)\left( {\lambda i + j - 3k} \right)}}{{\sqrt {35} .\sqrt {{\lambda ^2} + 10} }}$
$\frac{1}{5} = \left| {\frac{{3\lambda - 8}}{{\sqrt {35} .\sqrt {{\lambda ^2} + 10} }}} \right|$
Square $ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{25}} = \frac{{9{\lambda ^2} + 64 - 48\lambda }}{{35\left( {{\lambda ^2} + 10} \right)}}$
$ \Rightarrow 19{\lambda ^2} - 120\lambda + 125 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 19{\lambda ^2} - 95\lambda - 25\lambda + 125 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow x = 5,\frac{{25}}{{19}}$

Perpendicular distance of point
$\left( {38{\lambda _1},10{\lambda _2},2} \right) \equiv \left( {50,50,2} \right)$ from plane ${P_1}$
$ = \frac{{\left| {3 \times 50 - 5 \times 50 + 2 \times 7} \right|}}{{\sqrt {35} }} = \frac{{105}}{{\sqrt {35} }}$
Square $ = \frac{{105 \times 105}}{{35}} = 315$

Question: The foot of perpendicular of the point $\left( {2,0,5} \right)$ on the line $\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 1}}{5} = \frac{{z + 1}}{{ - 1}}$ is $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$. Then which of the following is NOT correct?
Solution:
$L:\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 1}}{5} = \frac{{z + 1}}{{ - 1}} = \lambda \,\,\left( {{\text{let}}} \right)$

Let foot of perpendicular is
$P\left( {2\lambda - 1,5\lambda + 1, - \lambda - 1} \right)$
$\overrightarrow {PA} = \left( {3 - 2\lambda } \right)\hat i - \left( {5\lambda + 1} \right)\hat j + \left( {6 + \lambda } \right)\hat k$
Direction ratio of line $ \Rightarrow \vec b = 2\hat i + 5\hat j - \hat k$
Now, $ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow {PA} \cdot \vec b = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 2\left( {3 - 2\lambda } \right) - 5\left( {5\lambda + 1} \right) - \left( {6 + \lambda } \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{ - 1}}{6}$
$P\left( {2\lambda - 1,5\lambda + 1, - \lambda - 1} \right) \equiv P\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = 2\left( { - \frac{1}{6}} \right) - 1 = - \frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow \alpha = - \frac{4}{3}$
$ \Rightarrow \beta = 5\left( { - \frac{1}{6}} \right) + 1 = \frac{1}{6} \Rightarrow \beta = \frac{1}{6}$
$ \Rightarrow \gamma = - \lambda - 1 = \frac{1}{6} - 1 \Rightarrow \gamma = \frac{{ - 5}}{6}$
$\therefore $ Check options.

Question: If ${L_1}$ is the line of intersection of the plane $2x - 2y + 3z - 2 = 0,\,\,x - y + z + 1 = 0$ and ${L_2}$ is the line of intersection of the plane $x + 2y - z - 3 = 0,\,\,3x - y + 2z - 1 = 0,$ then the distance of the origin from the plane containing the lines L<sub>1</sub> and L<sub>2</sub> is :
Solution:
Given, ${P_1}:2x - 2y + 3z - 2 = 0$, ${P_2}:x - y + z + 1 = 0$
1
 ${L_1}:{\vec v_1} = {\vec n_1} \times {\vec n_2} = \left( {1,1,0} \right)$
Also given, ${{\text{P}}_3}:x + 2y - z - 3 = 0$, ${{\text{P}}_4}:3x - y + 2z - 1 = 0$
$\therefore $ Direction ratios for line${L_2}$
${L_2}:{\vec v_2} = {\vec n_3} \times {\vec n_4} = \left( {3, - 5, - 7} \right)$
Thus, direction ratios of the plane containing${L_1}$and${L_2}$
$\vec n = {\vec v_1} \times {\vec v_2} = \left( { - 7,7, - 8} \right)$

${\text{P}}: - 7\left( {x - 0} \right) + 7\left( {y - 5} \right) - 8\left( {z - 4} \right) = 0$
or ${\text{P}}:7x - 7y + 8z + 3 = 0$
Thus, distance from (0, 0, 0) is $d = \frac{1}{{3\sqrt 2 }}$

Question: Let the line \[\frac{{x - 3}}{7} = \;\frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 1}} = \;\frac{{z - 3}}{{ - 4}}\] intersect the plane containing the line \[\frac{{x - 4}}{1} = \;\frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 2}} = \;\frac{z}{1}\] and \[4ax - y + \;5z - \;7a\; = \;0\] \[ = \;2x - 5y - z - 3,\;a\; \in \;\mathbb{R}\] at the point \[P\;(\alpha ,\;\beta ,\;\gamma ).\] Then the value of \[\alpha  + \;\beta  + \;\gamma \] equals …………. .
Solution:
Equation of plane
\[4ax - y + \;5z - \;7a + \;\lambda \;(2x - \;5y - z - 3)\; = \;0\] this satisfy \[(4,\; - 1,\;0)\]
\[16a + \;1 - \;7a\; + \;\lambda \;(8 + \;5 - \;3)\; = \;0\]
\[9a\; + \;1 + \;10\lambda \; = \;0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)\]
Normal vector of the plane A is \[(4a + \;2\lambda ,\; - 1 - \;5\lambda ,\;5 - \;\lambda )\] vector along the line which contained the plane A is
\[i - \;2j\; + \;k\]
\[\therefore \;\;4a + \;2\lambda + \;2 + \;10\lambda \; + \;5 - \;\lambda = 0\]
\[11\lambda \; + \;4a\; + \;7\; = \;0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 2 \right)\]
Solve (1) and (2) to get \[a = \;1,\;\lambda = - \,1\]
Now equation of plane
\[x + \;2y + \;3z - \;2 = \;0\]
Let the point in the line \[\frac{{x - 3}}{7} = \;\frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 1}} = \;\frac{{z - 3}}{{ - \,4}} = \;t\] is \[(7t + 3,\; - t + \;2,\; - 4t + \;3)\] satisfy the equation of plane A
\[7t + \;3 - \;2t + \;4 + \;9 - \;12t - \;2 = \;0\]
\[t = \;2\]
So \[\alpha + \;\beta + \;\gamma = \;2t + \;8 = \;12\]

Question: If the length of the perpendicular from the point \[\left( {\beta ,\,0,\,\beta } \right)\,\,\left( {\beta  \ne 0} \right)\,\,{\text{to the line, }}\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{0} = \frac{{z + 1}}{{ - 1}}\,\,{\text{is }}\sqrt {\frac{3}{2}} ,\] \[{\text{then }}\beta \,\,\,{\text{is equal}}\,{\text{to:}}\]
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

Given, \[\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{0} = \frac{{z + 1}}{{ - 1}}\]= p (let) and point P (β, 0, β)
Any point on line A = (p, 1, – p – 1)
Now, DR of AP ≡ < p – β, 1 – 0, – p – 1 – β >
Which is perpendicular to line.
\[\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  {\therefore (p - \beta )1 + 0.1 - 1( - p - 1 - \beta ) = 0} \\
  { \Rightarrow p - \beta + p + 1 + \beta = 0 \Rightarrow p = \frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \\
  {\therefore {\text{Point }}A\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2},1, - \frac{1}{2}} \right)} \\
  {{\text{Given that distance }}AP = \sqrt {\frac{3}{2}} \Rightarrow A{P^2} = \frac{3}{2}} \\
  \begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow {\left( {\beta + \frac{1}{2}} \right)^2} + 1 + {\left( {\beta + \frac{1}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{3}{2} \\
   \Rightarrow 2{\left( {\beta + \frac{1}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{1}{2} \\
   \Rightarrow \beta + \frac{1}{2} = \pm \frac{1}{2} \\
\end{gathered} \\
  {\therefore \beta = 0,\, - 1} \\
  {{\text{But}}\,\,\beta \ne 0,\,\,\therefore \,\,\,\beta = - 1}
\end{array}\]

Question: Let N be the foot of perpendicular from the point P (1, –2, 3) on the line passing through the points (4, 5, 8) and (1, –7, 5). Then the distance of N from the plane 2x – 2y + z + 5 = 0 is
Solution:
Equation of line
$\frac{{x - 4}}{{4 - 1}} = \frac{{y - 5}}{{5 - \left( { - 7} \right)}} = \frac{{z - 8}}{{8 - 5}}$
$\frac{{x - 4}}{3} = \frac{{y - 5}}{{12}} = \frac{{z - 8}}{3}$
Let point $N\left( {3\lambda + 4,12\lambda + 5,3\lambda + 8} \right)$
$\overrightarrow {PN} = \left( {3\lambda + 4 - 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {12\lambda + 5 - \left( { - 2} \right)} \right)\hat j + \left( {3\lambda + 8 - 3} \right)\hat k$
$\overrightarrow {PN} = \left( {3\lambda + 3} \right)\hat i + \left( {12\lambda + 7} \right)\hat j + \left( {3\lambda + 5} \right)\hat k$
And parallel vector to line $\left( {{\text{say}}\,\,\vec a = 3i + 12\hat j + 3\hat k} \right)$
Now, $\overrightarrow {PN} \cdot \vec a = 0$
$\left( {3\lambda + 3} \right)3 + \left( {12\lambda + 7} \right)12 + \left( {3\lambda + 5} \right)3 = 0$
$162\lambda + 108 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{ - 108}}{{162}} = \frac{{ - 2}}{3}$
So point N is $\left( {2, - 3,6} \right)$
Now distance is $ = \left| {\frac{{2\left( 2 \right) - 2\left( { - 3} \right) + 6 + 5}}{{\sqrt {4 + 4 + 1} }}} \right| = 7$

Question: A plane bisects the line segment joining the points (1, 2, 3) and (– 3, 4, 5) at right angles. Then this plane also passes through the point:
Solution:
Since the plane bisects the line segment joining the points, then the plane must meet the line at the midpoint of the line which is \[\left( {\frac{{1 - 3}}{2},\frac{{2{\mathbf{ + }}4}}{2},\frac{{5{\mathbf{ + }}3}}{2}} \right){\mathbf{ = }}\left( {\frac{{ - 2}}{2},\frac{6}{2},\frac{8}{2}} \right){\mathbf{ = }}\left( { - 1,3,4} \right)\] (As the line is perpendicular to the plane)
Now, the direction cosines of the plane are \[\left( { - 3 - 1,\,\,4 - 2,\,\,5 - 3} \right) = \left( { - 4,\,\,2,\,\,2} \right)\]
So the Equation of the plane must be \[{\mathbf{ = }} - 4x{\mathbf{ + }}2y{\mathbf{ + }}2z{\mathbf{ = }}\lambda \]
and now since the midpoint of the line is lying in the plane, it must satisfy the plane
\[\therefore \,\,\, - 4\left( { - 1} \right){\mathbf{ + }}2\left( 3 \right){\mathbf{ + }}2\left( 4 \right){\mathbf{ = }}\lambda \Rightarrow \lambda {\mathbf{ = }}18\]
Therefore, equation of plane \[ \Rightarrow \,\, - 4x{\mathbf{ + }}2y{\mathbf{ + }}2z{\mathbf{ = }}18\]
Now, out of the given option only one point \[\left( { - 3,\,\,2,\,\,1} \right)\] is satisfying the plane as follows
\[ \Rightarrow \,\, - 4\left( { - 3} \right){\mathbf{ + }}2\left( 2 \right){\mathbf{ + }}2\left( 1 \right){\mathbf{ = }}18\]

Question: The plane passing through the line \[L:\;\ell \;x - y + \;3\;(1 - \;\ell )\;z = \;1,\;x + 2y - z = \;2\] and perpendicular to the plane \[3x + \;2y + z = \;6\] is \[3x - \;8y + 7z = \;4.\] If \[\theta \] is the acute angle between the line L and the y-axis, then 415 \[{\cos ^2}\theta \] is equal to ……………. .
Solution:
\[{\vec n_1} = \;\ell \hat i - \;\hat j + \;3\;(1 - \;\ell )\;\hat k\]
\[{\vec n_2} = \;\hat i + \;2\hat j - \;\hat k\]
Direction ratio of line \[ = \;\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  \ell &{ - 1}&{3\,(1 - \ell )} \\
  1&2&{ - \,1}
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[ = \;(6\ell - \;5)\;\hat i + \;(3 - 2\ell )\;\hat j + \;(2\ell + \;1)\;\hat k\]
\[3x - \;8y + \;7z = \;4\] will contain the line
\[(6\ell - \;5)\;\hat i + \;(3 - 2\ell )\;\hat j + \;(2\ell + \;1)\;\hat k\]
Normal of \[3x - 8y + \;7z = \;4\] will be perpendicular to the line
\[ = \;3\;(6\ell - \;5)\; + \;(3 - 2\ell )\;( - 8)\; + \;7\;(2\ell + \;1)\; = \;0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\ell = \;\frac{2}{3}\]
\[\therefore \] direction ratio of line \[\left( { - 1,\;\frac{5}{3},\;\frac{7}{3}} \right)\]
Angle with y-axis
\[\cos \theta = \;\frac{{5/3}}{{\sqrt {1 + \;\frac{{25}}{9} + \;\frac{{49}}{9}} }}\]
\[\cos \theta = \;\frac{5}{{\sqrt {83} }}\]
\[\therefore \;\;415\;{\cos ^2}\theta = \;\frac{{25}}{{83}}\; \times \;415 = \;125\]

Question:
Let A(x, y, z) be a point in xy-plane, which is equidistant from three points (0, 3, 2), (2, 0, 3) and (0, 0, 1).
Let B = (1, 4, –1) and C = (2, 0, –2). Then among the statements
(S1) : ∆ABC is an isosceles right angled triangle and 
(S2) : the area of ∆ABC is \[\frac{{9\sqrt 2 }}{2}.\]
Solution:
A(x,y,z) Let P (0,3,2), Q(2,0,3), R(0,0,1)
AP = AQ = AR
x2 + (y–3)2 + (z – 2)2 = (x – 2)2 + y2 + (z – 3)2 = x2 + y2 + (z – 1)2
In xy plane z = 0
So, x2 – 4x + 4 + y2 + 9 = x2 + y2 + 1
x = 3
9 + y2 – 6y + 9 + 4 = x2 + y2 + 1
So, A(3,2,0) also B(1,4,–1) & C(2,0,–2)
Now \[AB = \sqrt {4 + 4 + 1} = 3\]
     \[AC = \sqrt {1 + 4 + 4} = 3\]
     \[BC = \sqrt {1 + 16 + 1} = \sqrt {18} \]
AB = AC
isosceles ∆ & AB2 + AC2 = BC2 right angle ∆
Area of ∆ ABC = \[\frac{1}{2}\] × base. Height
\[\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 3 = \frac{9}{2}\]
So only S1 is true

Question: If an angle between the line, \[\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{1} = \frac{{z - 3}}{{ - 2}}\]and the plane, \[x - 2y - Kz = 3\]is \[{\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2\sqrt 2 }}{3}} \right)\]then a value of K is
Solution:
Let angle between line and plane is θ , then
\[\begin{gathered}
  \sin \theta = \left| {\frac{{\bar b.\bar n}}{{\left| {\bar b} \right|.\left| {\bar n} \right|}}} \right| \\
  \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \left| {\frac{{\left( {2\hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k} \right).\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j - K\hat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt 9 .\sqrt {1 + 4 + {K^2}} }}} \right| \\
  \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \left| {\frac{{2 - 2 + 2K}}{{3\sqrt {5 + {K^2}} }}} \right| = \frac{{2\left| K \right|}}{{3\sqrt {5 + {K^2}} }} \\
\end{gathered} \]
Since, \[\cos \theta = \frac{{2\sqrt 2 }}{3}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\sin \theta = \frac{1}{3}\]
Then, \[\frac{{2\left| K \right|}}{{3\sqrt {5 + {K^2}} }} = \frac{1}{3}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,4{K^2} = 5 + {K^2}\]
\[3{K^2} = 5\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,K = \pm \sqrt {\frac{5}{3}} \]

Question: The shortest distance between the lines $\frac{{x - 5}}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{2} = \frac{{z - 4}}{{ - 3}}$ and $\frac{{x + 3}}{1} = \frac{{y + 5}}{4} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 5}}$ is
Solution:
Shortest distance between two lines
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{x - {x_1}}}{{{a_1}}} = \frac{{y - {y_1}}}{{{a_2}}} = \frac{{z - {z_1}}}{{{a_3}}} \\
  \frac{{x - {x_2}}}{{{b_1}}} = \frac{{y - {y_2}}}{{{b_2}}} = \frac{{z - {z_2}}}{{{b_3}}} \\
\end{gathered} \]
is given as
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {{x_1} - {x_2}}&{{y_1} - {y_2}}&{{z_1} - {z_2}} \\
  {{a_1}}&{{a_2}}&{{a_3}} \\
  {{b_1}}&{{b_2}}&{{b_3}}
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {{a_1}{b_3} - {a_3}{b_2}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{a_1}{b_3} - {a_3}{b_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{a_1}{b_2} - {a_2}{b_1}} \right)}^2}} }} \\
  \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {5 - (3)}&{2 - ( - 5)}&{4 - 1} \\
  1&2&{ - 3} \\
  1&4&{ - 5}
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{( - 10 + 12)}^2} + {{( - 5 + 3)}^2} + {{(4 - 2)}^2}} }} \\
  \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  8&7&3 \\
  1&2&{ - 3} \\
  1&4&{ - 5}
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(2)}^2}} }} \\
   = \frac{{\left| {8( - 10 + 12) - 7( - 5 + 3) + 3(4 - 2)} \right|}}{{\sqrt {4 + 4 + 4} }} \\
   = \frac{{16 + 14 + 6}}{{\sqrt {12} }} = \frac{{36}}{{\sqrt {12} }} = \frac{{36}}{{2\sqrt 3 }} = \frac{{18}}{{\sqrt 3 }} = 6\sqrt 3 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question:
Let the line L be the projection of the line :
\[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{1} = \frac{{z - 4}}{2}\]
In the plane \[x - 2y - z = 3.\] If d is the distance of the point \[\left( {0,\,\,0,\,\,6} \right)\] from L, then \[{d^2}\] is equal to _____.
Solution:
\[{L_1}:\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{1} = \frac{{z - 4}}{2}\]
For foot of \[ \bot \] r of \[\left( {1,\,\,3,\,\,4} \right)\] on \[x - 2y - z - 3 = 0\]
\[\left( {1 + t} \right) - 2\left( {3 - 2t} \right) - \left( {4 - t} \right) - 3 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,t = 2\]
So foot of \[ \bot \] \[ = \left( {3,\,\, - 1,\,\,2} \right)\]
And point of intersection of \[{L_1}\] with plane is \[\left( { - 11,\,\, - 3,\,\, - 8} \right)\]
dr's of L is \[ < 14,\,\,2,\,\,10 > \, \cong < 7,\,\,1,\,\,5 > \]


\[d = AB\sin \theta = \left| {\frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  3&{ - 1}&{ - 4} \\
  7&1&5
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{7^2} + {1^2} + {5^2}} }}} \right|\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,d = \frac{{{1^2} + {{\left( {43} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {10} \right)}^2}}}{{49 + 1 + 25}} = 26\]

Question: Let the position vectors of two points P and Q be $3\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k$ and $\hat i + 2\hat j - 4\hat k$, respectively. Let R and S be two points such that the direction ratios of lines PR and QS are \[\left( {4,\,\, - 1,\,\,2} \right)\] and \[\left( { - 2,\,\,1,\,\, - 2} \right)\], respectively. Let lines PR and QS intersect at T. If the vector $\overline {{\text{TA}}} $ is perpendicular to both $\overline {{\text{PR}}} $ and $\overline {{\text{QS}}} $ and the length of vector $\overline {{\text{TA}}} $ is $\sqrt 5 $ units, then the modulus of a position vector of A is
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

\[P\left( {3,\,\, - 1,\,\,2} \right)\]
\[Q\left( {1,\,\,2,\,\, - 4} \right)\]
\[\overline {PR} \parallel 4\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k\]
\[\overline {QS} \parallel - 2\hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k\]
Dr’s of normal to the plane containing P, T and Q will be proportional to
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  4&{ - 1}&2 \\
  { - 2}&1&{ - 2}
\end{array}} \right|\]

\[\therefore \,\,\,\frac{l}{0} = \frac{m}{4} = \frac{n}{2}\]
For point, \[T:\overline {PT} = \frac{{x - 3}}{4} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 2}}{2} = \lambda \]
\[\overline {QT} = \frac{{x - 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{y - 2}}{1} = \frac{{z + 4}}{{ - 2}} = \mu \]
\[T:\left( {4\lambda + 3,\,\, - \lambda - 1,\,\,2\lambda + 2} \right)\]
\[ \cong \left( { - 2\mu + 1,\,\,\mu + 2,\,\, - 2\mu - 4} \right)\]
\[4\lambda + 3 = - 2\mu + 1\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,2\lambda + \mu = - 1\]
\[\lambda + \mu = - 3\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda = 2\]
And \[\mu = - 5\]
So point \[T:\left( {11,\,\, - 3,\,\,6} \right)\]
\[\overline {OA} = \left( {11\hat i - 3\hat j + 6\hat k} \right) \pm \left( {\frac{{2\hat j + \hat k}}{{\sqrt 5 }}} \right)\sqrt 5 \]
\[\overline {OA} = \left( {11\hat i - 3\hat j + 6\hat k} \right) \pm \left( {2\hat j + \hat k} \right)\]
\[\overline {OA} = 11i - \hat j + 7\hat k\]
Or \[9\hat i - 5\hat j + 5\hat k\]
\[\left| {\overline {OA} } \right| = \sqrt {121 + 1 + 49} = \sqrt {171} \]

Question: The distance of the point $P\left( {4,6, - 2} \right)$ from the line passing through the point $\left( { - 3,2,3} \right)$ and parallel to a line with direction ratios $3,3, - 1$ is equal to:
Solution:
Equation of line is $\frac{{x + 3}}{3} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{{ - 1}} = \lambda $
$M\left( {3\lambda - 3,3\lambda + 2,3 - \lambda } \right)$
D.R. of $PM\left( {3\lambda - 7,3\lambda - 4,5 - \lambda } \right)$
Since $PM$ is perpendicular to line
$ \Rightarrow 3\left( {3\lambda - 7} \right) + 3\left( {3\lambda - 4} \right) - 1\left( {5 - \lambda } \right) = 0$

$ \Rightarrow M\left( {3,8,1} \right) \Rightarrow PM = \sqrt {14} $

Question: A plane P meets the coordinate axes at A, B and C respectively. The centroid of \[\Delta \] ABC is given to be (1, 1, 2). Then the equation of the line through this centroid and perpendicular to the plane P is:
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  G = \left( {\frac{\alpha }{3},\frac{\beta }{3},\frac{\gamma }{3}} \right) = \left( {1,1,2} \right) \\
  \alpha = 3,\beta = 3,\gamma = 6 \\
\end{gathered} \]
Equation of plane is
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{x}{\alpha } + \frac{y}{\beta } + \frac{z}{\gamma } = 1 \\
  \frac{x}{3} + \frac{y}{3} + \frac{z}{6} = 1 \\
  2x + 2y + z = 6 \\
\end{gathered} \]
Required line perpendicular to plane is \[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{1}\].

Question:
Let $d$ be the distance between the foot of perpendiculars of the points 
P (1, 2 – 1) and Q (2, – 1, 3) on the plane  $ - x + y + z = 1$. Then \[{d^2}\] is equal to _______
Solution:
Points P(1, 2, –1) and Q (2, –1, 3) lie on same side of the plane.
Perpendicular distance of point P from plane is
\[\left| {\frac{{ - 1 + 2 - 1 - 1}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {1^2}} }}} \right| = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]
Perpendicular distance of point Q from plane is
\[ = \left| {\frac{{ - 2 - 1 + 3 - 1}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {1^2}} }}} \right| = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;\overrightarrow {PQ} \]is parallel to given plane. So, distance between P and Q = distance between their foot of perpendiculars.
\[ \Rightarrow \left| {\overrightarrow {PQ} } \right| = \sqrt {{{(1 - 2)}^2} + {{(2 + 1)}^2}{{( - 1 - 3)}^2}} \]
\[ = \sqrt {26} \]
\[{\left| {\overrightarrow {PQ} } \right|^2} = 26 = {d^2}\]
Alternate:
\[ - x + y + z - 1 = 0\]

\[M\;({x_1},{y_1},{z_1})\]
\[\frac{{{x_1} - 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{{y_1} - 2}}{1} = \frac{{{z_1} + 1}}{1} = \frac{1}{3}\]
\[{x_1} = \frac{2}{3},{y_1} = \frac{7}{3},{z_1} = \frac{{ - 2}}{3}\]
\[M\;\left( {\frac{2}{3},\frac{7}{3},\frac{{ - 2}}{3}} \right)\]
\[N\,\,\left( {{x_2},{y_2},{z_2}} \right)\]
\[\frac{{{x_2} - 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{{y_2} + 1}}{1} = \frac{{{z_2} - 3}}{1} = \frac{1}{3}\]
\[{x_2} = \frac{5}{3},{y_2} = \frac{{ - 2}}{3},{z_2} = \frac{{10}}{3}\]
\[N = \left( {\frac{5}{3},\frac{{ - 2}}{3},\frac{{10}}{3}} \right)\]
\[{d^2} = {1^2} + {3^2} + {4^2} = 26\]

Question: Let a unit vector $\widehat {OP}$ make angle $\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma $ with the positive directions of the co-ordinate axes $OX,OY,OZ$ respectively, where $\beta \in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right)\widehat {OP}$ is perpendicular to the plane through points $\left( {1,2,3} \right),\left( {2,3,4} \right)$ and $\left( {1,5,7} \right),$ then which one of the following is true?
Solution:
Equation of plane:-
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - {x_1}}&{y - {y_1}}&{z - {z_1}} \\
  {{x_2} - {x_1}}&{{y_2} - {y_1}}&{{z_2} - {z_1}} \\
  {{x_3} - {x_1}}&{{y_3} - {y_1}}&{{z_3} - {z_1}}
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 1}&{y - 2}&{z - 3} \\
  1&1&1 \\
  0&3&4
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \left[ {x - 1} \right] - 4\left[ {y - 2} \right] + 3\left[ {z - 3} \right] = 0$
$ \Rightarrow x - 4y + 3z = 2$
D.R’s of normal of plane $ < 1, - 4,3 > $
D.C’s of $\left( { \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt {26} }}, \mp \frac{4}{{\sqrt {26} }}, \pm \frac{3}{{\sqrt {26} }}} \right)$
$\cos \beta \frac{4}{{\sqrt {26} }}$
$\cos \alpha = \frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt {26} }}\,\,\,\,\frac{\pi }{2} < \alpha < \pi $
$\cos \gamma = \frac{{ - 3}}{{\sqrt {26} }}\,\,\frac{\pi }{2} < \gamma < \pi $

Question:
Let A be a point on the line
$\vec r = \left( {1 - 3\mu } \right)\hat i + \left( {\mu - 1} \right)\hat j + \left( {2 + 5\mu } \right)\hat k$ and B(3, 2, 6) be a point in the space. Then the value of μ for which the vector $\overrightarrow {AB} $ is parallel to the plane $x - 4y + 3z = 1$ is
Solution:
$\because $ A be a point on given line
$\therefore $ position vector of A
$\overrightarrow {OA} = \vec r = \left( {1 - 3\mu } \right)\hat i + \left( {\mu - 1} \right)\hat j + \left( {2 + 5\mu } \right)\hat k$
Position vector of \[B = \overrightarrow {OB} = 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k\]
$\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \,\,\,\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} \\
   = \left( {3\mu + 2} \right)\hat i + \left( {3 - \mu } \right)\hat j + \left( {4 - 5\mu } \right)\hat k \\
\end{gathered} $
Equation of plane is: $x - 4y + 3z = 1$
$\because \overrightarrow {AB} $ is parallel to this plane
$\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \,\,1\left( {3\mu + 2} \right) - 4\left( {3 - \mu } \right) + 3\left( {4 - 5\mu } \right) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow 3\mu + 2 - 12 + 4\mu + 12 - 15\mu = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow 2 - 8\mu = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \mu = \frac{1}{4} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: If the equation of plane passing through the mirror image of a point (2, 3, 1) with respect to line \[\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \;\frac{{y - 3}}{1} = \;\frac{{z + 2}}{{ - 1}}\] and containing the line \[\frac{{x - \;2}}{3} = \;\frac{{1 - y}}{2} = \;\frac{{z + 1}}{1}\] is \[\alpha x + \;\beta y + \gamma z = \;24,\] then \[\alpha  + \beta  + \;\gamma \] is equal to :
Solution:
Let the foot of perpendicular from the point \[A\left( {2,3,1} \right)\] on the line \[\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{1} = \frac{{z + 2}}{{ - 1}}\] is \[M\left( {2\lambda - 1,\,\,\lambda + 3,\,\, - \lambda - 2} \right)\]
D.R.’s of line \[AM\] are \[2\lambda - 3,\,\,\lambda ,\,\, - \lambda - 3\]
\[AM \bot \,\,\,{\text{line}}\,\,\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{1} = \frac{{z + 2}}{{ - 1}}\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,2\left( {2\lambda - 3} \right) + 1\left( \lambda \right) - 1\left( { - \lambda - 3} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,6\lambda = 3,\,\,\lambda = \frac{1}{2}\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,M \equiv \left( {0,\,\,\frac{7}{2},\,\, - \frac{5}{2}} \right)\]
Let mirror image of point \[A\left( {2,3,1} \right)\] with respect to the line \[\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{1} = \frac{{z + 2}}{{ - 1}}\] is \[B\,.\]
\[M\] is mid-point of \[AB\,.\]
\[B \equiv 2\left( {0,\,\,\frac{7}{2},\,\, - \frac{5}{2}} \right) - \left( {2,3,1} \right) \equiv \left( { - 2,\,\,4,\,\, - 6} \right)\]
Now the equation of the plane passing through \[B\left( { - 2,\,\,4,\,\, - 6} \right)\] and also containing the line \[\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{1 - y}}{2} = \frac{{z + 1}}{1}\] i.e. \[\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{y - 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z + 1}}{1}\] is
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 2}&{y - 1}&{z + 1} \\
  { - 2 - 2}&{4 - 1}&{ - 6 + 1} \\
  3&{ - 2}&1
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 2}&{y - 1}&{z + 1} \\
  { - 4}&3&{ - 5} \\
  3&{ - 2}&1
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
\[ - 7\left( {x - 2} \right) - 11\left( {y - 1} \right) - \left( {z + 1} \right) = 0\]
\[ - 7x - 11y - z + 24 = 0\]
\[7x + 11y + z = 24\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,\alpha = 7,\,\,\beta = 11,\,\,\gamma = 1\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 19\]

Question: The distance of the point (1, - 2, 4) from the plane passing through the point (1, 2, 2) and perpendicular to the planes $x - y + 2z = 3$ and $2x - 2y + z + 12 = 0$, is
Solution:
Equation of plane$ \bot $ to the planes $x - y + 2z = 3\& 2x - 2y + z + 12 = 0$ and passes through (1, 2, 2) is
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 1}&{y - 2}&{z - 2} \\
  1&{ - 1}&2 \\
  2&{ - 2}&1
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$3\left( {x - 1} \right) + 3\left( {y - 2} \right) = 0$
$x + y = 3$ …(i)
distance of plane $x + y - 3 = 0$ from (1, -2, 4) is $ = \left| {\frac{{1 - 2 - 3}}{{\sqrt {1 + 1} }}} \right| = 2\sqrt 2 $

Question: The equation of the plane containing the straight line \[\frac{x}{2} = \frac{y}{3} = \frac{z}{4}\] and perpendicular to the plane containing the straight lines \[\frac{x}{3} = \frac{y}{4} = \frac{z}{2}\,{\text{and }}\frac{x}{4} = \frac{y}{2} = \frac{z}{3}\,\,{\text{is:}}\]
Solution:
Let the direction ratios of the plane containing lines,
\[\begin{gathered}
  \,\,\,\,\frac{x}{3} = \frac{y}{4} = \frac{z}{2}\,\,and{\text{ }}\frac{x}{4} = \frac{y}{2} = \frac{z}{3}\,\,is{\text{ }} < a,\,\,b,\,\,c > \\
  \therefore \,\,3a + 4b + 2c = 0 \\
  \,\,\,\,\,4a + 2b + 3c = 0 \\
  \therefore \,\,\,\frac{a}{{12 - 4}} = \frac{b}{{8 - 9}} = \frac{c}{{6 - 16}} \\
  \,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{a}{8} = \frac{b}{{ - 1}} = \frac{c}{{ - 10}} \\
\end{gathered} \]
∴ Direction ratio of plane= \[ < - 8,\,1,\,10 > \]
Let the direction ratio of required plane is \[ < l,\,m,\,n > \]
Then \[ - 8l + m + 10n = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 1 \right)\]
and \[2l + 3m + 4n = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)\]
From (1) and (2),
\[\frac{l}{{ - 26}} = \frac{m}{{52}} = \frac{n}{{ - 26}}\]
∴ D.R.s are \[ < 1,\, - 2,\,1 > \]
∴ Equation of plane: \[x - 2y + z = 0\]

Question: Let the plane P contain the line $2x + y - z - 3 = 0 = 4x - 3y + 4z + 9$ and be parallel to the line $\frac{{x + 2}}{2} = \frac{{3 - y}}{{ - 4}} = \frac{{3 - y}}{{ - 4}} = \frac{{z - 7}}{5}$. Then the distance of the point $A\left( {8, - 1, - 19} \right)$ from the plane P measured parallel to the line $\frac{x}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y - 5}}{4} = \frac{{2 - z}}{{ - 12}}$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
Plane $P \equiv {P_1} + \lambda {P_2} = 0$
$\left( {2x + y - z - 3} \right) + \lambda \left( {5x - 3y} \right) + 4z + 9 = 0$
$\left( {5\lambda + 2} \right)x + \left( {1 - 3\lambda } \right)y + \left( {4\lambda - 1} \right)z + 9\lambda - 3 = 0$
$\vec n.\,\vec b = 0$ where $\vec b\left( {2,4,5} \right)$
$2\left( {5\lambda + 2} \right) + 4\left( {1 - 3\lambda } \right) + 5\left( {4\lambda - 1} \right) = 0$
$\lambda = - \frac{1}{6}$
Plane $7x + 9y - 10z - 27 = 0$

Equation of line AB is
$\frac{{x - 8}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y + 1}}{4} = \frac{{z + 19}}{{12}} = \lambda $
Let $B = \left( {8 - 3\lambda , - 1 + 4\lambda , - 19 + 12\lambda } \right)$ lies on plane P
$\therefore \,\,7\left( {8 - 3\lambda } \right) + 9\left( {4\lambda - 1} \right) - 10\left( {12\lambda - 19} \right) = 27$
$\lambda = 2$
$\therefore \,\,$Point $B = \left( {2,7,5} \right)$
$AB = \sqrt {{6^2} + {8^2} + {{24}^2}} = 26$

Question:
If the shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \;\frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \;\frac{{z - 3}}{\lambda }\] and
\[\frac{{x - 2}}{1} = \;\frac{{y - 4}}{4} = \;\frac{{z - 5}}{5}\] is \[\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }},\] then the sum of all possible values of \[\lambda \] is :
Solution:
SHORTEST distance \[\frac{{\left| {({a_2} - {a_1})\; \cdot \;({b_1} \times \;{b_2})} \right|}}{{\left| {{b_1} \times \;{b_2}} \right|}}\]
\[{a_1} = \;(1,\;2,\;3)\]
\[{a_2} = \;(2,\;4,\;5)\]
\[{\vec b_2} = \;2\hat i + \;3\hat j + \;\lambda \hat k\]
\[{\vec b_2} = \;\hat i\; + \;4\hat j + \;5\hat k\]
\[S.D. = \;\frac{{\left| {((2 - 1)\;\hat i\; + \;(4 - 2)\;\hat j + \;(5 - 3)\;\hat k)\; \cdot \;({{\vec b}_1} \times \;{{\vec b}_2})} \right|}}{{|{b_1} \times \;{b_2}|}}\]
\[{\vec b_1} \times \;{\vec b_2}\; = \;\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&3&\lambda \\
  1&4&5
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[ = \;\hat i\;(15 - \;4\lambda )\; + \;\hat j\;(\lambda - \;10)\; + \;\hat k\;(5)\]
\[ = \;(15 - 4\lambda )\;\hat i + \;(\lambda - 10)\;\hat j + \;5\hat k\]
\[\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times \;{{\vec b}_2}} \right| = \;\sqrt {{{(15 - 4\lambda )}^2} + \;{{(\lambda - 10)}^2} + \;25} \]
Now
S.D. = \[\frac{{\left| {(\hat i + \;2\hat j + \;2\hat k)\; \cdot \;\left[ {(15 - 4\lambda ) + \;\hat i\;(\lambda - 10)\,\hat j + \;5\hat k} \right]} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{(15 - 4\lambda )}^2} + \;{{(\lambda - 10)}^2} + \;25} }}\]
\[\frac{{|15 - 4\lambda + 2\lambda - 20 + 10|}}{{\sqrt {{{(15 - 4\lambda )}^2} + \;{{(\lambda - 10)}^2} + \;25} }} = \;\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]
square both side
\[3\;{(5 - 2\lambda )^2} = \;225 + \;16{\lambda ^2} - 120\lambda + \;{\lambda ^2} + \;100 - 20\lambda + \;25\]
\[12{\lambda ^2} + \;75 - \;60\lambda = \;17{\lambda ^2} - \;140\lambda + \;350\]
\[5{\lambda ^2} - 80\lambda + \;275 = \;0\]
\[{\lambda ^2} - 16\lambda + \;55 = \;0\]
\[(\lambda - 5)\;(\lambda - 11)\; = \;0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\lambda = \;5,\;11\]
(a) is correct option.

Question: Let in a ∆ABC, the length of the side AC be 6, the vertex B be (1, 2, 3) and the vertices A, C lie on the line \[\frac{{x - 6}}{3} = \frac{{y - 7}}{2} = \frac{{z - 7}}{{ - 2}}\]. Then the area (in sq. units) of ∆ABC is
Solution:
Let M (3 λ + 6, 2 λ + 7, – 2λ + 7)
\[\overrightarrow {BM} = (3\lambda + 5)\hat k + (2\lambda + 5)\hat j + 2( - 2\lambda + 4)\hat k\]
\[\overrightarrow {AC} .\overrightarrow {BM} = 0 = 3(3\lambda + 5) + 2(2\lambda + 5) - 2( - 2\lambda + 4)\]
\[\overrightarrow {BM} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k\]
\[|\overrightarrow {BM} | = 7\]
\[Area = \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 7 = 21\]

Question: The equation of the line passing through (–4, 3, 1) parallel to the plane x + 2y – z – 5 = 0 and intersecting the line \[\frac{{x + 1}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y - 3}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{{ - 1}}\] is:
Solution:
Let any point on the intersecting line,
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{x + 1}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y - 3}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{{ - 1}} = \lambda \,\,\,\left( {{\text{say}}} \right) \\
  {\text{is }}\left( { - 3\lambda - 1,\,\,2\lambda + 3,\,\, - \lambda + 2} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]
Since, the above point lies on a line which passes through the point (–4, 3, 1)
Then, direction ratio of the required line
<- 3λ - 1 + 4, 2λ + 3 – 3, -λ + 2 - 1>
or <- 3λ + 3, 2λ, -λ + 1>
Since, line is parallel to the plane
x + 2y-z – 5 = 0
Then, perpendicular vector to the line is \[\hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k\]
Now (-3λ + 3)(1) + (2λ)(2) + (-λ +1)(- 1) = 0
⇒ λ = –1
Now direction ratio of the required line = < 6, - 2, 2 > or <3, - 1, 1>
Hence required equation of the line is
\[\frac{{\left( {x + 4} \right)}}{3} = \frac{{y - 3}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 1}}{1}\]

Question: The shortest distance between lines ${L_1}$ and ${L_2}$, where ${L_1}:\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{z + 4}}{2}$ and ${L_2}$ is the line, passing through the points $A\left( { - 4,4,3} \right),B\left( { - 1,6,3} \right)$ and perpendicular to the line $\frac{{x - 3}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{y}{3} = \frac{{z - 1}}{1}$, is
Solution:
${L_2} = \frac{{x + 4}}{3} = \frac{{y - 4}}{2} = \frac{{z - 3}}{0}$
$\therefore \,\,$S.D. $ = \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {{x_2} - {x_1}}&{{y_2} - {y_1}}&{{z_2} - {z_1}} \\
  2&{ - 3}&2 \\
  3&2&0
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\left| {{{\vec n}_1} \times {{\vec n}_2}} \right|}}$
$ = \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  5&{ - 5}&{ - 7} \\
  2&{ - 3}&2 \\
  3&2&0
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\left| {{{\vec n}_1} \times {{\vec n}_2}} \right|}}$
$ = \frac{{141}}{{\left| { - 4\hat i + 6\hat j + 13\hat k} \right|}}$
$ = \frac{{141}}{{\sqrt {16 + 36 + 169} }}$
$ = \frac{{141}}{{\sqrt {221} }}$

Question: If the equation of the line passing through the point \[\left( {0, - \frac{1}{2},0} \right)\]and perpendicular to the lines \[\vec r = \lambda \left( {\hat i + a\hat j + b\hat k} \right)\;and\] \[\;\vec r = \left( {\hat i - \hat j - 6\hat k} \right) + \mu \left( { - b\hat i + a\hat j + 5\hat k} \right)\] is \[\frac{{x - 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{y + 4}}{d} = \frac{{z - c}}{{ - 4}},\]then a + b+ c+ d is equal to :
Solution:
\[Line\;is\;{ \bot ^r}\;to\;2\;line\; \Rightarrow \]line will be parallel to
\[\left( {i + a\hat j + b\hat k} \right) \times \left( { - b\hat i + a\hat j + 5\hat k} \right)\]
Parallel vector along the required line is
\[\hat i\left( {5a - ab} \right) - \hat j\left( {{b^2} + 5} \right) + \hat k\left( {a + ab} \right)\]
Dr’s of required line α (5a –ab), – (b2 + 5), (a + ab)
Also Dr’s of required line α –2, d, –4
\[\therefore \;\frac{{5a - ab}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{ - \left( {{b^2} + 5} \right)}}{d} = \frac{{a + ab}}{{ - 4}}\;\;\;\;....\left( 1 \right)\]
\[Also\;{\text{point}}\;\left( {0,\;\frac{{ - 1}}{2},0} \right)\;will\;lie\;on\;\frac{{x - 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{y + 4}}{d} = \frac{{z - c}}{{ - 4}}\]
\[\frac{{0 - 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{\frac{{ - 1}}{2} + 4}}{d} = \frac{{0 - c}}{{ - 4}} \Rightarrow \;d = 7,\;c = 2\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  From\;\left( 1 \right)\;\frac{{5a - ab}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{ - {b^2} - 5}}{7} = \frac{{a + ab}}{{ - 4}} \\
  \frac{{5a - ab}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{a + ab}}{{ - 4}};\;\frac{{ - {b^2} - 5}}{7} = \frac{{a + ab}}{{ - 4}} \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
   - 20a + 4ab = - 2a - 2ab\;\;\;\;\;4{b^2} + 20 = 7a + 7ab \\
  18a = 6ab\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;36 + 20 = 7a + 21a \\
  b = 3\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;56 = 28a \Rightarrow \;a = 2 \\
\end{gathered} \]
a + b + c + d = 2 + 3 + 2 + 7 = 14

Question: If the image of the point P(1, 0, 3) in the line joining the points A(4, 7, 1) and B(3, 5, 3) is Q \[(\alpha ,{\text{ }}\beta ,{\text{ }}\gamma )\], then \[\alpha  + \beta  + \gamma \] is equal to
Solution:
P (1, 0, 3)
A(4, 7, 1), B(3, 5, 3)
Line AB \[ \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y - 5}}{2} = \frac{{z - 3}}{{ - 2}} = \lambda \]
Let foot of perpendicular of P on AB be
\[R \equiv \left( {\lambda + 3,2\lambda + 5, - 2\lambda + 3} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {\lambda + 3 - 1} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {2\lambda + 5 - 0} \right)\left( 2 \right) + \left( { - 2\lambda + 3 - 3} \right)\]
\[\left( { - 2} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda + 2 + 4\lambda + 10 + 4\lambda = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda = - \frac{4}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow R \equiv \left( {\frac{5}{3},\frac{7}{3},\frac{{17}}{3}} \right)\]
\[Q \equiv \left( {\frac{{10}}{3} - 1,\frac{{14}}{3} - 0,\frac{{34}}{3} - 3} \right) \equiv \left( {\frac{7}{3},\frac{{14}}{3},\frac{{25}}{3}} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \alpha + \beta + \gamma = \frac{{7 + 14 + 25}}{3} = \frac{{46}}{3}\]

Question:
Let \[L_1:\left( \frac{x-1}{3}, \frac{y-1}{-1}, \frac{z+1}{0} \right)\]
 and \[L_2:\left( \frac{x-2}{2}, \frac{y}{0}, \frac{z+4}{\alpha} \right)\] , \[\alpha \in R\] be two lines, which intersect at the point B. If P is the foot of perpendicular from the point A(1, 1, –1) on L2, then the value of 26 \[\alpha \] (PB)2 is ____.
Solution:
Point B
\[\left( {3\lambda + 1, - \lambda + 1, - 1} \right) \equiv \left( {2\mu + 2,0,\alpha \mu - 4} \right)\]
\[3\lambda + 1 = 2\mu + 2\]
\[ - \lambda + 1 = 0\]
\[ - 1 = \alpha \mu - 4\]
\[\lambda = 1,\mu = 1,\alpha = 3\]
\[B\left( {4,0, - 1} \right)\]
Let Point ‘P’ is \[\left( {2\delta + 2,0,3\delta - 4} \right)\]
Dr’s of AP \[ < 2\delta + 1, - 1,3\delta - 3 > \]
\[AP \bot {L_2} \Rightarrow \delta = \frac{7}{{13}}\]
\[P\left( {\frac{{40}}{{13}},0,\frac{{ - 31}}{{13}}} \right)\]
\[2\sigma \delta {\left( {PB} \right)^2} = 26 \times 3 \times \left( {\frac{{144}}{{169}} + \frac{{324}}{{169}}} \right) = 216\]

Question: The equation of the plane passing through the point \[\left( {1,\,\,2,\,\, - 3} \right)\]  and perpendicular to the planes \[3x + y - 2z = 5\] and \[2x - 5y - z = 7,\] is
Solution:
Normal vector of required plane is $\bar n = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\widehat i}&{\widehat j}&{\widehat k} \\
  3&1&{ - 2} \\
  2&{ - 5}&{ - 1}
\end{array}} \right| = - 11\widehat i - \widehat j - 17\widehat k$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,11\left( {x - 1} \right) + \left( {y - 2} \right) + 17\left( {z + 3} \right) = 0$
$11x + y + 17z + 38 = 0$

Question: Let $PQR$ be a triangle with $R\left( { - 1,4,2} \right)$. Suppose $M\left( {2,1,2} \right)$ is the mid point of $PQ$. The distance of the centroid of $\Delta PQR$ from the point of intersection of the lines $\frac{{x - 2}}{0} = \frac{y}{2} = \frac{{z + 3}}{{ - 1}}$ and $\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{z + 1}}{1}$ is
Solution:
Centroid G divides MR in $1:2$
$G\left( {1,2,2} \right)$
Point of intersection A of given lines is $\left( {2, - 6,0} \right)$
\[AG = \sqrt {69} \]

Question: Let Q be the mirror image of the point \[P\;(1,\;2,\;1)\] with respect to the plane \[x + \;2y + \;2z = \;16.\] Let T be a plane passing through the point Q and contains the line \[\vec r\; =  - \;\hat k\; + \;\lambda \;\left( {\hat i\; + \;\hat j + \;2\hat k} \right),\;\,\lambda \; \in \;R.\] Then, which of the following points lies on T ?
Solution:
Image of \[P\;(1,\;2,\;1)\] in \[x + \;2y + \;2z - \;16 = \;0\] is given by \[Q\;(4,\;8,\;7)\]
Eq. of plane \[T\; = \;\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  x&y&{z + \;1} \\
  4&8&6 \\
  1&1&2
\end{array}} \right|\; = \;0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;2x - \;z = \;1\;\] so \[B\;(1,\;2,\;1)\] lies on it.

Question: The angle between the lines whose direction ratios satisfy the equations $l + m + n = 0$ and ${l^2} = {m^2} + {n^2}$ is
Solution:
Given direction ratios satisfy the equations $l + m + n = 0$ and ${l^2} = {m^2} + {n^2}$
Substituting $l = - \left( {m + n} \right)$ in the equation ${l^2} = {m^2} + {n^2}$
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow {\left( { - \left( {m + n} \right)} \right)^2} = {m^2} + {n^2} \\
   \Rightarrow {m^2} + {n^2} + 2mn = {m^2} + {n^2} \\
   \Rightarrow mn = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $
$m = 0$ or $n = 0$
If $m = 0$
$ \Rightarrow l + n = 0$ or $l = - n$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{l}{1} = \frac{m}{0} = \frac{n}{{ - 1}}$
If $n = 0,$
$ \Rightarrow m + l = 0$ or $m = - 1$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{l}{1} = \frac{m}{{ - 1}} = \frac{n}{0}$
$\therefore $ Direction cosines of the lines are $\left\langle {1,0, - 1} \right\rangle $ and $\left\langle {1, - 1,0} \right\rangle $
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{1{\kern 1pt} \times 1 + 0 \times \left( { - 1} \right) + \left( { - 1} \right) \times 0}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2}} \sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2}} }}} \right| \\
   \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 \sqrt 2 }} \\
   \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{1}{2} \\
  \therefore \,\,\theta = \frac{\pi }{3} \\
\end{gathered} $
The angle between the lines is $\frac{\pi }{3}$.

Question: Let P be the foot of the perpendicular from the point Q(10, –3, –1) on the line \[\frac{{x - 3}}{7} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + 1}}{{ - 2}}\]. Then the area of the right angled triangle PQR, where R is the point (3, –2, 1), is
Solution:
\[\frac{{x - 3}}{7} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + 1}}{{ - 2}} = \lambda \]
\[ \Rightarrow 7\lambda + 3, - \lambda + 2, - 2\lambda - 1\]
dr’s of \[QP \Rightarrow \]\[7\lambda - 7, - \lambda + 5, - 2\lambda \]
Now
\[\left( {7\lambda - 7} \right).7 - \left( { - \lambda + 5} \right) + \left( {2\lambda } \right).2 = 0\]
\[54\lambda - 54 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1\]
\[\therefore P = \left( {10,1, - 3} \right)\]
\[\overrightarrow {PQ} = - 4\widehat j + 2\widehat k\]
\[\overrightarrow {PR} = - 7i - 3\widehat j + 4\widehat k\]
\[Area = \left| {\frac{1}{2}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  i&j&k \\
  0&{ - 4}&2 \\
  { - 7}&{ - 3}&4
\end{array}} \right|} \right| = 3\sqrt {30} \]

Question:
Let \[{L_1}:\frac{{x-1}}{1} = \frac{{y-2}}{{-1}} = \frac{{z-1}}{2}\] and \[{L_2}:\frac{{x + 1}}{{-1}} = \frac{{y-2}}{2} = \frac{z}{1}\] be two lines.
Let \[{L_3}\] be a line passing through the point \[\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)\] and be perpendicular to both \[{L_1}\] and \[{L_2}.\] If \[{L_3}\] intersects \[{L_1},\] then \[\left| {5\alpha -11\beta -8\gamma } \right|\] equals:
Solution:
DR’s of \[{L_3} = \vec m \times \vec n = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&{-1}&2 \\
  {-1}&2&1
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[ = -5\hat i-3\hat j + \hat k\]
\[{L_3}:\frac{{x-\alpha }}{{-5}} = \frac{{y-\beta }}{{-3}} = \frac{{z-\gamma }}{1} = \lambda \]
\[A\left( {\alpha -5\lambda ,\beta -3\lambda ,\gamma + \lambda } \right)\]
\[{L_1}:\frac{{x-1}}{1} = \frac{{y-2}}{{-1}} = \frac{{z-1}}{2} = k\]
\[B\left( {k + 1,-k + 2,2k + 1} \right)\]
Now
\[\alpha -5\lambda = k + 1 \Rightarrow \alpha = 5\lambda + k + 1\]
\[\beta -3\beta = -k + 2 \Rightarrow \beta = 3\lambda -k + 2\]
\[\gamma + \lambda = 2k-1 \Rightarrow \gamma = -\lambda + 2k + 1\]
\[\left| {5\alpha -11\beta -8\gamma } \right| = \left| {-25} \right|\]
\[ = 25\]

Question: Let the line $L_{1}$ be parallel to the vector $-3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}$ and pass through the point $(2,6,7)$ and the line $L_{2}$ be parallel to the vector $2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ and pass through the point $(4,3,5)$. If the line $\mathrm{L}_{3}$ is parallel to the vector $-3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+16 \hat{k}$ and intersects the lines $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ at the points C and D , respectively, then $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CD}}|^{2}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\quad L_{1}: \frac{x-2}{-3}=\frac{y-6}{2}=\frac{z-7}{4}$
Point C on $\mathrm{L}_{1}:\left(-3 \lambda_{1}+2,2 \lambda_{1}+6,4 \lambda_{1}+7\right)$
$\mathrm{L}_{2}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-4}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-3}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-5}{3}$
Point D on $\mathrm{L}_{2}:\left(2 \lambda_{2}+4, \lambda_{2}+3,3 \lambda_{2}+5\right)$
Dr's of line $L_{3}$ :
$\mathrm{L}_{3}: \frac{2 \lambda_{2}+3 \lambda_{1}+2}{-3}=\frac{\lambda_{2}-2 \lambda_{1}-3}{5}=\frac{3 \lambda_{2}-4 \lambda_{1}-2}{16}$
$\lambda_{1}=-3, \lambda_{2}=2$
C (11, 0, -5)
D (8, 5, 11)
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CD}}|^{2}=3^{2}+5^{2}+16^{2}=290$

Question: If the shortest distance between the straight lines \[3\left( {x - 1} \right) = 6\left( {y - 2} \right) = 2\left( {z - 1} \right)\] and \[4\left( {x - 2} \right) = 2\left( {y - \lambda } \right) = \left( {z - 3} \right),\lambda  \in R\] is \[\frac{1}{{\sqrt {38} }},\] then the integral value of \[\lambda \] is equal to :
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

\[{L_1}:\frac{{\left( {x - 1} \right)}}{2} = \frac{{\left( {y - 2} \right)}}{1} = \frac{{\left( {z - 1} \right)}}{3},\,\,\,\,\mathop {{r_1}}\limits^ \to = 2\mathop i\limits^ \wedge + \mathop j\limits^ \wedge + 3\mathop k\limits^ \wedge \]
\[{L_2}:\frac{{\left( {x - 2} \right)}}{1} = \frac{{y - \lambda }}{2} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4},\,\,\,\,\mathop {{r_2}}\limits^ \to = \mathop i\limits^ \wedge + 2\mathop j\limits^ \wedge + 4\mathop k\limits^ \wedge \]

Shortest distance = Projection of \[\mathop a\limits^ \to \] on \[\mathop {{r_1}}\limits^ \to \times \mathop {{r_2}}\limits^ \to \]
\[ = \frac{{\left| {\mathop a\limits^ \to .\left( {{{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_1} \times {{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_2}} \right)} \right|}}{{\left| {{{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_1} \times {{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_2}} \right|}}\]
\[\left| {\mathop a\limits^ \to .\left( {{{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_1} \times {{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_2}} \right)} \right| = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  1&{\lambda - 2}&2 \\
  2&1&3 \\
  1&2&4
\end{array}} \right| = \left| {14 - 5\lambda } \right|\]
\[\left| {\mathop {{r_1}}\limits^ \to \, \times \mathop {{r_2}}\limits^ \to \,} \right| = \sqrt {38} \]
\[\therefore \left| {14 - 5\lambda } \right| = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow 14 - 5\lambda = 1\] or \[14 - 5\lambda = - 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{13}}{5}\] or 3
\[\therefore \] Integral value of \[\lambda = 3\]

Question: Let the line L pass through the point $(-3,5,2)$ and make equal angles with the positive coordinate axes. If the distance of L from the point ( $-2, \mathrm{r}, 1$ ) is $\sqrt{\frac{14}{3}}$, then the sum of all possible values of $r$ is :
Solution:
Equation line is : $\frac{x+3}{1}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{z-2}{1}=\lambda$
$\therefore $ General point R on line is $\mathrm{R}(\lambda-3, \lambda+5, \lambda+2)$

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}} \equiv(\lambda-1, \lambda+5-\mathrm{r}, \lambda+1)$
Now $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}=0$
$\Rightarrow(\lambda-1) 1+(\lambda+5-\mathrm{r}) 1+(\lambda+1) 1=0$
$\Rightarrow 3 \lambda-\mathrm{r}+5=0$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{r}-5}{3}$
$\therefore \mathrm{R} \equiv\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}-5}{3}-3, \frac{\mathrm{r}-5}{3}+5, \frac{\mathrm{r}-5}{3}+2\right)$
$\mathrm{R} \equiv\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}-14}{3}, \frac{\mathrm{r}+10}{3}, \frac{\mathrm{r}+1}{3}\right)$
Now
$\mathrm{PR}=\sqrt{\frac{14}{3}} \Rightarrow(\mathrm{PR})^{2}=\frac{14}{3}$
$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}-14}{3}+2\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}+10}{3}-\mathrm{r}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}+1}{3}-1\right)^{2}=\frac{14}{3}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{(\mathrm{r}-8)^{2}}{9}+\frac{(10-2 \mathrm{r})^{2}}{9}+\frac{(\mathrm{r}-2)^{2}}{9}=\frac{14}{3}$
$\Rightarrow\left(r^{2}-16 r+64\right)+\left(100+4 r^{2}-40 r\right)+\left(r^{2}-4 r+4\right)=42$
$\Rightarrow 6 \mathrm{r}^{2}-60 \mathrm{r}+126=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}^{2}-10 \mathrm{r}+21=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=3,7$
sum of possible value of r is $=10$
