Question:
If two distinct point Q, R lie on the line of intersection of the planes
\[ - x + \;2y - \;z = \;0\] and \[3x - \;5y + \;2z = \;0\] and \[PQ = PR = \sqrt {18} \] where the point P is \[(1,\; - 2,\;3)\,,\] then the area of the triangle PQR is equal to
Solution:
\[ - x + 2y - z = 0\]
\[3x - 5y + 2z = 0\]
\[\vec n = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  { - 1}&2&{ - 1} \\
  3&{ - 5}&2
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[ = \hat i\;( - 1) - \hat j\;(1) + \hat k\,( - 1)\]
\[\vec n = - \hat i - \hat j - \hat k\]
Equation of LOI is \[\frac{x}{1} = \frac{y}{1} = \frac{z}{1}\]
\[DR:\,\,{\text{of}}\,PT\, \to \alpha - 1,\alpha + 2,\alpha - 3\]
\[DR:\,\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,QR\, \to 1,\] 1, 1
\[ \Rightarrow (\alpha - 1) \times 1 + (\alpha + 2) \times 1 + (\alpha - 3) \times 1 = 0\]
\[3\alpha = 2\]
\[\boxed{\alpha = \frac{2}{3}}\]
\[P{T^2} = \frac{1}{9} + \frac{{64}}{9} + \frac{{49}}{9}\]
\[P{T^2} = \frac{{114}}{9}\]
\[\boxed{PT = \frac{{\sqrt {114} }}{3}}\]
\[\cos \theta = \frac{{\sqrt {114} }}{3} \times \frac{1}{{3\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{\sqrt {57} }}{9} = \frac{{\sqrt {19 \times 3} }}{{3 \times 3}}\]
\[ = \frac{{\sqrt {19} }}{{3\sqrt 3 }}\]
\[\cos 2\theta = \frac{{2 \times 19}}{{27}} - 1 = \frac{{11}}{{27}}\]
\[\sin \;2\theta = \sqrt {1 - {{\left( {\frac{{11}}{{27}}} \right)}^2}} = \frac{{\sqrt {38} \sqrt {16} }}{{27}}\]
\[\frac{4}{{27}}\sqrt {38} \]
Area \[ = \frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt {18} \sqrt {18} \times \frac{4}{{27}}\sqrt {38} \]
\[ = \frac{{18}}{2} \times \frac{4}{{27}}\sqrt {38} = \frac{{36}}{{27}}\sqrt {38} \; = \frac{4}{3}\sqrt {38} \]

Question: A tetrahedron has vertices P(1, 2, 1), Q(2, 1, 3), R(−1, 1, 2) and O(0, 0, 0). The angle between the faces OPQ and PQR can be:
Solution:
Let \[{\bar v_1}\] and \[{\bar v_2}\] be the vectors perpendicular to the plane OPQ and PQR respectively.
\[\begin{gathered}
  {{\bar v}_1} = \overline {{\text{PQ}}} \times \overline {{\text{OQ}}} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&{ - 1}&2 \\
  2&1&3
\end{array}} \right| \\
   = - 5\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  {{\bar v}_2} = \overline {{\text{PQ}}} \times \overline {{\text{PR}}} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&{ - 1}&2 \\
  { - 2}&{ - 1}&1
\end{array}} \right| \\
   = \hat i - 5\hat j - 3\hat k \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\because \,\,\,\cos \theta = \frac{{{{\bar v}_1}.\,{{\bar v}_2}}}{{\left| {{{\bar v}_1}} \right|\left| {{{\bar v}_2}} \right|}} = \frac{{ - 5 - 5 - 9}}{{25 + 1 + 9}} = \frac{{ - 19}}{{35}}\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,\theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{ - 19}}{{35}}} \right)\]

Question: The shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x + 7}}{{ - 6}} = \;\frac{{y - 6}}{7} = z\] and \[\frac{{7 - x}}{2} = y - 2 = z - 6\] is

Let P be the plane, which contains the line of intersection of the plane, \[x + y + z - 6 = 0\] and $2x + 3y + z + 5 = 0$and it is perpendicular to the xy-plane. Then the distance of the point (0, 0, 256) from P is equal to:
Solution:
Let the plane be
$P \equiv \left( {2x + 3y + z + 5} \right) + \lambda \left( {x + y + z - 6} \right) = 0$
$\because $above plane is perpendicular to xy plane.
$\therefore \left( {\left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {3 + \lambda } \right)\hat j + \left( {1 + \lambda } \right)\hat k} \right).\hat k = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = - 1$
Hence, the equation of the plane is,
$P \equiv x + 2y + 11 = 0$
Distance of the plane P from $\left( {0,0,256} \right)$
$\left| {\frac{{0 + 0 + 11}}{{\sqrt 5 }}} \right| = \frac{{11}}{{\sqrt 5 }}$

Question: The distance of the point (1, 1, 9) from the point of intersection of the line \[\frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y - 4}}{2} = \frac{{z - 5}}{2}\] and the plane \[x + y + z = 17\] is
Solution:
\[\frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y - 4}}{2} = \frac{{z - 5}}{2} = \lambda \]

\[ \Rightarrow x = \lambda + 3,y = 2\lambda + 4,z = 2\lambda + 5\]
Which lies on given plane hence
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda + 3 + 2\lambda + 4 + 2\lambda + 5 = 17\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda = \frac{5}{5} = 1\]
Hence, point of intersection is Q (4, 6, 7)
\[\therefore \] Required distance = PQ
                                  \[ = \sqrt {9 + 25 + 4} \]
                                  \[ = \sqrt {38} \]

Question:
Let Q be the foot of the perpendicular from the point \[P\left( {7,\; - 2,\;13} \right)\] on the plane containing the lines, \[\frac{{x + 1}}{6} = \frac{{y - 1}}{7} = \frac{{z - 3}}{8}\] and \[\frac{{x - 1}}{3} = \frac{{y - 2}}{5} = \frac{{z - 3}}{7}.\]
Then \[{\left( {PQ} \right)^2},\] is equal to _______.
Solution:
Containing the line \[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x + 1}&{y - 1}&{z - 3} \\
  6&7&8 \\
  3&5&7
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
\[9\left( {x + 1} \right) - 18\left( {y - 1} \right) + 9\left( {z - 3} \right) = 0\]
\[x - 2y + z = 0\]
\[PQ = \left| {\frac{{7 + 4 + 13}}{{\sqrt 6 }}} \right| = 4\sqrt 6 \]
\[P{Q^2} = 96\]

Question: Let the equation of the plane P containing the line $x + 10 = \frac{{8 - y}}{2} = z$ be $ax + by + 3z = 2\left( {a + b} \right)$ and the distance of the plane P from the point $\left( {1,27,7} \right)$ be $c$. Then ${a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
The line \[\frac{{x + 10}}{1} = \frac{{y - 8}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{z}{1}\] have a point $\left( { - 10,8,0} \right)$ with d.r. $\left( {1, - 2,1} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,$The plane $ax + by + 3z = 2\left( {a + b} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow b = 2a$
& dot product of d.r.’s is zero
$\therefore \,\,a - 2b + 3 = 0$
$\therefore \,\,a = 1\,\,\,\& \,\,\,b = 2$
Distance from $\left( {1,27,7} \right)$ is
$c = \frac{{1 + 54 + 21 - 6}}{{\sqrt {14} }} = \frac{{70}}{{\sqrt {14} }} = 5\sqrt {14} $
$\therefore \,\,{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2} = 1 + 4 + 350 = 355$

Question: Let the line $L:\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{1}$ intersect the plane $2x + y + 3z = 16$ at the point $P$. Let the point $Q$ be the foot of perpendicular from the point $R\left( {1, - 1,3} \right)$ on the line $L$. If $\alpha $ is the area of triangle $PQR$, then ${\alpha ^2}$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
Any point on $L\left( {\left( {2\lambda + 1} \right),\left( { - \lambda - 1} \right),\left( {\lambda + 3} \right)} \right)$
$2\left( {2\lambda + 1} \right) + \left( { - \lambda - 1} \right) + 3\left( {\lambda + 3} \right) = 16$
$6\lambda + 10 = 16 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1$
$\therefore \,\,P = \left( {3, - 2,4} \right)$
DR of $QR = \left\langle {2\lambda - \lambda ,\lambda + 6} \right\rangle $
DR of $L = \left\langle {2, - 1,1} \right\rangle $
$4\lambda + \lambda + \lambda + 6 = 0$
$6\lambda + 6 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = - 1$
$Q = \left( { - 1,0,2} \right)$


$\overrightarrow {QR} = 2\hat i - \hat j - 5\hat k$
$\overrightarrow {QP} = 4\hat i - 2\hat j + 2\hat k$
$\overrightarrow {QR} \times \overrightarrow {QP} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&{ - 1}&{ - 5} \\
  4&{ - 2}&2
\end{array}} \right| = - 12\hat i - 24\hat j$
$\alpha = \frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt {144 + 576} $
$ \Rightarrow {\alpha ^2} = \frac{{720}}{4} = 180$

Question: If the foot of the perpendicular from the point \[A\;( - 1,\;4,\;3)\] on the plane \[P:\;2x + \;my + \;nz = \;4,\] is \[\left( { - 2,\;\frac{7}{2},\;\frac{3}{2}} \right)\,,\] then the distance of the point A from the plane P, measured parallel to a line with direction ratios \[3,\; - 1,\; - 4,\]is equal to :
Solution:
Let B be foot of \[ \bot \] coordinates of \[B = \;\left( { - 2,\;\frac{7}{2},\;\frac{3}{2}} \right)\]
Direction ratio of line AB is \[ < \,2,\;1,\;3\; > \] so \[m = \;1,\;n\; = \;3\]
So equation of AC is \[\frac{{x + 1}}{3} = \;\frac{{y - 4}}{{ - 1}} = \;\frac{{z - 3}}{{ - \,4}} = \;\lambda \]
So point C is \[(3\lambda - 1,\; - \lambda + 4,\; - 4\lambda + \;3).\] But C lies on the plane, so
\[6\lambda - 2 - \lambda + \;4 - \;12\lambda + \;9 = \;4\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\lambda = 1\; \Rightarrow \;C\;(2,\;3,\; - 1)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;AC = \;\sqrt {26} \]

Question: The plane which bisects the line segment joining the points (−3, 3, 4) and (3. 7, 6) at right angles, passes through which one of the following points?
Solution:
Since, direction ratios of normal to the plane is
$\vec n = 6\hat i + 10\hat j + 2\hat k$
Then, equation of the plane is
$\begin{gathered}
  \left( {x - 0} \right)6 + \left( {y - 2} \right)10 + \left( {z - 5} \right)2 = 0 \\
  3x + 5y - 10 + z - 5 = 0 \\
  3x + 5y + z = 15\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,.....\left( 1 \right) \\
\end{gathered} $
since, plane (i) satisfies the point (4, 1, - 2 )
Hence, required point is (4, 1, - 2)

Question: A perpendicular is drawn from a point on the line \[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{z}{1}\] to the plane $x + y + z = 3$ such that the foot the perpendicular Q also lies on the plane $x - y + z = 3.$Then the co-ordinates of Q are:
Solution:
Let co-ordinates of Q be $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right),{\text{then}}$
$\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 3\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\,\left( i \right)$
$\alpha - \beta + \gamma = 3\,\,\,\,\,....\left( {ii} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha + \gamma = 3\,\,\,{\text{and }}\,\beta {\text{ = 0}}$
Equating direction ratio’s of PQ, we get
$\frac{{\alpha - 2\lambda - 1}}{1} = \frac{{\lambda + 1}}{1} = \frac{{\gamma - \lambda }}{1}$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = 3\lambda + 2,\gamma = 2\lambda + 1$
Substituting the values of $\alpha $and $\gamma $ in equation (i), we get
\[ \Rightarrow 5\lambda + 3 = 3 \Rightarrow \lambda = 0\]
Hence, point is $Q\left( {2,0,1} \right)$

Question: A line with direction ratios $2,1,2$ meets the lines $x = y + 2 = z$ and $x + 2 = 2y = 2z$ respectively at the points $P$ and $Q$. If the length of the perpendicular from the point $\left( {1,2,12} \right)$ to the line $PQ$ is $l,$ then ${l^2}$ is ____.
Solution:
Let $P\left( {t,t - 2,t} \right)$ and $Q\left( {2s - 2,s,s} \right)$
D.R’s of PQ are 2, 1, 2
$\frac{{2s - 2 - t}}{2} = \frac{{s - t + 2}}{1} = \frac{{s - t}}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow t = 6$ and $s = 2$
$ \Rightarrow P\left( {6,4,6} \right)$ and $Q\left( {2,2,2} \right)$
$PQ:\frac{{x - 2}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{1} = \frac{{z - 2}}{2} = \lambda $
Let $F\left( {2\lambda + 2,\lambda + 2,2\lambda + 2} \right)$
$A\left( {1,2,12} \right)$
$\overrightarrow {AF} \cdot \overrightarrow {PQ} = 0$
$\therefore \,\,\lambda = 2$
So, F(6, 4, 6) and $AF = \sqrt {65} $

Question: Let the acute angle bisector of the two planes \[x - 2y - 2z + 1 = 0\] and \[2x - 3y - 6z + 1 = 0\] be the plane P. Then which of the following points lies on P
Solution:
\[{P_1}:x - 2y - 2z + 1 = 0\]
\[{P_2}:2x - 3y - 6z + 1 = 0\]
\[\left| {\frac{{x - 2y - 2z + 1}}{{\sqrt {1 + 4 + 4} }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{2x - 3y - 6z + 1}}{{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {6^2}} }}} \right|\]
\[\frac{{x - 2y - 2z + 1}}{3} = \pm \frac{{2x - 3y - 6z + 1}}{7}\]
Since \[{a_1}{a_2} + {b_1}{b_2} + {c_1}{c_2} = 20 > 0\]
\[\therefore \] Negative sing will give
acute bisector
\[7x - 14y - 14z + 7 = - \left[ {6x - 9y - 18z + 3} \right]\]
\[ \Rightarrow 13x - 23y - 32z + 10 = 0\]
\[\left( { - 2,0, - \frac{1}{2}} \right)\] satisfy it .

Question: Let L be a line obtained from the intersection of two planes \[x + 2y + z = 6\] and \[y + 2z = 4\]. If point \[P\left( {\alpha ,\,\,\beta ,\,\,\gamma } \right)\] is the foot of perpendicular from (3, 2, 1) on L, then the value of \[21\left( {\alpha  + \beta  + \gamma } \right)\] equals:
Solution:
A vector in the direction of line L is
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\widehat i}&{\widehat j}&{\widehat k} \\
  1&2&1 \\
  0&1&2
\end{array}} \right| = 3\widehat i - 2\widehat j + \widehat k\]
So, D.R. of line L are \[3, - 2,1\]
A Point on line L is \[\left( { - 2,4,0} \right)\]
So, equation of line L is
\[\frac{{x + 2}}{3} = \frac{{y - 4}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{z}{1}\]
Let \[P\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right) = \left( {3\lambda - 2, - 2\lambda + 4,\lambda } \right)\] and \[A\left( {3,2,1} \right)\]
D.R. of AP are \[3\lambda - 5, - 2\lambda + 2,\lambda - 1\]
\[\because AP \bot \,\,line\,{\text{L}}\]
\[\therefore \,\,3\left( {3\lambda - 5} \right) - 2\left( { - 2\lambda + 2} \right) + 1\left( {\lambda - 1} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 14\lambda - 20 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{10}}{7}\]
So, \[P\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right) = \left( {\frac{{16}}{7},\frac{8}{7},\frac{{10}}{7}} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow 21\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right) = 21\left( {\frac{{34}}{7}} \right) = 102\]

Question: The equation of the plane containing the line \[2x - 5y + z = 3,\,\,x + y + 4z = 5\]<sub> </sub>and parallel to the plane \[x + 3y + 6z = 1,\] is
Solution:
Equation of required plane is
\[2x - 5y + z - 3 + \lambda \left( {x + y + 4z - 5} \right) = 0\]
\[x\left( {2 + \lambda } \right) + y\left( {\lambda - 5} \right) + z\left( {4\lambda + 1} \right) - 3 - 5\lambda = 0\]
This plane is parallel to the plane\[x + 3y + 6z = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{\lambda + 2}}{1} = \frac{{\lambda - 5}}{3} = \frac{{4\lambda + 1}}{6}\]
\[3\lambda + 6 = \lambda - 5\]
\[2\lambda = - 11\]
\[\lambda = \frac{{ - 11}}{2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \] equation of plane \[\frac{{ - 7x}}{2} - \frac{{21y}}{2} - 21z + \frac{{49}}{2} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,7x + 21y + 42z - 49 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,x + 3y + 6z - 7 = 0\]

Question:
If for some \[\alpha \] and \[\beta \] in R, the intersection of the following three planes
$\begin{gathered}
  x + 4y - 2z = 1 \\
  x + 7y - 5z = \beta \\
  x + 5y + \alpha z = 5 \\
\end{gathered} $
is a line in R3, then $\alpha + \beta $is equal to:
Solution:
The given planes intersect in a line
$D = {D_x} = {D_y} = {D_z} = 0$
$D = 0$
$\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  { \Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  1&4&{ - 2} \\
  1&7&{ - 5} \\
  1&5&\alpha
\end{array}} \right| = 0 \Rightarrow 7\alpha + 25 - 4\alpha - 20 + 4 = 0} \\
  { \Rightarrow \alpha = - 3}
\end{array}$
$\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  {{D_z} = 0} \\
  { \Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  1&4&1 \\
  1&7&\beta \\
  1&5&5
\end{array}} \right| = 0 \Rightarrow 35 - 5\beta - 20 + 4\beta - 2 = 0} \\
  { \Rightarrow \beta = 13} \\
  {\therefore \alpha + \beta = 10}
\end{array}$

Question: If the angle between the lines, \[\frac{x}{2}\,{\mathbf{ = }}\,\frac{y}{2}\,{\mathbf{ = }}\,\frac{z}{1}\] and \[\frac{{5\, - x}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{7y\, - 14}}{p} = \frac{{z\, - 3}}{4}\] is \[{\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{2}{3}} \right),\] then p is equal to :
Solution:
Given lines are
\[\frac{x}{2} = \frac{y}{2} = \frac{z}{1}\,\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\,\,\frac{{x - 5}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{\frac{p}{7}}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}\]
Angle between both lines is
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{2}{3}} \right) = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{4 + \frac{{2P}}{7} + 4}}{{3\sqrt {4 + \frac{{{P^2}}}{{49}} + 16} }}} \right) \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{2}{3} = \frac{{56 + 2{P}}}{{3\sqrt {{P^2} + 980} }} \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,\sqrt {{P^2} + 980} = P + 28 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,{P^2} + 980 = {P^2} + 56P + 784 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,56P = 196 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,P = \frac{7}{2} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: If the mirror image of the point $P\left( {3,4,9} \right)$ in the line $\frac{{x - 1}}{3} = \frac{{y + 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{1}$ is $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$, then $14\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right)$ is:
Solution:
$\overrightarrow {PN} .\,\vec b = 0?$
$3\left( {3\lambda - 2} \right) + 2\left( {2\lambda - 5} \right) + \left( {\lambda - 7} \right) = 0$
$14\lambda = 23 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{23}}{{14}}$
$N\left( {\frac{{83}}{{14}},\frac{{32}}{{14}},\frac{{51}}{{14}}} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,\frac{{\alpha + 3}}{2} = \frac{{83}}{{14}} \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{{62}}{7}$
$\frac{{\beta + 4}}{2} = \frac{{32}}{{14}} \Rightarrow \beta = \frac{4}{7}$
$\frac{{\gamma + 9}}{2} = \frac{{51}}{{14}} \Rightarrow \gamma = \frac{{ - 12}}{7}$
Ans. $14\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right) = 108$

Question: Let \[\vec a = \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k\] and \[\vec b = 2\hat i + 7\hat j + 3\hat k.\] Let \[{L_1}:\vec r = \left( {-\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right) + \lambda \vec a,\lambda  \in R\] and \[{L_2}:\vec r = \left( {\hat j + \hat k} \right) + \mu \vec b,\mu  \in R\] be two lines. If the line \[{L_3}\] passes through the point of intersection of \[{L_1}\] and \[{L_2}\], and is parallel to \[\vec a + \vec b,\] then \[{L_3}\] passes through the point:
Solution:
\[{L_1}:\vec r = \left( {-\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \vec r = \left( {\lambda -1} \right)\hat i + 2\left( {\lambda + 1} \right)\hat j + \left( {\lambda + 1} \right)\hat k\]
\[{L_2}:\vec r = \left( {\hat j + \hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {2\hat i + 7\hat j + 3\hat k} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \vec r = 2\mu \hat i + \left( {1 + 7\mu } \right)\hat j + \left( {1 + 3\mu } \right)\hat k\]
For point of intersection equating respective components
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda -1 = 2\mu \] \[...\left( 1 \right)\]
\[2\left( {\lambda + 1} \right) = 1 + 7\mu \] \[...\left( 2 \right)\]
\[\lambda + 1 = 1 + 3\mu \] \[...\left( 3 \right)\]
We get
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda = 3\] and \[\mu = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \vec a + \vec b = 3\hat i + 9\hat j + 4\hat k\]
\[{L_3}:\vec r = 2\hat i + 8\hat j + 4\hat k + \alpha \left( {3\hat i + 9\hat j + 4\hat k} \right)\]
For \[\alpha = 2,\vec r = 8\hat i + 26\hat j + 12\hat k\]

Question:
If the shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x - 1}}{\alpha } = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{z}{1},\,\,\left( {\alpha  \ne  - 1} \right)\] and 

<!-StartFragment->\[\frac{{x }}{2 } = \frac{{y - 1}}{{ 1}} = \frac{z + 2}{-3}\]<!-EndFragment->   then a value of \[\alpha \] is

Let the image of the point $\left( {1,0,7} \right)$ in the line $\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3}$ be the point $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$. Then which one of the following points lies on the line passing through $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$ and making angles $\frac{{2\pi }}{3}$ and $\frac{{3\pi }}{4}$ with $y$-axis and $z$-axis respectively and an acute angle with $x$-axis?
Solution:
${L_1} = \frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3} = \lambda $

$M\left( {\lambda ,1 + 2\lambda ,2 + 3\lambda } \right)$
$\overrightarrow {PM} = \left( {\lambda - 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {1 + 2\lambda } \right)\hat j + \left( {3\lambda - 5} \right)\hat k$
$\overrightarrow {PM} $ is perpendicular to line ${L_1}$
$\overrightarrow {PM} .\,\vec b = 0\,\,\,\left( {\vec b = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda - 1 + 4\lambda + 2 = 9\lambda - 15 = 0$
$14\lambda = 14 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1$
$\therefore \,\,M = \left( {1,3,5} \right)$
$\vec Q = 2\vec M - \vec P$ [M is midpoint of $\vec P\,\,\& \,\,\vec Q$]
$\vec Q = 2\hat i + 6\hat j + 10\hat k - \hat i - 7\hat k$
$\vec Q = \hat i + 6\hat j + 3\hat k$
$\therefore \,\,\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right) = \left( {1,6,3} \right)$
Required line having direction cosine $\left( {l,m,n} \right)$
${l^2} + {m^2} + {n^2} = 1$
$ \Rightarrow {l^2} + {\left( { - \frac{1}{2}} \right)^2} + {\left( { - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)^2} = 1$
${l^2} = \frac{1}{4}$
$\therefore \,\,l = \frac{1}{2}$ [Line make acute angle with $x$-axis]
Equation of line passing through (1, 6, 3) will be
$\vec r = \left( {\hat i + 6\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {\frac{1}{2}\hat i - \frac{1}{2}\hat j - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat k} \right)$
Option (b) satisfying for $\mu = 4$

Question: The equation of the plane which contains the y-axis and passes through the point (1, 2, 3) is :
Solution:
Let the equation of the plane be \[a\left( {x - 1} \right) + b\left( {y - 2} \right) + c\left( {z - 3} \right) = 0\]
\[y - \]axis lies on it D.R. ‘s of \[y - \]axis are \[0,1,0\]
\[\therefore 0.\,a + 1.b + 0.c = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow b = 0\]
\[\therefore \] Equation of plane is \[a\left( {x - 1} \right) + c\left( {z - 3} \right) = 0\]
\[x = 0,z = 0\] also satisfy it
\[ - a - 3c = 0 \Rightarrow a = - 3c\]
\[ - 3c\left( {x - 1} \right) + c\left( {z - 3} \right) = 0\]
\[ - 3x + 3 + z - 3 = 0\]
\[3x - z = 0\]

Question:
Let a line L be perpendicular to both the lines
$\mathrm{L}_{1}: \frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+3}{5}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+5}{7}$ and
$\mathrm{L}_{2}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-2}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-4}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-6}{7}$.
If $\theta$ is the acute angle between the lines L and $\mathrm{L}_{3}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-\frac{8}{7}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-\frac{4}{7}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{2}$, then $\tan \theta$ is equal to:
Solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & 4 & 7\end{array}\right|=7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-14 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\equiv 7(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})$
Now,

$\because \cos \theta=\frac{2-2+2}{\sqrt{1+4+1} \sqrt{4+1+4}}$
$\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{2}{3 \sqrt{6}} \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\frac{5 \sqrt{2}}{2}$

Question: The line ${l_1}$ passes through the point $\left( {2,6,2} \right)$ and is perpendicular to the plane $2x + y - 2z = 10$. Then the shortest distance between the line ${l_1}$ and the line $\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 4}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{z}{2}$ is:
Solution:
Line $l$, is given by
${L_1}:\frac{{x - 2}}{2} = \frac{{y - 6}}{1} = \frac{{z - 2}}{{ - 2}}$
Shortest Distance $ = \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {{x_1} - {x_2}}&{{y_1} - {y_2}}&{{z_1} - {z_2}} \\
  {{a_1}}&{{b_1}}&{{c_1}} \\
  {{a_2}}&{{b_2}}&{{c_2}}
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\sqrt {\sum {{{\left( {{a_1}{b_2} - {a_2}{b_1}} \right)}^2}} } }}$ (Use it)

Shortest distance $ = \left| {\frac{{\overrightarrow {AB} .\overrightarrow {MN} }}{{MN}}} \right|$
$\overrightarrow {AB} = 3\hat i + 10\hat j + 2\hat k$
$\overrightarrow {MN} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&1&{ - 2} \\
  2&{ - 3}&2
\end{array}} \right| = - 4\hat i - 8\hat j - 8\hat k$
$MN = \sqrt {16 + 64 + 64} = 12$
Shortest distance $ = \left| {\frac{{ - 12 - 80 - 16}}{{12}}} \right| = 9$
Option (d) is correct.

Question: The shortest distance between the lines $\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{y + 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 6}}{2}$ and $\frac{{x - 6}}{3} = \frac{{1 - y}}{2} = \frac{{z + 8}}{0}$ is equal to _______.
Solution:
Shortest distance between the lines
$ = \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  4&2&{ - 14} \\
  3&2&2 \\
  3&{ - 2}&0
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\left| {\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  3&2&2 \\
  3&{ - 2}&0
\end{array}} \right|} \right|}}$
$ = \frac{{16 + 12 + 168}}{{\left| { - 4\hat i + 6\hat j - 12\hat k} \right|}} = \frac{{196}}{{14}} = 14$

Question: One vertex of a rectangular parallelopiped is at the origin O and the lengths of its edges along $x,y$ and $z$ axes are 3, 4 and 5 units respectively. Let P be the vertex $\left( {3,4,5} \right)$. Then the shortest distance between the diagonal OP and an edge parallel to z axis, not passing through O or P is:
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

Equation of OP is $\frac{x}{3} = \frac{y}{4} = \frac{z}{5}$
${a_1} = \left( {0,0,0} \right)$
${a_2} = \left( {3,0,5} \right)$
${b_1} = \left( {3,4,5} \right)$
${b_2} = \left( {0,0,1} \right)$
Equation of edge parallel to z axis
$\frac{{x - 3}}{0} = \frac{{y - 0}}{0} = \frac{{z - 5}}{1}$
S.D. $ = \frac{{\left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right) \cdot \left( {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}$
$\frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  3&0&5 \\
  3&4&5 \\
  0&0&1
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\left| {\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  3&4&5 \\
  0&0&1
\end{array}} \right|} \right|}} = \frac{{3\left( 4 \right)}}{{\left| {4\hat i - 3\hat j} \right|}} = \frac{{12}}{5}$

Question: Let A and B be two distinct points on the line \[L:\frac{{x - 6}}{3} = \frac{{y - 7}}{2} = \frac{{z - 7}}{{ - 2}}.\]Both A and B are at a distance \[2\sqrt {17} \]from the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point (1,2,3) on the line L. If O is the origin, then \[\overrightarrow {OA} .\overrightarrow {OB} \] is equal to:

 A (–3, 1, 13) and B (9, 9, 5)

Question: Let the line passing through the points (–1, 2, 1) and parallel to the line $\frac{x}{2} = \frac{y}{3} = \frac{z}{4}$ intersect the line \[\frac{{x + 2}}{3} = \frac{{y - 3}}{2} = \frac{{z - 4}}{1}\] at the point P. Then the distance of P from the point Q(4,-5,1) is:
Solution:
Equation of line through point (–1, 2,1) is →
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 1}}{4} - (2) = \lambda \]

So, \[\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x = 2\lambda - 1} \\
  {y = 3\lambda + 2} \\
  {z = 4\lambda + 1}
\end{array}} \right.\]
By (1) \[ \to \frac{{x + 2}}{3} = \frac{{y - 3}}{2} = \frac{{z - 4}}{1} = \mu (Let)\]
\[So,\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x = 3\mu - 2} \\
  {y = 2\mu + 3} \\
  {z = \mu = 4}
\end{array}} \right.\]
For intersection point ‘P’
x = 2λ – 1 = 3μ – 2
\[y = 3\lambda + 2 = 2\mu = 3\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\lambda = 1} \\
  {\mu = 1}
\end{array}} \right]\]
z = 4λ + 1 = µ + 4
So, point P(x, y, z) = (1, 5, 5)
& Q(4, -5, 1)
\[\therefore PQ = \sqrt {9 + 100 + 16} \]
\[ = \sqrt {125} = 5\sqrt 5 \]

Question: Let the vertices Q and R of the triangle PQR lie on the line \[\frac{{x + 3}}{5} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z + 4}}{3},\]QR = 5 and the coordinates of the point P be (0, 2, 3). If the area of the triangle PQR is \[\frac{m}{n}\]then:
Solution:
M(5λ – 3, 2λ + 1, 3λ – 4)
Drs of PM ⇒ 5λ – 3, 2λ – 1, 3λ – 7
Drs of line L ⇒ 5, 2, 3
PM ⊥ L
⇒ ( 5λ – 3)5 + (2λ – 1)2 + (3λ – 7)3 = 0
⇒ λ = 1
∴ M(2, 3, –1)
\[PM = \sqrt {4 + 1 + 16} = \sqrt {21} \]
\[Area = \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times \sqrt {21} = \frac{m}{n}\]
\[2m - 5\sqrt {21} n = 0\]

Question: The shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x - 1}}{0} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{z}{1}\;\,{\text{and}}\,\;x + y + z + 1 = 0,\]\[2x - y + z + 3 = 0\]is:
Solution:
Plane through line of intersection is
\[x + y + z + 1 + \lambda \left( {2x - y + z + 3} \right) = 0\]
It should be parallel to given line
\[0\left( {1 + 2\lambda } \right) - 1\left( {1 - \lambda } \right) + 1\left( {1 + \lambda } \right) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 0\]
Plane: \[x + y + z + 1 = 0\]
Shortest distance of (1, -1, 0) from this plane \[ = \frac{{|1 - 1 + 0 + 1|}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {1^2}} }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]

Question: The square of the distance of the point ( $-2,-8,6$ ) from the line $\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z}{-1}$ along the line $\frac{x+5}{1}=\frac{y+5}{-1}=\frac{z}{2}$ is equal to:
Solution:
DR or AB is $1,-1,2$
∴ Line AB is $\frac{\mathrm{x}+2}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+8}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-6}{2}=\lambda$
B is ( $\lambda-2,-\lambda-8,2 \lambda+6$ )
B is $(\mu+1,2 \mu+1,-\mu)$
Solving point B is $(-3,-7,4)$
$\mathrm{AB}=\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+(1)^{2}+(2)^{2}}=\sqrt{6}$
$\mathrm{AB}^{2}=6$

Question:
Let P be the plane passing through the point (1, 2, 3) and the line of intersection of the planes \[\overrightarrow r .\left( {\widehat i + \widehat j + 4\widehat k} \right) = 16\] and \[\overrightarrow r .\left( { - \widehat i + \widehat j + \widehat k} \right) = 6.\]
Then which of the following points lie on P?
Solution:
\[\left( {x + y + 4z - 16} \right) + \lambda \left( { - x + y + z - 6} \right) = 0\]
Passes through (1, 2, 3)
\[\begin{gathered}
   - 1 + \lambda \left( { - 2} \right)\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\; = - \frac{1}{2} \\
  2\left( {x + y + z - 16} \right) - \left( { - x + y + z - 6} \right) = 0 \\
  3x + y + z - 26 = 0 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A plane passing through the point (3, 1, 1) contains two lines whose direction ratios are 1, –2, 2 and 2, 3, –1 respectively. If this plane also passes through the point \[(\alpha , - 3,5),\] then \[\alpha \] is equal to :
Solution:
Required plane is
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 3}&{y - 1}&{z - 1} \\
  1&{ - 2}&2 \\
  2&3&{ - 1}
\end{array}} \right|\; = \;0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\; - 4\;(x - 3)\; + \;5\;(y - 1) + \;7(z - 1) = \;0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;4x - 5y - 7z = 0\]
\[(\alpha , - 3,\;5)\] lies on 4x – 5y – 7z = 0
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;\alpha = 5\]

Question: If the points \[\left( {1,\,\,1,\,\,\lambda } \right)\] and \[\left( { - 3,\,\,0,\,\,1} \right)\] are equidistant from the plane,\[3x + 4y - 12z + 13 = 0\], then \[\lambda \] satisfies the equation
Solution:
\[\left| {3 + 4 - 12\lambda + 13} \right| = \left| { - 9 + 0 - 12 + 13} \right|\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\left| {20 - 12\lambda } \right| = 8\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,12\lambda - 20 = \pm 8\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,12\lambda = 20 \pm 8\]
\[\lambda = 1\,\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\,\lambda = \frac{7}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda \] satisfies the equation \[{x^2} - \left( {1 + \frac{7}{3}} \right)x + \frac{7}{3} = 0\]
\[3{x^2} - 10x + 7 = 0\]

Question: Equation of a plane at a distance \[\sqrt {\frac{2}{{21}}} \] from the origin, which contains the line of intersection of the planes \[x-y-z-1 = 0\] and \[2x + y-3z + 4 = 0,\]is :
Solution:
Required equation of plane
\[{P_1} + \lambda {P_2} = 0\]
\[\left( {x-y-z-1} \right) + \lambda \left( {2x + y-3z + 4} \right) = 0\]
Given that its distance from origin is \[\sqrt {\frac{2}{{21}}} \]
Thus \[\frac{{\left| {4\lambda -1} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {2\lambda + 1} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\lambda -1} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {-3\lambda -1} \right)}^2}} }} = \frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{{\sqrt {21} }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 21{\left( {4\lambda -1} \right)^2} = 2\left( {14{\lambda ^2} + 8\lambda + 3} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow 336{\lambda ^2}-168\lambda + 21 = 28{\lambda ^2} + 16\lambda + 6\]
\[ \Rightarrow 308{\lambda ^2}-184\lambda + 15 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 308{\lambda ^2}-154\lambda -30\lambda + 15 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {2\lambda -1} \right)\left( {154\lambda -15} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{2}\] or \[\frac{{15}}{{154}}\]
For \[\lambda = \frac{1}{2}\], the required equation of plane is
\[4x-y-5z + 2 = 0\]

Question: If the square of the shortest distance between the lines \[\,\frac{{x - 2}}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z + 3}}{{ - 3}}\] \[and\,\,\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z + 5}}{{ - 5}}\] is \[\frac{m}{n}\], where m, n are coprime numbers, then m + n is equal to:
Solution:
\[\vec a = (2,1, - 3)\]
\[\vec b = ( - 1, - 3, - 5)\]
\[\vec p \times \vec q = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&2&{ - 3} \\
  2&4&{ - 5}
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[ = 2\hat i - \hat j\]
$\vec b - \vec a = - 3\hat i - 4\hat j - 2\hat k$
${S_d} = \frac{{\left| {\left( {\vec b - \vec a} \right) \cdot \left( {\vec p \times \vec q} \right)} \right|}}{{\left| {\vec p \times \vec q} \right|}}$
\[ = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 5 }}\]
\[{\left( {{{\text{S}}_{\text{d}}}} \right)^2} = \frac{4}{5}\]
\[{\text{m}} = 4,{\text{n}} = 5 \Rightarrow {\text{m}} + {\text{n}} = 9\]

Question: Let P be a plane passing through the points (1, 0, 1), (1, –2, 1) and (0, 1, –2). Let a vector \[\mathop a\limits^ \to   = \alpha \mathop i\limits^ \wedge   + \beta \mathop j\limits^ \wedge   + \gamma \mathop k\limits^ \wedge  \] be such that \[\mathop a\limits^ \to  \] is parallel to the plane P, perpendicular to \[\left( {\mathop i\limits^ \wedge   + 2\mathop j\limits^ \wedge   + 3\mathop k\limits^ \wedge  } \right)\] and \[\mathop a\limits^ \to  .\left( {\mathop i\limits^ \wedge   + \mathop j\limits^ \wedge   + 2\mathop k\limits^ \wedge  } \right) = 2,\] then \[{\left( {\alpha  - \beta  + \gamma } \right)^2}\] equals ____.
Solution:
Equation of plane:
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 1}&{y - 0}&{z - 1} \\
  {1 - 1}&2&{1 - 1} \\
  {1 - 0}&{0 - 1}&{1 + 2}
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 3x - z - 2 = 0$
Given,
$\vec a = \alpha \hat i + \beta \hat j + \gamma \hat k\,||$ to $3x - z - 2 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 3\alpha - \gamma = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)$
$\vec a = \alpha \hat i + \beta \hat j + \gamma \hat k\,\,{ \bot ^r}$ to $\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha + 2\beta + 3\gamma = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 2 \right)$
and
$\vec a.\left( {\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right) = 2$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha + \beta + 2\gamma = 2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 3 \right)$
On solving (1), (2) & (3)
$\alpha = 1,\beta = - 5,\gamma = 3$
$\therefore {\left( {\alpha - \beta + \gamma } \right)^2} = {\left( {1 + 5 + 3} \right)^2} = 81$

Question: Let \[{l_1}\] be the line in xy-plane with x and y intercepts \[\frac{1}{8}\] and \[\frac{1}{{4\sqrt 2 }}\] respectively, and \[{l_2}\] be the line in zx-plane with x and z intercepts \[ - \frac{1}{8}\] and \[ - \frac{1}{{6\sqrt 3 }}\] respectively. If d is the shortest distance between the line \[{l_1}\] and \[{l_2},\] then \[{d^{ - 2}}\] is equal to
Solution:
\[8x + 4\sqrt 2 y = 1,\,z = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{x - \frac{1}{8}}}{1} = \frac{{y - 0}}{{ - \sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{z - 0}}{0} = \lambda \]
\[ - 8x - 6\sqrt 3 z = 1,y = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{x + \frac{1}{8}}}{{3\sqrt 3 }} = \frac{{y - 0}}{0} = \frac{{z - 0}}{{ - 4}}\]
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\frac{1}{4}}&0&0 \\
  1&{ - \sqrt 2 }&0 \\
  {3\sqrt 3 }&0&{ - 4}
\end{array}} \right| = \sqrt 2 \]
\[d = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {51} }}\]
\[\frac{1}{{{d^2}}} = 51\]

Question:
If the lines \[\vec r\; = \;(\hat i - \;\hat j + \;\hat k) + \;\lambda \;(3\hat j - \;\hat k)\] and \[\vec r = \;(\alpha \hat i - \;\hat j)\; + \;\mu \;(2\hat i - \;3\hat k)\] are 
co-planar, then distance of the plane containing these two lines from the point \[(\alpha ,\;0,\;0)\] is :

Let the plane 2x + 3y + z + 20 = 0 be rotated through a right angle about its line of intersection with the plane x – 3y + 5z = 8. If the mirror image of the point \[\left( {2, - \frac{1}{2},2} \right)\] in the rotated plane is B (a, b, c), then :
Solution:
Let equation of rotated plane be :
\[(2x + 3y + z + 20) + \lambda (x - 3y + 5z - 8) = 0\]
\[(2 + \lambda )x + (3 - 3\lambda )y + (1 + 5\lambda )z + 20 - 80\lambda = 0\]
Above plane is perpendicular to \[2x + 3y + z + 20 = 0\]
So, \[(2 + \lambda ).2 + (3 - 3\lambda ).3 + (1 + 5\lambda ).1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 7\]
\[ \Rightarrow \]Equation of rotated plane : \[x - 2y + 4z - 4 = 0\]
Mirror image of \[A\left( {2,\frac{{ - 1}}{2},2} \right)\] in rotated plane is B(a, b, c)
Equation of AB : \[\frac{{x - 2}}{1} = \frac{{y + 1/2}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 2}}{4} = k\]
Let coordinate of B be \[\left( {2 + k,\frac{{ - 1}}{2} - 2k,2 + 4k} \right)\]
midpoint of AB is \[\left( {2 + \frac{k}{2},\frac{{ - 1}}{2} - k,2 + 2k} \right)\] which will lie on the plane x – 2y + 4z – 4 = 0
Hence \[k = \frac{{ - 2}}{3}\]
Therefore B is \[\left( {\frac{4}{3},\frac{5}{6},\frac{{ - 2}}{3}} \right) \equiv \left( {\frac{8}{6},\frac{5}{6},\frac{{ - 4}}{6}} \right)\]
So, \[\frac{a}{8} = \frac{b}{5} = \frac{c}{{ - 4}}\]

Question: Let $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$ be the image of the point $P\left( {2,3,5} \right)$ in the plane $2x + y - 3z = 6$. Then $\alpha  + \beta  + \gamma $ is equal to
Solution:
$\frac{{\alpha - 2}}{2} = \frac{{\beta - 3}}{1} = \frac{{\gamma - 5}}{{ - 3}} = - 2\left( {\frac{{2{x^2} + 3 - 3 \times 5 - 6}}{{{2^2} + {1^2} + 1 - {3^2}}}} \right) = 2$
$\frac{{\alpha - 2}}{2} = 2 \Rightarrow \alpha = 6$
$\beta - 3 = 2 \Rightarrow \beta = 5$
$\gamma - 5 = - 6 \Rightarrow \gamma = - 1$

$\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 10$

Question: If the line, $\frac{{x - 3}}{2} = \frac{{y + 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + 4}}{3}$ lies in the plane, $lx + my - z = 9$, then ${l^2} + {m^2}$ is equal to:
Solution:
Given plane is
$lx + my - z = 9$
Given line is
$\frac{{x - 3}}{2} = \frac{{y + 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + 4}}{3}$
A fixed point on this line is $P\left( {3, - 2, - 4} \right)$
Line lies in the plane and Point P also lies on plane
$3l - 2m + 4 = 9 \Rightarrow 3l - 2m = 5\,\,\,\,\,\,.....\left( 1 \right)$
$2l - m - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow 2l - m = 3\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,......\left( 2 \right)$
On solving eq. (1) & (2)
$\begin{gathered}
  l = 1 \\
  m = - 1 \\
\end{gathered} $
$\therefore {l^2} + {m^2} = 2$

Question: If the equation of the plane containing the line $x + 2y + 3z - 4 = 0 = 2x + y - z + 5$ and perpendicular to the plane $\vec r = \left( {\hat i - \hat j} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k} \right)$ is $ax + by + cz = 4$, then $\left( {a - b + c} \right)$ is equal to
Solution:
D.R’s of line ${\vec n_1} = 5\hat i + 7\hat j - 3\hat k$
D.R’s of normal of second plane
${\vec n_2} = 5\hat i - 2\hat j - 3\hat k$
${\vec n_1} \times {\vec n_2} = - 27\hat i - 30\hat j - 25\hat k$
A point on the required plane is $\left( {0, - \frac{{11}}{5},\frac{{14}}{5}} \right)$
The equation of required plane is
$27x + 30y + 25z = 4$
$\therefore \,\,\,a - b + c = 22$

Question: If the lines \[\frac{{x - k}}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{2} = \frac{{z - 3}}{3}\]and \[\frac{{x + 1}}{3} = \frac{{y + 2}}{2} = \frac{{z + 3}}{1}\] are co-planar, then the value of k is _____________.
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {k + 1}&4&6 \\
  1&2&3 \\
  3&2&1
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
{\[\because \] Shortest distance between them is zero}
\[\left( {k + 1} \right)\left[ {2 - 6} \right] - 4\left[ {1 - 9} \right] + 6\left[ {2 - 6} \right] = 0\]
\[k = 1\]

Question: Let the plane $P:8x + {\alpha _1}y + {\alpha _2}z + 12 = 0$ be parallel to the line $L:\frac{{x + 2}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{3} = \frac{{z + 4}}{5}$. If the intercept of $P$ on the y-axis is 1, then the distance between $P$ and $L$ is:
Solution:
$P:8x + {\alpha _1}y + {\alpha _2}z + 12 = 0$
$L:\frac{{x + 2}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{3} = \frac{{z + 4}}{5}$
$\because \,\,P$ is parallel to $L$
$ \Rightarrow 8\left( 2 \right) + {\alpha _1}\left( 3 \right) + 5\left( {{\alpha _2}} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 3{\alpha _1} + 5\left( {{\alpha _2}} \right) = - 16$
Also, y-intercept of plane $P$ is 1
$ \Rightarrow {\alpha _1} = - 12$
And ${\alpha _2} = 4$
$ \Rightarrow $ Equation of plane $P$ is $2x - 3y + z + 3 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow $ Distance of line $L$ from plane $P$ is
$ = \left| {\frac{{0 - 3\left( 6 \right) + 1 + 3}}{{\sqrt {4 + 9 + 1} }}} \right|$
$ = \sqrt {14} $

Question: A plane E is perpendicular to the two planes \[2x - 2y + z = 0\] and \[x - y\; + \;2z = \;4,\] and passes through the point \[P\;(1,\; - 1,\;1).\] If the distance of the plane E from the point Q (a, a, 2) is \[3\sqrt 2 ,\] then \[{(PQ)^2}\] is equal to
Solution:
First plane, \[{P_1} = 2x - 2y + z = \;0,\] normal vector \[ \equiv \;{\bar n_1} = \;(2,\; - 2,\;1)\]
Second plane, \[{P_2}\; \equiv \;x - \;y + \;2z\; = \;4,\] normal vector \[ \equiv \;{\bar n_2} = \;(1,\; - 1,\;2)\]
Plane perpendicular to \[{P_1}\] and \[{P_2}\] will have normal vector \[{\bar n_3}\]
Where \[{\bar n_3} = \;({\bar n_1}\; \times \;{\bar n_2})\]
Hence, \[{\bar n_3} = \;( - 3,\; - \;3,\;0)\]
Equation of plane E through P (1, –1, 1) and \[{\bar n_3}\] as normal vector
\[(x - 1,\;y + 1,\;z - 1)\;( - 3,\; - 3,\;0)\; = \;0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;x\; + \;y = \;0\; \equiv \;E\]
Distance of PQ $\left( {a,a,2} \right)$ from \[E = \;\left| {\frac{{2a}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right|\]
as given, \[\left| {\frac{{2a}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right|\; = \;3\sqrt 2 \;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;a = \; \pm \;3\]
Hence, \[Q\; \equiv \;( \pm 3,\; \pm 3,\;2)\]
Distance \[7Q\; = \;\sqrt {21} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\;{(PQ)^2} = \;21\]

Question: If a point R(4, y, z) lies on the line segment joining the points P(2, –3, 4) and Q(8, 0, 10), then distance of R from the origin is :
Solution:
Here, P, Q, R are collinear
$\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \overrightarrow {PR} = \lambda \overrightarrow {PQ} \\
  2\hat i + \left( {y + 3} \right)\hat j + \left( {z - 4} \right)\hat k = \lambda \left[ {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k} \right] \\
   \Rightarrow 6\lambda = 2,y + 3 = 3\lambda ,z - 4 = 6\lambda \\
   \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{3},y = - 2,z = 6 \\
  \therefore {\text{ Point }}R\left( {4, - 2,6} \right) \\
  {\text{Now, }}OR = \sqrt {{{\left( 4 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2} + {{\left( 6 \right)}^2}} = \sqrt {56} = 2\sqrt {14} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question:
If the shortest distance between the lines
\[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}\]and \[\frac{x}{1} = \frac{y}{\alpha } = \frac{{z - 5}}{1}\]is \[\frac{5}{{\sqrt 6 }},\]then the sum of all possible values of \[\alpha \] is
Solution:
\[{L_1}:\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}\]
\[{L_1}:\frac{x}{1} = \frac{y}{\alpha } = \frac{{z - 5}}{1}\]
\[\vec x = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&3&4 \\
  1&\alpha &1
\end{array}} \right| = \hat i\left( {3 - 4\alpha } \right) - \hat j\left( { - 2} \right) + \hat k\left( {2\alpha - 3} \right)\]
\[S.D. = \left| {\frac{{\overrightarrow {BA} .\overrightarrow n }}{{\left| {\vec n} \right|}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{\left( {\hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k} \right).\vec n}}{{\left| {\vec n} \right|}}} \right|\]
6(13 – 8\[\alpha \])2 = 25((4\[\alpha \]–3)2 + (2\[\alpha \] – 3)2 + 16)
6(64a2 – 280\[\alpha \] + 169) = 25(20\[\alpha \]2 – 36\[\alpha \] + 34)
\[ \Rightarrow 116{\alpha ^2} + 348\alpha - 164 = 0\]
\[{\alpha _1} + {\alpha _2} = \frac{{ - 348}}{{116}} = - 3\]

Question: Let the lines \[\frac{{x - 1}}{\lambda } = \frac{{y - 2}}{1} = \frac{{z - 3}}{2}\] and \[\frac{{x + 26}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{y + 18}}{3} = \frac{{z + 28}}{\lambda }\] be coplanar and P be the plane containing these two lines. Then which of the following points does NOT lies on P?
Solution:
Given, \[{L_1}:\frac{{x - 1}}{\lambda } = \frac{{y - 2}}{1} = \frac{{z - 3}}{2}\]
and \[{L_2}:\frac{{x + 26}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{y + 18}}{3} = \frac{{z + 28}}{\lambda }\] are coplanar
\[ \Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {27}&{20}&{31} \\
  \lambda &1&2 \\
  { - 2}&3&\lambda
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda = 3\]
Now, normal of plane P, which contains L1 and L2
\[ = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  3&1&2 \\
  { - 2}&3&3
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[ = - 3\hat i - 13\hat j + 11\hat k\]
\[ \Rightarrow \]Equation of required plane P :
\[3x + \;13y\; - \;11z + \;4 = \;0\]
(0, 4, 5) does not lie on plane P.

Question: The plane passing through the point $(4,-1,2)$ and parallel to the lines $\frac{{x + 2}}{3} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + 1}}{2}$ and $\frac{{x - 2}}{1} = \frac{{y - 3}}{2} = \frac{{z - 4}}{3}$ also passes through the point:
Solution:
Equation of required plane is
\[\begin{gathered}
  \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 4}&{y + 1}&{z - 2} \\
  3&{ - 1}&2 \\
  1&2&3
\end{array}} \right| = 0 \\
  \left( {x - 4} \right)\left( { - 3 - 4} \right) - \left( {y + 1} \right)\left( {9 - 2} \right) + \left( {z - 2} \right)\left( {6 + 1} \right) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow - 7\left( {x - 4} \right) - 7\left( {y + 1} \right) + 7\left( {z - 2} \right) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow x - 4 + y + 1 - z + 2 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow x + y - z - 1 = 0 \\
\end{gathered} \]
$\because $ point (1, 1, 1) satisfies this equation
$\therefore $ point (1, 1, 1) lies on the plane

Question: The distance of the point \[P\left( {3,4,4} \right)\] from the point of intersection of the line joining the points \[Q\left( {3, - 4, - 5} \right)\] and \[R\left( {2, - 3,1} \right)\] and the plane \[2x + y + z = 7,\] is equal to ______.
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow {QR} :\frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y + 4}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + 5}}{{ - 6}} = r\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {x,y,z} \right) \equiv \left( {r + 3, - r - 4, - 6r - 5} \right)\]
Now, satisfying it in the given plane.
\[2\left( {r + 3} \right) + \left( { - r - 4} \right) + \left( { - 6r - 5} \right) = 7\]
We get \[r = - 2.\]
so, required point of intersection is \[T\left( {1, - 2,7} \right)\]
Hence, \[PT = 7.\]

Question: Let $\theta $ be the angle between the planes ${P_1} = \vec r.\left( {\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right) = 9$ and ${P_2} = \vec r.\left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) = 15$. Let $L$ be the line that meets ${P_2}$ at the point $\left( {4, - 2,5} \right)$ and makes an angle $\theta $ with the normal of ${P_2}$. If $\alpha $ is the angle between $L$ and ${P_2}$ then $\left( {{{\tan }^2}\theta } \right)\left( {{{\cot }^2}\alpha } \right)$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
$\cos \theta = \frac{{\left( {\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right) \cdot \left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right)}}{6} = \frac{{2 - 1 + 2}}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$
$\theta = \frac{\pi }{3},\,\,\alpha = \frac{\pi }{6}$
$\left( {{{\tan }^2}\theta } \right)\left( {{{\cot }^2}\alpha } \right)$
$\left( 3 \right)\left( 3 \right) = 9$

Question: The distance of the line \[\frac{{x - 2}}{2} = \frac{{y - 6}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}\] from the point (1, 4, 0) along the line \[\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{2} = \frac{{z + 3}}{3}\] is
Solution:
Let the parallel line is
\[\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 4}}{2} = \frac{{z - 0}}{3}\]
so their point of intersection is
(λ + 1, 2λ + 4 3λ ) = (2t + 2, 3t + 6, 4t + 3)
λ = 2t + 1
2λ + 4 = 3t + 6 ⇒ t = 0
so POI is (2,6,3)
so distance = \[\sqrt {{{(2 - 1)}^2} + {{\left( {6 - 4} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {3 - 0} \right)}^2}} = \sqrt {14} \]

Question: The shortest distance between the lines $\frac{{x + 2}}{1} = \frac{y}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 5}}{2}$ and $\frac{{x - 4}}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z + 3}}{0}$ is
Solution:
Given lines
$\frac{{x + 2}}{1} = \frac{y}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 5}}{2}$ & $\frac{{x - 4}}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z + 3}}{0}$
Formula for shortest distance
S.D. $ = \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {{x_2} - {x_1}}&{{y_2} - {y_1}}&{{z_2} - {z_1}} \\
  {{a_1}}&{{b_1}}&{{c_1}} \\
  {{a_2}}&{{b_2}}&{{c_2}}
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  {{a_1}}&{{b_1}}&{{c_1}} \\
  {{a_2}}&{{b_2}}&{{c_2}}
\end{array}} \right|}}$
$ = \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  6&1&{ - 8} \\
  1&{ - 2}&2 \\
  1&2&0
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&{ - 2}&2 \\
  1&2&0
\end{array}} \right|}} = \frac{{54}}{6} = 9$

Question: If the equation of the plane passing through the point $\left( {1,1,2} \right)$ and perpendicular to the line $x - 3y + 2z - 1 = 0,4x - y + z$ is $Ax + By + Cz = 1,$ then $140\left( {C - B + A} \right)$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
$x - 3y + 2z - 1 = 0$
$4x - y + z = 0$
${\vec n_1} \times {\vec n_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&{ - 3}&2 \\
  4&{ - 1}&1
\end{array}} \right|$
$ = - \hat i + 7\hat j + 11\hat k$
Drs of normal to the plane is -1, 7, 11
Equation of plane:
$ - 1\left( {x - 1} \right) + 7\left( {y - 1} \right) + 11\left( {z - 2} \right) = 0$
$ - x + 7y + 11z = 28$
$\frac{{ - 1}}{{28}}x + \frac{{7y}}{{28}} + \frac{{11z}}{{28}} = 1$
$Ax + By + Cz = 1$
$140\left( {C - B + A} \right) = 140\left( {\frac{{11}}{{28}} - \frac{7}{{28}} - \frac{1}{{28}}} \right)$
$ = 140 \times \frac{3}{{28}} = 15$

Question: Let $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$ be the foot of perpendicular from the point $\left( {1,2,3} \right)$ on the line $\frac{{x + 3}}{5} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z + 4}}{3}$. Then $19\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right)$ is equal to:
Solution:
Let foot $P\left( {5k - 3,2k + 1,3k - 4} \right)$
DR’s → AP: $5k - 4,2k - 1,3k - 7$
DR’s → Line: 5, 2, 3
Condition of perpendicular lines $\left( {25k - 20} \right) + \left( {4k - 2} \right) + \left( {9k - 21} \right) = 0$
Then $k = \frac{{43}}{{38}}$
Then $19\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right) = 101$

Question: If Q (0, –1, –3) is the image of the point P in the plane 3x – y + 4z = 2 and R is the point (3, –1 , –2), then the area (in sq. units) of  ∆PQR is :
Solution:
Image of Q (0, –1, –3) in plane is,
\[\frac{{(x - 0)}}{3} = \frac{{(y + 1)}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + 3}}{{ + 4}} = \frac{{ - 2(1 - 12 - 2)}}{{9 + 1 + 16}} = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow x = 3,y = - 2,z = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow P(3, - 2,1),Q(0, - 1, - 3),R(3, - 1, - 2)\]
\[\therefore {\text{ }}Area{\text{ }}of{\text{ }}\Delta PQR{\text{ }}is\]
\[\frac{1}{2}|\vec {QP} \times \vec {QR}| = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&k \\
  3&{ - 1}&4 \\
  3&0&1
\end{array}} \right|} \right|\]
\[ = \frac{1}{2}|\{ \hat i( - 1) - \hat j(3 - 12) + \hat k(3)\} |\]
\[ = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {(1 + 81 + 9)} = \frac{{\sqrt {91} }}{2}\]

Question:
Let the direction cosines of two lines satisfy the equations: $4 \ell+m-n=0$ and $2 m n+10 n \ell+3 \ell m=0$.
Then the cosine of the acute angle between these lines is :
Solution:
Direction cosines of two lines satisfy the equation $\Rightarrow 4 \ell+\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{n}=0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
2 \mathrm{mn}+10 \mathrm{n} \ell+3 \ell \mathrm{~m}=0 \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
& we know
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow \ell^{2}+\mathrm{m}^{2}-\mathrm{n}^{2}=1 \tag{3}
\end{equation*}
$$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=4 \ell+\mathrm{m}$ putting in eqn.
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}(2 \mathrm{~m}+10 \ell)+3 \ell \mathrm{~m}=0$
$\Rightarrow(4 \ell+\mathrm{m})(2 \mathrm{~m}+10 \ell)+3 \ell \mathrm{~m}=0$
$\Rightarrow 8 \ell \mathrm{~m}+40 \ell^{2}+2 \mathrm{~m}^{2}+10 \ell \mathrm{~m}+3 \ell \mathrm{~m}=0$
$\Rightarrow 40 \ell^{2}+21 \ell \mathrm{~m}+2 \mathrm{~m}^{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow(8 \ell+m)(5 \ell+2 m)=0$

Case 1: $8 \ell+\mathrm{m}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}=-8 \ell$

Case 2: $5 \ell+2 \mathrm{~m}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}=\frac{-5}{2} \ell$

So direction ratio of $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ is $\ell,-8 \ell,-4 \ell$
& direction ratio of $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ is $\ell, \frac{-5 \ell}{2}, \frac{3 \ell}{2}$
$\cos \theta=\left|\frac{\ell^{2}+20 \ell^{2}-6 \ell^{2}}{\sqrt{\ell^{2}+64 \ell^{2}+16 \ell^{2}} \sqrt{\ell^{2}+\frac{25 \ell^{2}}{4}+\frac{9 \ell^{2}}{4}}}\right|$
$=\frac{15 \ell^{2}}{(9 \ell) \frac{\sqrt{38} \ell}{2}}=\frac{10}{3 \sqrt{38}}$

Ans. $=\frac{10}{3 \sqrt{38}}$

Question: Let $P$ be the plane, passing through the point $\left( {1, - 1, - 5} \right)$ and perpendicular to the line joining the points $\left( {4,1, - 3} \right)$ and $\left( {2,4,3} \right)$. Then the distance of $P$ from the point $\left( {3, - 2,2} \right)$ is
Solution:
Equation of plane:
$2\left( {x - 1} \right) - 3\left( {y + 1} \right) - 6\left( {z + 5} \right) = 0$
Or $2x - 3y - 6z = 35$
$ \Rightarrow $ Required distance $ = \frac{{\left| {2\left( 3 \right) - 3\left( { - 2} \right) - 6\left( 2 \right) - 35} \right|}}{{\sqrt {4 + 9 + 36} }}$
$ = 5$

Question: Let P be an arbitrary point having sum of the squares of the distances from the planes \[x + y + z = 0,\,\,\,lx - nz = 0\] and \[x - 2y + z = 0,\] equal to 9. If the locus of the point P is \[{x^2} + \;{y^2} + \;{z^2} = \;9,\] then the value of \[l - n\] is equal to ................. .
Solution:
Let point P is \[\left( {\alpha ,\,\,\beta ,\,\,\gamma } \right)\]
\[{\left( {\frac{{\alpha + \beta + \gamma }}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)^2} + {\left( {\frac{{l\alpha - n\gamma }}{{\sqrt {{l^2} + {n^2}} }}} \right)^2} + {\left( {\frac{{\alpha - 2\beta + \gamma }}{{\sqrt 6 }}} \right)^2} = 9\]
Locus is \[\frac{{{{\left( {x + y + z} \right)}^2}}}{3} + \frac{{{{\left( {lx - nz} \right)}^2}}}{{{l^2} + {n^2}}} + \frac{{{{\left( {x - 2y + z} \right)}^2}}}{6} = 9\]
In this equation, Coefficient of \[xz\,\, = \frac{2}{3} + \frac{2}{6} - \frac{{2ln}}{{{l^2} + {n^2}}} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{2ln}}{{{l^2} + {n^2}}} = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,{\left( {l - n} \right)^2} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,l - n = 0\]

Question: Let a plane P contain two lines \[\bar r = \;\hat i + \;\lambda \;(\hat i + \;\hat j),\;\lambda \; \in R\] and \[\bar r =  - \;\hat j + \;\mu \;(\hat j - \hat k),\;\mu \; \in \;R.\] If \[Q\;(\alpha ,\;\beta ,\;\gamma )\] is the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point M (1,0, 1) to P, then \[3\;(\alpha  + \;\beta  + \;\gamma )\] equals ………. .
Solution:
\[\left. {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\bar r\; = \;\hat i + \;\lambda \;(\hat i + \;\hat j)} \\
  {\bar r = \; - \hat j + \;\mu \;(\hat j - \hat k)}
\end{array}} \right]\]
\[\bar n\; = \;\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&1&0 \\
  0&1&{ - 1}
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[ = - \hat i + \hat j + \hat k\]
equation of plane
\[ - 1\;(x - 1)\; + \;1\;(y - 0)\; + \;1\;(z - 0)\; = \;0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;x - y - z - 1\; = \;0\]
Foot of \[{ \bot ^r}\] from M(1, 0, 1)
\[\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \;\frac{{y - 0}}{{ - 1}} = \;\frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 1}} = - \;\frac{{(1 - 0 - 1 - 1)}}{3}\]
\[x = \;\frac{4}{3},\;y = \;\frac{{ - 1}}{3},\;z = \;\frac{2}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;\left. {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\alpha = \;\frac{4}{3}} \\
  {\beta = \;\frac{{ - 1}}{3}} \\
  {\gamma = \;\frac{2}{3}}
\end{array}} \right]\]
\[\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \;\frac{4}{3} - \frac{1}{3} + \frac{2}{3} = \;\frac{5}{3}\]
\[3\;(\alpha + \beta + \gamma )\; = \;5\]

Question: If the line of intersection of the planes \[ax + \;by = \;3\] and \[ax + \;by + \;cz = \;0,\;a\; > \;0\] makes an angle \[30^\circ \] with the plane \[y - z + \;2 = \;0,\] then the direction cosines of the line are :
Solution:
\[\vec n\; = \;\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  a&b&0 \\
  a&b&c
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[ = \;bc\hat i - \;ac\hat j\]
Direction ratios of line are b, –a, 0
Direction ratios of normal of the plane are 0, 1, –1
\[\cos \,{60^o}\; = \;\left| {\frac{{ - \,a}}{{|\sqrt 2 |\;\sqrt {{b^2} + \;{a^2}} }}} \right| = \;\frac{1}{2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;b = \; \pm \;a\]
So, D.R.’s can be \[( \pm \;a,\; - a,\;0)\]
\[\therefore \] D.C.’s can be \[ \pm \;\left( {\frac{{ \pm \,1}}{{\sqrt 2 }},\; - \;\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }},\;0} \right)\]

Question: Let the line L pass through (1, 1, 1) and intersect the lines \[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{3} = \frac{{z - 1}}{4}\] and \[\frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y - 4}}{2} = \frac{z}{1}\] Then, which of the following points lies on the line L?
Solution:
\[Dr's{\text{ of AC}} \Rightarrow {\text{2}}\lambda {\text{, 3}}\lambda - 2,4\lambda \]
\[Dr's{\text{ of BC}} \Rightarrow \mu + 2,{\text{ }}2\mu + 3,\;\mu - 1\].
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{\mu + 2}}{{2\lambda }} = \frac{{2\mu + 3}}{{3\lambda - 2}} = \frac{{\mu - 1}}{{4\lambda }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2(\mu + 2) = \mu - 1 \Rightarrow \mu = - 5\]
\[ \Rightarrow Dr's{\text{ of BC}} \Rightarrow {\text{3,7,6}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow equation{\text{ of L}} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 1}}{3} = \frac{{y - 1}}{7} = \frac{{z - 1}}{6}\]
\[(7,15,13){\text{ satisfies}}{\text{.}}\]

Question: If the lines x = ay + b, z = cy + d and x = a'z + b', y = c' z + d' are perpendicular, then:
Solution:
First line is: x = ay + b, z = cy + d
\[\frac{{x - b}}{a} = \frac{y}{1} = \frac{{z - d}}{c}\]
and another line is: \[x = a'z + b',\,\,y = c'z + d'\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\frac{{x - b'}}{{a'}} = \frac{{y - d'}}{{c'}} = \frac{z}{1}\]
∵ Both lines are perpendicular to each other,
∴ \[aa' + c' + c = 0\]

Question: The length of the perpendicular from the point \[(1,\; - 2,\;5)\] on the line passing through \[(1,\;2,\;4)\] and parallel to the line \[x + \;y - z = \;0\; = x\; - 2y\; + \;3z - 5\] is :
Solution:
D.r’s of the line \[ = \;\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&1&{ - 1} \\
  1&{ - 2}&3
\end{array}} \right| = \;\hat i - 4\hat j - 3\hat k\]
\[\therefore \] equation of line is
\[\vec r = \;\hat i + \;2\hat j + \;4\hat k + \;\lambda \;(\hat i - \;4\hat j - \;3\hat k)\]
Let A (1, 2, 4) and P be \[(1 + \;\lambda ,\;2 - 4\lambda ,\;4 - \;3\lambda )\]
\[\therefore \;\;\overrightarrow {PA} \,.\;(\hat i - \;4\hat j - \;3\hat k) = \;0\]
\[\lambda \; = \;\frac{1}{2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;P\;\left( {\frac{1}{2},\;2,\;\frac{{ - 5}}{2}} \right)\]
\[|AP|\; = \;\sqrt {\frac{{21}}{2}} \]

Question: ABC is a triangle in a plane with vertices $A\left( {2,3,5} \right),\,\,B\left( { - 1,3,2} \right)$ and $C\left( {\lambda ,5,\mu } \right)\,.$ If the median through A is equally inclined to the coordinate axes, then the value of $\left( {{\lambda ^3} + {\mu ^3} + 5} \right)$ is

If a plane passes through the points $\left( { - 1,k,0} \right),\left( {2,k, - 1} \right),\left( {1,1,2} \right)$ and is parallel to the line $\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{2y + 1}}{2} = \frac{{z + 1}}{{ - 1}},$ then the value of $\frac{{{k^2} + 1}}{{\left( {k - 1} \right)\left( {k - 2} \right)}}$ is
Solution:
$\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{2y + 1}}{2} = \frac{{z + 1}}{{ - 1}}$
$\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y + \frac{1}{2}}}{1} = \frac{{z + 1}}{{ - 1}}$
Points: $A\left( { - 1,k,0} \right),B\left( {2,k, - 1} \right),C\left( {1,1,2} \right)$
$\overrightarrow {CA} = - 2\hat i + \left( {k - 1} \right)\hat j - 2\hat k$
$\overrightarrow {CB} = \hat i + \left( {k - 1} \right)\hat j - 3\hat k$
$\overrightarrow {CA} \times \overrightarrow {CB} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  { - 2}&{k - 1}&{ - 2} \\
  1&{k - 1}&{ - 3}
\end{array}} \right|$
$ = \hat i\left( { - 3k + 3 + 2k - 2} \right) - \hat j\left( {6 + 2} \right) + \hat k\left( { - 2\hat k + 2 - k + 1} \right)$
$ = \left( {1 - k} \right)\hat i - 8\hat j + \left( {3 - 3k} \right)\hat k$
The line $\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y + \frac{1}{2}}}{1} = \frac{{z + 1}}{{ - 1}}$ is perpendicular to normal vector.
$\therefore 1.\left( {1 - k} \right) + 1\left( { - 8} \right) + \left( { - 1} \right)\left( {3 - 3k} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 1 - k - 8 - 3 + 3k = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 2k = 10 \Rightarrow \boxed{k = 5}$
$\therefore \frac{{{k^2} + 1}}{{\left( {k - 1} \right)\left( {k - 2} \right)}} = \frac{{26}}{{4 \cdot 3}} = \frac{{13}}{6}$

Question: The distance, of the point $\left( {7, - 2,11} \right)$ from the line $\frac{{x - 6}}{1} = \frac{{y - 4}}{0} = \frac{{z - 8}}{3}$ along the line $\frac{{x - 5}}{2} = \frac{{y - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{z - 5}}{6}$ is:
Solution:
$B = \left( {2\lambda + 7, - 3\lambda - 2,6\lambda + 11} \right)$

Point B lies on $\frac{{x - 6}}{1} = \frac{{y - 4}}{0} = \frac{{z - 8}}{3}$
$\frac{{2\lambda + 7 - 6}}{1} = \frac{{ - 3\lambda - 2 - 4}}{0} = \frac{{6\lambda + 11 - 8}}{3}$
$ - 3\lambda - 6 = 0$
$\lambda = - 2$
$B \Rightarrow \left( {3,4, - 1} \right)$
$AB = \sqrt {{{\left( {7 - 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {4 + 2} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {11 + 1} \right)}^2}} $
$ = \sqrt {16 + 36 + 144} $
$ = \sqrt {196} = 14$

Question: Consider the line L passing through the points (1, 2, 3) and (2, 3, 5). The distance of the point $\left( {\frac{{11}}{3},\,\frac{{11}}{3},\,\frac{{19}}{3}} \right)$ from the line L along the line $\frac{{3x - 11}}{2} = \frac{{3y - 11}}{1} = \frac{{3z - 19}}{2}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\frac{{x - 1}}{{2 - 1}} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{3 - 2}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{{5 - 3}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{1} = \frac{{z - 3}}{2} = \lambda $

$B\left( {1 + \lambda ,\,2 + \lambda ,\,3 + 2\lambda } \right)$
D.R. of \[AB = - < \frac{{3\lambda - 8}}{3},\,\frac{{3\lambda - 5}}{3},\,\frac{{6\lambda - 10}}{3} > \]
$B\left( {\frac{5}{3},\,\frac{8}{3},\,\frac{{13}}{3}} \right)\,\frac{{3\lambda - 8}}{{3\lambda - 5}} = \frac{2}{1} \Rightarrow 3\lambda - 8 = 6\lambda - 10$
$3\lambda = 2$
$\lambda = \frac{2}{3}$
$AB = \frac{{\sqrt {36 + 9 + 36} }}{3} = \frac{9}{3} = 3$

Question: The angle between the straight lines, whose direction cosines are given by the equation \[2l + 2m - n = 0\] and \[mn + nl + lm = 0,\] is :
Solution:
We have
\[2l + 2m - n = 0\]
 \[n = 2\left( {l + m} \right)\] and
\[mn + nl + lm = 0,\]
\[lm + n\left( {l + m} \right) = 0\]
\[lm + 2{\left( {l + m} \right)^2} = 0\]
\[2{l^2} + 2{m^2} + 5ml = 0\]
\[2{\left( {\frac{l}{m}} \right)^2} + 2 + 5\left( {\frac{l}{m}} \right) = 0\]
Let \[\frac{l}{m} = t\]
\[2{t^2} + 5t + 2 = 0\]
\[\left( {t + 2} \right)\left( {2t + 1} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow t = - 2\] or \[t = - \frac{1}{2}\]
(i) \[t = - 2\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{l}{m} = - 2\] and \[\frac{n}{m} = - 2\]
\[\therefore \left( { - 2m,\,\,m,\,\, - 2m} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( { - 2,\,\,1,\, - 2} \right)\]
(ii) \[t = - \frac{1}{2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{l}{m} = - \frac{1}{2}\] and \[n = - 2l\]
\[\therefore \left( {l,\,\, - 2l,\,\, - 2l} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {1, - 2, - 2} \right)\]
\[\cos \theta = \frac{{ - 2 - 2 + 4}}{{\sqrt 9 \sqrt 9 }} = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{2}\]

Question: Let $a,b \in R$. If the mirror image of the point P(a, 6, 9) with respect to the line $\frac{{x - 3}}{7} = \frac{{y - 2}}{5} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 9}}$ is $\left( {20,b, - a - 9} \right)$, then $\left| {a + b} \right|$ is equal to
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

Mid – point of $p\left( {a,\;6,\;9} \right)$ and Q$\left( {20,\;b,\; - a - 9} \right)$ is $\left( {\frac{{a + 20}}{2},\;\frac{{6 + b}}{2},\;\frac{{ - a}}{2}} \right)$ lies on the line
So $\frac{{\frac{{a + 20}}{2} - 3}}{7} = \frac{{\frac{{6 + b}}{2} - 2}}{5} = \frac{{\frac{{ - a}}{2} - 1}}{{ - 9}}$
$\frac{{a + 14}}{{14}} = \frac{{2 + b}}{{10}} = \frac{{a + 2}}{{18}}\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;b = - 32,\;a = - 56$
$ \Rightarrow \;\;\left| {a + b} \right| = 88$

Also $PQ \bot $ to given line for $a =  - 56,b =  - 32$

Question:
Let the plane containing the line of intersection of the planes
${P_1}:x + \left( {\lambda + 4} \right)y + z = 1$ and
${P_2}:2x + y + z = 2$ pass through the points $\left( {0,1,0} \right)$ and $\left( {1,0,1} \right)$. Then the distance of the point $\left( {2\lambda ,\lambda - \lambda } \right)$ from the plane \[{P_2}\] is
Solution:
Equation of plane passing through point of intersection of ${P_1}$ and ${P_2}$
$P = {P_1} + k{P_2}$
$\left( {x + \left( {\lambda + 4} \right)y + z - 1} \right) + k\left( {2x + y + z - 2} \right) = 0$
Passing through $\left( {0,1,0} \right)$ and $\left( {1,0,1} \right)$
$\left( {\lambda + 4 - 1} \right) + k\left( {1 - 2} \right) = 0$
$\left( {\lambda + 3} \right) - k = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)$
Also passing $\left( {1,0,1} \right)$
$\left( {1 + 1 - 1} \right) + k\left( {2 + 1 - 2} \right) = 0$
$1 + k = 0$
$k = - 1$
Put in (1)
$\begin{gathered}
  \lambda + 3 + 1 = 0 \\
  \lambda = - 4 \\
\end{gathered} $
Then point $\left( {2\lambda ,\lambda , - \lambda } \right)$
$\left( { - 8, - 4,4} \right)$
$d = \left| {\frac{{ - 16 - 4 + 4 - 2}}{{\sqrt 6 }}} \right|$
$d = \frac{{18}}{{\sqrt 6 }} \times \frac{{\sqrt 6 }}{{\sqrt 6 }} = 3\sqrt 6 $

Question: The shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x - 3}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 1}}\] and \[\frac{{x + 3}}{2} = \frac{{y - 6}}{1} = \frac{{z - 5}}{3}\] is:
Solution:
\[\frac{{x - 3}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 1}}\]
\[\frac{{x + 3}}{2} = \frac{{y - 6}}{1} = \frac{{z - 5}}{3}\]
A = (3, 2, 1) B = (3, 6, 5)
\[\overrightarrow {{n_1}} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k\]
\[\overrightarrow {{n_2}} = 2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k\]
\[\overrightarrow {BA} = 6\hat i - 4\hat j - 4\hat k\]
SHORTEST DISTANCE \[ = \frac{{\left[ {\overrightarrow {BA} \,\overrightarrow {{n_1}} \,\overrightarrow {{n_2}} } \right]}}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{n_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{n_2}} } \right|}}\]
\[\overrightarrow {{n_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{n_2}} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&3&{ - 1} \\
  2&1&3
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[ = 10\hat i - 8\hat j - 4\hat k\]
\[[\overrightarrow {BA} \,\,\overrightarrow {{n_1}} \,\,\overrightarrow {{n_2}} ] = 60 + 32 + 16 = 108\]
\[\left| {\overrightarrow {{n_1}} \, \times \,\overrightarrow {{n_2}} } \right| = \sqrt {100 + 64 + 16} = \sqrt {180} \]
\[S.D = \frac{{108}}{{\sqrt {180} }} = \frac{{108}}{{6\sqrt 5 }} = \frac{{18}}{{\sqrt 5 }}\]

Question: The plane $2x - y + z = 4$ intersects the line segment joining the points $A\left( {a, - 2,4} \right)$ and $B\left( {2,b, - 3} \right)$ at the point $C$ in the ratio $2:1$ and the distance of the point $C$ from the origin is $\sqrt 5 $. If $ab > 0$ and $P$ is the point $\left( {a - b,b,2b - a} \right)$ then $C{P^2}$ is equal to:
Solution:
$A\left( {a, - 2,4} \right),B\left( {2,b, - 3} \right)$
$AC:CB = 2:1$
$ \Rightarrow C \equiv \left( {\frac{{a + 4}}{3},\frac{{2b - 2}}{3},\frac{{ - 2}}{3}} \right)$
$C$ lies on $2x - y + 2 = 4$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{2a + 8}}{3} = \frac{{2b - 2}}{3} - \frac{2}{3} = 4$
$ \Rightarrow a - b = 2\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)$
Also $OC = \sqrt 5 $
$ \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{a + 4}}{3}} \right)^2} + {\left( {\frac{{2b - 2}}{3}} \right)^2} + \frac{4}{9} = 5\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)$
Solving (1) and (2)
${\left( {b + 6} \right)^2} + {\left( {2b - 2} \right)^2} = 41$
$ \Rightarrow 5{b^2} + 4b - 1 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow b = - 1$ or $\frac{1}{5}$
$ \Rightarrow a = 1$ or $\frac{{11}}{5}$
But $ab < 0 \Rightarrow \left( {a,b} \right) = \left( {1, - 1} \right)$
$C \equiv \left( {\frac{5}{3},\frac{{ - 4}}{3},\frac{{ - 2}}{3}} \right),\,\,P \equiv \left( {2, - 1, - 3} \right)$
$C{P^2} = \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{9} + \frac{{49}}{9} = \frac{{51}}{9} = \frac{{17}}{3}$

Question: Let the plane \[P:\vec r.\,\vec a = d\] contain the line of intersection of two planes \[\vec r.\,\,(\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k) = 6\] and\[\vec r\;( - 6\hat i + 5\hat j - \hat k) = 7\]. If the plane P passes through the point \[\left( {2,\,\,3,\,\,\frac{1}{2}} \right),\] then the value of \[\frac{{{{\left| {13\vec a} \right|}^2}}}{{{d^2}}}\] is equal to
Solution:
Equation of plane passing through line of intersection of planes \[{P_1}:\vec r\;((\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k)) = 6\] and \[{P_2}:\vec r.\;( - 6\hat i + 5\hat j - \hat k) = 7\] is
\[{P_1} + \lambda {P_2} = 0\]
\[(\bar r.\,(\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k) - 6) + \lambda \,(\bar r.( - 6\hat i + 5\hat j - \hat k) - 7) = 0\]
and it passes through point \[\left( {2,3,\frac{1}{2}} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {2 + 9 - \frac{1}{2} - 6} \right) + \lambda \left( { - 12 + 15 - \frac{1}{2} - 7} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda = 1\]
Equation of plane is \[\bar r.\;( - 5\hat i + 8\hat j - 2\hat k) = 13\]
\[{\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} = 25 + 64 + 4 = 93;d = 13\]
Value of \[\frac{{{{\left| {13\bar a} \right|}^2}}}{{{d^2}}} = 93\]

Question: The plane passing through the points (1, 2, 1), (2, 1, 2) and parallel to the line, 2x = 3y, z = 1 also passes through the point :
Solution:
Two points on the line L
\[\frac{x}{3} = \frac{y}{2},\,\,z = 1\] are \[\left( {0,\,\,0,\,\,1} \right)\] and \[\left( {3,\,\,2,\,\,1} \right)\]
\[{\bar V_L}:\] Dr’s of line is (3, 2, 0)

\[{\bar n_p} = \overline {AB} \times {\bar V_L}\]

\[{\bar n_p} = \left( { - 2,\,\,3,\,\,5} \right)\]
\[\therefore \] Equation of plane :\[-2\left( {x - 1} \right) + 3\left( {y - 2} \right) + 5\left( {z - 1} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\, - 2x + 3y + 5z + 2 - 6 - 5 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,2x - 3y - 5z = - 9\]
\[\therefore \] The plane passes through the point (-2, 0, 1)

Question: The distance of the point (7, 10, 11) from the line \[\frac{{x - 4}}{1} = \frac{{y - 4}}{0} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3}\]along the line \[\frac{{x - 9}}{2} = \frac{{y - 13}}{3} = \frac{{z - 17}}{6}\] is
Solution:
\[\because \;line\;PQ\;is\;parallel\;to\;line\;\frac{{x - 9}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{3} = \frac{{z - 17}}{6}\]

Q = (3,4,–1)

Question: If the image of the point $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{a}, 2, \mathrm{a})$ in the line $\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{a}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{1}$ is Q and the image of Q in the line $\frac{\mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{~b}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{a}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+2 \mathrm{~b}}{-5}$ is P , then $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}$ is equal to.
Solution:
Now,

$\frac{2 \lambda-a+a}{2}=-5 \mu-2 b$
and $\frac{\mathrm{a}+4 \lambda-\mathrm{a}}{2}=2 \mu+2 \mathrm{~b}$
and $\frac{2 \lambda-2 \mathrm{a}-2+2}{2}=\mu+\mathrm{a}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda-\mu=\mathrm{b}$
$\lambda-\mu=2 \mathrm{a}$
$\lambda+5 \mu=-2 \mathrm{~b}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{b}=2 \mathrm{a}$ and $\lambda=\mathrm{a}, \mu=-\mathrm{a}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& (a \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+0 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=0 \\
& 2 a-2=0 \\
& a=1 \\
& b=2 a=2 \times 1=2 \\
& a+b=1+2=3
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: Let P be the foot of the perpendicular from the point (1, 2, 2) on the line\[L:\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 2}}{2}.\] Let the line \[\vec r = \left( { - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right),\lambda  \in R,\] intersect the line L at Q. Then 2(PQ)<sup>2</sup> is equal to:
Solution:
\[L:\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 2}}{2} = \mu \]
\[P(\mu + 1, - \mu - 1,\,2\mu + 2)\]
\[\overrightarrow {AP} .\vec d = 0 \Rightarrow (\mu , - \mu - 3,2\mu )(1, - 1,2) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \mu + \mu + 3 + 4\mu = 0 \Rightarrow \mu = - \frac{1}{2}\]
\[\therefore P\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2} + 1, + \frac{1}{2} - 1,2\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right) + 2} \right)\]
\[P\left( {\frac{1}{2},\frac{{ - 1}}{2},1} \right)\]
Now general pt. on L2 is Q(–1 + λ , 1 – λ , –2 + λ )
Equate it with general pt of L
$\mu + 1 = - 1 + \lambda \Rightarrow \mu = \lambda - 2$
$ - \mu - 1 = 1 - \lambda \Rightarrow \mu = \lambda - 2$
$2\mu + 2 = - 2 + \lambda $
$2\left( {\lambda - 2} \right) + 2 = - 2 + \lambda $
$2\lambda - 4 + 2 = - 2 + \lambda $
∴ μ = –2 , λ = 0
∴ Q ≡ (–1 ,1 –2)
\[P\left( {\frac{1}{2},\frac{{ - 1}}{2},1} \right)and\,Q( - 1,1, - 2)\]
\[PQ = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{1}{2} + 1} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2} - 1} \right)}^2} + {{(1 + 2)}^2}} \]
\[ = \sqrt {\frac{9}{4} + \frac{9}{4} + 9} = \sqrt {\frac{{54}}{4}} \]
\[\therefore 2{(PQ)^2} = 2\left( {\frac{{54}}{4}} \right) = 27\]

Question: Let $\mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c})$ be the image of the point $\mathrm{P}(3,2,1)$ in the line $\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{1}$. Then the distance of Q from the line $\frac{x-9}{3}=\frac{y-9}{2}=\frac{z-5}{-2}$ is
Solution:

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{Q} \equiv(1,2,3)$

Question: Let the mirror image of the point \[\left( {1,\;3,\;a} \right)\] with respect to the plane \[\vec r\; \cdot \;(2\hat i - \;\hat j + \;\hat k) - b = 0\] be \[\left( { - 3,\;5,\;2} \right).\] Then, the value of \[\left| {a + b} \right|\] is equal to _______.
Solution:
Plane : \[2x - y + z = b\]
\[R \equiv \left( { - 1,\;4,\;\frac{{a + 2}}{2}} \right) \to \] on plane
\[\therefore \;\; - 2 - 4 + \frac{{a + 2}}{2} = b\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;a + 2 = 2b + 12\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,a = 2b + 10\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {\text{i}} \right)\]
\[ < PQ > = < 4,\; - 2,\;a - 2 > \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \;\;\frac{2}{4} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{1}{{a - 2}}\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;a - 2 = 2\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;a = 4,\;b = - 3 \\
  \therefore \;\;\left| {a + b} \right| = 1 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The foot of perpendicular from the origin O to a plane P which meets the co-ordinate axes at the points $A,B,C$ is $\left( {2,a,4} \right),a \in N$, If the volume of the tetrahedron OABC is 144 unit3, then which of the following points is NOT on P?
Solution:
Equation of Plane:
$\left( {2\hat i + a\hat j + 4\hat k} \right).\left[ {\left( {x - 2} \right)\hat i + \left( {y - a} \right)\hat j + \left( {z - 4} \right)\hat k} \right] = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 2x + ay + 4z = 20 + {a^2}$
$ \Rightarrow A \equiv \left( {\frac{{20 + {a^2}}}{2},0,0} \right)$
$B \equiv \left( {0,\frac{{20 + {a^2}}}{a},0} \right)$
$C \equiv \left( {0,0,\frac{{20 + {a^2}}}{4}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow $ Volume of tetrahedron
$ = \frac{1}{6}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\vec a}&{\vec b}&{\vec c}
\end{array}} \right]$
$ = \frac{1}{6}\vec a.\left( {\vec b \times \vec c} \right)$
$ = \frac{1}{6}\left( {\frac{{20 + {a^2}}}{2}} \right) \cdot \left( {\frac{{20 + {a^2}}}{a}} \right) \cdot \left( {\frac{{20 + {a^2}}}{4}} \right) = 144$
$ \Rightarrow {\left( {20 + {a^2}} \right)^3} = 144 \times 48 \times a$
$ \Rightarrow a = 2$
$ \Rightarrow $ Equation of plane is $2x + 2y + 4z = 24$
Or $x + y + 2z = 12$
$ \Rightarrow \left( {3,0,4} \right)$ not lies on the plane
$x + y + 2z = 12$

Question: If the equation of a plane P, passing through the intersection of the planes, x + 4y – z + 7 = 0 and 3x + y + 5z = 8 is ax + by + 6z =15 for some a, b \[ \in \] R, then the distance of the point (3, 2, –1) from the plane P is……. units.
Solution:
\[{p_{\mathbf{1}}}{\mathbf{ + }}\lambda {p_{\mathbf{2}}}{\mathbf{ = }}0\]
(x + 4y – z + 7) + \[\lambda \](3x + y +5z – 8) = ax + by + 6z – 15
\[\frac{{1{\mathbf{ + }}3\lambda }}{a}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{4{\mathbf{ + }}\lambda }}{b}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{{\mathbf{ - }}1{\mathbf{ + }}5\lambda }}{6}{\mathbf{ = }}\frac{{7{\mathbf{ - }}8\lambda }}{{{\mathbf{ - }}15}}\]
\[\therefore \] \[15 - 75\lambda \, = 42 - 48\lambda \]
– 27 = 27\[\lambda \]
\[\lambda \] = –1
\[\therefore \]plane is – 2x + 3y – 6z + 15 = 0
\[d{\mathbf{ = }}\left| {\frac{{ - 6{\mathbf{ + }}6{\mathbf{ + }}6{\mathbf{ + }}15}}{{\sqrt {4{\mathbf{ + }}9{\mathbf{ + }}36} }}} \right|{\mathbf{ = }}3\,units\]

Question: Let the line $\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{6 - y}}{2} = \frac{{z + 8}}{5}$ intersect the lines $\frac{{x - 5}}{4} = \frac{{y - 7}}{3} = \frac{{z + 2}}{1}$ and $\frac{{x + 3}}{6} = \frac{{3 - y}}{3} = \frac{{z - 6}}{1}$ at the end points $A$ and $B$ respectively. Then the distance of the mid-point of the line segment $AB$ from the plane $2x - 2y + z = 14$ is
Solution:
$\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - 6}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z + 8}}{5} = \lambda \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)$
$\frac{{x - 5}}{4} = \frac{{y - 7}}{3} = \frac{{z + 2}}{1} = \mu \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)$
$\frac{{x + 3}}{4} = \frac{{y - 3}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{z - 6}}{1} = \gamma \,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 3 \right)$
Intersection of (1) & (2) “A”
$\left( {\lambda , - 2\lambda + 6,5\lambda - 8} \right)$ & $\left( {4\mu + 5,3\mu + 7,\mu - 2} \right)$
$\lambda = 1,\,\,\mu = - 1$
$A\left( {1,4, - 3} \right)$
Intersection of (1) & (3) “B”
$\left( {\lambda , - 2\lambda + 6,5\lambda - 8} \right)$ & $\left( {6\gamma - 3, - 3\gamma + 3,\,\,\gamma + 6} \right)$
$\lambda = 3$
$\gamma = 1$
$B\left( {3,0,7} \right)$
Mid point of A & B $ \Rightarrow \left( {2,2,2} \right)$
Perpendicular distance from the plane
$2x - 2y + z = 14$
$ \Rightarrow \left| {\frac{{2\left( 2 \right) - 2\left( 2 \right) + 2 - 14}}{{\sqrt {4 + 4 + 1} }}} \right| = 4$

Question: Let P (α, β, γ) be the image of the point Q(3, –3, 1) in the line \[\frac{{x - 0}}{1} = \frac{{y - 3}}{1} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 1}}\]and R be the point (2, 5, –1). If the area of the triangle PQR is λ and λ<sup>2</sup> = 14K, then K is equal to:
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  RQ = \sqrt {1 + 64 + 4} = \sqrt {69} \\
  \overrightarrow {RQ} = \hat \ell - 8\hat j + 2\hat k \\
  \overrightarrow {RS} = \hat \ell + \hat j - \hat k \\
  \cos \theta = \frac{{\overrightarrow {RQ} .\overrightarrow {RS} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {RQ} } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow {RS} } \right|}} = \left| {\frac{{1 - 8 - 2}}{{\sqrt {69} \sqrt 3 }}} \right| = \frac{9}{{3\sqrt {23} }} \\
  \cos \theta = \frac{3}{{\sqrt {23} }} = \frac{{RS}}{{RQ}} = \frac{{RS}}{{\sqrt {69} }} \\
  RS = 3\sqrt 3 \\
  \sin \theta = \frac{{\sqrt {14} }}{{\sqrt {23} }} = \frac{{QS}}{{\sqrt {69} }} \\
  QS = \sqrt {42} \\
  area = \frac{1}{2}.2QS.RS = \sqrt {42} .3\sqrt 3 \\
  \lambda = 9\sqrt {14} \\
  {\lambda ^2} = 81.14 = 14k \\
  k = 81 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Let the plane passing through the point (–1,0, –2) and perpendicular to each of the planes \[2x + y - z = 2\] and \[x - y - z = 3\] be \[ax + by + cz + 8 = 0\], then the value of \[a + b + c\]  is equal to:
Solution:
Normal of req. plane \[\left( {2\mathop i\limits^ \wedge + \mathop j\limits^ \wedge - \mathop k\limits^ \wedge } \right) \times \left( {\mathop i\limits^ \wedge - \mathop j\limits^ \wedge - \mathop k\limits^ \wedge } \right)\] =\[ - 2\mathop i\limits^ \wedge + \mathop j\limits^ \wedge - 3\mathop k\limits^ \wedge \]
Equation of plane
\[ - 2\left( {x + 1} \right) + 1\left( {y - 0} \right) - 3\left( {z + 2} \right) = 0\]
\[ - 2x + y - 3z - 8 = 0\]
\[2x - y + 3z + 8 = 0\]
\[a + b + c = 4\]

Question: Let the point A be the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, 0)$ on the line $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{1}=\frac{z-\alpha}{3}$. If the midpoint of the line segment PA is $\left(0, \frac{3}{4}, \frac{-1}{4}\right)$, then the value of $\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2} +\alpha^{2}$ is equal to :
Solution:
$\frac{2 \mathrm{r}+1+\mathrm{a}}{2}=0 \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{r}+\mathrm{a}=-1$
$\frac{\mathrm{r}+2+\mathrm{b}}{2}=\frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}+\mathrm{b}=-\frac{1}{2}$

$\frac{3 r+\alpha}{2}=\frac{-1}{4} \Rightarrow 3 r+\alpha=\frac{-1}{2}$
$2 \cdot a+1 \cdot(b-3 / 4)+3 \cdot \frac{1}{4}=0 \Rightarrow a+b=0$
$\Rightarrow 2(-1-2 \mathrm{r})+(-\mathrm{r}-1 / 2)=0$
$\Rightarrow-3 / 2=3 \mathrm{r}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=-1 / 2, \mathrm{a}=0, \mathrm{~b}=0, \alpha=1$
$\therefore \mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}+\alpha^{2}=1$

Question: Let the image of the point $P\left( {1,2,6} \right)$ in the plane passing through the points $A\left( {1,2,0} \right),B\left( {1,4,1} \right)$ and $C\left( {0,5,1} \right)$ be $Q\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$. Then $\left( {{\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2} + {\gamma ^2}} \right)$ is equal to
Solution:
Equation of plane $A\left( {x - 1} \right) + B\left( {y - 2} \right) + C\left( {z - 0} \right) = 0$
Put $\left( {1,4,1} \right) \Rightarrow 2B + C = 0$
Put $\left( {0,5,1} \right) \Rightarrow - A + 3B + C = 0$
By Subtracting $B - A = 0 \Rightarrow A = B,\,\,C = - 2B$
$1\left( {x - 1} \right) + 1\left( {y - 2} \right) - 2\left( {z - 0} \right) = 0$
$x + y - 2z - 3 = 0$
Image is $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$
Point $ \equiv \left( {1,2,6} \right)$
$\frac{{\alpha - 1}}{1} = \frac{{\beta - 2}}{1} = \frac{{\gamma - 6}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{ - 2\left( {1 + 2 - 12 - 3} \right)}}{6}$
$\frac{{\alpha - 1}}{1} = \frac{{\beta - 2}}{1} = \frac{{\gamma - 6}}{{ - 2}} = 4$
$\alpha = 5,\,\,\beta = 6,\,\,\gamma = - 2$
$ \Rightarrow {\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2} + {\gamma ^2} = 25 + 36 + 4 = 65$

Question: If the line $x = y = z$ intersects the line $x\sin A + y\sin B + z\sin C - 18 = 0 = x\sin 2A + y\sin 2B + z\sin 2C - 9$, where $A,B,C$ are the angles of a triangle $ABC$, then $80\left( {\sin \frac{A}{2}\sin \frac{B}{2}\sin \frac{C}{2}} \right)$ is equal to ____.
Solution:
$\sin A + \sin B + \sin C = \frac{{18}}{x}$
$\sin 2A + \sin 2B + \sin 2C = \frac{9}{x}$
$\therefore \,\,\sin A + \sin B + \sin C = 2\left( {\sin 2A + \sin 2B + \sin 2C} \right)$
$4\cos \frac{A}{2}\cos \frac{B}{2}\cos \frac{C}{2} = 2\left( {4\sin A\sin B\sin C} \right)$
$16\sin \frac{A}{2}\sin \frac{B}{2}\sin \frac{C}{2} = 1$
Hence Answer = 5

Question: Let the foot of the perpendicular from the point (1, 2, 4) on the line \[\frac{{x + 2}}{4} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z + 1}}{3}\] be P. Then the distance of P from the plane 3x+ 4y+ 12z + 23=0
Solution:
\[\frac{{x + 2}}{4} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z + 1}}{3} = \lambda \]
\[(x,y,z) = (4\lambda - 2,2\lambda + 1,3\lambda - 1)\]
\[\overrightarrow {AP} = (4\lambda - 3)\hat i + (2\lambda - 1)\hat j + (3\lambda - 5)\hat k\]
\[\vec b = 4\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k\]
\[\overrightarrow {AP} \,\,.\,\,\vec b\, = \,0\]
\[4(4\lambda - 3) + 2(2\lambda - 1) + 3(3\lambda - 5) = 0\]
\[29\lambda = 12 + 2 + 15 = 29\]
\[\lambda = 1\]
P = (2, 3, 2)
3x + 4y + 12z + 23 = 0
\[d = \left| {\frac{{6 + 12 + 24 + 23}}{{\sqrt {9 + 16 + 144} }}} \right|\]
\[d = \left| {\frac{{65}}{{13}}} \right| = 5\]

Question: If the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point (1 ,0, 3) on a line passing through (\[\alpha \], 7, 1) is \[\left( {\frac{5}{3},\frac{7}{3},\frac{{17}}{3}} \right)\], then \[\alpha \] is equal to _____
Solution:
Given points P(1,0,3) and \[Q\left( {\frac{5}{3},\frac{7}{3},\frac{{17}}{3}} \right)\]
Direction ratios of line \[L:\left( {\alpha - \frac{5}{3},7 - \frac{7}{3},1 - \frac{{17}}{3}} \right)\]
\[ = \left( {\frac{{3\alpha - 5}}{3},\frac{{14}}{3}, - \frac{{14}}{3}} \right)\]
Direction ratios of \[PQ:\left( { - \frac{2}{3}, - \frac{7}{3}, - \frac{8}{3}} \right)\]
As line L is perpendicular to PQ
So, \[\left( {\frac{{3\alpha - 5}}{3}} \right)\left( { - \frac{2}{3}} \right) + \left( {\frac{{14}}{3}} \right)\left( { - \frac{7}{3}} \right) + \left( { - \frac{{14}}{3}} \right)\left( { - \frac{8}{3}} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\, - 6\alpha + 10 - 98 + 112 = 0\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,6\alpha = 24\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\alpha = 4\]
