Question: If the length of the perpendicular drawn from the point \[P\;(a,\;4,\;2),\;a\; > \;0\]  on the line \[\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{3} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 1}}\] is \[2\sqrt 6 \] units and \[Q\;({\alpha _1},{\alpha _2},{\alpha _3})\] is the image of the point P in this line, then \[a + \sum\limits_{i = 1}^3 {{\alpha _i}} \] is equal to:
Solution:
\[(2\lambda - 1 - a)\;2 + (3\lambda - 1)\;3 + ( - \lambda - 1)\;( - 1) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 4\lambda - 2 - 2a + 9\lambda - 3 + \lambda + 1 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 14\lambda - 4 - 2a = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 7\lambda - 2 - a = 0\]
and,
\[{(2\lambda - 1 - a)^2} + {(3\lambda - 1)^2} + {(\lambda + 1)^2} = 24\]
\[ \Rightarrow {(5\lambda - 1)^2} + {(3\lambda - 1)^2} + {(\lambda + 1)^2} = 24\]
\[ \Rightarrow 35{\lambda ^2} - 14\lambda - 21 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow (\lambda - 1)\;(35\lambda + 21) = 0\]
For\[\lambda = 1\] \[ \Rightarrow \]a = 5
Let \[({\alpha _1},{\alpha _2},{\alpha _3})\] be reflection of point P
\[{\alpha _1} + 5 = 2\] \[{\alpha _2} + 4 = 12\] \[{\alpha _3} + 2 = 0\]
\[{\alpha _1} = - 3\] \[{\alpha _2} = 8\] \[{\alpha _3} = - 2\]
\[a + {\alpha _1} + {\alpha _2} + {\alpha _3} = 8\]

Question: Each of the angles β and γ that a given line makes with the positive y– and z–axes, respectively, is half of the angle that this line makes with the positive x-axes. Then the sum of all possible values of the angle β is
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \beta = \frac{\alpha }{2},\;\gamma = \frac{\alpha }{2} \\
  {\cos ^2}\alpha + {\cos ^2}\beta + {\cos ^2}\gamma = 1 \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\cos ^2}\alpha + 2{\cos ^2}\frac{\alpha }{2} = 1 \\
  {\cos ^2}\alpha + \cos \alpha = 0 \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \cos \alpha \left( {\cos \alpha + 1} \right) = 0 \\
  \cos \alpha = 0, - 1 \\
  \alpha = \frac{\pi }{2},\pi \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
  Now\;\beta = \frac{\alpha }{2} \Rightarrow \frac{\pi }{4},\;\frac{\pi }{2} \\
  so\;sum\;is\;\frac{{3\pi }}{4} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The sum of the intercepts on the coordinate axes of the plane passing through the point (–2, –2, 2) and containing the line joining the points (1, –1, 2) and (1, 1, 1), is :
Solution:
The equation plane
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - {x_1}} \\
  {{x_2} - {x_1}} \\
  {{x_3} - {x_1}}
\end{array}\,\,\,\,\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {y - {y_1}} \\
  {{y_2} - {y_1}} \\
  {{y_3} - {y_1}}
\end{array}\,\,\,\,\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {z - {z_1}} \\
  {{z_2} - {z_1}} \\
  {{z_3} - {z_1}}
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
     \[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x + 2} \\
  {1 + 2} \\
  {1 + 2}
\end{array}\,\,\,\,\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {y + 2} \\
  { - 1 + 2} \\
  {1 + 2}
\end{array}\,\,\,\,\,\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {z - 2} \\
  {2 - 2} \\
  {1 - 2}
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
     \[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x + 2} \\
  3 \\
  3
\end{array}\,\,\,\,\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {y + 2} \\
  1 \\
  3
\end{array}\,\,\,\,\,\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {z - 2} \\
  0 \\
  { - 1}
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
     \[ \Rightarrow \] (z – 2) (9 – 3) – 1 (x + 2 – 3y – 6) = 0
     \[ \Rightarrow \]6z – 12 – x – 2 + 3y + 6 = 0
     \[ \Rightarrow \] –x + 3y + 6z – 8 = 0
     \[ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{x}{8} - \frac{{3y}}{8} - \frac{{6z}}{8} + \frac{8}{8} = 0\]
     \[ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{x}{8} - \frac{y}{{\frac{8}{3}}} - \frac{z}{{\frac{8}{6}}} = 1\]
     \[ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{x}{{ - 8}} + \frac{y}{{\frac{8}{3}}} + \frac{z}{{\frac{8}{6}}} = 0\]
     Sum of intercepts
     \[ = - 8 + \frac{8}{3} + \frac{8}{6}\]
     \[ = - 8 + \frac{{16 + 8}}{6}\]
     \[ = - 8 + \frac{{24}}{6}\]
     = – 8 + 4
       = – 4

Question: The direction ratios of normal to the plane through the points (0, -1, 0) and (0, 0, 1) and making an angle $\frac{\pi }{4}$ with the plane $y - z + 5 = 0$
Solution:
d. r. of AB
$ = \left( {0,\;1,\;1} \right)$
$a.0 + b.1 + c.1 = 0$
$b + c = 0$

$b + c = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {\text{i}} \right)$
$\cos \frac{\pi }{4} = \frac{{a.0 + b\left( 1 \right) + c\left( { - 1} \right)}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} .\sqrt 2 }}$
$\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{b - c}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }}.\sqrt 2 $
$ = {b^2} + {c^2} - 2bc$
$ \Rightarrow \;\;{a^2} = - 2bc\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {{\text{ii}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \;\;{a^2} = - 2b\left( { - b} \right)$
${a^2} = 2{b^2}\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;a = \pm \sqrt 2 b$
$\frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{b}{1}$
From (i) $\frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{b}{1} = \frac{c}{{ - 1}}$
or $\frac{a}{{ - \sqrt 2 }} = \frac{b}{1}$
$ \Rightarrow \;\;\frac{a}{{ - \sqrt 2 }} = \frac{b}{1} = \frac{c}{{ - 1}}$ from (i)
$\frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{b}{{ - 1}} = \frac{c}{1}$
dr will be $\left( {\sqrt 2 ,\;1,\; - 1} \right)\;{\text{or}}\;\left( {\sqrt 2 ,\; - 1,\;1} \right)$

Question: Let the plane $x + 3y - 2z + 6 = 0$ meet the co-ordinate axes at the points ${\text{A}},{\text{B}},{\text{C}}$. If the orthocenter of the triangle ${\text{ABC}}$ is $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\frac{6}{7}} \right)$, then $98{(\alpha  + \beta )^2}$ is equal to
Solution:
$A\left( { - 6,0,0} \right),B\left( {0, - 2,0} \right),C\left( {0,0,3} \right)$
$\overrightarrow {AB} = 6\hat i - 2\hat j,\,\,\overrightarrow {BC} = 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\overrightarrow {AC} = 6\hat i + 3\hat k$

$\overrightarrow {AH} \cdot \overrightarrow {BC} = 0$
$\left( {\alpha + 6,\beta ,\frac{6}{7}} \right) \cdot \left( {0,2,3} \right) = 0$
$\beta = \frac{{ - 9}}{7}$
$\overrightarrow {CH} \cdot \overrightarrow {AB} = 0$
$\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\frac{{ - 15}}{7}} \right) \cdot \left( {6, - 2,0} \right) = 0$
$6\alpha - 2\beta = 0$
$\alpha = \frac{{ - 3}}{7}$
$98{\left( {\alpha + \beta } \right)^2} = \left( {98} \right)\frac{{\left( {144} \right)}}{{49}} = 288$

Question: The number of distinct real values of $\lambda $  for which the lines $\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{2} = \frac{{z + 3}}{{{\lambda ^2}}}$ and $\frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{{\lambda ^2}}} = \frac{{z - 1}}{2}$ are coplanar is
Solution:
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  1&2&{{\lambda ^2}} \\
  1&{{\lambda ^2}}&2 \\
  {3 - 1}&{2 - 2}&{1 + 3}
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  1&2&{{\lambda ^2}} \\
  1&{{\lambda ^2}}&2 \\
  2&0&4
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$\begin{gathered}
  4{\lambda ^2} - 2\left( 0 \right) + {\lambda ^2}\left( { - 2{\lambda ^2}} \right) = 0 \\
  2{\lambda ^2}\left[ {2 - {\lambda ^2}} \right] = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $
$\lambda = 0,\,\, \pm \sqrt 2 $

Question: The square of the distance of the point \[\left( {\frac{{15}}{7},\frac{{32}}{7},7} \right)\] from the line \[\frac{{x + 1}}{3} = \frac{{y + 3}}{5} = \frac{{z + 5}}{7}\] in the direction of the vector \[\widehat i + 4\widehat j + 7\widehat k\] is:
Solution:
\[L = \frac{{x + 1}}{3} = \frac{{y + 3}}{5} = \frac{{z + 5}}{7}\]
\[PQ = \frac{{x - \frac{{15}}{7}}}{1} = \frac{{y - \frac{{32}}{7}}}{4} = \frac{{z - 7}}{7} = \lambda \]
\[ \Rightarrow Q\left( {\lambda + \frac{{15}}{7},4\lambda + \frac{{32}}{7},7\lambda + 7} \right)\]
Since Q lies on line L
So, \[\frac{{\lambda + \frac{{15}}{7} + 1}}{3} = \frac{{7\lambda + 7 + 5}}{7}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 7\lambda + 22 = 21\lambda + 36\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda = - 1\]
\[\therefore \,{\text{Point}}\,Q\left( {\frac{8}{7},\frac{4}{7},0} \right)\]
\[PQ = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{{15}}{7} - \frac{8}{7}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{32}}{7} - \frac{4}{7}} \right)}^2} + \left( {7 - 0} \right)} \]
\[PQ = \sqrt {66} \]
\[ \Rightarrow {\left( {PQ} \right)^2} = \sqrt {66} \]

Question: Let \[P\] be the point \[\left( {10,-2,-1} \right)\] and \[Q\] be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point \[R\left( {1,7,6} \right)\] on the line passing through the points \[\left( {2,-5,11} \right)\] and \[\left( {-6,7,-5} \right).\] Then the length of the line segment \[PQ\] is equal to ________.
Solution:
Line: \[\frac{{x + 6}}{{-8}} = \frac{{y-7}}{{12}} = \frac{{z + 5}}{{-16}}\]
\[\frac{{x + 6}}{2} = \frac{{y-7}}{{-3}} = \frac{{z + 5}}{4} = \lambda \]
\[Q\left( {2\lambda -6,7-3\lambda ,4\lambda -5} \right)\]
\[\overline {QR} \left( {2\lambda -7,-3\lambda ,4\lambda -11} \right)\]
\[\overline {QR} .dr's\] of line \[ = 0\]
\[4\lambda -14 + 9\lambda + 16\lambda -44 = 0\]
\[29\lambda = 58 \Rightarrow \lambda = 2\]
\[Q\left( {-2,1,3} \right)\]
\[PQ = \sqrt {144 + 9 + 16} = \sqrt {169} = 13\]

Question: The shortest distance between the lines $\frac{{x - 3}}{4} = \frac{{y + 7}}{{ - 11}} = \frac{{z - 1}}{5}$ and $\frac{{x - 5}}{3} = \frac{{y - 9}}{{ - 6}} = \frac{{z + 2}}{1}$ is:
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow n = \overrightarrow p \times \overrightarrow q \]
$\overrightarrow n = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  4&{ - 11}&5 \\
  3&{ - 6}&1
\end{array}} \right| = 19\hat i + 11\hat j + 9\hat k$
S.d. = Projection of $\overrightarrow {AB} $ on $\overrightarrow n $
$ = \left| {\frac{{\overrightarrow {AB} .\,\overrightarrow {\,n} }}{{|\overrightarrow n |}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{\left( {2\hat i + 16\hat j - 3\hat k} \right).\left( {19\hat i + 11\hat j + 9\hat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt {361 + 121 + 81} }}} \right|$
$ = \frac{{38 + 176 - 27}}{{\sqrt {563} }}$
S.d $ = \frac{{187}}{{\sqrt {563} }}$

Question: The perpendicular distance, of the line \[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + 3}}{2}\] from the point \[P(2, - 10,1),\] is
Solution:
\[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{x + 3}}{2} = \lambda (let)\]
\[(2\lambda + 1, - \lambda - 2,\,\,\,2\lambda - 3)\]
\[\because \overrightarrow {PA} .\vec n = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow (2\lambda - 1)2 + \left( { - \lambda + 8} \right)( - 1) + \left( {2\lambda - 4} \right)2 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 4\lambda - 2 + \lambda - 8 + 4\lambda - 8 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 9\lambda - 18 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 2\]
\[\therefore A(5, - 4,1)\]
\[\therefore AP = \sqrt {{3^2} + {6^2} + {0^2}} = \sqrt {45} = 3\sqrt 5 \]

Question: Let S be the mirror image of the point \[Q\left( {1,3,4} \right)\] with respect to the plane \[2x - y + z + 3 = 0\] and let \[R\left( {3,5,\gamma } \right)\] be a point on this plane. Then the square of the length of the line segment SR is _______.
Solution:
Since \[R\left( {3,5,\gamma } \right)\]lies on the plane \[2x - y + z + 3 = 0.\]
Therefore, \[6 - 5 + \gamma + 3 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \gamma = - 4\]
Now,
Direction ratios of line \[QS\] are 2, –1,1
 equation of line \[QS\] is
\[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 4}}{1} = \lambda \] (say)
\[ \Rightarrow F\left( {2\lambda + 1, - \lambda + 3,\lambda + 4} \right)\]
\[F\] lies in the plane
\[ \Rightarrow 2\left( {2\lambda + 1} \right) - \left( { - \lambda + 3} \right) + \left( {\lambda + 4} \right) + 3 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 4\lambda + 2 + \lambda - 3 + \lambda + 7 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 6\lambda + 6 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = - 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow F\left( { - 1,4,3} \right)\]
Since, \[F\] is mid-point of \[QS\].
Therefore, co-ordinates of \[S\] are \[\left( { - 3,5,2} \right).\]
So, \[SR = \sqrt {36 + 0 + 36} = \sqrt {72} \]
\[S{R^2} = 72\]

Question: If the distance of the point $\mathrm{P}(43, \alpha, \beta), \beta<0$, from the line $\vec{r}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{k}+\mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}), \mu \in \mathbf{R}$ along a line with direction ratios $3,-1,0$ is $13 \sqrt{10}$, then $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\quad \frac{x-43}{3}=\frac{y-\alpha}{-1}=\frac{z-\beta}{0} \Rightarrow P_{1}(43+3 \lambda, \alpha-\lambda, \beta)$
$\frac{\mathrm{x}-4}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{0}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+1}{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}_{1}(2 \mu+4,0,3 \mu-1)$
$\therefore \mu=\frac{3 \lambda+39}{2}, \alpha=\lambda, \beta=\frac{9 \lambda-115}{2}$
$P(43, \alpha, \beta), P_{1}(43+3 \alpha, 0, \beta)$
$\left(\mathrm{PP}_{1}\right)^{2}=1690=10 \alpha^{2}, \therefore \alpha=13, \beta=1$
$\therefore \alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=170$

Question: Let P be the plane containing the straight line \[\frac{{x - 3}}{9} = \;\frac{{y + \;4}}{{ - 1}} = \;\frac{{z - 7}}{{ - 5}}\] and perpendicular to the plane containing the straight lines \[\frac{x}{2} = \;\frac{y}{3} = \;\frac{z}{5}\] and \[\frac{x}{3} = \;\frac{y}{7} = \;\frac{z}{8}.\] If $d$ is the distance of P from the point \[(2,\; - 5,\;11),\] then \[{d^2}\] is equal to :

Let a line $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ pass through the origin and be perpendicular to the lines
$L_{2}: \vec{r}=(3+t) \hat{i}+(2 t-1) \hat{j}+(2 t+4) \hat{k}$ and $L_{3}: \vec{r}=(3+2 s) \hat{i}+(3+2 s) \hat{j}+(2+s) \hat{k}, t$, $\mathrm{s} \in \mathbf{R}$. If $(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}), \mathrm{a} \in \mathbf{Z}$, is the point on $\mathrm{L}_{3}$ at a distance of $\sqrt{17}$ from the point of intersection of $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{L}_{2}$, then $(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})^{2}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$。
Solution:
$\mathrm{L}_{2}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-4}{2}=\mathrm{t}$
$\mathrm{L}_{5}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-3}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-2}{1}=\mathrm{s}$
vector $\perp^{\text {ar }}$ to $\mathrm{L}_{2} \& \mathrm{~L}_{3}$
$\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{i} & \mathrm{j} & \mathrm{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right|=\hat{\mathrm{i}}(-2)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(-3)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(-2)$
$\Rightarrow-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\Rightarrow-(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})$
Now, $\mathrm{L}_{1}: \frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{-3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{2}=\ell$
intersection of $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{L}_{2}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}+3=2 \ell, 2 \mathrm{t}-1=-3 \ell, 2 \mathrm{t}+4=2 \ell$
$\mathrm{t}+3=2 \mathrm{t}+4$
$\mathrm{t}=-1$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{P} \equiv(2,-3,2)$
A point on $\mathrm{L}_{3}$ is $\mathrm{Q}(2 \mathrm{~s}+3,2 \mathrm{~s}+3, \mathrm{~s}+2)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}^{2}=(2 \mathrm{~s}+1)^{2}+(2 \mathrm{~s}+6)^{2}+\mathrm{s}^{2}=17$
$\Rightarrow 9 \mathrm{~s}^{2}+28 \mathrm{~s}+20=0$
$\Rightarrow(9 \mathrm{~s}+10)(\mathrm{s}+2)=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~s}=-2$
$\therefore \mathrm{Q} \equiv(-1,-1,0) \equiv(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}) \Rightarrow(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})^{2}=4$

Question:
Let a line having direction ratios 1, –4, 2 intersect the lines
\[\frac{{x - 7}}{3} = \;\frac{{y - 1}}{{ - 1}} = \;\frac{{z + 2}}{1}\] and \[\frac{x}{2} = \;\frac{{y - 7}}{3} = \;\frac{z}{1}\]
at the point A and B. Then \[{(AB)^2}\] is equal to ..................... .
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"


DR's of AB
\[(3\lambda - 2\mu + \;7,\; - \lambda - 3\mu - 6,\;\lambda - \;\mu - 2)\]
\[\frac{{3\lambda - 2\mu + 7}}{1} = \;\frac{{ - \lambda - 3\mu - 6}}{{ - 4}} = \;\frac{{\lambda - \mu - 2}}{2}\]
Taking first (2) \[ - \;12\lambda \; + \;8\mu \; - \;28 = - \;\lambda - \;3\mu - \;6\]
\[\lambda - \;\mu \; + \;2 = \;0\]
Taking second & third \[ - 2\lambda - 6\mu - 12 = \; - 4\lambda + \;4\mu + \;8\]
\[\lambda - \;5\mu - \;10 = \;0\]
After solving above two equation \[\lambda = - 5,\;\mu \; = - \;3\]
\[A = \;( - 8,\;6,\;7)\]
\[B = \;( - 6,\; - 2,\; - 3)\]
\[{(AB)^2} = \;4\; + \;64 + \;16 = \;84\]

Question: An angle between the plane, $x + y + z = 5$ and the line of intersection of the planes, $3x + 4y + z - 1 = 0$ and $5x + 8y + 2z + 14 = 0$,is:
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \overrightarrow {{x_1}} :x + y + z = 5 \\
  \overrightarrow {{r_1}} :3x + 4y + z - 1 = 0 \\
  \overrightarrow {{r_2}} :5x + 8y + 2z + 14 = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $
Thus line of intersection of plane is given by


   $\begin{gathered}
  \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\widehat i}&{\widehat j}&{\widehat k} \\
  3&4&1 \\
  5&8&2
\end{array}} \right| \\
   = \widehat i\left( 0 \right) - \widehat j\left( {6 - 5} \right) + \widehat k\left( {24 - 20} \right) \\
   = - \widehat j + 4\widehat k \\
\end{gathered} $
Now
$\begin{gathered}
  \sin \theta = \frac{{ - 1 + 4}}{{\sqrt 3 \sqrt {17} }} = \sqrt {\frac{3}{{17}}} \\
  \therefore \theta = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt {\frac{3}{{17}}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: If for \[a > 0\], the feet of perpendiculars from the points \[A\left( {a,\,\, - 2a,\,\,3} \right)\] and \[B\left( {0,\,\,4,\,\,5} \right)\] on the plane \[lx + my + nz = 0\] are points \[C\left( {0, - a,\,\, - 1} \right)\] and D respectively, then the length of line segment CD is equal to
Solution:
C lies on plane
\[ \Rightarrow \,\, - ma - n = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{m}{n} = - \frac{1}{a}\,\,\,\,...\left( 1 \right)\]
\[\overline {CA} \parallel l\hat i + m\hat j + n\hat k\]
\[\frac{{a - 0}}{l} = \frac{{ - a}}{m} = \frac{4}{n}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{m}{n} = - \frac{a}{4}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)\]
From (1) and (2)
\[ - \frac{1}{a} = \frac{{ - a}}{4}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,{a^2} = 4\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,a = 2\] \[\left( {{\text{since}}\,\,\,a > 0} \right)\]
From (2) \[\frac{m}{n} = \frac{{ - 1}}{2}\]
Let \[m = - t\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,n = 2t\]
\[\frac{2}{l} = \frac{{ - 2}}{{ - t}}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,l = t\]
So plane: \[t\left( {x - y + 2z} \right) = 0\]
\[BD = \frac{6}{{\sqrt 6 }} = \sqrt 6 \] \[C \cong \left( {0,\,\, - 2,\,\, - 1} \right)\]
\[CD = \sqrt {B{C^2} - B{D^2}} \]
\[ = \sqrt {\left( {{0^2} + {6^2} + {6^2}} \right) - {{\left( {\sqrt 6 } \right)}^2}} \]
\[ = \sqrt {66} \]

Question:
Let $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ be the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point $(5,4,2)$ on the line $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})$.
Then the length of the projection of the vector $\alpha \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\beta \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\gamma \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ on the vector $6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ is :
Solution:
$\overline{\mathrm{r}}=(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})$
$\frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-3}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{-1}=\lambda$
Any general point P on the line is
$(2 \lambda-1,3 \lambda+3,-\lambda+1)$
Let the given point is $\mathrm{A}(5,4,2)$
$\overline{\mathrm{AP}}=(2 \lambda-6) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(3 \lambda-1) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(-\lambda-1) \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\because \overline{\mathrm{AP}} \perp^{\mathrm{r}}$ Line $(\mathrm{L})$
$\therefore \overline{\mathrm{AP}} \cdot(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})=0$
$2(2 \lambda-6)+3(3 \lambda-1)-1(-\lambda-1)=0$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=1$
$\therefore \alpha=1$
$\beta=6$
$\gamma=0$
Let the vector $\overline{\mathrm{u}}=\alpha \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\beta \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\gamma \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$$
\overline{\mathrm{u}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{O} \hat{\mathrm{k}}
$$
$\& \overline{\mathrm{w}}=6 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
So projection $=\frac{|\overline{\mathrm{u}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{w}}|}{|\overline{\mathrm{w}}|}=\frac{18}{7}$

Question:
The line L1 is parallel to the vector \[\vec a = - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k\]and passes through the point
(7, 6, 2) and the line L2 is parallel to the vector \[\vec b = 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k\]and passes through the point
(5, 3, 4). The shortest distance between the lines L1 and L2 is :
Solution:
\[{L_1}:\left( {7\hat i + 6\hat j + 2k} \right) + \lambda \left( { - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4k} \right)\]
\[{L_2}:\left( {5\hat i + 3\hat j + 4k} \right) + \lambda \left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 3k} \right)\]
Distance between skew lines
\[ = \frac{{\left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j - 2\hat k} \right).\left( {2\hat i + 17\hat j - 7\hat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt {342} }}\]
\[ = \frac{{69}}{{\sqrt {342} }} = \frac{{69}}{{3\sqrt {38} }} = \frac{{23}}{{\sqrt {38} }}\]

Question: Let the line $l:x = \frac{{1 - y}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{\lambda },\lambda  \in \mathbb{R}$ meet the plane $P:x + 2y + 3z = 4$ at the point $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$. If the angle between the line $l$ and the plane P is ${\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sqrt {\frac{5}{{14}}} } \right),$ then $\alpha  + 2\beta  + 6\gamma $ is equal to __________.
Solution:
$l:x = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 3}}{\lambda },\lambda \in R$
DR’s of line $l\left( {1,2,\lambda } \right)$
DR’s of normal vector of plane $P:x + 2y + 3z = 4$ are $\left( {1,2,3} \right)$
Now, angle between line $l$ and plane P is given by
$\sin \theta = \left| {\frac{{1 + 4 + 3\lambda }}{{\sqrt {5 + {\lambda ^2}} \cdot \sqrt {14} }}} \right| = \frac{3}{{\sqrt {14} }}$ (given $\cos \theta = \sqrt {\frac{5}{{14}}} $)
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{2}{3}$
Let variable point on line $l$ is $\left( {t,2t + 1,\frac{2}{3}t + 3} \right)$ lies on plane P.
$ \Rightarrow t = - 1$
$ \Rightarrow \left( { - 1, - 1,\frac{7}{3}} \right) \equiv \left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha + 2\beta + 6\gamma = 11$

Question: Let Q be the mirror image of the point P (1, 0, 1) with respect to the plane \[S\;:\;x + \;y + \;z = \;5.\] If a line L passing through \[(1,\; - 1,\; - 1),\] parallel to the line PQ meets the plane S at R, then \[Q{R^2}\] is equal to :
Solution:
Let parallel vector of \[L = \;\vec b\]
mirror image of Q on given plane \[x + \;y + \;z\; = \;5\]
\[\frac{{a - 1}}{1} = \;\frac{{b - 0}}{1} = \;\frac{{c - 1}}{1} = \;\frac{{ - 2\;(2 - 5)}}{3}\]
\[a = \;3,\;b = \;2,\;c = \;3\]
\[Q \equiv \;(3,\;2,\;3)\]
\[\because \;\;\;\vec b\;|\;|\;\overrightarrow {PQ} \]
so, \[\vec b\; = \;(1,\;1,\;1)\]
Equation of line
\[L\;:\;\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \;\frac{{y + 1}}{1} = \;\frac{{z + 1}}{1}\]
Let point R, \[(\lambda + \;1,\;\lambda - 1,\;\lambda - 1)\]
lying on plane \[x\; + \;y\; + \;z\; = \;5,\]
so, \[3\lambda \; - \;1\; = \;5\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;\lambda \; = \;2\]
Point R is (3, 1, 1)
\[Q{R^2} = \;5\] Ans.

Question: The shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x - 3}}{3} = \frac{{y - 8}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{1}\] and \[\frac{{x + 3}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y + 7}}{2} = \frac{{z - 6}}{4}\] is:
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \overline {AB} = - 3\hat i - 7\hat j + 6\hat k - \left( {3\hat i + 8\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) = - 6\hat i - 15\hat j + 3\hat k \\
  \bar p = 3\hat i - \hat j + \hat k \\
  \bar q = - 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 4\hat k \\
  \bar p \times \bar q = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  3&{ - 1}&1 \\
  { - 3}&2&4
\end{array}} \right| = - 6\hat i - 15\hat j + 3\hat k \\
\end{gathered} \]
Shortest distance \[ = \frac{{\left| {\overline {AB} .\left( {\bar p \times \bar q} \right)} \right|}}{{\left| {\bar p \times \bar q} \right|}} = \frac{{\left| {36 + 225 + 9} \right|}}{{\sqrt {36 + 225 + 9} }} = 3\sqrt {30} \]

Question: Let a triangle PQR be such that P and Q lie on the line $\frac{x+3}{8}=\frac{y-4}{2}=\frac{z+1}{2}$ and are at a distance of 6 units from $\mathrm{R}(1,2,3)$. If $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ is the centroid of $\Delta \mathrm{PQR}$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma$ is equal to:
Solution:
Let the point P is $(8 \lambda-3,2 \lambda+4,2 \lambda-1)$
$\mathrm{PR}=6 \Rightarrow \mathrm{PR}^{2}=36$
$(8 \lambda-4)^{2}+(2 \lambda+2)^{2}+(2 \lambda-4)^{2}=36$
$(4 \lambda-2)^{2}+(\lambda+1)^{2}+(\lambda-2)^{2}=9$
$\lambda^{2}-\lambda=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=0,1$
$\lambda=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}(-3,4,-1)$
$\lambda=1 \Rightarrow P(5,6,1)$
$\mathrm{R}(1,2,3)$
G(1,4,1)
$=\alpha+\beta+\gamma=6$

Question:
Let the image of the point $\mathrm{P}(0,-5,0)$ in the line $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z+1}{-2}$ be the point $R$ and the image of the point $\mathrm{Q}\left(0, \frac{-1}{2}, 0\right)$ in the line $\frac{x-1}{-1}=\frac{y+9}{4}=\frac{z+1}{1}$ be the point S. Then the square of the area of the parallelogram PQRS is
$\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\mathrm{P}(0,-5,0)$

$$
\frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+1}{-2}=\mathrm{k}
$$
Let $\mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}, \mathrm{y}_{1}, \mathrm{z}_{1}\right)$
$2 \mathrm{k}+1, \mathrm{k},-2 \mathrm{k}-1$
$(2 \mathrm{k}+1)(2)+(\mathrm{k}+5)(1)+(-2 \mathrm{k}-1)(-2)=0$
$\Rightarrow 9 \mathrm{k}+9=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=-1$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{M}_{1} \Rightarrow(-1,-1,1)$
$$
R \Rightarrow(-2,3,2)
$$
Q(0, -1/2, 0)
$\frac{x-1}{-1}=\frac{y+9}{4}=\frac{z+1}{1}=\lambda$
$(-\lambda+1,4 \lambda-9, \lambda-1)$


$\mathrm{S}\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}, \mathrm{y}_{2}, \mathrm{z}_{2}\right)$
$(-\lambda+1)(-1)+\left(4 \lambda-\frac{17}{2}\right)(4)+(\lambda-1)(1)=0$
$\Rightarrow 18 \lambda-36=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=2$
$\mathrm{M}_{2} \Rightarrow(-1,-1,1)$
$\mathrm{S} \Rightarrow(-2,-3 / 2,2)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{PR}=\sqrt{4+64+4}=\sqrt{72}=6 \sqrt{2} \\
& \mathrm{QS}=\sqrt{4+1+4}=\sqrt{9}=3 \\
& \text { Ar. }=\frac{1}{2} \cdot 6 \sqrt{2} \cdot 3=9 \sqrt{2} \\
& (\text { Ar. })^{2}=(9 \sqrt{2})^{2}=162 \text { Sq.unit }
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: Let $\alpha x + \beta y + yz = 1$ be the equation of a plane passing through the point $\left( {3, - 2,5} \right)$ and perpendicular to the line joining the points $\left( {1,2,3} \right)$ and $\left( { - 2,3,5} \right)$. Then the value of $\alpha \beta y$ is equal to
Solution:
Given Equation is not equation of plane as yz is present. If we consider $y$ is $\gamma $ then answer would be 6 .
Normal vector of plane $ = 3\hat i - \hat j - 2\hat k$
Plane : $3{\text{x}} - {\text{y}} - 2{\text{z}} + \lambda = 0$
Point $\left( {3, - 2,5} \right)$ satisfies the plane
$\lambda = - 1$
$3x - y - 2z = 1$
$\alpha \beta y = 6$

Question: Consider a line L passing through the points P(1, 2, 1) and Q(2, 1, -1). If the mirror image of the point ${\text{A}}(2,\,\,2,\,\,2)$ in the line L is$(\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma )$, then $\alpha  + \beta  + 6\gamma $is equal to _______ .
Solution:
DR's of Line L ≡ –1 : 1 : 2
DR's of AB ≡ α – 2 : β – 2 : γ – 2
AB ⊥ar L ⇒ 2 – α + β – 2 + 2 γ – 4 = 0
2γ + β – α = 4 ... (1)
Let C is mid-point of AB
${\text{C}}\left( {\frac{{\alpha + 2}}{2},\frac{{\beta + 2}}{2},\frac{{\gamma + 2}}{2}} \right)$
${\text{DR's}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{PC}}\, = \frac{\alpha }{2}:\frac{{\beta - 2}}{2}:\frac{\gamma }{2}$

α = – 2K
β = 2K + 2
γ = 4K
use in (1) $ \Rightarrow \,\,K = \frac{1}{6}$
value of α + β + 6γ = 24K + 2 = 6

Question:
If (x, y, z) be an arbitrary point lying on a plane P which passes through the points (42, 0, 0), (0, 42, 0) and (0, 0, 42), then the value of the expression
$3 + \frac{{x - 11}}{{{{(y - 19)}^2}{{(z - 12)}^2}}} + \frac{{y - 19}}{{{{(x - 11)}^2}{{(z - 12)}^2}}} + \frac{{z - 12}}{{{{(x - 11)}^2}{{(y - 19)}^2}}} - \frac{{x + y + z}}{{14(x - 11)(y - 19)(z - 12)}}$
Is equal to
Solution:
Equation of plane is $x + y + z = 42$
$ \Rightarrow \left( {x - 11} \right) + \left( {y - 19} \right) + \left( {z - 12} \right) = 0$
Let $x - 11 = u;\,\,y - 19 = v;\,\,z - 12 = w$
i.e. $u + v + w = 0$
$\therefore \,\,\frac{u}{{{v^2} \cdot {w^2}}} + \frac{v}{{{u^2} \cdot {w^2}}} + \frac{w}{{{u^2} \cdot {v^2}}} - \frac{3}{{u \cdot v \cdot w}} + 3$
$ = \left( {\frac{{{u^3} + {v^3} + {w^3} - 3uvw}}{{{u^2}{v^2} \cdot {w^2}}}} \right) + 3 = 3$
$\left[ {\because \,\,u + v + w = 0 \Rightarrow {u^3} + {v^3} + {w^3} = 3uvw} \right]$

Question: Let the mirror image of the point (a, b, c) with respect to the plane \[3x - \;4y + \;12z + \;19 = \;0\] be \[(a - \;6,\;\beta ,\;\gamma ).\] If \[a + \;b + c\; = \;5,\] then \[7\beta  - 9\gamma \] is equal to ………. .
Solution:
\[M = \;\left( {a - 3,\;\frac{{\beta + b}}{2},\;\frac{{\gamma + c}}{2}} \right)\]
Since M lies on \[3x + \;4y + 12z + \;19 = \;0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;6a - 4b + \;12c - 4\beta + \;12\gamma + \;20 = \;0\] ………. (1)
Since PP’ is parallel to normal of the plane then \[\frac{6}{3} = \;\frac{{b - \;\beta }}{{ - 4}} = \;\frac{{c - \;\gamma }}{{12}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;\beta = \;b + \;8,\] \[\gamma = \;c - \;24\]
\[a + \;b + c = \;5\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;a + \;\beta - \;8\; + \;\gamma + \;24 = \;5\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;a = \; - \beta \; - \;\gamma \; - \;11\]
Now putting these values in (1) we get
\[6\;( - \beta - \gamma - 11)\; - \,4\;(\beta - \;8)\; + \;12\;(\gamma + \;24)\; - \;4\beta + \;12\gamma + \;20\; = \;0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;7\beta - \;9\gamma = \;170 - \;33\; = \;137\]

Question: If the shortest distance between the line joining the points $\left( {1,2,3} \right)$ and $\left( {2,3,4} \right)$, and the line $\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 2}}{0}$ is $\alpha $, then $28{\alpha ^2}$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
$\vec r = \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {\vec r = \vec a + \lambda \vec p} \right)$
$\vec r = \left( {\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {2\hat i - \hat j} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {\vec r = \vec b + \mu \vec q} \right)$
$\vec p \times \vec q = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&1&1 \\
  2&{ - 1}&0
\end{array}} \right| = \hat i + 2\hat j - 3\hat k$
$d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {\vec b - \vec a} \right) \cdot \left( {\vec p \times \vec q} \right)}}{{\left| {\vec p \times \vec q} \right|}}} \right|$
$d = \left| {\frac{{\left( { - 3\hat j + \hat k} \right) \cdot \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt {14} }}} \right|$
$ = \left| {\frac{{ - 6 + 3}}{{\sqrt {14} }}} \right| = \frac{3}{{\sqrt {14} }}$
$\alpha = \frac{3}{{\sqrt {14} }}$
Now, $28{\alpha ^2} = 28 \times \frac{9}{{14}} = 18$

Question: The square of the distance of the point of intersection of the line $\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{a}-\hat{j})$, $\mathrm{a} \neq 0$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{a} \hat{\mathrm{k}})$ from the origin is :
Solution:
Equating both the equations
$(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(a \hat{i}-\hat{j})=(4 \hat{i}-\hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+a \hat{k})$
$1+\mathrm{a} \lambda=4+2 \mu$
$1-\lambda=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=1$
$-1=-1+a \mu$
$\mathrm{a} \mu=0 \Rightarrow \mu=0$
$\mathrm{a}=3$
point of intersection $=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}+3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{k}$
Ans. 17

Question: The shortest distance between the lines $\frac{\mathrm{x}-4}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-3}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-2}{-3}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{x}+2}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-6}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-5}{-5}$ is:
Solution:
SD $=\frac{\left|\begin{array}{ccc}4-(-2) & 3-6 & 2-5 \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 4 & -5\end{array}\right|}{\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 4 & -5\end{array}\right|}$
$S D=\frac{|6(-10+12)-(-3)(-5+6)-3(4-4)|}{|2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}|}$
$\mathrm{SD}=\left|\frac{12+3}{\sqrt{5}}\right|$
$=\frac{15}{\sqrt{5}}$
$\mathrm{SD}=3 \sqrt{5}$

Question: The acute angle between the planes ${P_1}$ and ${P_2}$, when ${P_1}$ and ${P_2}$ are the planes passing through the intersection of the planes $5x + 8y + 13z - 29 = 0$ and $8x - 7y + z - 20 = 0$ and the points (2, 1, 3) and (0, 1, 2), respectively, is
Solution:
Equation of plane passing through the intersection of planes $5x + 8y + 13z - 29 = 0$ and $8x - 7y + z - 20 = 0$ is
 \[5x + 8y + 3z - 29 + \lambda \;(8x - 7y + z - 20) = 0\] and if it is passing through (2, 1, 3) then \[\lambda = \frac{7}{2}\]
\[{P_1}:\] Equation of plane through intersection of $5x + 8y + 13z - 29 = 0$ and $8x - 7y + z - 20 = 0$ and the point (2, 1, 3) is
\[5x + 8y + 3z - 29 + \frac{7}{2}(8x - 7y + z - 20) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,2x - y + z = 6\]
Similarly, P2 : Equation of plane through intersection of
$5x + 8y + 13z - 29 = 0$ and $8x - 7y + z - 20 = 0$ and the point (0, 1, 2) is
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,x + y + 2z = 5\]
Angle between planes \[ = \theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{3}{{\sqrt 6 \sqrt 6 }}} \right) = \frac{\pi }{3}\]

Question: Let $O$ be the origin, and $M$ and $N$ be the points on the lines $\frac{{x - 5}}{4} = \frac{{y - 4}}{1} = \frac{{z - 5}}{3}$ and $\frac{{x + 8}}{{12}} = \frac{{y + 2}}{5} = \frac{{z + 11}}{9}$ respectively such that $MN$ is the shortest distance between the given lines. Then $\overrightarrow {OM} \cdot \overrightarrow {ON} $ is equal to _____.
Solution:
${L_1}:\frac{{x - 5}}{4} = \frac{{y - 4}}{1} = \frac{{z - 5}}{3} = \lambda $
Drs (4, 1, 3) $ = {b_1}$
$M\left( {4\lambda + 5,\lambda + 4,3\lambda + 5} \right)$
${L_2}:\frac{{x + 8}}{{12}} = \frac{{y + 2}}{5} = \frac{{z + 11}}{9} = \mu $
$N\left( {12\mu - 8,5\mu - 2,9\mu - 11} \right)$
$\overrightarrow {MN} = \left( {4\lambda - 12\mu + 12,\lambda - 5\mu + 6,3\lambda - 9\mu + 16} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 1 \right)$
Now
${\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  4&1&3 \\
  {12}&5&9
\end{array}} \right| = - 6\hat i + 8\hat k\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 2 \right)$
Equation (1) and (2)
$\therefore \,\,\frac{{4\lambda - 12\mu + 13}}{{ - 6}} = \frac{{\lambda - 5\mu + 6}}{0} = \frac{{3\lambda - 9\mu + 16}}{8}$
I and II
$\lambda - 5\mu + 6 = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 3 \right)$
I and III
$\lambda - 3\mu + 4 = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 4 \right)$
Solve (3) and (4) we get
$\lambda = - 1,\,\,\mu = 1$
$\therefore \,\,M\left( {1,3,2} \right),\,\,N\left( {4,3, - 2} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,\,\overrightarrow {OM} \cdot \overrightarrow {ON} = 4 + 9 - 4 = 9$

Question: The lines \[\bar r = \;(\hat i - \hat j) + \;l\;(2\hat i + \hat k)\;\,\,{\text{and}}\;\,\,\bar r = \;(2\hat i - \hat j) + m\;(\hat i + \hat j - \hat k)\]
Options: do not intersect for any values of l and m
intersect for all values of l and m
Solution:
\[\bar r = \;(\hat i - \hat j) + \;l\;(2\hat i + \hat k)\] ………(1)
\[\bar r = \;(2\hat i - \hat j) + m\;(\hat i + \hat j - \hat k)\] ………(2)
Let two lines \[\bar r = \;\bar a + t\bar b\]
and \[\bar r = \;\bar c + t\bar d\]
shortest distance \[(d) = \;\frac{{|(\bar a - \bar c)\;.\;(\bar b\; \times \;\bar d)|}}{{|\bar b \times \bar d|}}\]
\[ = \;\frac{{|\{ (\hat i - \hat j) - (2\hat i - \hat j)\} \,.\;\{ (2\hat i + \hat k) \times \;(\hat i + \hat j - \hat k))|}}{{|(2\hat i + \hat k)\; \times \;(\hat i + \hat j - \hat k)}}\]
\[ = \;\frac{{|( - \hat i)\,.\;( - \hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k)|}}{{| - \hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k|}}\]
\[ = \;\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{{( - 1)}^2} + \;{{( - 1)}^2} + {2^2}} }}\]
\[ = \;\frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\]
Since shortest distance between these lines exist
Hence lines do not intersect.

Question:
A line ‘$l$’ passing through origin is perpendicular to the lines
$\begin{gathered}
  {l_1}:\overrightarrow r = \left( {3 + t} \right)\widehat i + \left( { - 1 + 2t} \right)\widehat j + \left( {4 + 2t} \right)\widehat k \\
  {l_2}:\overrightarrow r = \left( {3 + 2s} \right)\widehat i + \left( {3 + 2s} \right)\widehat j + \left( {2 + s} \right)\widehat k \\
\end{gathered} $
If the co-ordinates of the point in the first octant on ‘${l_2}$’ at a distance of $\sqrt {17} $ from the point of intersection of ‘$l$’ and ‘${l_1}$’ are $\left( {a,\;b,\;c} \right),$ then $18\left( {a + b + c} \right)$ is equal to:
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

$\begin{gathered}
  {l_1}:\overrightarrow r = \left( {3 + t} \right)\widehat i + \left( { - 1 + 2t} \right)\widehat j + \left( {4 + 2t} \right)\widehat k \\
  {l_2}:\overrightarrow r = \left( {3 + 2s} \right)\widehat i + \left( {3 + 2s} \right)\widehat j + \left( {4 + s} \right)\widehat k \\
\end{gathered} $
DR of ${l_1} \equiv \left( {1,\;2,\;2} \right)$
DR of ${l_2} \equiv \left( {2,\;2,\;1} \right)$
DR of $l\left( {{\text{line}}\; \bot \;{\text{to}}\;{l_1}\;{\text{and}}\;{l_2}} \right)$
$ = \left( { - 2,\;3,\; - 2} \right)$
$\therefore \;\;l:\overrightarrow r = - 2\mu \widehat i + 3\mu \widehat j - 2\mu \widehat k$
For intersection of $l\;{\text{and}}\;{l_1}$
$\begin{gathered}
  3 + t = - 2\mu \\
   - 1 + 2t = 3\mu \\
  4 + 2t = - 2\mu \\
\end{gathered} $
$ \Rightarrow \;\;t = - 1\;{\text{and}}\;\mu = - 1$
$\therefore \;\;$Point of intersection $P \equiv \left( {2,\; - 3,\;2} \right)$
Let point on ${l_2}$ be $Q\left( {3 + 2s,\;3 + 2s,\;2 + s} \right)$
Given $PQ = \sqrt {17} \;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;{\left( {PQ} \right)^2} = 17$
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \;\;{\left( {2s + 1} \right)^2} + {\left( {6 + 2s} \right)^2} + {\left( s \right)^2} = 17 \\
   \Rightarrow \;\;9{s^2} + 28s + 20 = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $
$ \Rightarrow \;\;s = - 2,\; - \frac{{10}}{9}$
$s \ne - 2$ as point lies on 1st octant.
$\therefore \;\;a = 3 + 2\left( { - \frac{{10}}{9}} \right) = \frac{7}{9}$
$\begin{gathered}
  b = 3 + 2\left( { - \frac{{10}}{9}} \right) = \frac{7}{9} \\
  c = 2 + \left( { - \frac{{10}}{9}} \right) = \frac{8}{9} \\
\end{gathered} $
$\therefore \;\;18\left( {a + b + c} \right) = 18\left( {\frac{{22}}{9}} \right) = 44$

Question: A plane containing the point (3, 2, 0) and the line \[\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{5} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}\] also contains the point
Solution:
Equation of the required plane is
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 1}&{y - 2}&{z - 3} \\
  2&0&{ - 3} \\
  1&5&4
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 15\left( {x - 1} \right) - 11\left( {y - 2} \right) + 10\left( {z - 3} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 15x - 11y + 10z - 15 + 22 - 30 = 0$
\[15x - 11y + 10z = 23\]
(0, 7, 10) satisfies this.

Question: If the distance of the point $(a, 2,5)$ from the image of the point $(1,2,7)$ in the line $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-2}{2}$ is 4, then the sum of all possible values of a is equal to :
Solution:
$P Q \perp L \Rightarrow(\alpha-1)+(\beta-2)+2(\gamma-7)=0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow \alpha+\beta+2 \gamma=17 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$

M is mid point of PQ which will satisfy L
$\frac{\alpha+1}{2}=\frac{\frac{\beta+2}{2}-1}{1}=\frac{\frac{\gamma+7}{2}-2}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\alpha+1}{2}=\frac{\beta}{2}=\frac{\gamma+3}{4}$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow \alpha+1=\beta \tag{2}
\end{equation*}
$$
and $2 \beta=\gamma+3$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow \alpha=3, \beta=4, \gamma=5 \tag{3}
\end{equation*}
$$
Distance from (a, 2, 5) is $=\sqrt{(a-3)^{2}+4+0}=4$
$(\mathrm{a}-3)^{2}+4=16 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}^{2}-6 \mathrm{a}-3=0 \Rightarrow$ sum of values of $\mathrm{a}=6$

Question: Let $S$ be the set of all values of $\lambda $, for which the shortest distance between the lines $\frac{{x - \lambda }}{0} = \frac{{y - 3}}{4} = \frac{{z + 6}}{1}$ and $\frac{{x + \lambda }}{3} = \frac{y}{{ - 4}} = \frac{{z - 6}}{0}$ is 13. Then $8\left| {\sum\limits_{\lambda  \in S} \lambda  } \right|$ is equal to
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

Shortest distance $ = \frac{{\left| {\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  0&4&1 \\
  3&{ - 4}&0 \\
  {2\lambda }&3&{ - 12}
\end{array}} \right|} \right|}}{{\left| {\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  0&4&1 \\
  3&{ - 4}&0
\end{array}} \right|} \right|}}$
$13 = \frac{{\left| {153 + 8\lambda } \right|}}{{\left| {4\hat i + 3\hat j - 12\hat k} \right|}}$
$ = \frac{{\left| {153 + 8\lambda } \right|}}{{13}}$
$\left| {153 + 8\lambda } \right| = 169$
$153 + 8\lambda = 169, - 169$
$\lambda = \frac{{16}}{8},\frac{{ - 322}}{8}$
$8\left| {\sum\limits_{\lambda \in S} \lambda } \right| = 306$

Question:
Let A be the point of intersection of the lines
\[{L_1}:\frac{{x - 7}}{1} = \frac{{y - 5}}{0} = \frac{{z - 3}}{{ - 1}}\] and \[{L_2}:\frac{{x - 1}}{3} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z + 7}}{5}\]. Let B and C be the point on the lines L1 and L2 respectively such that AB = AC = \[\sqrt {15} \]. Then the square of the area of the triangle ABC is:
Solution:
Angle between both lines
\[\cos \theta = \left| {\frac{{3 + 0 - 5}}{{\sqrt 2 \sqrt {50} }}} \right|\]
\[\sin \theta = \frac{2}{{10}} = \frac{1}{5}\]
\[{\text{area}} = \frac{1}{2}ab\sin \theta \]
\[\frac{1}{2}\sqrt {15} \sqrt {15} \frac{{\sqrt {24} }}{5}\]
square of area \[\frac{{15.15.24}}{{4.25}}\]

Question: The distance of the point (–1, 2, –2) from the line of intersection of the planes  $2x + 3y + 2z = 0$ and $x - 2y + z = 0$ is:
Solution:
\[{P_1}:2x + 3y + 2z = 0\]
$ \Rightarrow {\vec n_1} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k$
${P_2} = x - 2y + z = 0$
$ \Rightarrow {\vec n_2} = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k$
Direction vector of line L which is line of intersection of ${P_1}$ & ${P_2}$
$\vec r = {\vec n_1} \times {\vec n_2} = 7\hat i - 7\hat k$
DR’s of L are (1, 0, -1)
$ \Rightarrow $ Equation of $L:\frac{x}{1} = \frac{y}{0} = \frac{z}{{ - 1}} = \lambda $

DR’s of $\overrightarrow {PQ} = \left( {\lambda + 1, - 2,2 - \lambda } \right)$
$\because \,\,\overrightarrow {PQ} \bot \vec r$
$ \Rightarrow \left( {\lambda + 1} \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( { - 2} \right)\left( 0 \right) + \left( {2 - \lambda } \right)\left( { - 1} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow Q\left( {\frac{1}{2},0,\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow PQ = \frac{{\sqrt {34} }}{2}$

Question: The distance of the point (1, –2, 3) from the plane x – y + z = 5 measured parallel to the line \[\frac{x}{2} = \frac{y}{3} = \frac{z}{{ - 6}}\] is :
Solution:
Equation of line through (1, –2, 3) whose d.r.s. are (2, 3, –6)
\[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{{ - 6}} = \lambda \]
any point on line \[(2\lambda + 1,\,3\lambda - 2,\, - 6\lambda + 3)\]
Put in (x – y + z = 5)
\[2\lambda + 1 - 3\lambda + 2 - 6\lambda + 3 = 5\]
\[ - 7\lambda = - 1\]
\[\lambda = \frac{1}{7}\]
distance = \[\sqrt {{{(2\lambda )}^2} + {{(3\lambda )}^2} + {{(6\lambda )}^2}} \]
\[\sqrt {4{\lambda ^2} + 9{\lambda ^2} + 36{\lambda ^2}} = 7\lambda = 1\]

Question: Let the lines $L_{1}: \vec{r}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})$, $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ and $L_{2}: \vec{r}=(4 \hat{i}+\hat{j})+\mu(5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}), \mu \in \mathbb{R}$, intersect at the point R . Let P and Q be the points lying on lines $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{L}_{2}$, respectively, such that $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}}|=\sqrt{29}$ and $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}|=\sqrt{\frac{47}{3}}$. If the point P lies in the first octant, then $27(\mathrm{QR})^{2}$ is equal to
Solution:
For POI
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4 ; 3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1 ; 4 \lambda+3=\mu \\
& \Rightarrow \lambda=\mu=-1 \\
& \mathrm{R}(-1,-1,-1) \quad \mathrm{P}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3) \\
& \mathrm{PR}^{2}=29 \Rightarrow(2 \lambda+2)^{2}+(3 \lambda+3)^{2}+(4 \lambda+4)^{2}=29 \\
& \Rightarrow \lambda=0 \text { or } \lambda=-2 \text { (Reject) } \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{P}(1,2,3) \\
& \mathrm{Q}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu) \\
& |\mathrm{PQ}|=\sqrt{\frac{47}{3}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}^{2}=\frac{47}{3} \\
& \Rightarrow(5 \mu+3)^{2}+(2 \mu-1)^{2}+(\mu-3)^{2}=\frac{47}{3} \\
& \Rightarrow \mu=-\frac{1}{3} \\
& \mathrm{Q}=\left(\frac{7}{3}, \frac{1}{3},-\frac{1}{3}\right) \\
& (\mathrm{QR})^{2}=\left(\frac{7}{3}+1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{3}+1\right)^{2}+\left(-\frac{1}{3}+1\right)^{2} \\
& =\frac{100+16+4}{9}=\frac{120}{9} \\
& \Rightarrow 27 \times(\mathrm{QR})^{2}=27 \times \frac{120}{9}=360
\end{aligned}
$$

Question: The length of the perpendicular drawn from the point (2, 1, 4) to the plane containing the lines \[\bar r = \left( {\hat i + \hat j} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k} \right)\] and \[\bar r = \left( {\hat i + \hat j} \right) + \mu \left( { - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k} \right)\] is ______.
Solution:
The equation of plane containing two given lines is,
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 1}&{y - 1}&z \\
  1&2&{ - 1} \\
  { - 1}&1&{ - 2}
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
On expanding, we get x – y – z = 0
Now, the length of perpendicular from (2, 1 , 4) to this plane
\[ = \left| {\frac{{2 - 1 - 4}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {1^2}} }}} \right| = \sqrt 3 \]

Question: The vertices B and C of a ΔABC lie on the line, $\frac{{x + 2}}{3} = \frac{{y - 1}}{0} = \frac{z}{4}$such that BC = 5 units. Then the area (in sq. units) of this triangle, given that the point \[A\left( {1, - 1,2} \right),\]is:
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

Let a point D on BC $ = \left( {3\lambda - 2,\,\,1,\,\,4\lambda } \right)$
$\overline {AD} = \left( {3\lambda - 3} \right)\hat i + 2\hat j + \left( {4\lambda - 2} \right)\hat k$
\[\because \overrightarrow {AD} \bot \overrightarrow {BC} ,\overrightarrow {AD} .\overrightarrow {BC} = 0\]
$ \Rightarrow \left( {3\lambda - 3} \right)\left( 3 \right) + 2\left( 0 \right) + \left( {4\lambda - 2} \right)4 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{17}}{{25}}$


Hence, $D = \left( {\frac{1}{{25}},1,\frac{{68}}{{25}}} \right)$
$\left| {\overrightarrow {{\text{AD}}} } \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{1}{{25}} - 1} \right)}^2} + {{\left( 2 \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{68}}{{25}} - 2} \right)}^2}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{{{\left( {24} \right)}^2} + 4{{\left( {25} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {18} \right)}^2}}}{{{{25}^2}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3400}}{{{{25}^2}}}} = \frac{{2\sqrt {34} }}{5}$
Area of triangle $ = \frac{1}{2} \times \left| {\overrightarrow {BC} } \right| \times \left| {\overrightarrow {{\text{AD}}} } \right|$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times \frac{{2\sqrt {34} }}{5} = \sqrt {34} $ $\left[ { \mapsto {\text{BC}} = 5} \right]$

Question: The shortest distance between the lines $\frac{{x - 4}}{4} = \frac{{y + 2}}{5} = \frac{{z + 3}}{3}$ and $\frac{{x - 1}}{3} = \frac{{y - 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 4}}{2}$ is

Question:
Consider the lines ${L_1}$ and ${L_2}$ given by
${L_1}:\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{1} = \frac{{z - 2}}{2}$
${L_2}:\frac{{x - 2}}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{2} = \frac{{z - 3}}{3}$
A line ${L_3}$ having direction ratios $1, - 1, - 2$, intersects ${L_1}$ and ${L_2}$ at the points $P$ and $Q$ respectively. Then the length of line segment $PQ$ is
Solution:
Let $P = \left( {2\lambda + 1,\lambda + 3,2\lambda + 2} \right)$
$Q = \left( {\mu + 2,2\mu + 2,3\mu + 3} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{2\lambda - \mu - 1}}{1} = \frac{{\lambda - 2\mu + 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{2\lambda - 3\mu - 1}}{{ - 2}}$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = \mu = 3$
$ \Rightarrow P\left( {7,6,8} \right)$ and $Q\left( {5,8,12} \right)$
$PQ = 2\sqrt 6 $

Question: The shortest distance between the z-axis and the line \[x + y + 2z - 3 = 0 = 2x + 3y + 4z - 4,\] is
Solution:
Given line is
$x + y + 2z - 3 = 0 = 2x + 3y + 4z - 4\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 1 \right)$
Equation of a plane passing through line (1) is
$2x + 3y + 4z - 4 + \lambda \left( {x + y + 2z - 3} \right) = 0$
This plane is parallel to z-axis, if
$2\lambda + 4 = 0\,\,\, \Rightarrow \lambda = - 2$
So, plane becomes
$y + 2 = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 2 \right)$
Shortest distance between z-axis and line (1)
= Distance between z-axis and plane (2)
= Distance between (0, 0, 0) and plane (2)
= 2

Question: A plane passes through the points $A\left( {1,\;2,\;3} \right),\;B\left( {2,\;3,\;1} \right)$ and $C\left( {2,\;4,\;2} \right).$ If O is the origin and P is $\left( {2,\; - 1,\;1} \right),$ then the projection of $\overrightarrow {OP} $ on this plane is of length:
Solution:
Normal to plane \[\overrightarrow n = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\widehat i}&{\widehat j}&{\widehat k} \\
  1&1&{ - 2} \\
  0&1&1
\end{array}} \right|\]
$ = 3\widehat i - \widehat j + \widehat k$
$\overrightarrow {OP} = 2\widehat i - \widehat j + \widehat k$
$\cos \theta = \frac{{6 + 1 + 1}}{{\sqrt 6 \sqrt {11} }} = \frac{8}{{\sqrt {66} }}\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;\sin \theta = \sqrt {\frac{2}{{66}}} $
$\therefore \;\;$Projection of $\overrightarrow {OP} $ on plane $ = \left| {\overrightarrow {OP} } \right|\sin \theta $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{2}{{11}}} $

Question: Let ${P_1}$ be the plane $3x - y - 7z = 11$ and ${P_2}$ be the plane passing through the points (2, –1, 0), (2, 0, –1), and (5, 1, 1). If the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point (7, 4, –1) on the line of intersection of the planes ${P_1}$ and ${P_2}$ is $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$, then $\alpha  + \beta  + \gamma $ is equal to
Solution:
Given,
${P_1}:3x - y - 7z = 11,\,\,{\vec n_1} = \left( {3, - 1, - 7} \right)$
${P_2}:\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 2}&{y + 1}&{z - 0} \\
  {2 - 2}&{0 + 1}&{ - 1 - 0} \\
  {5 - 2}&{1 + 1}&{1 - 0}
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$ \Rightarrow x - y - z = 3;\,\,{\vec n_2} = \left( {1, - 1, - 1} \right)$
Vector along line of intersection is $\vec n = {\vec n_1} \times {\vec n_2}$
$\vec n = 6\hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k$
We need a point on L.O.I.: put $z = 0$ in plane equations, solving eq. we get $x = 4,y = 1$
Required line of intersection
$L:\frac{{x - 4}}{6} = \frac{{y - 1}}{4} = \frac{{z - 0}}{2} = \lambda \left( {{\text{let}}} \right)$
Any point on line $F \equiv \left( {6\lambda + 4,4\lambda + 1,2\lambda } \right)$

F being foot of perpendicular from A
$\overrightarrow {AF} .\,\vec n = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{2}$
$F \equiv \left( {7,3,1} \right) \equiv \left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$

Question: Let the foot of perpendicular from a point \[P\left( {1,2, - 1} \right)\] to the straight line \[L = \frac{x}{1} = \frac{y}{0} = \frac{z}{{ - 1}}\] be N. Let a line be drawn from P parallel to the plane \[x + y + 2z = 0\] which meets L at point Q. If \[\alpha \] is the acute angle between the lines PN and PQ, then \[\cos \alpha \] is equal to ______________ ?
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow {PN} .\left( {\widehat i - \widehat k} \right) = 0\]
\[. \Rightarrow N\left( {1,0, - 1} \right)\]

\[\overrightarrow {PQ} .\left( {\widehat i + \widehat j + 2\widehat k} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \mu = - 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow Q\left( { - 1,0,1} \right)\]
\[\overrightarrow {PN} = 2\widehat j\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\overrightarrow {PQ} = 2\widehat i + 2\widehat j - 2\widehat k\]
\[ \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\].

Question: Consider the lines L<sub>1</sub> : x –1 = y–2 = z and L<sub>2</sub> : x –2 =y=z–1. Let the feet of the perpendiculars from the point P(5,1, –3) on the lines L<sub>1</sub> and L<sub>2</sub> be Q and R respectively. If the area of the triangle PQR is A, then 4A<sup>2</sup> is equal to :
Solution:
\[{L_1}:\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{1} = \frac{{z - 0}}{2}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  Let\;Q\;\left( {\lambda + 1,\;\lambda + 2,\;\lambda } \right) \\
  \overrightarrow {PQ} = \left( {\lambda - 4,\;\lambda - 1,\;\lambda + 3} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\overrightarrow {PQ} .\vec m = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow \;\lambda - 4 + \lambda + 1,\;\lambda + 3 = 0\]
⇒ 3λ = 0
λ = 0
\[ \Rightarrow \;Q\left( {1,2,0} \right)\]
\[{L_2}:\frac{{x - 2}}{1} = \frac{{y - 0}}{1} = \frac{{z - 1}}{2}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  Let\;R\left( {\mu + 2,\;\mu ,\mu + 1} \right)\;\overrightarrow {PR} = \left( {\mu - 3,\;\mu - 1,\;\mu + 4} \right) \\
  \overrightarrow {PR} .\vec n = 0 \\
\end{gathered} \]
μ –3 + μ –1 + μ + 4 = 0
≠ μ = 0
R(2,0,1)
\[Area\;of\;\Delta PQR\,\left( A \right) = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\overrightarrow {PQ} \times \overrightarrow {PR} } \right|\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  A = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\left( { - 4\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k} \right) \times \left( { - 3\hat i + \hat j + 4\hat k} \right)} \right| \\
  A = \frac{1}{2}\left| {7\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right)} \right| \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  { - 4}&1&3 \\
  { - 3}&{ - 1}&4
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   = 7\hat i + 7\hat j + 7\hat k \\
  4{A^2} = 49 \times 3 = 147 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: If the shortest distance between the lines $\frac{{x - 4}}{1} = \frac{{y + 1}}{2} = \frac{z}{{ - 3}}$ and $\frac{{x - \lambda }}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{4} = \frac{{z - 2}}{{ - 5}}$ is $\frac{6}{{\sqrt 5 }}$, then the sum of all possible values of $\lambda $ is:
Solution:
$\frac{{x - 4}}{1} = \frac{{y + 1}}{2} = \frac{z}{{ - 3}}$
$\frac{{x - \lambda }}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{4} = \frac{{z - 2}}{{ - 5}}$
The shortest distance between the lines
$ = \left| {\frac{{\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right) \cdot \left( {{{\vec d}_1} \times {{\vec d}_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec d}_1} \times {{\vec d}_2}} \right|}}} \right|$
$ = \left| {\frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\lambda - 4}&0&2 \\
  1&2&{ - 3} \\
  2&4&{ - 5}
\end{array}} \right|}}{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&2&{ - 3} \\
  2&4&{ - 5}
\end{array}} \right|}}} \right|$
$ = \left| {\frac{{\left( {\lambda - 4} \right)\left( { - 10 + 12} \right) - 0 + 2\left( {4 - 4} \right)}}{{\left| {2\hat i - 1\hat j + 0\hat k} \right|}}} \right|$
$ = \frac{6}{{\sqrt 5 }} = \left| {\frac{{2\left( {\lambda - 4} \right)}}{{\sqrt 5 }}} \right|$
$ = 3 = \left| {\lambda - 4} \right|$
$\lambda - 4 = \pm 3$
$\lambda = 7,1$
Sum of all possible values of $\lambda $ is = 8

Question: If $\left(2 \alpha+1, \alpha^{2}-3 \alpha, \frac{\alpha-1}{2}\right)$ is the image of $(\alpha, 2 \alpha, 1)$ in the line $\frac{\mathrm{x}-2}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{1}$, then the possible value(s) of $\alpha$ is(are) :
Solution:
$\therefore \mathrm{P}$ is mid point of AB and lies on the given line
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{P}\left(\frac{3 \alpha+1}{2}, \frac{\alpha^{2}-\alpha}{2}, \frac{\alpha+1}{4}\right)$

Now put coordinates of P in the line
$\Rightarrow \frac{\frac{3 \alpha+1}{2}-2}{3}=\frac{\frac{\alpha^{2}-\alpha}{2}-1}{2}=\frac{\alpha+1}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\alpha-1}{2}=\frac{\alpha+1}{4}=\frac{\alpha^{2}-\alpha-2}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha=3$
Now for $\alpha=3$, clearly AB is perpendicular to $3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{k}$
for $\alpha=3$ point B is image of point A

Question: A vector $\vec v$ in the first octant is inclined to the x-axis at $60^\circ $, to the y-axis at $45^\circ $ and to the z-axis at an acute angle. If a plane passing through the points $\left( {\sqrt 2 ,1,1} \right)$ and $\left( {a,b,c} \right)$ is normal to $\vec v$, then
Solution:
$\hat v = \cos 60^\circ \hat i + \cos 45^\circ \hat j + \cos \gamma \hat k$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{2} + {\cos ^2}\gamma = 1\,\,\left( {\gamma \to {\text{Acute}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \cos \gamma = \frac{1}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \boxed{\gamma = 60^\circ }$
Equation of plane is
$\frac{1}{2}\left( {x - \sqrt 2 } \right) + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\left( {y + 1} \right) + \frac{1}{2}\left( {z - 1} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow x + \sqrt 2 y + z = 1$
$\left( {a,b,c} \right)$ lies on it.
$ \Rightarrow a + \sqrt 2 b + c = 1$

Question: Let the foot of the perpendicular from the point $A\left( {4,3,1} \right)$ on the plane $P:x - y + 2z + 3 = 0$ be $N$. If $B\left( {5,\alpha ,\beta } \right),\alpha ,\beta  \in \mathbb{Z}$ is a point on plane $P$ such that the area of the triangle $ABN$ is $3\sqrt 2 $, then ${\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2} + \alpha \beta $ is equal to ______.
Solution:
$AN = \sqrt 6 $
$5 - \alpha + 2\beta + 3 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = 8 + 2\beta \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( 1 \right)$
N is given by
$\frac{{x - 4}}{1} = \frac{{y - 3}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 1}}{2} = \frac{{ - \left( {4 - 3 + 2 + 3} \right)}}{{1 + 1 + 4}}$
$ \Rightarrow x = 3,y = 4,z = - 1$
$ \Rightarrow N$ is $\left( {3,4, - 1} \right)$
$BN = \sqrt {4 + {{\left( {\alpha - 4} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\beta + 1} \right)}^2}} $
$ = \sqrt {4 + {{\left( {2\beta + 4} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\beta + 1} \right)}^2}} $
Area of $\Delta ABN = \frac{1}{2}AN \times BN = 3\sqrt 2 $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt 6 \times BN = 3\sqrt 2 $
$BN = 2\sqrt 3 $
$ \Rightarrow 4 + {\left( {2\beta + 4} \right)^2} + {\left( {\beta + 1} \right)^2} = 12$
${\left( {2\beta + 4} \right)^2} + {\left( {\beta + 1} \right)^2} - 8 = 0$
$5{\beta ^2} + 18\beta + 9 = 0$
$\left( {5\beta + 3} \right)\left( {\beta + 3} \right) = 0$
$\beta = - 3$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = 2$
$ \Rightarrow {\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2} + \alpha \beta = 9 + 4 - 6 = 7$

Question: The equation of a plane containing the line of intersection of the planes 2x – y – 4 = 0 and y + 2z – 4 = 0 and passing through the point (1, 1, 0) is:

$ \Rightarrow \lambda = - 1$
Then, equation of required plane is,
$\begin{gathered}
  \left( {2x - y - 4} \right) - \left( {y + 2z - 4} \right) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow 2x - 2y - 2z = 0 \Rightarrow x - y - z = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: If the shortest distance between the line \[\vec r = \;( - \hat i + \;3\hat k)\; + \;\lambda \;(\hat i - \;a\,\hat j)\] and \[\vec r\; = \;( - \hat j\; + \;2\hat k)\; + \;\mu \;(\hat i\; - \;\hat j + \;\hat k)\] is \[\sqrt {\frac{2}{3}} ,\] then the integral value of a is equal to
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

\[{a_1} = \;( - 1,\;0,\;3)\]
\[{a_2} = \;(0,\; - 1,\;2)\]
\[{b_1}\; = \;(1,\; - a,\;0)\;\]dr's of line (1)
\[{b_2}\; = \;(1,\; - 1,\;1)\] dr's of line (2)
\[{\bar a_2} - \;{\bar a_1}\; = \;(1,\; - 1,\; - 1)\]
\[{\bar b_1}\; \times \;{\bar b_2} = \;\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&{ - a}&0 \\
  1&{ - 1}&1
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[{\bar b_1}\; \times \;{\bar b_2} = \;\hat i\;( - a) - \;\hat j + \;\hat k\;(a - 1)\]
\[|{\bar b_1}\; \times \;{\bar b_2}|\; = \;\sqrt {{a^2} + 1 + \;{{(a - 1)}^2}} \]
\[{a_2} - \;{a_1}\,.\,{\bar b_1}\; \times \;{\bar b_2}\; = \;2 - \;2a\]
\[\frac{{2\;(1 - a)}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + 1 + \;{{(a - 1)}^2}} }} = \;\sqrt {\frac{2}{3}} \]
Squaring an both the side
After solving \[a = \;2,\;\frac{1}{2}\]

Question: Let ${L_1}:\vec r = \left( {\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k} \right),\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$, ${L_2}:\vec r = \left( {\hat j - \hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {3\hat i + \hat j + p\hat k} \right),\mu \in \mathbb{R},$ and ${L_3}:\vec r = \delta \left( {l\hat i + m\hat j + n\hat k} \right),\delta \in \mathbb{R}$ be three lines such that ${L_1}$ is perpendicular to ${L_2}$ and ${L_3}$ is perpendicular to both ${L_1}$ and ${L_2}$. Solution:

For $\delta = 1$ the point will be $\left( { - 1,7,4} \right)$

Question: Let two vertices of a triangle $ABC$ be $\left( {2,4,6} \right)$ and $\left( {0, - 2, - 5} \right)$, and its centroid be $\left( {2,1, - 1} \right)$. Solution:

Question: The mirror image of the point (1, 2, 3) in a plane is \[\left( { - \frac{7}{3}, - \frac{4}{3}, - \frac{1}{3}} \right)\]. Which of the following points lies on this plane?
Solution:
Image of point P(1, 2, 3) w.r.t. a plane \[ax + by + cz + d = 0\] is \[Q\left( { - \frac{7}{3}, - \frac{4}{3}, - \frac{1}{3}} \right)\]
Direction ratios of PQ : \[ - \frac{{10}}{3}, - \frac{{10}}{3}, - \frac{{10}}{3} = 1,1,1\]
Direction ratios of normal to plane is 1, 1, 1
Mid-point of PQ lies on the plane
The mid-point of PQ =\[\left( { - \frac{2}{3},\frac{1}{3},\frac{4}{3}} \right)\]
Equation of plane is \[x + \frac{2}{3} + y - \frac{1}{3} + z - \frac{4}{3} = 0\]

Question: If equation of the plane that contains the point $\left( { - 2,3,5} \right)$ and is perpendicular to each of the planes $2x + 4y + 5z = 8$ and $3x - 2y + 3z = 5$ is $\alpha x + \beta y + \gamma z + 97 = 0$ then $\alpha  + \beta  + \gamma  = $
Solution:
The equation of plane passing through $\left( { - 2,3,5} \right)$ is
$a\left( {x + 2} \right) + b\left( {y - 3} \right) + c\left( {z - 5} \right) = 0$
It is perpendicular to $2x + 4y + 5z = 8$ & $3x - 2y + 3z = 5$
$\therefore \,\,2a + 4b + 5c = 0$
$3a - 2b + 3c = 0$
$\therefore \,\,\frac{a}{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  4&5 \\
  { - 2}&3
\end{array}} \right|}} = \frac{{ - b}}{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  2&5 \\
  3&3
\end{array}} \right|}} = \frac{c}{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  2&4 \\
  3&{ - 2}
\end{array}} \right|}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{a}{{22}} = \frac{b}{9} = \frac{c}{{ - 16}}$
$\therefore $ Equation of Plane is
$22\left( {x + 2} \right) + 9\left( {y - 3} \right) - 16\left( {z - 5} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 22x + 9y - 16z + 97 = 0$
Comparing with $\alpha x + \beta y + \gamma z + 97 = 0$
We get $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 22 + 9 - 16 = 15$

Question: The square of the distance of the point of intersection of the line \[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z + 1}}{6}\] and the plane \[2x - y + z = 6\] from the point \[\left( { - 1, - 1,2} \right)\] is _________ ?
Solution:
\[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z + 1}}{6} = \lambda \]
\[x = 2\lambda + 1,y = 3\lambda + 2,z = 6\lambda - 1\]
For point of intersection of line and plane
\[2\left( {2\lambda + 1} \right) - \left( {3\lambda + 2} \right) + \left( {6\lambda - 1} \right) = 6\]
\[7\lambda = 7 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1\]
point : \[\left( {3,5,5} \right)\]
\[{\left( {{\text{distance}}} \right)^2} = {\left( {3 + 1} \right)^2} + {\left( {5 + 1} \right)^2} + {\left( {5 - 2} \right)^2}\]
\[ = 16 + 36 + 9 = 61\]

Question: If the image of the point P(1, –2, 3) in the plane, \[2x + 3y - 4z + 22 = 0\] measured parallel to the line, \[\frac{x}{1} = \frac{y}{4} = \frac{z}{5}\] is Q, then PQ is equal to:

The equation of line PR

Question: Let a line L pass through the point $P\left( {2,3,1} \right)$ and be parallel to the line $x + 3y - 2z - 2 = 0 = x - y + 2z.$ If the distance of L from the point $\left( {5,3,8} \right)$ is $\alpha ,$ then $3{\alpha ^2}$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&3&{ - 2} \\
  1&{ - 1}&2
\end{array}} \right| = 4\hat i - 4\hat j - 4\hat k$
$\therefore $ Equation of line is $\frac{{x - 2}}{1} = \frac{{y - 3}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 1}}$
Let Q be $\left( {5,3,8} \right)$ and foot of $ \bot $ from Q on this line be R.
Now, $R \equiv \left( {k + 2, - k + 3, - k + 1} \right)$
DR of QR are $\left( {k - 3, - k, - k - 7} \right)$
$\therefore \left( 1 \right)\left( {k - 3} \right) + \left( { - 1} \right)\left( { - k} \right) + \left( { - 1} \right)\left( { - k - 7} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow k = - \frac{4}{3}$
$\therefore {\alpha ^2} = {\left( {\frac{{13}}{3}} \right)^2} + {\left( {\frac{4}{3}} \right)^2} + {\left( {\frac{{17}}{3}} \right)^2} = \frac{{474}}{9}$
$\therefore 3{\alpha ^2} = 158$

Question: Consider the line L given by the equation  \[\frac{{x - 3}}{2} = \frac{{y - 1}}{1} = \frac{{z - 2}}{1}\]. & Passes through the point \[\left( {2,3, - 1} \right)\]
Then the equation of plane P is
\[2\left( {x - 2} \right) + 1\left( {y - 3} \right) + 1\left( {z + 1} \right) = 0\]
\[2x + y + z - 6 = 0\]
The point \[\left( {1,2,2} \right)\] satisfies the above equation.

Question: Let P be a plane passing through the points (2, 1, 0), (4, 1, 1) and (5, 0, 1) and R be any point (2,1,6). Then the image of R in the plane P is:
Solution:
Points \[A\left( {2,\,\,1,\,\,0} \right),\,\,B\left( {4,\,\,1,\,\,1,} \right),\,\,C\left( {5,\,\,0,\,\,1} \right)\]
\[\overline {AB} = (2,0,1)\]
\[\overline {AC} = (3, - 1,1)\]
\[\bar n = \overline {AB} \times \overline {AC} = \left( {1,1, - 2} \right)\]
Equation of the plane is \[x + y - 2z = 3.......\left( 1 \right)\]
Let the image of point \[{\text{(2,1,6) is (l,m,n)}}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{l - 2}}{1} = \frac{{m - 1}}{1} = \frac{{n - 6}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{ - 2\left( { - 12} \right)}}{6} = 4 \\

Hence the image of R in the plane P is (6,5, -2)

Question:
Let a line $l$ pass through the origin and be perpendicular to the lines
${l_1}:\vec r = \left( {\hat i - 11\hat j - 7\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k} \right),\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ and ${l_2}:\vec r = \left( {-\hat i + \hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {2\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right),\mu \in \mathbb{R}$.
If $P$ is the point of intersection of $l$ and ${l_1}$, and $Q\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$ is the foot of perpendicular from $P$ on ${l_2}$, then $9\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right)$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

Let $l = \left( {0\hat i + 0\hat j + 0\hat k} \right) + \gamma \left( {a\hat i + b\hat j + c\hat k} \right)$
$ = \gamma \left( {a\hat i + b\hat j + c\hat k} \right)$
$a\hat i + b\hat j + c\hat k = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&2&3 \\
  2&2&1
\end{array}} \right|$
$ = \hat i\left( {2 - 6} \right) - \hat j\left( {1 - 6} \right) + \hat k\left( {2 - 4} \right)$
$ = - 4\hat i + 5\hat j - 2\hat k$
$l = \gamma \left( { - 4\hat i + 5\hat j - 2\hat k} \right)$
$P$ is intersection of $l$ and ${l_1}$
$ - 4\gamma = 1 + \lambda ,5\gamma = - 11 + 2\lambda , - 2\gamma = - 7 + 3\lambda $
By solving these equation $\gamma = - 1,\,P\left( {4, - 5,2} \right)$
Let $Q\left( { - 1 + 2\mu ,2\mu ,1 + \mu } \right)$
$\overrightarrow {PQ} \cdot \left( {2\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right) = 0$
$ - 2 + 4\mu + 4\mu + 1 + \mu = 0$
$9\mu = 1$
$\mu = \frac{1}{9}$
$Q\left( {\frac{{ - 7}}{9},\frac{2}{9},\frac{{10}}{9}} \right)$
$9\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right) = 9\left( {\frac{{ - 7}}{9} + \frac{2}{9} + \frac{{10}}{9}} \right)$
$ = 5$

Question: The shortest distance between the lines $\frac{x}{2} = \frac{y}{2} = \frac{z}{1}$ and $\frac{{x + 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{y - 4}}{8} = \frac{{z - 5}}{4}$ lies in the interval :
Solution:
The shortest distance between the lines $\frac{x}{2} = \frac{y}{2} = \frac{z}{1}$ and $\frac{{x + 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{y - 4}}{8} = \frac{{z - 5}}{4}$ is
$ = \left| {\frac{{\left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right) \cdot \left( {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}} \right|$
Here ${\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&2&1 \\
  { - 1}&8&4
\end{array}} \right| = - 9\hat j + 18\hat k = 9\left( { - \hat j + 2\hat k} \right)$
$\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right| = 9\sqrt 5 $
${\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = - 2i + 4j + 5k$
$\therefore \,\,$ S.D. = $\left| {\frac{{\left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right) \cdot \left( {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{{9\left( { - 4} \right) + 10}}{{9\sqrt 5 }}} \right| = \frac{6}{{\sqrt 5 }}$
Which lies in the interval (2, 3]

Question: Let the equation of the plane, that passes through the point \[\left( {1,4, - 3} \right)\] and contains the line of intersection of the planes \[3x - 2y + 4z - 7 = 0\] and \[x + 5y - 2z + 9 = 0,\] be \[\alpha x + \beta y + \gamma Z + 3 = 0\] then \[\alpha  + \beta  + \gamma \] is equal to ?
Solution:
Equation of plane is
\[3x - 2y + 4z - 7 + \lambda \left( {x + 5y - 2z + 9} \right) = 0\]
\[\left( {3 + \lambda } \right)x + \left( {5\lambda - 2} \right)y + \left( {4 - 2\lambda } \right)z + 9\lambda - 7 = 0\]
passing through \[\left( {1,4, - 3} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3 + \lambda + 20\lambda - 8 - 12 + 6\lambda + 9\lambda - 7 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{2}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \] equation of plane is
\[ - 11x - 4y - 8z + 3 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \alpha + \beta + \gamma = - 23\].

Question: If the plane $2x - y + 2z + 3 = 0$ has the distances $\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{2}{3}$ units from the planes $4x - 2y + 4z + \lambda  = 0$ and $2x - y + 2z + \mu  = 0$, respectively, then the maximum value of $\lambda  + \mu $ is equal to :
Solution:
Let, ${P_1}:2x - y + 2z + 3 = 0$
${P_2}:2x - y + 2z + \frac{\lambda }{2} = 0$
${P_3}:2x - y + 2z + \mu = 0$
Given, distance between ${P_1}$and ${P_2}$ is $\frac{1}{3}$
$\frac{1}{3} = \frac{{\left| {3 - \frac{\lambda }{2}} \right|}}{{\sqrt 9 }} \Rightarrow \left| {3 - \frac{\lambda }{2}} \right| = 1 \Rightarrow {\lambda _{\max }} = 8$
And distance between ${P_1}$ and ${P_3}$ is $\frac{2}{3}$
$\frac{2}{3} = \frac{{\left| {\mu - 3} \right|}}{{\sqrt 9 }} \Rightarrow {\mu _{\max }} = 5$
$ \Rightarrow {\left( {\lambda + \mu } \right)_{\max }} = 13$

Question: Let \[{L_1}:\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}\] and \[{L_2}:\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{y - 4}}{4} = \frac{{z - 5}}{5}\] be two lines. Then which of the following points lies on the line of the shortest distance between L<sub>1</sub> and L<sub>2</sub> ?
Solution:
\[P\left( {2\lambda + 1,3\lambda + 2,4\lambda + 3} \right)\] on L1
\[Q\left( {3\mu + 2,4\mu + 4,5\mu + 5} \right)\] on L2
Dr’s of \[PQ = 3\mu - 2\lambda + 1,4\mu - 3\lambda + 2,5\mu - 4\lambda + 2\]
\[PQ \bot {L_1}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {3\mu - 2\lambda + 1} \right)2 + \left( {4\mu - 3\lambda + 2} \right)3 + \left( {5\mu - 4\underset{\raise0.3em\hbox{$\smash{\scriptscriptstyle-}$}}{\lambda } + 2} \right)4 = 0\]
\[50\mu - 38\lambda + 21 = 0\,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)\]
By (1) & (2)
\[\lambda = \frac{1}{3};\mu = \frac{{ - 1}}{6}\]
\[\therefore \,P\left( {\frac{5}{3},3,\frac{{13}}{3}} \right)\,\,\,\,\& \,\,Q\left( {\frac{3}{2},\frac{{10}}{3},\frac{{25}}{6}} \right)\]
Line PQ
\[\frac{{x - \frac{5}{3}}}{{\frac{1}{6}}}\,\,\,\,\frac{{y - 3}}{{\frac{{ - 1}}{3}}}\,\,\,\,\frac{{z - \frac{{13}}{3}}}{{\frac{1}{6}}}\]
\[\frac{{x - \frac{5}{3}}}{1} = \frac{{y - 3}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - \frac{{13}}{3}}}{1}\]
Point \[\left( {\frac{{14}}{3}, - 3,\frac{{22}}{3}} \right)\]
lies on the line PQ

Question: Let the foot of perpendicular from the point $(\lambda, 2,3)$ on the line $\frac{x-4}{1}=\frac{y-9}{2}=\frac{z-5}{1}$ be the point ( $1, \mu, 2$ ). Then the distance between the lines $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z+4}{6}$ and $\frac{x-\lambda}{2}=\frac{y-\mu}{3}=\frac{z+5}{6}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\mathrm{Q}(1, \mu, 2)$ satisfies the line L
$\therefore \frac{1-4}{1}=\frac{\mu-9}{2}=\frac{2-5}{1}$
$\Rightarrow-3=\frac{\mu-9}{2}=-3 \quad \Rightarrow \mu=3$
D.R. of PQ : $\lambda-1,2-\mu, 1$
Since PQ $\perp \mathrm{L} \Rightarrow(\lambda-1) .1+2(2-\mu)+1.1=0$
$\Rightarrow \lambda-2 \mu+4=0$
$\therefore \lambda=2$
Now lines : $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z+4}{6}$ and
$\frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{y-3}{3}=\frac{z+5}{6}$
i.e. $\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+t_{1}(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})$
$\& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mathrm{t}_{2}(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$
Distance
$=\frac{|((\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})-(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})) \times(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})|}{|2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}|}$
$=\frac{|-9 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-\hat{k}|}{7}=\frac{\sqrt{146}}{7}$

Question:
If the plane P passes through the intersection of two mutually perpendicular planes \[2x + \;5y - \;5z = \;1\] and \[3kx - \;ky + \;z = \;5,\] \[k\; < \;3\] and intercepts a unit length on positive 
x-axis, then the intercept made by the plane P on the y-axis is
Solution:
Two given planes mutually perpendicular
\[2\;(3k)\; + \;k\;( - k)\; + \;( - 5)\;1\; = \;0\]
\[k\; = \;1,\;5\]
But $k < 3$ so, $k = 1$
Plane passing through these planes is
\[2x + \;y - 5z - 1\; + \;\lambda \;(3x - y + \;z - \;5)\; = \;0\]
\[\frac{x}{{\frac{{5\lambda + 1}}{{2 + \;3\lambda }}}} + \;\frac{y}{{\frac{{5\lambda + 1}}{{1 - \lambda }}}}\; + \;\frac{z}{{\frac{{5\lambda + \;1}}{{\lambda - 5}}}} = \;1\]
Given \[\frac{{5\lambda + 1}}{{2 + \;3\lambda }} = \;1\; \Rightarrow \;\lambda \; = \;\frac{1}{2}\]
So intercept on y-axis \[ = \;\frac{{5\lambda + 1}}{{1 - \;\lambda }} = \;7\]

Question: Let P be the plane passing through the line $\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{z + 5}}{7}$ and the point (2, 4, –3). If the image of the point (–1, 3, 4) in the plane P is $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$ then $\alpha  + \beta  + \gamma $ is equal to
Solution:
Vector perpendicular to plane is given by

$\vec n = \lambda \left( {\left( {1,2,2} \right) \times \left( {1, - 3,7} \right)} \right)$
$\vec n = \lambda \left( {4\hat i - \hat j - \hat k} \right)$
Equation of plane is given by
$\overrightarrow {AP} \bot \vec n \Rightarrow \overrightarrow {AP} \cdot \vec n = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \left( {\left( {x - 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {y - 2} \right)\hat j + \left( {z + 5} \right)\hat k} \right) \cdot \left( {4\hat i - \hat j - \hat k} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 4x - y - z - 7 = 0$
Image of point $\left( { - 1,3,4} \right)$ in plane $4x - y - z - 7 = 0$, is given by
$\frac{{\alpha + 1}}{4} = \frac{{\beta - 3}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{\gamma - 4}}{{ - 1}} = - 2\left( {\frac{{4\left( { - 1} \right) - 3 - 4 - 7}}{{{4^2} + {1^2} + {1^2}}}} \right)$
$\alpha = 7,\beta = 1,\gamma = 2$
$\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 10$

Question:
The line of intersection of the planes
\[\bar r.\left( {3\widehat i - \widehat j + \widehat k} \right) = 1\;{\text{and}}\;\bar r.\left( {\widehat i + 4\widehat j - 2\widehat k} \right) = 2,\] is:
Solution:
Here, \[{\bar n_1} = 3\widehat i - \widehat j + \widehat k\;{\text{and}}\;{\bar n_2} = \widehat i + 4\widehat j - 2\widehat k\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \bar n = {{\bar n}_1} \times {{\bar n}_2} \\
  \,\,\,\,\, = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\widehat i}&{\widehat j}&{\widehat k} \\
  3&{ - 1}&1 \\
  1&4&{ - 2}
\end{array}} \right| \\
  \,\,\,\,\, = \widehat i\left( { - 2} \right) - \widehat j\left( { - 7} \right) + \widehat k\left( {13} \right) \\
  \bar n = - 2\widehat i + 7\widehat j + 13\widehat k \\
\end{gathered} \]
Now, \[\bar r = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \,\,3x - y + z = 1\,\,\,\,{\text{and}} \\
  x + 4y - 2z = 2 \\
  {\text{but}}\;z = 0 \\
  3x - y = 1 \times 4 \\
  x + 4y = 2 \\
  13x = 6 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,x = \frac{6}{{13}},\;\,y = \frac{5}{{13}}\,\;{\text{and}}\,\,\,z = 0 \\
\end{gathered} \]
Therefore required line is
\[\frac{{x - \frac{6}{{13}}}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{y - \frac{5}{{13}}}}{7} = \frac{{z - 0}}{{13}}\]
or
\[\frac{{x - \frac{6}{{13}}}}{2} = \frac{{y - \frac{5}{{13}}}}{{ - 7}} = \frac{z}{{ - 13}}\]

Question:
Let the lines ${l_1}:\frac{{x + 5}}{3} = \frac{{y + 4}}{1} = \frac{{z - \alpha }}{{ - 2}}$ and ${l_2}:3x + 2y + z - 2 = 0 = x - 3y + 2z - 13$
be coplanar. If the point ${\text{P}}\left( {a,b,c} \right)$ on ${l_1}$ is nearest to the point ${\text{Q}}\left( { - 4, - 3,2} \right)$, then $\left| a \right| + \left| b \right| + \left| c \right|$ is equal to
Solution:
$\left( {3x + 2y + z - 2} \right) + \mu \left( {x - 3y + 2z - 13} \right) = 0$
$3\left( {3 + \mu } \right) + 1.\left( {2 - 3\mu } \right) - 2\left( {1 + 2\mu } \right) = 0$
$9 - 4\mu = 0$
$\mu = \frac{9}{4}$
$4\left( { - 15 - 8 + \alpha - 2} \right) + 9\left( { - 5 + 12 + 2\alpha - 13} \right) = 0$
$ - 100 + 4\alpha - 54 + 18\alpha = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = 7$
Let $P \equiv \left( {3\lambda - 5,\lambda - 4, - 2\lambda + 7} \right)$
Direction ratio of $PQ\left( {3\lambda - 1,\lambda - 1, - 2\lambda + 5} \right)$
But $PQ \bot {l_1}$
$ \Rightarrow 3\left( {3\lambda - 1} \right) + 1\left( {\lambda - 1} \right) - 2\left( { - 2\lambda + 5} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = 1$
$P\left( { - 2, - 3,5} \right) \Rightarrow \left| a \right| + \left| b \right| + \left| c \right| = 10$

Question: If the two lines \[{l_1}:\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 2}},\;z = \;2\] and \[{l_2}:\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{2y + 3}}{\alpha } = \frac{{z + 5}}{2}\] perpendicular, then an angle between the lines \[{l_2}\] and \[{l_3}:\frac{{1 - x}}{3} = \frac{{2y - 1}}{{ - 4}} = \frac{z}{4}\] is:
Solution:
\[{l_1}:\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 2}}{0}\]
\[{l_2}:\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3/2}}{{\alpha /2}} = \frac{{z + 5}}{2}\]
\[{l_3}:\frac{{x - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y - 1/2}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 0}}{4}\]
\[{l_1} \bot {l_2} \Rightarrow \frac{{\left| {3 - \alpha + 0} \right|}}{{\sqrt {13} \sqrt {1 + \frac{{{\alpha ^2}}}{4} + 4} }} = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha = 3\]
angle between \[{l_2}\& {l_3}\]
\[\cos \theta = \frac{{\left| {1 \times ( - 3) + ( - 2)(\alpha /2) + 2 \times 4} \right|}}{{\sqrt {1 + 4 + \frac{{{\alpha ^2}}}{4}\sqrt {9 + 16 + 4} } }}\]
\[\cos \theta = \frac{{\left| { - 3 - \alpha + 8} \right|}}{{\sqrt {5 + \frac{{{\alpha ^2}}}{4}\sqrt {29} } }}\]
put \[\alpha = 3\]
\[\cos \theta = \frac{2}{{\sqrt {\frac{{29}}{4}\sqrt {29} } }} = \frac{4}{{29}}\]
\[\theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{4}{{29}}} \right) \Rightarrow \theta = {\sec ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{29}}{4}} \right)\]

Question: The distance of the point $\left( { - 1,2,3} \right)$ from the plane $\vec r \cdot \left( {\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) = 10$ parallel to the line of the shortest distance between the lines $\vec r = \left( {\hat i - \hat j} \right) + \lambda \left( {2\hat i + \hat k} \right)$ and $\vec r = \left( {2\hat i - \hat j} \right) + \mu \left( {\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right)$ is
Solution:
Let ${L_1}:\vec r = \left( {\hat i - \hat j} \right) + \lambda \left( {2\hat i + \hat k} \right)$
${L_2}:\vec r = \left( {2\hat i - \hat j} \right) + \mu \left( {\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right)$
$\vec n = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&0&1 \\
  1&{ - 1}&1
\end{array}} \right|$
$\vec n = \hat i - \hat j - 2\hat k$

Equation of line along shortest distance of ${L_1}$ and ${L_2}$
$\frac{{x + 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{{ - 2}} = r$
$ \Rightarrow \left( {x,y,z} \right) \equiv \left( {r - 1,2 - r,3 - 2r} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \left( {r - 1} \right) - 2\left( {2 - r} \right) + 3\left( {3 - 2r} \right) = 10$
$ \Rightarrow r = - 2$
$ \Rightarrow Q\left( {x,y,z} \right) \equiv \left( { - 3,4,7} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow PQ = \sqrt {4 + 4 + 16} = 2\sqrt 6 $

Question: If the foot of the perpendicular from point (4, 3, 8) on the line ${{\text{L}}_1}:\frac{{x - {\text{a}}}}{l} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - {\text{b}}}}{4}$, $l \ne 0$ is (3, 5, 7), then the shortest distance between the line L<sub>1</sub> and line ${{\text{L}}_2}:\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{y - 4}}{4} = \frac{{z - 5}}{5}$ is equal to
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

Direction ratio of line ${L_1}:\frac{{x - a}}{l} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - b}}{4}$ is \[l,3,4\]
Let \[P\left( {4,3,8} \right)\]and \[Q\left( {3,5,7} \right)\]
Now, direction ratio of \[PQ \equiv \left( { - 1,2, - 1} \right)\]
\[\therefore PQ \bot {L_1}\]
\[ \Rightarrow - l + 6 - 4 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow l = 2\]
Now point \[Q\left( {3,5,7} \right)\] satisfies line \[{L_1},\]
\[ \Rightarrow a = 1,\,\,b = 3\]
Now shortest distance between
\[{L_1}:\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}\] and ${{\text{L}}_2}:\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{y - 4}}{4} = \frac{{z - 5}}{5}$ is
\[d = \frac{{\left( {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right) \cdot \left( {\overrightarrow {{a_2}} - \overrightarrow {{a_1}} } \right)}}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}\]
\[\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\widehat i}&{\widehat j}&{\widehat k} \\
  2&3&4 \\
  3&4&5
\end{array}} \right| = - \widehat i + 2\widehat j - \widehat k\]
\[\overrightarrow {{a_2}} - \overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \widehat i + 2\widehat j + 2\widehat k\]
\[\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right| = \sqrt 6 \]
\[d = \frac{{\left( { - 1 + 4 - 2} \right)}}{{\sqrt 6 }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\]
