Question: Let the plane P: $4x - y + z = 10$ be rotated by an angle $\frac{\pi }{2}$ about its line of intersection with the plane $x + y - z = 4$. If $\alpha $. is the distance of the point $\left( {2,3, - 4} \right)$ from the new position of the plane ${\text{P}}$, then $35\alpha $ is equal to
Solution:
Let equation in new position is
$\left( {4x - y + z - 10} \right) + \lambda \left( {x + y - z - 4} \right) = 0$
$4\left( {4 + \lambda } \right) - 1\left( { - 1 + \lambda } \right) + 1\left( {1 - \lambda } \right) = 0$
$\lambda = - 9$
So equation in new position is
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{{54}}{{15}}$

Question: Let the points on the plane P be equidistant from the points \[( - 4,\;2,\;1)\] and \[(2,\; - 2,\;3).\] Then the acute angle between the plane P and the plane $2x + y + 3z = 1$ is
Solution:
Normal vector \[ = \;\overrightarrow {AB} = \;(\overrightarrow {OB} \; - \;\overrightarrow {OA} )\]
\[ = \;(6\hat i\; - \;4\hat j + \;2\hat k)\]
or \[2\;(3\hat i - 2\hat j + \;\hat k)\]
\[P\; \equiv \;3\;(x + 1) - \;2(y) + \;1\;(z - 2) = \;0\]
\[P \equiv \;3x - 2y + \;z + 1\; = \;0\]
\[P' \equiv \;2x + y\; + \;3z - 1\; = \;0\]
angle between \[P\& \;P' = \;\left| {\,\frac{{{{\hat n}_1}\, \cdot \;{{\hat n}_2}}}{{|{n_1}|\;|{n_2}|}}\,} \right| = \;\cos \;\theta \]
\[\theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\;\left( {\frac{{6 - 2 + 3}}{{\sqrt {14} \times \;\sqrt {14} }}} \right)\]
\[\theta = \;{\cos ^{ - 1}}\;\left( {\frac{7}{{14}}} \right) = \;{\cos ^{ - 1}}\;\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right) = \;\frac{\pi }{3}\]
Option (c) is correct.

Question: A variable plane passes through a fixed point (3, 2, 1) and meets x, y and z axes at A, B and C respectively. A plane is drawn parallel to yz-plane through A, a second plane is drawn parallel zx-plane through B and a third plane is drawn parallel to xy-plane through C. Then the locus of the point of intersection of these three planes, is:
Solution:
Let a, b, c be the intercepts of the variable plane on the
x, y, z axes respectively, then the equation of the plane is
\[\frac{x}{a}{\mathbf{ + }}\frac{y}{b}{\mathbf{ + }}\frac{z}{c}{\mathbf{ = }}1.\]
And the point of intersection of the planes parallel to the xy, yz, zx planes is clearly the point (a, b, c)
Since the point (3, 2, 1) lies on the variable plane, we have \[\frac{3}{a}{\mathbf{ + }}\frac{2}{b}{\mathbf{ + }}\frac{1}{c}{\mathbf{ = }}1.\]
Thus, the required locus is \[\frac{3}{x}{\mathbf{ + }}\frac{2}{y}{\mathbf{ + }}\frac{1}{z}{\mathbf{ = }}1.\]

Question:
Consider the three planes
$\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  {{P_1}:3x + 15y + 21z = 9} \\
  {{P_2}:x - 3y - z = 5,{\text{ and }}} \\
  {{P_3}:2x + 10y + 14z = 5}
\end{array}$
Then, which one of the following is true?
Options: P<sub>1</sub>, P<sub>2</sub> and P<sub>3</sub> are parallel
Solution:
\[{P_1}:x + 5y + 7z = 3\]
\[{P_2}:x - 3y - z = 5\]
\[{P_3}:x + 5y + 7z = \frac{5}{2}\]
\[{P_1}\] and \[{P_3}\] are parallel as dr’s of normal are same

Question: If the distances of the point $(1,2, a)$ from the line $\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z-1}{1}$ along the lines $\mathrm{L}_{1}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-2}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\mathrm{L}_{2}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-2}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{c}}$ are equal, then $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\mathrm{L}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{1}$
$\mathrm{L}_{1}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-2}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}=\lambda$
$\mathrm{L}_{2}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-2}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{c}}=\mu$
Let $\mathrm{A}(3 \lambda+1,4 \lambda+2, \mathrm{~b} \lambda+\mathrm{a})$
It lies on L
$\therefore \frac{3 \lambda}{1}=\frac{4 \lambda+2}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{b} \lambda+\mathrm{a}-1}{1}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=1$ and $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-1=3$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}(4,6,4), \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=4 \tag{1}
\end{equation*}
$$
Let $\mathrm{B}(\mu+1,4 \mu+2, \mathrm{c} \mu+\mathrm{a})$
It also lies on L
$\frac{\mu}{1}=\frac{4 \mu+2}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{c} \mu+\mathrm{a}-1}{1}$
$\Rightarrow 2 \mu=4 \mu+2$
$\Rightarrow \mu=-1$
$\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{c}-1=-1$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{c}$
$\ldots$...(2) & $\mathrm{B}(0,-2,0)$
also $\mathrm{PA}=\mathrm{PB}, \mathrm{P}(1,2, \mathrm{a}), \mathrm{A}(4,6,4)$
$\Rightarrow 9+16+(\mathrm{a}-4)^{2}=1+16+\mathrm{a}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 16+8=8 \mathrm{a}$
$\mathrm{a}=3 \quad \therefore \mathrm{c}=3, \mathrm{~b}=1$
$\therefore \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=7$

Question: Let the point \[\left( {-1,\alpha ,\beta } \right)\] lie on the line of the shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x + 2}}{{-3}} = \frac{{y-2}}{4} = \frac{{z-5}}{2}\] and \[\frac{{x + 2}}{{-1}} = \frac{{y + 6}}{{-1}} = \frac{{z-1}}{0}.\] Then \[{\left( {\alpha -\beta } \right)^2}\] equal to _________.
Solution:
\[P\left( {-3\lambda -2,4\lambda + 2,2\lambda + 5} \right)\]
\[Q\left( {-\mu -2,2\mu -6,1} \right)\]
DRS of \[PQ = \left( {3\lambda -\mu ,2\mu -4\lambda -8,-2\lambda -4} \right)\]
DRS of \[PQ = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  {-1}&2&0 \\
  {-3}&4&2
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[ = \left( {4\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k} \right)\]
OR
\[\left( {2,1,1} \right)\]
\[\frac{{3\lambda -\mu }}{2} = \frac{{2\mu -4\lambda }}{1} = \frac{{-2\lambda -4}}{1}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \mu = \lambda + 2\] and \[7\lambda = \mu -8\]
\[\boxed{\lambda = -1}\] \[\boxed{\mu = 1}\]
\[Q:\left( {-3,-4,1} \right)\]
\[{L_{PQ}} = \frac{{x + 3}}{2} = \frac{{y + 4}}{1} = \frac{{z-1}}{1}\]
\[\left( {-1,\alpha ,\beta } \right) \Rightarrow 1 = \frac{{\alpha + 4}}{1} = \frac{{\beta -1}}{1}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \alpha = -3,\beta = 2\]
\[{\left( {\alpha -\beta } \right)^2} = 25\]

Question: Let (λ , 2, 1) be a point on the plane which passes through the point (4, –2, 2). If the plane is perpendicular to the line joining the points (–2, –21, 29) and (–1, –16, 23), then ${\left( {\frac{\lambda }{{11}}} \right)^2} - \frac{{4\lambda }}{{11}} - 4$ is equal to ____ .
Solution:
Direction ratio of normal to the plane is (– 1, – 5, 6 ) & plane is passing through (4, –2, 2) Equation of plane is \[x + 5y - 6z + 18 = 0\]
Now, \[\left( {\lambda ,2,1} \right)\] is point on the plane
\[\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \lambda + 10 - 6 + 18 = 0 \\
  \lambda = - 22 \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\therefore \] Req. value
\[{\left( {\frac{\lambda }{{11}}} \right)^2} - \frac{{4\lambda }}{{11}} - 4 = {\left( {\frac{{ - 22}}{{11}}} \right)^2} - \frac{{4\left( { - 22} \right)}}{{11}} - 4 = 8\]

Question: The foot of the perpendicular from a point on the circle \[{x^2} + \;{y^2} = 1,\;z = \;0\] to the plane \[2x + \;3y + z = \;6\] lies on which one of the following curves ?
Solution:
\[\frac{{h - \cos \theta }}{2} = \;\frac{{k - \sin \,\theta }}{3} = \;\frac{{w - 0}}{1}\]
\[ = \;\frac{{ - 1\;(2\cos \theta + \;3\sin \;\theta - 6)}}{{14}}\]
\[h = \;\cos \theta + \;\frac{{ - \,2\;(2\cos \theta + \;3\sin \theta - \;6)}}{{14}}\]
\[ = \;\frac{{10\;\cos \theta - \;6\sin \;\theta + \;12}}{{14}}\]
\[k = \;\sin \;\theta - \frac{3}{{14}}\;(2\cos \theta + \;3\sin \;\theta - 6)\]
\[k = \;\frac{{5\;\sin \theta - \;6\,\cos \theta + \;18}}{{14}}\]
Eliminating \[\sin \theta \;\,{\text{and}}\;\,\cos \theta \]
\[{(5h + \;6k - 12)^2} + \;4\;{(3h + \;5k - 9)^2} = \;1\]

Question:
If the shortest distance between the lines
\[\begin{gathered}
  {L_1}:\vec r = \left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {1 - 3\lambda } \right)\hat j + \left( {3 + 4\lambda } \right)\hat k,\;\lambda \in R \\
  {L_2}:\vec r = 2\left( {1 + \mu } \right)\hat i + 3\left( {1 + \mu } \right)\hat j + \left( {5 + \mu } \right)\hat k,\;\mu \in R \\
\end{gathered} \]
is \[\frac{m}{{\sqrt n }},\] where gcd (m, n) = 1, then the value of m + n equals.
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  Shortes\;{\text{distance}}\;(CD) = \;\left| {\frac{{\overline {AB} \;.\;\vec p \times \vec q}}{{\left| {\vec p \times \vec q} \right|}}} \right| \\
   = \left| {\frac{{\left( {0\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k} \right).\left( { - 15\hat i + 7\hat j + 9\hat k} \right)}}{{\sqrt {355} }}} \right| \\
  \therefore \;m + n = 32 + 355 = 387 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question:
Let the area of the triangle formed by the lines
${\text{x}} + 2 = {\text{y}} - 1 = {\text{z}},\frac{{{\text{x}} - 3}}{5} = \frac{{\text{y}}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{{\text{z}} - 1}}{1}$and $\frac{{\text{x}}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{{\text{y}} - 3}}{3} = \frac{{{\text{z}} - 2}}{1}$ be A. Then A2 is equal to ______

L1 : x + 2 = y – 1 = z = $l$

${{\text{L}}_3}:\frac{{\text{x}}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{{\text{y}} - 3}}{5} = \frac{{{\text{z}} - 2}}{1} = {\text{n}}$
Point of intersection of L1 and L2
$\left. {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}

\end{array}} \right\}\ell = 0,\,m = - 1\,\,A( - 2,\,\,\,1,\,\,0)$
Point of intersection of L2 and L3

\end{array}} \right\}{\text{m}} = 0,{\text{n}} = - 1,{\text{B}}(3,0,1)$
Point of intersection L3 and L4
$\left. {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  { - 3{\text{n}} = \ell - 2} \\
  {3{\text{n}} + 3 = \ell + 1} \\
  {{\text{n}} + 2 = \ell }

$Ar\left( {\Delta ABC} \right) = \left| {\frac{1}{2}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}

\end{array}} \right|} \right|$
  $A = \frac{1}{2}\left| {\hat i\left( 4 \right) - \hat j\left( { - 8} \right) + \hat k\left( { - 12} \right)} \right|$
${\text{A}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {16 + 64 + 144} = \sqrt {56} $

${\text{A}^2} =  {56} $

Question: A plane which bisects the angle between the two given planes \[2x - y + 2z - 4 = 0\]and \[x + 2y + 2z - 2 = 0\], passes through the point:
Solution:
The equation of angle bisectors are,
$\frac{{x + 2y + 2z - 2}}{3} = \pm \frac{{2x - y + 2z - 4}}{3}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,x - 3y - 2 = 0$
or $3x + y + 4z - 6 = 0$
(2, -4, 1) lies on the second plane.

Question: Let P be the plane passing through the intersection of the plane\[\vec r.\left( {\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k} \right) = 5\]and\[\vec r.\left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right) = 3\], and the point (2,1 – 2). Let the position vectors of the points X and Y be \[\hat i - 2\hat j + 4\hat k\] and \[5\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k\] respectively. Then the points
Options: X and X + Y are on the same side of P
Y and Y – X are on the opposite sides of P
X and Y are on the opposite sides of P
X + Y and X – Y are on the same side of P
Solution:
\[{P_1} + \lambda {P_2} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow (x + 3y - z - 5) + \lambda (2x - y + z - 3) = 0\]
(2,1, – 2) lies on this plane
\[\therefore \;\;\lambda = 1 \Rightarrow \]plane is \[3x + 2y - 8 = 0\]

Question: The equation of the planes parallel to the plane \[x - 2y + 2z - 3 = 0\] which are at unit distance from the point (1, 2, 3) is \[ax + by + cz + d = 0\]. If \[\left( {b - d} \right) = K\left( {c - a} \right)\], then the positive value of K is ...................... .
Solution:
Let plane is \[x - 2y + 2z + \lambda = 0\]
Distance from \[\left( {1,\,\,2,\,\,3} \right) = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{\left| {\lambda + 3} \right|}}{3} = 1\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda = 0,\,\, - 6\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,a = 1,\,\,b = - 2,\,\,c = 2,\,\,d = - 6\,\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\,0\]
\[b - d = 4\,\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\, - 2,\,\,c - a = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,k = 4\,\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\, - 2\]

Question: The equation of the line through the point (0, 1, 2) and perpendicular to the line $\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{3} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 2}}$ is:
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"


$\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{3} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 2}} = r$
$ \Rightarrow \;\;P = \left( {2r + 1,\;3r - 1,\; - 2r + 1} \right)$
Direction ratio of $PQ = \left( {2r + 1,\;3r - 2,\; - 2r - 1} \right)$
Since, $\overrightarrow {QP} \bot \left( {2\widehat i + 3\widehat j - 2\widehat k} \right)$
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \;\;4r + 2 + 9r - 6 + 4r + 2 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \;\;r = \frac{2}{{17}} \\
   \Rightarrow \;\;P\left( {\frac{{21}}{{17}},\;\frac{{ - 11}}{{17}},\;\frac{{13}}{{17}}} \right) \\
\end{gathered} $
Simplified direction ratios of PQ will be $ - 3,\;4,\;3$
So, $\overrightarrow {QP} :\frac{x}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y - 1}}{4} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3}$

Question: The distance of the point (1, –5, 9) from the plane x – y + z = 5 measured along the line x = y = z is :
Solution:
Given plane x – y + z = 5 …(1)
and line x = y = z …(2)

$\because $ Given line is parallel to line PM $\therefore $ DR’s of line PM = DR’s of given line = < 1, 1, 1 >
So equation of line PM is
\[x - 1 = y + 5 = z - 9\]
Let \[{\text{M}}\left( {\lambda + 1,\lambda - 5,\lambda + 9} \right)\]
$\because $ Point M lies on the plane $x - y + z = 5$
$ \Rightarrow \left( {\lambda + 1} \right) - \left( {\lambda - 5} \right) + \left( {\lambda + 9} \right) = 5$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda + 15 = 5$
$\lambda = - 10$
$\therefore M\left( { - 9, - 15, - 1} \right)$
$\therefore PM = \sqrt {{{\left( {1 + 9} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 5 + 15} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {9 + 1} \right)}^2}} = \sqrt {100 + 100 + 100} = 10\sqrt 3 $

Question: The vector equation of the plane through the line of intersection of the planes x + y + z = 1 and 2x + 3y + 4z = 5 which is perpendicular to the plane x – y + z = 0 is:
Solution:
Equation of the plane passing through the line of intersection of $x + y + z = 1{\text{ and }}2x + 3y + 4z = 5$ is,
\[\begin{gathered}
  \left( {2x + 3y + 4z - 5} \right) + \lambda \left( {x + y + z - 1} \right) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \left( {2 + \lambda } \right)x + \left( {3 + \lambda } \right)y + \left( {4 + \lambda } \right)z + \left( { - 5 - \lambda } \right) = 0....(i) \\
  \because {\text{plane (i) is perpendicular to the plane}}\,x - y + z = 0 \\
  \therefore \left( {2 + \lambda } \right)\left( 1 \right) + \left( {3 + \lambda } \right)\left( { - 1} \right) + \left( {4 + \lambda } \right)\left( 1 \right) = 0 \\
  2 + \lambda - 3 - \lambda + 4 + \lambda = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = - 3 \\
\end{gathered} \]
Hence, equation of required plane is
$\begin{gathered}
   - x + z - 2 = 0{\text{ or }}x - z + 2 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \bar r.\left( {\hat i - \hat k} \right) + 2 = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A plane $P$ contains the line of intersection of the plane $\vec r \cdot \left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right) = 6$ and $\vec r \cdot \left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) =  - 5$. If $P$ passes through the point $\left( {0,2, - 2} \right)$, then the square of distance of the point $\left( {12,12,18} \right)$ from the plane $P$ is
Solution:
Equation of plane P is
$\left( {x + y + z - 6} \right) + \lambda \left( {2x + 3y + 4z + 5} \right) = 0$
Plane passes through the point $\left( {0,2, - 2} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,\,\left( {2 - 2 - 6} \right) + \lambda \left( {6 - 8 + 5} \right) = 0$
$ - 6 + \lambda \left( 3 \right) = 0$
$\lambda = 2$
Equation of plane P is
$\left( {x + y + z - 6} \right) + 2\left( {2x + 3y + 4z + 5} \right) = 0$
$5x + 7y + 9z + 4 = 0$
$d = \left| {\frac{{5 \times 12 + 7 \times 12 + 9 \times 18 + 4}}{{\sqrt {{5^2} + {7^2} + {9^2}} }}} \right|$
$d = \left| {\frac{{60 + 84 + 162 + 4}}{{\sqrt {25 + 49 + 81} }}} \right|$
$d = \frac{{310}}{{\sqrt {155} }}$
${d^2} = \frac{{310 \times 310}}{{155}} = 620$

Question: The distance of the point $Q\left( {0,2, - 2} \right)$ from the line passing through the point $P\left( {5, - 4,3} \right)$ and perpendicular to the lines $\vec r = \left( { - 3\hat i + 2\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k} \right),\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\vec r = \left( {\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k} \right) + \mu \left( { - \hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right),\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ is:
Solution:
A vector in the direction of the required line can be obtained by cross product of
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&3&5 \\
  { - 1}&3&2
\end{array}} \right|$
$ = - 9\hat i - 9\hat j + 9\hat k$
Required line,
$\vec r = \left( {5\hat i - 4\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) + \lambda '\left( { - 9\hat i - 9\hat j + 9\hat k} \right)$
$\vec r = \left( {5\hat i - 4\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i + \hat j - \hat k} \right)$
Now distance of $\left( {0,2, - 2} \right)$

P.V. of $P \equiv \left( {5 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {\lambda - 4} \right)\hat j + \left( {3 - \lambda } \right)\hat k$
$\overrightarrow {AP} = \left( {5 + \lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {\lambda - 6} \right)\hat j + \left( {5 - \lambda } \right)\hat k$
$\overrightarrow {AP} \cdot \left( {\hat i + \hat j - \hat k} \right) = 0$
$5 + \lambda + \lambda - 6 - 5 + \lambda = 0$
$\lambda = 2$
$\left| {\overrightarrow {AP} } \right| = \sqrt {49 + 16 + 9} $
$\left| {\overrightarrow {AP} } \right| = \sqrt {74} $

Question: The length of the perpendicular from the point (2, –1, 4) on the straight line, $\frac{{x + 3}}{{10}} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 7}} = \frac{z}{1}$ is:
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

Let P be the foot of perpendicular from point T (2, –1, 4) on the given line.
So P can be assumed as $P\left( {10\lambda - 3, - 7\lambda + 2,\lambda } \right)$

DR's of TP is proportional to $10\lambda - 5,\,\,\, - 7\lambda + 3,\,\,\lambda - 4$
⸪ TP and given lines are perpendicular, so
$\begin{gathered}
  10\left( {10\lambda - 5} \right) - 7\left( { - 7\lambda + 3} \right) + 1\left( {\lambda - 4} \right) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{2} \\
   \Rightarrow TP = \sqrt {{{\left( {10\lambda - 5} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 7\lambda + 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\lambda - 4} \right)}^2}} \\
   = \sqrt {0 + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{{49}}{4}} = \sqrt {12.5} = 3.54 \\
\end{gathered} $
Hence, the length of perpendicular is greater than 3 but less than 4.

Question: If for some \[\alpha \]\[ \in \] R, the lines \[{L_1}:\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 1}}{1}\]and \[{L_2}:\frac{{x + 2}}{\alpha } = \frac{{y + 1}}{{5 - \alpha }} = \frac{{z + 1}}{1}\] are coplanar, then the line L<sub>2</sub> passes through the point:
Solution:
Given,
\[A\;( - 1,\;2,\;1)\,\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\,B\;( - 2, - 1, - 1)\]
and \[{L_1},\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{L_2}\] are coplanar
\[\left[ {\overline {AB} \,\overline {{b_1}} \,\overline {{b_2}} } \right] = 0\]
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  { - 1}&{ - 3}&{ - 2} \\
  2&{ - 1}&1 \\
  \alpha &{5 - \alpha }&1
\end{array}} \right| = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\, - 1\;( - 1 + \alpha - 5) + 3\;(2 - \alpha ) - 2\;(10 - 2\alpha + \alpha ) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\,6 - \alpha + 6 - 3\alpha + 2\alpha - 20 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\, - 8 - 2\alpha = 0\]
\[\alpha = - 4\]
\[{L_2}:\frac{{x + 2}}{{ - 4}} = \frac{{y + 1}}{9} = \frac{{z + 1}}{1}\]
any point on L2 is:
\[( - 4\lambda - 2,\;9\lambda - 1,\;\lambda - 1) = \left( {2,\,\, - 10,\,\, - 2} \right)\] \[\]

Question: If the image of the point $\mathrm{P}(1,2, a)$ in the line $\frac{\mathrm{x}-6}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-7}{2}=\frac{7-\mathrm{z}}{2}$ is $\mathrm{Q}(5, \mathrm{~b}, \mathrm{c})$, then $\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}+ \mathrm{c}^{2}$ is equal to
Solution:
Point $\mathrm{M} \equiv\left(3, \frac{\mathrm{~b}}{2}+1, \frac{\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}}{2}\right)$ satisfies the line
$\frac{3-6}{3}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{b}}{2}+1-7}{2}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}}{2}-7}{-2}$
$-1=\frac{\mathrm{b}-12}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}-14}{-4}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{b}=8 \quad \ldots$ (1) & $\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}=18 \ldots$ (2)
Now $\mathrm{PQ} \perp \mathrm{L}$
$\Rightarrow(4 \mathrm{i}+(\mathrm{b}-2) \mathrm{j}+(\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{a}) \mathrm{k}) \cdot(3 \mathrm{i}+2 \mathrm{j}-2 \mathrm{k})=0$
$\Rightarrow 12+2(\mathrm{~b}-2)-2(\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{a})=0$
$\Rightarrow 6+(\mathrm{b}-2)-(\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{a})=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}+4=0$
$\Rightarrow 8-\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}+4=0$
$$
\begin{equation*}
\Rightarrow \mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}=12 \tag{3}
\end{equation*}
$$
From (2) & (3)
$\mathrm{c}=15 \& \mathrm{a}=3$
So $\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}+\mathrm{c}^{2}=9+64+225=298$

Question: A line with direction ratios $1,-1,2$ intersects the lines $\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+1}{3}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-2}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{4}$ at the points P and Q , respectively. If the length of the line segment PQ is $\alpha$, then $225 \alpha^{2}$ is equal to:
Solution:
$\mathrm{P}(2 \lambda, 3 \lambda, 3 \lambda-1)$ and $\mathrm{Q}(-\mu-1, \mu+2,4 \mu)$
D.R of PQ $2 \lambda+\mu+1,3 \lambda-\mu-2,3 \lambda-4 \mu-1$, $\frac{2 \lambda+\mu+1}{1}=\frac{3 \lambda-\mu-2}{-1}=\frac{3 \lambda-4 \mu-1}{2}$
Solving $\lambda=\frac{1}{5}$ and $\mu=\frac{-8}{15}$
$\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{2}{5}, \frac{3}{5}, \frac{-2}{5}\right) \& \mathrm{Q}\left(\frac{-7}{15}, \frac{22}{15}, \frac{-32}{15}\right)$
$\mathrm{PQ}^{2}=\left(\frac{13}{15}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{13}{15}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{26}{15}\right)^{2}$
$\alpha^{2}=\frac{1014}{225} \therefore 225 \alpha^{2}=1014$

Tags.Subject: Math
Tags.Topic: Equation of lines in 3D and vector form

Question: Let the equation of plane passing through the line of intersection of the planes $x + 2y + az = 2$ and $x - y + z = 3$ be $5x - 11y + bz = 6a - 1$. For $c \in \mathbb{Z}$, if the distance of this plane from the point $\left( {a, - c,c} \right)$ is $\frac{2}{{\sqrt a }}$, then $\frac{{a + b}}{c}$ is equal to
Solution:
$\left( {x + 2y + az - 2} \right) + \lambda \left( {x - y + z - 3} \right) = 0$
$\frac{{1 + \lambda }}{5} = \frac{{2 - \lambda }}{{ - 11}} = \frac{{a + \lambda }}{b} = \frac{{2 + 3\lambda }}{{6a - 1}}$
$\lambda = - \frac{7}{2},a = 3,b = 1$
$\frac{2}{{\sqrt a }} = \left| {\frac{{5a + 11c - bc - 6a + 1}}{{\sqrt {25 + 121 + 1} }}} \right|$
$c = - 1$
$\therefore \,\,\frac{{a + b}}{c} = \frac{{3 + 1}}{{ - 1}} = - 4$

Question: Let $P$ and $Q$ be the points on the line $\frac{{x + 3}}{8} = \frac{{y - 4}}{2} = \frac{{z + 1}}{2}$ which are at a distance of 6 units from the point $R\left( {1,2,3} \right)$. If the centroid of the triangle $PQR$ is $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right),$ then ${\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2} + {\gamma ^2}$ is:
Solution:
$P\left( {8\lambda - 3,2\lambda + 4,2\lambda - 1} \right)$
$PR = 6$
${\left( {8\lambda - 4} \right)^2} + {\left( {2\lambda + 2} \right)^2} + {\left( {2\lambda - 4} \right)^2} = 36$
$\lambda = 0,1$
Hence $P\left( { - 3,4, - 1} \right)$ & $Q\left( {5,6,1} \right)$
Centroid of $\Delta PQR = \left( {1,4,1} \right) \equiv \left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$
$\left( {{\alpha ^2} + {\beta ^2} + {\gamma ^2}} \right) = 18$

Tags.Subject: Math
Tags.Topic: Equation of lines in 3D and vector form

Question: If (a, b, c) is the image of the point (1, 2, –3) in the line, \[\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 3}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{z}{{ - 1}}\], then a + b + c is
Solution:
\[\overline {PM} \bot (2\hat i - 2\hat j - \hat k)\]
\[ \Rightarrow (2\lambda - 2).2 + (1 - 2\lambda )\;( - 2) + (3 - \lambda )\;( - 1) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 4\lambda - 4 + 4\lambda - 2 + \lambda - 3 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 9\lambda = 9 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow M\;(1,1, - 1)\]
Now, \[P'\]= 2M – P
               = 2(1, 1, –1) – (1, 2, –3)
               = (1, 0,1)
a + b + c = 2

Question:
Let the shortest distance between the lines
\[\frac{{x - 3}}{3} = \frac{{y - \alpha }}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{1}\,and\,\frac{{x + 3}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{y + 7}}{2} = \frac{{z - \beta }}{4}\] be \[3\sqrt {30} \]. Then the positive value of 5α + β is
Solution:
A(3, α , 3) & B(–3, –7, β )
\[\overrightarrow {BA} = 6\hat i + (\alpha + 7)\hat j + (3 - \beta )\hat k\]
\[\vec p \times \vec q = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  3&{ - 1}&1 \\
  { - 3}&2&4
\end{array}} \right|\]
\[\frac{{\left| {\overrightarrow {BA} .\,(\vec p \times \vec q)} \right|}}{{\left| {\vec p \times \vec q} \right|}} = 3\sqrt {30} \]
\[36 + 15(\alpha + 7) - 3(3 - \beta ) = {\left( {3\sqrt {30} } \right)^2}\]
\[36 + 15\alpha + 105 - 9 + 3\beta = 270\]
\[15\alpha + 3\beta = 138\]
5α + β = 46

Question: Let P \[\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)\] be the image of the point Q(l, 6, 4) in the line \[\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3}.\] Then \[2\alpha  + \beta  + \gamma \] is equal to _______ .
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  A\left( {t,\;2t + 1,\;3t + 2} \right) \\
  \overrightarrow {QA} = \left( {t - 1} \right)\hat i + \left( {2t - 5} \right)\hat j + \left( {3t - 2} \right)\hat k \\
  \overrightarrow {QA} ,\vec b = 0 \\
  \left( {t - 1} \right) + 2\left( {2t - 5} \right) + 3\left( {3t - 2} \right) = 0 \\
  14t = 17 \\
  \alpha = \frac{{20}}{{14}}\;\;\beta = \frac{{12}}{{14}}\;\;\gamma = \frac{{102}}{{14}} \\
  2\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \frac{{154}}{{14}} = 11 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Let P be plane \[lx + my + nz = 0\] containing the line, $\frac{{1 - x}}{1} = \frac{{y + 4}}{2} = \frac{{z + 2}}{3}$. If plane P divides the line segment AB joining points \[A\left( { - 3,\,\, - 6,\,\,1} \right)\] and \[B\left( {2,\,\,4,\,\, - 3} \right)\] in ratio \[k:1\] then the value of k is equal to
Solution:
Point $C$ is \[\left( {\frac{{2k - 3}}{{k + 1}},\,\,\frac{{4k - 6}}{{k + 1}},\,\,\frac{{ - 3k + 1}}{{k + 1}}} \right)\]
The equation of the line is
\[\frac{{x - 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{y + 4}}{2} = \frac{{z + 2}}{3}\] and
Plane \[lx + my + nz = 0\]
\[l\left( { - 1} \right) + m\left( 2 \right) + n\left( 3 \right) = 0\]
\[ - l + 2m + 3n = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 1 \right)\]
It also satisfy point \[\left( {1,\,\, - 4,\,\, - 2} \right)\]
\[l - 4m - 2n = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right)\]
Solving (1) and (2)
\[2m + 3n = 4m + 2n\]
\[n = 2m\]
\[l - 4m - 4m = 0\]
\[l = 8m\]
\[\frac{l}{8} = \frac{m}{1} = \frac{n}{2}\]
\[l:m:n = 8:1:2\]
Plane is \[8x + y + 2z = 0\]
It will satisfy point \[C\]
\[8\left( {\frac{{2k - 3}}{{k + 1}}} \right) + \left( {\frac{{4k - 6}}{{k + 1}}} \right) + 2\left( {\frac{{ - 3k + 1}}{{k + 1}}} \right) = 0\]
\[16k - 24 + 4k - 6 - 6k + 2 = 0\]
\[14k = 28\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,k = 2\]

Question: The distance of the point \[\left( {1,\,\,-2,\,\,3} \right)\] from the plane \[x-y + z = 5\] measured parallel to a line, whose direction ratios are \[2,\,\,3,\,\,-6\] is:
Solution:
Now,
\[x-y + z = 5\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {1 + 2\lambda } \right) + 2-3\lambda + 3-6\lambda = 5\]
\[ \Rightarrow 6-7\lambda = 5 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{7}\]
\[P = \left( {\frac{9}{7},\,\,-\frac{{11}}{7},\,\,\frac{{15}}{7}} \right)\]
\[AP = \sqrt {{{\left( {1-\frac{9}{7}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {-2 + \frac{{11}}{7}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {3-\frac{{15}}{7}} \right)}^2}} \]
\[AP = \sqrt {\left( {\frac{4}{{49}}} \right) + \frac{9}{{49}} + \frac{{36}}{{49}}} \]
\[\therefore AP = 1\]

Question:
Let the lines
\[{L_1}:\;\;\vec r\; = \;\lambda \;(\hat i + \;2\hat j + \;3\hat k),\;\lambda \; \in \;R\]
\[{L_2}:\;\;\vec r\; = \;(\hat i + \;3\hat j + \;\hat k)\; + \;\mu \;(\hat i + \;\hat j + \;5\hat k);\;\mu \; \in \;R\]
intersect at the point S. If a plane \[ax\; + \;by\; - z\; + \;d = \;0\] passes through S and is parallel to both the lines \[{L_1}\] and \[{L_2},\] then the value of \[a + \;b + \;d\] is equal to ............... .
Solution:
Both the lines lie in the same plane

\[\therefore \] equation of the plane
\[\left| {\,\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  x&y&z \\
  1&2&3 \\
  1&1&5
\end{array}\,} \right| = \;0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\;7x - \;2y - \;z = \;0\]
\[\therefore \;\;\;a + \;b + \;d\; = \;5\]

Question: If the line, \[\frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y + 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + \lambda }}{{ - 2}}\] lies in the plane, \[2x - 4y + 3z = 2,\] then the shortest distance between this line and the line, \[\frac{{x - 1}}{{12}} = \frac{y}{9} = \frac{z}{4}\] is:
Solution:
\[\frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y + 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + \lambda }}{{ - 2}} = k\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {{\text{let}}} \right)\]
Let P be any point on the line
\[P = \left( {k + 3,\,\, - k - 2,\,\, - 2k - \lambda } \right)\]
P lies on the plane \[2x - 4y + 3z = 2\]
\[2\left( {k + 3} \right) - 4\left( { - k - 2} \right) + 3\left( { - 2k - \lambda } \right) = 2\]
\[2k + 6 + 4k + 8 - 6k - 3\lambda = 2\]
\[14 - 3\lambda = 2\]
\[3\lambda = 12\]
\[\lambda = 4\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,{\text{line}}\,\,{\text{one}}\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,\,\frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y + 2}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + 4}}{{ - 2}}\]
Another line is \[\frac{{x - 1}}{{12}} = \frac{y}{9} = \frac{z}{4}\]
Shortest distance \[ = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {{x_1} - {x_2}}&{{y_1} - {y_2}}&{{z_1} - {z_2}} \\
  {{l_1}}&{{m_1}}&{{n_1}} \\
  {{l_2}}&{{m_2}}&{{n_2}}
\end{array}} \right|\]
                            \[ = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {3 - 1}&{ - 2 - 0}&{ - 4 - 0} \\
  1&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \\
  {12}&9&4
\end{array}} \right|\]

                            \[ = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  2&{ - 2}&{ - 4} \\
  1&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \\
  {12}&9&4
\end{array}} \right|\]
                            \[\begin{gathered}
   = 2\left( {14} \right) + 2\left( {4 + 24} \right) - 4\left( {9 + 12} \right) \\
   = 28 + 56 - 84 \\
   = 0 \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\therefore \] Shortest distance is 0

Question: Let Q be the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point P (1, 2, 3) to the plane \[x + \;2y + z = \;14.\] If R is a point on the plane such that \[\angle PRQ = \;60^\circ ,\] then the area of \[\Delta PQR\] is equal to :
Solution:
Length of perpendicular
\[PQ = \;\left| {\frac{{1 + 4 + \;3 - \;14}}{{\sqrt 6 }}} \right| = \;\sqrt 6 \]
\[QR = \;(PQ)\;\cot \;60^\circ = \;\sqrt 2 \]
\[\therefore \] Area of \[\Delta PQR = \;\frac{1}{2}\;(PQ)\;(QR) = \;\sqrt 3 \]

Question: The distance of the point (1, 0, 2) from the point of intersection of the line\[\frac{{x - 2}}{3} = \frac{{y + 1}}{4} = \frac{{z - 2}}{{12}}\] and the plane \[x - y + z = 16,\] is
Solution:
Let, Point of intersection is
\[\left( {3\lambda + 2,\,\,4\lambda - 1,\,\,12\lambda + 2} \right)\]
It lies on the plane \[x - y + z = 16\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3\lambda + 2 - 4\lambda + 1 + 12\lambda + 2 = 16\]
\[11\lambda = 11\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda = 1\]
So, Point of intersection is \[\left( {5,\,\,3,\,\,14} \right)\]
Its distance from point (1, 0, 2) \[ = \sqrt {16 + 9 + 144} = \sqrt {169} = 13\]

Question: Let $\left( {\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma } \right)$ be the mirror image of the point $\left( {2,3,5} \right)$ in the line$\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z - 3}}{4}.$ Then, $2\alpha + 3\beta + 4\gamma $ is equal to
Solution:
$\because \,\,\overrightarrow {PR} \bot \left( {2,3,4} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,\overrightarrow {PR} .\left( {2,3,4} \right) = 0$
$\left( {\alpha - 2,\beta - 3,\gamma - 5} \right).\left( {2,3,4} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 2\alpha + 3\beta + 4\gamma = 4 + 9 + 20 = 33$

Tags.Subject: Math

Question: Let a straight line L pass through the point P(2,–1,3) and be perpendicular to the lines \[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{1} = \frac{{z - 3}}{{ - 2}}\,\,and\,\,\frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y - 2}}{3} = \frac{{z + 2}}{4}\]. If the line L intersects the yz-plane at the point Q, then the distance between the points P and Q is :
Solution:
Vector parallel to ‘L’
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&1&{ - 2} \\
  1&3&4
\end{array}} \right| = 10\hat i - 10\hat j + 5\hat k\]
\[ = 5\left( {2\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k} \right)\]
Equation of ‘L’
\[\frac{{x - 2}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 3}}{1} = \lambda \,\,(say)\]
Let Q(2λ + 2, –2λ – 1, λ + 3)
⇒ 2λ + 2 = 0 ⇒ λ = –1
⇒ Q (0, 1, 2)
d(P, Q) = 3

Tags.Subject: Math
Tags.Topic: Equation of lines in 3D and vector form

Question: If the mirror image of the point (1, 3, 5) with respect to the plane \[4x - 5y + 2z = 8\] is \[\left( {\alpha ,\,\,\beta ,\,\,\gamma } \right),\] then \[5\left( {\alpha  + \beta  + \gamma } \right)\] equals :
Solution:
\[\frac{{\alpha - 1}}{4} = \frac{{\beta - 3}}{{ - 5}} = \frac{{\gamma - 5}}{2} = \frac{{ - 2\left( {4 - 15 + 10 - 8} \right)}}{{\left( {16 + 25 + 4} \right)}} = \frac{2}{5}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{{13}}{5},\beta = 1,\gamma = \frac{{29}}{5}\]
So, \[5\left( {\alpha + \beta + \gamma } \right) = 5\alpha + 5\beta + 5\gamma = 13 + 5 + 29 = 47\]

Question: The distance of the point (1, 3, –7) form the plane passing through the point (1, –1, –1), having normal perpendicular to both the lines \[\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y + 2}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 4}}{3}\] and \[\frac{{x - 2}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + 7}}{{ - 1}},\] is:
Solution:
Given equation of lines are \[\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y + 2}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 4}}{3}\] and \[\frac{{x - 2}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 1}} = \frac{{z + 7}}{{ - 1}}\]
Let \[{\bar n_1} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k\,\,\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\,{\bar n_2} = 2\hat i - \hat j - \hat k\]
\[\therefore \] Any vector \[\hat n\] perpendicular to both \[{n_1},\,\,{n_2}\] is given by
\[\hat n = \left| {{{\bar n}_1} \times {{\bar n}_2}} \right|\]
\[\widehat n = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\widehat i}&{\widehat j}&{\widehat k} \\
  1&{ - 2}&3 \\
  2&{ - 1}&{ - 1}
\end{array}} \right| = 5\widehat i - \widehat j\left( { - 7} \right) + 3\widehat k\]
Equation of plane \[5\left( {x - 1} \right) + 7\left( {y + 1} \right) + 3\left( {z + 1} \right) = 0\]
\[5x + 7y + 3z + 5 = 0\]
Perpendicular distance of a point (1, 3, –7) from the plane is \[ = \frac{{\left| {5 + 21 - 21 + 5} \right|}}{{\sqrt {25 + 49 + 9} }}\]
                                                                                                 \[ = \frac{{10}}{{\sqrt {83} }}\]

Question: The square of the distance of the image of the point (6, 1, 5) in the line $\frac{{x - 1}}{3} = \frac{y}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{4},$ from the origin is _______.
Solution:
Let $M\left( {3\lambda + 1,\,2\lambda ,\,4\lambda + 2} \right)$
$\overrightarrow {AM} \cdot \vec b = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 9\lambda - 15 + 4\lambda - 2 + 16\lambda - 12 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 29\lambda = 29$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = 1$
$M\left( {4,2,6} \right),\,I = \left( {2,3,7} \right)$
Required distance $ = \sqrt {4 + 9 + 49} = \sqrt {62} $

Tags.Subject: Math
Tags.Topic: Equation of lines in 3D and vector form

Question: Let the line passing through the points $P\left( {2, - 1,2} \right)$ and $Q\left( {5,3,4} \right)$ meet the plane $x - y + z = 4$ at the point R. Then the distance of the point R from the plane $x + 2y + 3z + 2 = 0$ measured parallel to the line $\frac{{x - 7}}{2} = \frac{{y + 3}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{1}$ is equal to
Solution:
Line: $\frac{{x - 5}}{3} = \frac{{y - 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 4}}{2} = \lambda $
$R\left( {3\lambda + 5,4\lambda + 3,2\lambda + 4} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,3\lambda + 5 - 4\lambda - 3 + 2\lambda + 4 = 4$
$\lambda + 6 = 4$
$\therefore \,\,\lambda = - 2$
$\therefore \,\,R \equiv \left( { - 1,5,0} \right)$
Line: $\frac{{x + 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 5}}{2} = \frac{{z - 0}}{1} = \mu $
Point $T = \left( {2\mu - 1,2\mu - 5,\mu } \right)$
It lies on plane
$2\mu - 1 + 2\left( {2\mu - 5} \right) + 3\mu + 2 = 0$
$\mu = 1$
$\therefore \,\,T = \left( {1, - 3,1} \right)$
$\therefore \,\,RT = 3$

Question: Let a line L passing through the point $\mathrm{P}(1,1,1)$ be perpendicular to the lines $\frac{x-4}{4}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-1}{1}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{x}-17}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-71}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{0}$. Let the line L intersect the yz -plane at the point Q . Another line parallel to L and passing through the point $\mathrm{S}(1,0,-1)$ intersects the yz -plane at the point R . Then the square of the area of the parallelogram PQRS is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
Solution:
$\quad \mathrm{d}_{1}=\overline{<4,1,1>}$ and $\mathrm{d}_{2}=<1,1,0>$
$\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{d}_{1} \times \mathrm{d}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\mathrm{i} & \mathrm{j} & \mathrm{k} \\ 4 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 0\end{array}\right|=\langle-1,1,3\rangle$
Line L passes through $\mathrm{P}<1,1,1>$ with $\mathrm{d}_{2}=<-1,1,3>$
$r(\mathrm{t})=<1,1,1>+\mathrm{t}<-1,1,3>$
$=<1-\mathrm{t}, 1+\mathrm{t}, 1+3 \mathrm{t}>$
$=<1-\mathrm{t}, 1+\mathrm{t}, 1+3 \mathrm{t}>$
For point $\mathrm{Q} ; \mathrm{x}=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=1$
$\mathrm{Q}=<0,2,4>$
Another
Line parallel to L passes through $\mathrm{S}<1,0,-1>$
with $\mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{L}}=<-1,1,3>$
$\mathrm{r}^{\prime}(\mathrm{u})=<1,0,-1>+\mu<-1,1,3>$
$=<1-\mu, \mu,-1+3 \mu>$
for point $\mathrm{R}, \mathrm{x}=0$
$\Rightarrow \mu=1$
$\mathrm{R}<0,1,2>$
Area of parallelogram with adjacent vectors $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PS}}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}=\langle-1,1,3\rangle$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PS}}=<0,-1,-2>$
Area of parallelogram
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{PS}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\mathrm{i} & \mathrm{j} & \mathrm{k} \\ -1 & 1 & 3 \\ 0 & -1 & -2\end{array}\right|=<1,-2,1>$
Area $=\sqrt{1^{2}+(-2)^{2}+1^{2}}=\sqrt{6}$

Question: The largest value of a, for which the perpendicular distance of the plane containing the lines \[\vec r = \;(\hat i + \;\hat j)\; + \;\lambda \;(\hat i + \;a\hat j - \hat k)\] and \[\vec r = \;(\hat i + \;\hat j)\; + \;\mu \;( - \hat i + \;\hat j - a\hat k)\] from the point (2, 1, 4) is \[\sqrt 3 ,\] is ……….. .
Solution:
\[\vec r = \;(\hat i + \;\hat j)\; + \;\lambda \;(\hat i + \;a\hat j - \;\hat k)\]
\[\vec r = \;(\hat i + \;\hat j)\; + \;\mu \;( - \hat i + \;\hat j - \;a\hat k)\]
D.R’s of plane containing these lines is
\[\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  1&a&{ - 1} \\
  { - 1}&1&{ - a}
\end{array}} \right| = \;\hat i\;(1 - {a^2}) - \;\hat j\;( - a - 1)\; + \;\hat k\;(1 + a)\]
\[\vec n\; = \;(1 - a)\;\hat i + \;\hat j + \;\hat k\]
One point in plane : (1, 1, 0)
\[\therefore \] equation of plane is
\[(1 - a)\;(x - 1)\; + \;(y - 1)\; + \;(z - 0)\; = \;0\]
\[(1 - a)\;x + \;y + z + a - 2 = \;0\]
\[\therefore \;\;D = \;\frac{{|(1 - a)\;2 + 1 + 4 + a - 2|}}{{\sqrt {{{(1 - a)}^2} + 1 + 1} }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;|5 - a|\; = \;\sqrt 3 \;.\;\sqrt {{a^2} - 2a + \;3} \]
\[ \Rightarrow \;{a^2} + \;2a\; - \;8\; = \;0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;a = \;2,\; - \;4\]
\[\therefore \] largest value of a = 2

Question: Let the values of p, for which the shortest distance between the lines \[\frac{{x + 1}}{3} = \frac{y}{4} = \frac{z}{5}\] and \[\overrightarrow r  = \left( {p\widehat i + 2\widehat j + \widehat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {2\widehat i + 3\widehat j + 4\widehat k} \right)\] is \[\frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\], be a, b, (a < b). Then the length of the latus rectum of the ellipse \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1\] is:-
Solution:
Shortest distance = \[\left| {\frac{{\left( {\bar a - \bar b} \right).\left( {\bar p \times \bar q} \right)}}{{\left| {\bar p \times \bar q} \right|}}} \right|\]
Where
\[\overrightarrow a = - \widehat i + 0\widehat j + 0\widehat k \]
\[\overline b = p\widehat i + 2\widehat j + \widehat  k \   =>   \      \overline a - \overline b = \left( { - 1 - p} \right)\widehat i - 2\widehat j - \widehat k\]
\[\overline p = 3\widehat i + 4\widehat j + 5\widehat k\]
\[\overline q = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat j + 4\widehat k\]
\[\frac{1}{\sqrt 6} = \frac{{\left| { - 1 - p + 4 - 1} \right|}}{{\sqrt 6 }}\]
\[\left| { - p + 2} \right| = 1\]
\[p = 3\,\,\,\,\,\& \,\,\,\,\,p = 1\]
\[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{1^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{3^3}}} = 1\]
\[L.R = \frac{{2{a^2}}}{b} = \frac{{2 \times 1}}{3} = \frac{2}{3}\]

Question:
A plane P is parallel to two lines whose direction ratios are \[ - 2,\;1,\; - 3,\] and 
\[ - 1,\;2,\; - 2\] and it contains the point \[(2,\;2,\; - 2).\] Let P intersect the co-ordinate axes at the points A, B, C making the intercepts \[\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma .\] If V is the volume of the tetrahedron OABC, where O is the origin and \[p = \alpha  + \beta  + \gamma ,\] then the ordered pair (V, p) is equal to
Solution:
Normal of plane P:
\[ = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  { - 2}&1&{ - 3} \\
  { - 1}&2&{ - 2}
\end{array}} \right| = 4\hat i - \hat j - 3\hat k\]
Equation of plane P which passes through \[(2,\;2,\; - 2)\]
is \[4x - y - 3z - 12 = 0\]
Now, \[A\;(3,\;0,\;0),\;B\;(0,\; - 12,\;0),\;C\;(0,\;0,\; - 4)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \alpha = 3,\,\,\beta = - 12,\,\,\gamma = - 4\]
\[ \Rightarrow p = \alpha + \beta + \gamma = - \,13\]
Now, volume of tetrahedron OABC
\[V = \left| {\frac{1}{6}\;\overrightarrow {OA} \,.\;(\overrightarrow {OB} \;\, \times \,\,\overrightarrow {OC} )} \right| = 24\]
\[(V,\;p)\; = \;(24,\; - 13)\]

Question: The lines \[x = ay - 1 = z - 2\] and \[x = 3y - 2 = bz - 2,\,\,\left( {ab \ne 0} \right)\] are coplanar, if ?
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

Lines are $x = ay - 1 = z - 2$
$\therefore \,\,\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - \frac{1}{a}}}{{\frac{1}{a}}} = \frac{{z - 2}}{1}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( {\text{i}} \right)$
and $x = 3y - 2 = bz - 2$
$\therefore \,\,\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - \frac{2}{3}}}{{\frac{1}{3}}} = \frac{{z - \frac{2}{b}}}{{\frac{1}{b}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,....\left( {{\text{ii}}} \right)$
$\because $ The lines are co-planar
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  0&{\frac{{ - 1}}{a} + \frac{2}{3}}&{ - 2 + \frac{2}{b}} \\
  1&{\frac{1}{a}}&1 \\
  1&{\frac{1}{3}}&{\frac{1}{b}}
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$\therefore \,\,\,\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  0&{\frac{2}{3} - \frac{1}{a}}&{\frac{2}{b} - 2} \\
  0&{\frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{3}}&{1 - \frac{1}{b}} \\
  1&{\frac{1}{3}}&{\frac{1}{b}}
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$\therefore \,\,\frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{{ab}} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow b = 1$ and $a \in R - \left\{ 0 \right\}$

Question:
A solution of phenol in chloroform when treated with aqueous NaOH gives compound P as a major product. The mass percentage of carbon in P is .......... (to the nearest integer)
(Atomic mass: C = 12; H = 1 ; O = 16)
Solution:
Mass of Salicylaldehyde = 12 × 7 + 6 × 1 + 16 × 2 = 122
Mass of carbon = 12 × 7 = 84
The mass % of carbon \[ = \frac{{84}}{{122}} \times 100 = 68.85\% \].
